Service Engine application logs apply the same structured fields at the data-plane layer, with each entry recording Timestamp, Resource Name, Resource Type, Event Code, and Description for virtual service and pool events. This extends structured logging beyond administrative actions on the Controller to application-layer events processed by the Service Engines.

The Controller's global event log, accessible via Operations > All Events, displays events across all objects including deleted objects and sub-objects such as profiles and certificates. Object-specific event logs are also available from the Events tab on detail pages for Virtual Services, Pools, Service Engines, and Controllers, automatically filtered to that object. The analytics API endpoint /api/analytics/logs retrieves event logs with configurable type, duration (up to one year), page_size, and end parameters to support queries across specific time periods.

Avi Controller and Service Engines log audit records for trusted channel initiation, termination, and function failures to Events, Syslog, and Splunk. The Avi Web Application Firewall (WAF) logs matched application-layer events including matched variable name and matched variable content, with audit log parts configurable to include full event data. Avi Controller also logs denial of service attack events with event_id, attack_count, threshold values, and alert level.

The Controller's Syslog-Audit-Persistence alert action template supports forwarding events to external syslog servers for audit compliance. Note that syslog notifications push alerts but do not automatically export virtual service logs; virtual service logs must be pulled from an external logging system through the API or scripted to push from the Controller to a remote destination.

### Control AU-03(01)

> Content of Audit Records | Additional Audit Information Generate audit records containing the following additional information: [Assignment: organization-defined additional information].

**SCF Controls:** MON-03.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF protects sensitive and regulated data in log files through a layered approach that spans design philosophy, access controls, data masking, centralized log management, and cryptographic protections. The Consumption layer, including VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), vSphere namespaces, and the Kubernetes control plane, extends these protections to workload infrastructure running on the VCF platform.

At the hypervisor layer, VMware ESX log files are not encrypted at rest because they are designed not to contain sensitive data such as passwords or encryption keys. This design principle reduces the risk of sensitive data exposure in host-level logs by avoiding the problem at the source. The platform documentation acknowledges that core files and audit logs may contain sensitive information, which drives the design of access restrictions and handling procedures around log artifacts. When support bundles or core files are collected, administrators should be aware that these artifacts might contain sensitive information and handle them accordingly. vSphere also supports encrypting Fault Tolerance log traffic to reduce exposure of sensitive virtual machine state data during replication operations. This is configured via the Encrypted FT option in VM Options; placing Fault Tolerance logging traffic on a dedicated private network provides additional network-level isolation.

VCF Operations for Networks includes a control that allows Admin role users to view and enable or disable Personally Identifiable Information (PII) in audit logs. This capability gives administrators direct control over whether sensitive identity data is recorded in log output, reducing exposure of regulated data at the point of generation.

At the Kubernetes API server layer, VKS clusters support a configurable audit policy that determines what data appears in API server audit logs. Audit policy rules are defined in a configuration file and support four logging levels: None (no event recorded), Metadata (request metadata only, without request or response bodies), Request (request metadata and body), and RequestResponse (full metadata, request, and response bodies). Applying the Metadata level to sensitive resource types such as Secrets and ConfigMaps allows administrators to capture meaningful audit trails while keeping the contents of sensitive resources out of log records. Audit log files are written to a protected path on the control plane node using the --audit-log-path flag, and the Kubernetes audit logger records all actions taken by the API for later analysis in the event of a security incident.

Access to log data is controlled through role-based access at multiple levels. VCF Log Management requires permissions to be granted to VCF SSO users and groups before they can access the instance, restricting who can view and search log data. VCF Operations provides role-based access to audit data, restricting who can view users, objects, and collected information. Auditing capabilities are designed to meet audit requirements for business-critical applications that contain sensitive data. Audit data can be filtered by User ID, User Name, Auth Source, Session, Client IP, Category, and Message, allowing administrators to scope access to log content and limit exposure of sensitive fields. Event auditing registers each platform interaction, increasing user accountability by tracking who accessed or modified log-related configurations. At the Kubernetes layer, access to log data through the API, such as retrieving pod logs with kubectl logs or querying cluster events, is governed by Kubernetes RBAC. ClusterRoles and custom roles restrict which principals are permitted to retrieve log output, reducing the exposure of sensitive data that workloads write to standard output. Audit log files on control plane node filesystem paths are subject to host operating system file permissions.

Centralized log collection through VCF Operations reduces the number of locations where sensitive log data resides. Rather than leaving application log messages distributed across local file systems on individual components, VCF Log Management aggregates log sources into a managed environment. The VCF Log Management UI supports log source configuration and partitioning, which allows administrators to isolate sensitive log streams from general operational data. For workloads running in vSphere namespaces, the LogAgentConfiguration Custom Resource can be applied to a namespace to stream syslog messages from vSphere Pods to a remote syslog server; each forwarded message carries structured metadata fields including namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name. On VKS clusters, Fluent Bit logging agents deployed in the vmware-system-logging namespace collect and forward logs from cluster components. Fluent Bit tags Kubernetes API server audit logs separately from container logs and Linux daemon logs, which allows different forwarding, access control, and retention policies to be applied to audit data; Fluent Bit supports forwarding to VCF Log Management, Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk.

For log data in transit, VCF supports forwarding logs to external syslog targets using TLS encryption. NSX Manager syslog supports the TLS protocol and requires port 6514 to be open on both the NSX Manager and the remote log server for encrypted log transmission. Fluent Bit on VKS clusters supports TLS for log forwarding to syslog targets. When forwarding to VCF Log Management, Fluent Bit connects via CFAPI on port 9543 for TLS-encrypted connections or port 9000 for non-TLS connections. For environments that require validated cryptographic protections, VCF Log Management supports FIPS Compliance Mode as a deployment option. When enabled, this mode applies FIPS-validated cryptographic algorithms to data handling within the log management infrastructure. Kubernetes documentation recommends deploying cryptographic protections in high-assurance environments to help keep logs tamper-resistant and confidential.

Log retention and lifecycle management help reduce exposure of any inadvertently logged sensitive data. VCF Operations allows administrators to edit log file folders, limit retained log size, and set logging levels. The log.keepOld parameter controls how many log files are retained, and administrators can limit log file size on virtual machines. By default, ESX log files are stored in an in-memory file system and are lost when the host reboots; configuring persistent logging to a datastore preserves audit log continuity across host restarts and reduces the risk of losing sensitive audit data. These controls reduce the window during which sensitive data remains on disk.

NSX audit log messages include a structured data field (audit="true") that distinguishes audit records from operational logs, enabling targeted retention and access policies for audit-sensitive content.

Pod Security Admission (PSA) on VKS clusters provides an additional mechanism for generating security-relevant audit annotations. In PSA audit mode, security policy violations trigger an audit annotation added to the event recorded in the Kubernetes API server audit log without blocking the workload. This allows administrators to identify workloads that violate Pod security standards while maintaining operations, and those audit annotations can be captured and forwarded through the Fluent Bit pipeline for centralized review.

Kubernetes documentation recommends that applications running on VKS avoid logging Secret values in the clear after reading them from environment variables or mounted volumes, so that credentials and other regulated data do not appear in container standard output or standard error streams. This is design-time guidance rather than a platform enforcement mechanism; organizations deploying workloads on VKS are responsible for implementing log sanitization at the application level.

Defining what counts as sensitive log content in the organization's context, configuring the toggles per log destination, operating access policy on log management infrastructure, defining and enforcing Kubernetes audit policy rules for sensitive resource types, configuring application workloads to avoid logging credentials or regulated data, and verifying that log storage systems apply appropriate access controls and encryption at rest sit with the organization.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend protects sensitive and regulated data in its log files through TLS-encrypted log forwarding, structured log content, and integration with centralized log management.

For log data in transit, the Malware Prevention Service VM can be configured to forward log messages to a remote logging server using TLS with mutual certificate authentication. The configuration accepts logging server IP address, protocol, log level, server CA certificate, client CA certificate, client certificate, and client key. The Malware Prevention Service VM currently supports only the SECURITY message ID for log filtering when transmitting to a remote server, which limits the log categories forwarded.

Malware Prevention Service event logs record a detailed set of file analysis lifecycle events: file intercepted, verdict cache hit, file sent for local static analysis, file sent for cloud dynamic analysis, verdict obtained, and policy enforced. Each entry includes structured JSON attributes covering file hashes (SHA256, SHA1, MD5), file metadata (fileName, fileType, fileSize), malware classification, malware family, error codes, HTTP domain, HTTP method, user agent, file upload status, submission status, verdict classification (such as BENIGN), and analysis score. Given the sensitivity of this data, access to these logs at the receiving system should be restricted to authorized personnel.

VMware vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS can send events to external syslog consumers by enabling the ids_events_to_syslog setting on an IDS/IPS profile; the setting is disabled by default. IDS/IPS logs include the user ID whose traffic generated the event, providing identity context alongside detection metadata. vDefend components write to log files in /var/log, with syslog messages conforming to RFC 5424 on vDefend appliances and RFC 3164 on ESX hosts. Both NSX Manager and ESX hosts must be configured to forward syslog to the central log repository for complete IDS/IPS event coverage.

Network Detection and Response supports SSL verification for event logs sent over HTTPS, protecting detection event data in transit when logs are forwarded to an external receiving system.

Identity Firewall Event Log Sources can be configured to collect logs from Active Directory Event Log Sources or from VCF Log Management. VCF Log Management provides a centralized log management framework for vDefend; logs are forwarded by configuring VCF Log Management as a syslog server, where centralized access controls, retention policies, and enterprise-wide log governance can be applied.

While vDefend provides TLS transport protection for Malware Prevention logs, IDS/IPS event forwarding with identity context, SSL-verified transport in Network Detection and Response, and integration paths to centralized logging, the broader log protection framework including enterprise-wide retention policies, centralized access governance, and log access controls at the receiving system requires VCF platform components and organizational processes outside vDefend's scope.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) addresses this control through a combination of by-design log content restrictions, access controls, log storage options, and database-engine-level audit configurations that reduce the exposure of sensitive data in audit and operational log files.

DSM audit logs are structured around a defined set of fields: Component, Event Type, Event Details, Event Time (UTC), Status, and Source. The Source field records either the initiating user's email identifier or "System" for automated operations. Gateway audit log files contain standard Kubernetes audit events, and the structured field schema limits what data appears in log records by design. This design is documented under Audit Logs in Data Services Manager in the DSM documentation.

Access to audit log data within DSM is controlled through the role-based access model. The System Audit Events view in the Provider interface is the designated location for reviewing non-database-related operations and tasks; access is scoped to authenticated DSM sessions. Audit events can be exported in CSV or XLSX format through the ACTIONS drop-down in the System Audit Events view, and exports reflect events visible to the requesting user based on their role.

Gateway audit log files are stored on the Provider VM at /var/log/tdm/provider/audit/ with filenames in the format gateway-audit-timestamp.log. Access to these files is governed by the Provider VM's operating system controls. DSM can be configured to archive gateway audit log files periodically to an S3-compatible repository using the Provider Log Repo Url setting under Managing Storage Settings, which allows organizations to consolidate audit logs in a separately protected storage system. DSM also supports log forwarding, configurable through the log-forwarding setting in Managing Storage Settings, allowing logs to be sent to a central log aggregation system such as a syslog-compatible platform where additional access restrictions can be applied.

For SQL Server databases, DSM provides additional audit logging controls at the engine level. When creating a SQL Server cluster, administrators can configure Login Auditing to select the level of audit logging for system login events and set a log retention period. DSM uses server-level DDL triggers to restrict the file paths available for SQL Server audit logs, limiting where those logs can be written. The errorLog.numErrorLogs engine setting controls how many error log files are retained before recycling.

DSM restricts what is logged and provides mechanisms to forward and archive logs. Organizations are responsible for implementing access controls on the S3 log repository, configuring the receiving log aggregation system with appropriate restrictions, applying log retention and integrity policies, and reviewing log contents to confirm that no application-level data patterns create inadvertent sensitive data exposure in event detail fields.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer includes a Sensitive Log Profile (sensitive_log_profile) that masks or removes sensitive data from application-layer client logs on a per-virtual-service basis. The profile is configured through the Analytics Profile, under the Sensitive Log Information section of the Analytics tab in the Avi Controller UI, or via the CLI using the `configure analyticsprofile` command. Administrators define match criteria to identify and redact fields such as REQUEST_COOKIES, Args:Password, Basic Authorization tokens, OAuth credentials, and other personally identifiable information (PII) before those values are written to the log. Both masking (replacing the value with a placeholder) and removal (excluding the field from the log entry entirely) are supported, applied at the application delivery layer where Avi processes traffic. Sensitive log profile configurations can also be deployed and managed through Ansible and Terraform interfaces.

For traffic inspected by Avi's Web Application Firewall (WAF), the Sensitive Log Profile extends to WAF match logs and application log headers, restricting exposure of session tokens or credential data that would otherwise appear in WAF event records. OAuth-related sensitive fields, including Basic Authorization headers, can be masked before being written to the application log.

Avi's configuration audit log records administrative changes, capturing event type, object name, tenant, timestamp, and the identity of the user who performed the action. These audit events can be forwarded to external syslog servers using the Syslog-Audit-Persistence alert action template configured under Alert Actions. Avi Controller and Service Engine audit records for trusted channel initiation, termination, and failures can be delivered to Events, Syslog, and Splunk destinations. Event Logs are filterable by event type (for example, event_id=CONFIG_CREATE or event_id=CONFIG_UPDATE) to support targeted audit review. For assured retention of all virtual service logs, Avi documentation recommends streaming them directly to an external server such as Splunk.

In Kubernetes deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO), the log verbosity level (AKOSettings.logLevel) can be configured to INFO, DEBUG, WARN, or ERROR, controlling the detail captured in operator logs.

An operational note: Avi system component debug logs and Avi Event Logs are not delivered through the OS-level syslog configuration. Debug logs are available only through Tech Support bundle generation, and Event Logs require separate API-based or scripted export to external logging systems.

Protection of the underlying storage holding Avi Controller and Service Engine VM disks, including data-at-rest encryption and hypervisor integrity controls, is handled by the VCF platform layer rather than by Avi itself.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control AU-04

> Allocate audit record storage capacity to accommodate [Assignment: organization- defined audit record retention requirements]. Base Control

**SCF Controls:** MON-04

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple layers of log storage capacity management across its component products, covering local log retention controls, centralized log aggregation with scalable architecture, proactive capacity monitoring with forward-looking projections, and Kubernetes-native container log bounding for workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS).

At the VMware ESX host level, administrators can configure log file retention and rotation policies. The log.keepOld parameter controls how many log files are retained, and when that limit is reached, the oldest file is automatically deleted. VMware recommends a log file size of 500 KB per file for host logs. The syslog directory location is configurable through the Syslog.global.logDir parameter, allowing administrators to direct log output to a storage volume with sufficient capacity. These same retention controls apply to virtual machine log files, where the number of retained log files can be limited through the log.keepOld setting. Per-folder log size limits prevent any single log source from consuming disproportionate storage, and logging levels can be adjusted per log folder to balance detail against storage consumption. ESX also exposes dedicated audit record storage parameters: the Syslog.global.auditRecord.storageCapacity parameter defines the size of the local audit record storage directory in MiB, and this capacity can be set or adjusted at any time using the esxcli system auditrecords local set --size command. VMware vCenter provides equivalent log rotation controls, with the ability to limit the number of retained log files through the same log.keepOld parameter.

For VKS clusters, the kubelet exposes two key parameters for container log management that can be configured through ClusterClass variables during cluster provisioning. The containerLogMaxSizeMiB setting defines the maximum size of a container log file before rotation, with a default of 10 MiB. The containerLogMaxFiles setting controls how many rotated log files are retained per container, with a valid range of 1 to 100 and a default of 5; the oldest files are removed automatically when the limit is exceeded during rotation. Together these parameters establish a predictable ceiling on per-node storage consumption from container logs. The Kubernetes API server's audit log backend provides complementary controls: the audit-log-truncate-enabled flag activates event and batch truncation, and the audit-log-truncate-max-event-size parameter (default 10,240 bytes) limits the size of each individual audit event sent to the backend. The --audit-log-batch-max-size flag bounds the number of events per batch. For environments where audit completeness is a compliance requirement, administrators should size the --audit-log-batch-buffer-size appropriately, as events are dropped rather than queued indefinitely if the buffer overflows. The audit policy configured for the API server directly affects overall storage consumption: selecting appropriate verbosity levels (None, Metadata, Request, or RequestResponse) for different resource types controls the rate at which audit records accumulate, and restricting verbose logging to high-value operations such as secret access or privileged resource modifications reduces the overall storage footprint. The Kubernetes apiserver_audit_event_total Prometheus metric tracks the total number of audit events exported, providing a mechanism to monitor audit throughput and detect developing capacity pressure before events begin to drop. A companion metric, apiserver_audit_level_total, provides a per-request counter partitioned by the audit level assigned by the active policy, giving administrators visibility into audit volume by verbosity tier to inform storage sizing and policy tuning decisions.

Namespace-level storage governance for VKS workloads is available through LimitRange and ResourceQuota objects. LimitRange allows cluster administrators to set per-PersistentVolumeClaim storage request ceilings, while ResourceQuota caps total storage consumption per namespace, enabling cluster-wide storage budget planning and preventing any single project from exhausting its allotment. For persistent log-related storage in standard packages, the Harbor container registry job log PVC defaults to 1 GB (configured via persistence.persistentVolumeClaim.jobLog.size) and can be expanded to 5 GB or higher after verifying available disk space; the Harbor main registry storage PVC defaults to 10 GB and can be increased to 20 GB or higher. The Alertmanager standard package PVC defaults to 2 GiB.

For centralized log management, VCF Log Management is the primary log aggregation platform. It collects syslog feeds from ESX hosts and pulls tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances. The platform supports a High Availability deployment model with multiple instances behind an internal load balancer, and it provides both scale-up and scale-out capabilities to accommodate growing log volumes. The deployment model supports a configurable retention notification threshold and the ability to add disk nodes, giving administrators direct control over when they are alerted about storage consumption and the ability to expand capacity without service interruption. Administrators can configure log retention policies by log type and source, controlling both log file size and the number of retained log files.

VCF Operations provides configurable data retention settings that allow administrators to tune how long historical data is kept, directly managing the storage space consumed by the system. Support log properties can be edited to limit retained log size and set logging levels for individual log file folders, providing granular control over which logs consume the most storage. The orchestrator scripting log size can also be defined in megabytes through a dedicated configuration parameter. VCF Operations retains recent task data for actions for 14 days, after which the data is purged from the system.

For proactive capacity monitoring, VCF Operations collects disk log metrics including used, total, and available space for node logs. The platform publishes a Capacity Analytics metric for disk space usage that provides the remaining capacity in GB, calculated as the maximum point between usable capacity and projected utilization over the next three days. This forward-looking projection allows administrators to identify and address storage shortfalls before they affect log collection. Capacity metrics can also be calculated for datastore objects, providing visibility into the underlying storage that hosts log data. VCF Operations generates disk capacity alerts when utilization reaches 80% (Critical) or 90% (Immediate) severity levels, providing automated notification when log storage is approaching exhaustion. VCF Operations also includes a Kubernetes Capacity dashboard that displays Kubernetes workload resource usage against configured requests and limits, providing unified visibility into K8s storage consumption for capacity planning.

Syslog forwarding to external targets provides an additional safeguard for log storage capacity. ESX hosts can be configured to forward syslog messages to a remote log server, offloading log storage from local host volumes to dedicated infrastructure with managed capacity. vCenter supports forwarding to up to three remote syslog servers using TCP, UDP, TLS, or RELP protocols. For VKS clusters, the Fluent Bit standard package can forward container and system logs to Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, or Splunk targets, offloading log storage from cluster node volumes to dedicated log infrastructure. When Fluent Bit is configured to collect kube-apiserver audit logs from VKS cluster nodes, the audit log stream operates with a 50 MB in-memory buffer limit, bounding the local storage footprint for audit events that are pending forwarding. On the vSphere Supervisor control plane, kube-apiserver audit logs are collected by Fluent Bit from /var/log/vmware/audit/kube-apiserver.log and stored locally at /var/log/vmware/fluentbit/flb_audit_apiserver before any external forwarding is applied; directing these logs to an external target reduces the storage load on the Supervisor control plane nodes. Workloads running in vSphere Namespaces can stream their logs to a remote Syslog server by applying a LogAgentConfiguration Custom Resource to the namespace; log messages are enriched with namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name for traceability. By forwarding logs to external SIEM or log management systems, organizations maintain a secondary copy of log data that is independent of local storage constraints, so that even if local storage is exhausted, the forwarded copies remain intact. Administrators should note that ESX hosts deployed with Auto Deploy store logs on a RAM disk, which provides limited local log capacity; for those deployments, configuring remote syslog forwarding is particularly important.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) includes several mechanisms for managing the storage capacity used by its observability and logging components. The most direct is the PAISConfiguration custom resource (CR), which governs PAIS observability. Within the CR, the metrics-retention property sets the data retention period for Prometheus metrics, and the Prometheus metrics database is backed by a Kubernetes Persistent Volume whose size is inferred from this retention setting. Operators configure this alongside a storage class via observability.prometheusRuntime when activating PAIS observability, giving them direct control over how much storage is allocated to the metrics backend and how long data is retained before being pruned. The metrics retention period has a default of 14 days, which establishes a predictable upper bound on the metrics volume an operator must size storage for.

For LLM request tracing, PAIS supports forwarding LLM traces to an OpenTelemetry Collector by uncommenting the spec.observability.llmTraces section of the PAISConfiguration CR. This externalizes trace storage to whichever observability platform the organization operates, so trace volume does not accumulate within the PAIS cluster itself. PAIS Controller metrics are also exposed at a Prometheus endpoint on the pais-controller-manager service for collection by external monitoring systems, which keeps long-term metric history out of the PAIS namespace.

Deep Learning VMs write logs to well-known paths: JupyterLab authentication events and cloud-init provisioning output go to /var/log/dl.log, and vGPU and NVIDIA data center GPU driver installation events go to /var/log/gpu-install.log. The Triton Inference Server on Deep Learning VMs generates inference request logs and performance metrics that can be collected for audit and compliance review. The vLLM inference server on model endpoints supports a VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL environment variable that controls logging verbosity, allowing operators to reduce log volume by selecting an appropriate level for production workloads. Model endpoints also have a configurable tempoMountSize on the /tmp mount point that defaults to 1Gi, which bounds ephemeral working storage on each endpoint.

Capacity for the supporting Kubernetes storage on VKS worker nodes is sized through PAIS-provided defaults. VKS worker node pools provisioned for PAIS use kubelet-dir and containerd-dir storage volumes with a default capacity of 100Gi each, mounted to vSAN storage policies. Data Indexing and Retrieval worker nodes use a storage policy configured through the spec.worker.storageClassName section of the PAISConfiguration YAML. For environments that host a private Harbor registry for AI container images, PAIS guidance recommends allocating registry storage space to accommodate at least three versions of each container image planned for deployment, so that registry growth is anticipated rather than reactive.

The foundational storage layer, including vSAN storage policies, namespace storage limits, and platform-level capacity monitoring, is managed through VCF rather than PAIS itself.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) provides several log storage capacity management mechanisms for its security event data.

At the Distributed Firewall (DFW) layer, packet logging uses bounded log rotation: the DFWPKTLOGS.log file rotates at a 50 MB per-file limit, and the system retains a maximum of 10 log files at a time. This bounds DFW packet log storage on transport nodes while retaining sufficient history for troubleshooting and forensic analysis.

vDefend IDS/IPS includes an automated protective mechanism that handles high-volume event scenarios. When incoming IDS/IPS events and PCAP data would otherwise exhaust memory, the mechanism selectively drops non-critical events and PCAPs to maintain system stability. Administrators can observe event volumes through the IDS/IPS Security Dashboard and Security Overview Dashboard.

IDS/IPS event data can be forwarded to external syslog consumers by enabling the ids_events_to_syslog setting under IDS/IPS Settings. Exporting events externally offloads local storage consumption so that log accumulation on transport nodes is bounded by the rotation policy rather than event volume. Remote syslog must be configured on the ESX host to receive IDS/IPS events exported directly from ESX hosts. vDefend components write logs to /var/log, with syslog messages conforming to RFC 5424 on vDefend appliances and RFC 3164 on ESX hosts.

Guest Introspection Host Agent (MUX) logs are written to /var/log/syslog.log on ESX hosts, with each log message including a timestamp, ContextMUX identifier, and thread ID.

The Security Services Platform (SSP), which hosts Security Intelligence and Network Detection and Response analytics, has documented storage sizing baselines for capacity planning. Each SSP controller node requires 225 GB of storage and each worker node also requires 225 GB. Storage requirements scale with worker node count, adding approximately 155 GB per additional worker node. For a maximum configuration of 10 worker nodes (14 total nodes), the recommended persistent volume claim is 3,545 GB. These sizing baselines enable administrators to allocate storage capacity appropriately at deployment time.

vDefend logs can be forwarded to VCF Log Management by configuring it as a syslog server. VCF Log Management provides a centralized log management framework and supports querying log data by time range through Interactive Analytics.

These mechanisms address event log storage capacity for vDefend's security event data. Broader log storage governance across the VCF platform, including infrastructure-wide capacity monitoring and centralized retention policies, is handled by VCF Log Management and VCF Operations.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides configurable log storage capacity management through file size limits, file count limits, and per-component logging level controls that help prevent log storage exhaustion.

PNR Server log files are managed through the vmware-dr.xml configuration file. The <maxFileSize> parameter controls the maximum size of individual log files, with a default of 10,485,760 bytes (10 MB). The <maxFileNum> parameter controls the maximum number of log files retained, with a default of 20 files. PNR also raises a low disk space event when available disk space on the server falls below a configurable threshold, controlled by the localSiteStatus.minDiskSpace parameter with a default of 100 MB. This event can trigger alarms that alert administrators before log storage is exhausted.

Configurable logging levels per component allow administrators to reduce log verbosity when log files are growing too quickly, or increase it temporarily for troubleshooting. Named per-component settings configurable through the Reconfigure Protection and Recovery Settings interface include logManager.Storage, logManager.StorageProvider, logManager.Licensing, and logManager.VvolProvider, each with a default value of verbose. This allows log output from storage, storage provider, licensing, and Virtual Volumes subsystems to be tuned independently.

The PNR Appliance supports forwarding log files to a remote syslog server, allowing log volume to be offloaded to centralized log management infrastructure. In environments using the Live Cyber Recovery (LCR) clean room, PNR can forward events from the clean room to VCF Log Management (version 9.0 or above), Microsoft Sentinel, or Splunk Cloud.

vSphere Replication, which is part of PNR, supports event logging and allows definition of alarms for each event. vSphere Replication maintains dedicated audit logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hms/hms-audit.log, with broker logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hbr on the Appliance and at /var/run/log on ESX hosts.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides configurable log storage settings and two-tier capacity alerting that support proactive management of event log storage.

DSM Provider logging includes configurable log storage settings accessible through the Provider interface under Storage Settings. Administrators configure the Provider Log Repo URL to direct logs to an S3-compatible object storage repository and bucket; the Bucket Name and optional Region settings complete the S3 object store configuration. Gateway audit log files are stored locally on the Provider VM at `/var/log/tdm/provider/audit/` using filenames in the format `gateway-audit-timestamp.log`, and are archived periodically to an S3 subpath named `gateway audit` when a provider log repository is configured. The Provider VM ships with a default storage configuration of three disks totaling 595 GB (80 GB, 15 GB, and 500 GB), which can be sized for the anticipated log and backup volume at deployment time.

DSM includes two-tier capacity alerting to notify administrators before storage is exhausted. The `data-disk-warning-threshold` setting governs a WARNING global alert when data disk usage reaches 70%; the `data-disk-critical-threshold` setting governs a CRITICAL global alert at 90%. When either threshold is breached, administrators can increase the storage allocation using the `storage-amount` setting. These alerts are visible to DSM administrators and prompt timely remediation before log capacity is exceeded.

For SQL Server databases, DSM exposes the `errorLog.numErrorLogs` custom engine setting, which controls how many error log files are retained before recycling. Configuring this setting allows administrators to manage database-engine-level log storage consumption directly.

S3-compatible storage sizing for DSM depends on the volume of data backups and the configured retention policy. Organizations must assess their log generation rates, retention requirements, and backup volumes to right-size the S3 repository. DSM does not automatically provision or expand storage capacity, so organizational processes for periodic capacity review and storage expansion remain necessary to fully satisfy this control.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides configurable log storage capacity at both the Controller and Service Engine layers, giving organizations the mechanisms to allocate and proactively manage event log storage for application delivery traffic.

At the Service Engine layer, disk capacity for logs is calculated dynamically based on total disk size, CPU cores, RAM, and the configurable maximum-storage-not-allocated-for-logs setting, adjustable through SE runtime properties. These values drive two derived pools: client-logs-capacity (total disk minus debug logs capacity, with adjustments based on configured minimum storage allocated for logs and SE RAM) and debug-logs-capacity (calculated as the SE total disk minus the maximum storage not allocated for logs, divided by 100). A minimally sized Service Engine with two vCPUs, 2 GB of RAM, and 10 GB of storage can hold approximately 8 million log entries. When storage is exhausted, the oldest Significant Log entries are phased out on a per-Service Engine basis and their disk space freed automatically, keeping the Service Engine operational without requiring operator intervention.

Increasing disk capacity on a Service Engine directly extends both the volume of logs retained and the total retention duration. Avi documentation recommends provisioning Controllers and Service Engines with larger storage allocations in environments that require extended log retention. Adding CPU, memory, and disk resources to Service Engines and Controllers scales log throughput and retention proportionally.

For deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) within VCF, the AKO persistentVolumeClaimSpec.storage parameter allocates 2 GiB of storage for the log Persistent Volume Claim by default, providing a configurable log storage allocation for Kubernetes-based workloads.

For log retention beyond what local disk supports, the Avi Controller's Syslog-Audit-Persistence feature streams alerts and audit events to external syslog servers. The Controller also supports sending audit records for trusted channel initiation, termination, and failures to Events, Syslog, and Splunk destinations. Offloading to an external syslog or Splunk infrastructure decouples event log retention from local Service Engine disk allocation and supports longer retention windows managed at the logging tier. Note that Syslog notification actions push alert events to syslog servers but do not directly export virtual service logs; virtual service logs must be pulled from an external logging system through the API or scripted to be pushed from the Controller.

### Control AU-04(01)

> Audit Log Storage Capacity | Transfer to Alternate Storage Transfer audit logs [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to a different system, system component, or media other than the system or system component conducting the logging.

**SCF Controls:** MON-08.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides native syslog forwarding capabilities across all major components, allowing event logs to be sent to remote log collection infrastructure that is physically separate from any SIEM or centralized logging tool. VCF components write application and audit logs to their local file systems and can forward those logs to one or more remote destinations for backup and long-term retention.

VMware ESX hosts can be configured to send syslog messages to a remote log server using the esxcli system syslog config set command with a specified log server IP address and port. ESX syslog messages conform to RFC 3164. Configurable syslog log levels allow administrators to control the verbosity of forwarded logs, and settings can be applied and reloaded using esxcli system syslog commands. The ESX syslog directory location can also be configured via the Syslog.global.logDir parameter for local log storage. The Syslog.global.auditRecord.remote parameter controls whether audit records are forwarded to the remote hosts defined by Syslog.global.logHost, enabling targeted delivery of security audit messages to remote log collection infrastructure.

VMware vCenter provides a syslog forwarding API accessible through the vCenter Management Interface at port 5480. This API supports GET, PUT, and POST operations to retrieve, configure, and test syslog forwarding connections. The forwarding configuration supports TCP, UDP, TLS, and RELP protocols, giving administrators flexibility in how logs are transmitted to remote systems. vCenter supports a maximum of three remote syslog forwarding targets, and the remote server must be running rsyslog. The API includes a test endpoint to verify connectivity with remote syslog servers before relying on them for log collection. TLS support protects log data in transit to backup destinations. vCenter events can also be streamed to a remote syslog server by enabling the vpxd.event.syslog.enabled option through the vCenter Management Interface.

NSX Manager supports syslog export to a remote server with configurable protocol, port, and log level. NSX Nodes can be configured to send syslog messages to multiple remote syslog servers, each with independently specified port numbers and protocols. NSX audit logs, which record security-related events in /var/log/audit/audit.log, are sent directly to the configured remote syslog server rather than being written only to local syslog. On ESX hosts, NSX-specific logs are stored in the /var/log/nsx-syslog directory and can also be forwarded through the host's syslog configuration.

VCF Log Management is a centralized log aggregation platform that collects logs from vCenter, ESX hosts, NSX, and other VCF management components. It can be configured to pull tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances and receive syslog feeds from ESX hosts through its Log Collections configuration. Because VCF Log Management runs on its own cluster of nodes, separate from the components generating the logs, it provides a physically different system for log backup and can itself forward to separate backup infrastructure for additional separation.

Additional VCF components support remote syslog forwarding. VCF Operations supports configuration of a remote syslog host for centralized log collection through its Host System Properties. VCF Operations can also be configured to send its user activity log to an external syslog server. The Foundation Load Balancer supports forwarding logs to a syslog server via IP address configuration. VCF Operations HCX forwards log messages from both the management console and appliance virtual machines to a configured remote syslog server, and appliances deployed after the external syslog server is added automatically begin forwarding. VCF Operations for Networks requires remote syslog servers configured to accept syslog traffic over UDP.

For Kubernetes workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), the Consumption layer of VCF provides Fluent Bit as a standard package available on VKS clusters. Fluent Bit collects application logs (stdout/stderr from container workloads), Kubernetes system component logs, and audit records from cluster nodes, then forwards them to one or more external destinations. Supported forwarding targets include Syslog servers (UDP on port 514 or TCP on port 6514), HTTP endpoints, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk. Fluent Bit can also forward logs to VCF Log Management using the CFAPI protocol, integrating the Kubernetes workload log stream into the same centralized logging infrastructure used by the VCF management plane. The architecture separates the log collection point (the VKS cluster) from the storage destination, providing the technical foundation for storing logs on a physically separate system. A logging management server must be deployed to store and analyze the forwarded logs.

The Kubernetes kube-apiserver on VKS clusters provides an audit log backend that writes audit events to a local file in JSONlines format. A webhook backend is also available to ship those events to an external receiver, enabling a two-copy strategy where audit event data resides on both the local node and a separate external system. The audit policy configuration determines which events are recorded and at what level of detail. Control plane audit events captured on VKS cluster nodes can be forwarded downstream through the Fluent Bit logging pipeline to the designated external log store. For Windows-based worker nodes in VKS clusters, LogMonitor collects Windows Event Log entries from sources including Security, Hyper-V, Host Network, and Containers, piping them to stdout where the Kubernetes logging pipeline captures them and Fluent Bit can forward them to the external log store.

VCF backup operations provide an additional mechanism for preserving event log data. vCenter backup can optionally include statistics, events, and tasks in the backup scope alongside core inventory and configuration state. Simultaneous backups of component nodes help maintain logical consistency across the backup set. The restore operation includes all optional parts that were specified at the time the backup file was created. vCenter file-based backup data is not stored on the vCenter appliance itself; it is transmitted to a remote server using FTP, FTPS, HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP, NFS, or SMB. Backup files can optionally be encrypted with a password that is required for the restore procedure, protecting backup data that may contain sensitive configuration and key material.

Because each VCF component supports independent syslog forwarding to one or more remote destinations, administrators can configure log delivery to multiple physically separate systems simultaneously. Event logs can reside on both the primary SIEM and on an independent log archive or backup system, providing physical separation between the SIEM and log backup infrastructure. The variety of supported protocols (TCP, UDP, TLS, RELP) and the per-component configuration granularity allow organizations to tailor their log backup architecture to meet their specific redundancy and security requirements.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides mechanisms to forward appliance logs and events to systems that are physically separate from the recovery infrastructure.

The Protection and Recovery Appliance includes built-in syslog forwarding configured through the appliance management interface. Administrators specify a remote syslog server hostname or IP address, select the forwarding protocol from a drop-down menu, and configure the destination port, which defaults to 514. A test message capability allows verification that the remote server is receiving messages before relying on the configuration in production. This forwarding covers appliance-level logs including vSphere Replication security messages, authorization errors with timestamps and component information, and replication state change events.

The Clean Room Orchestrator component of PNR, which supports ransomware recovery in isolated environments, provides event forwarding to VCF Log Management, Microsoft Sentinel, or Splunk Cloud. Events forwarded to Microsoft Sentinel use a Data Collection Endpoint and Data Collection Rule configured in a Log Analytics workspace, and each event record includes timestamp, priority level, message content, component name, severity level, configuration ID, component ID, and event type. Event forwarding to VCF Log Management requires version 9.0 or above. All supported forwarding destinations are hosted on infrastructure physically separate from the PNR appliance and recovery environment.

The physical separation of log destinations from any SIEM is an organizational and infrastructure design decision. PNR provides the forwarding mechanisms, but the remote syslog server, external SIEM, or VCF Log Management instance must be independently deployed and configured by the organization.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) supports the architectural separation of event log storage from monitoring tools through two complementary mechanisms: log forwarding to external systems and gateway audit log archival to object storage.

DSM's log forwarding capability, configured through the Log Forwarding setting in the Managing Settings section, allows administrators to send logs to either a syslog server or VCF Log Management. The syslog configuration requires specifying the protocol, hostname or IP address, and port of the receiving server; database cluster nodes communicate over TCP port 514. Log forwarding covers both the DSM Provider VM and provisioned databases, giving administrators a single control point for externalizing logs from all managed components. The Log Forwarding toggle in the Settings interface enables or disables forwarding to external log management systems.

Gateway audit log files are maintained locally on the Provider VM at /var/log/tdm/provider/audit/ with filenames in the format gateway-audit-timestamp.log. These files contain standard Kubernetes audit events. DSM periodically archives gateway audit log files to an S3 repository subpath when a default S3 provider log repository is configured, placing the archived logs on object storage infrastructure separate from the DSM Provider VM. The Gateway Audit Logs section of the provider UI provides access to these events, and DSM allows export of audit event information in CSV and XLSX formats to support transfer to alternate storage systems. Exported records include Component, Event Type, Event Details, Event Time (UTC), Status, and Source fields.

The System Audit Events view tracks non-database-related operations and tasks; it is the only DSM UI location for monitoring provider-level actions. DSM generates system audit events for user actions performed through the DSM UI and API, exportable using the ACTIONS drop-down menu in the System Audit Events view.

The separation between the log forwarding destination (a syslog server or VCF Log Management) and a SIEM receiving those logs is an architectural decision made at the organizational level. DSM provides the mechanisms to forward and archive logs to external systems, but the configuration of those external systems and the topology that places log backup storage on physically separate infrastructure from the SIEM is an organizational responsibility that must be addressed through deployment design.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control AU-05

> a. Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] in the event of an audit logging process failure; and b. Take the following additional actions: [Assignment: organization-defined additional actions].

**SCF Controls:** MON-05

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides built-in mechanisms to detect log processing failures, alert operations personnel, and support remediation of disruptions across its monitoring stack.

VCF Operations monitors the health of its own alerting and log processing infrastructure. When no SNMP targets are configured for alert delivery, VCF Operations generates a critical alert (vmwSNMPNotConfiguredEvent), flagging that the alerting pipeline is not operational. It also generates critical alerts for invalid SNMP connection events (vmwSNMPConnectionInvalidEvent). At the network layer, VCF Operations monitors the status of the NSX Management Node SNMP Service and generates a warning-severity alert when the service stops running. NSX-T itself generates an alert event when the Syslog Service has stopped running on NSX-T Manager nodes. The NSX Manager Logging Monitor adds a further detection mechanism through the System Health Agent framework, tracking each log's generation rate and rotation settings to estimate how long log data will be retained and raising log duration alarms when abnormal rates risk flushing log data before it is collected, so operators can intervene before records are lost. These self-monitoring capabilities detect failures in the event log processing and alert delivery chain before administrators need to discover them manually.

The alert framework in VCF Operations supports definitions built from one or more symptom sets. Symptom definitions identify conditions on attributes, properties, metrics, and events. The framework supports multiple event types relevant to log processing failures, including System Degradation, Data Availability, Collector Down, and Object Error. Administrators can create alert definitions with tighter symptom parameters to track problems before users encounter further issues. Recommendations can be attached to alert definitions so that engineers know how to resolve the alert when it fires, providing clear remediation guidance tied to specific failure conditions. Alerts are generated when collected data for an object is compared against the alert definitions for that object type and the defined symptoms evaluate to true.

For alert delivery, VCF supports multiple notification channels. vCenter can send both SNMP traps and email notifications when alarms are triggered, with configurable recipient addresses and subject lines. The vCenter SNMP agent supports SNMPv1, v2c, and v3 trap formats, including SNMPv3 with all security levels. VCF Operations supports outbound SNMP trap notifications to up to four destinations and can filter notifications based on defined criteria. As a fallback, VCF Operations can log alerts to a file on each of its nodes, providing an on-node record that persists even if external alert delivery is disrupted. This multi-channel approach helps ensure that alerts about log processing disruptions reach the appropriate personnel even if one delivery mechanism is impaired.

VCF Operations provides log-based alerting through Symptom Definitions, accessible under Infrastructure Operations > Configurations. Administrators can create log-based conditions and symptoms that trigger alerts when specific log patterns are detected, including patterns that indicate log processing failures or disruptions. The Log File plug-in supports notification rules that filter and route alerts to the appropriate teams. Log-based alert definitions can monitor host systems for specific conditions such as cluster performance failures, and they generate alerts when those conditions are detected. These alerts can be forwarded to external monitoring systems via SNMP traps.

VCF Log Management is a centralized log collection platform that pulls tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances and receives syslog feeds from ESX hosts. The audit log in VCF Operations is integrated with syslog, and the syslog collector can be configured to collect all audit logs. This centralized architecture makes it possible to detect when expected log streams stop arriving, which is itself an indicator of a log processing failure. When audit record transmission is active on ESX hosts, the syslog daemon (vmsyslogd) emits records documenting its own operational status, including daemon start, stop, and error conditions, enabling detection of syslog service disruptions at the host level. vCenter event streaming to a remote syslog server is configured via the vpxd.event.syslog.enabled option in the vCenter Management Interface.

VCF Operations logs task execution details including Script Return Result status, script names, target object types, parameters passed, and unexpected exceptions. When automated remediation tasks fail, these logs provide the diagnostic detail needed to understand what went wrong and why. Task events are displayed in the messages area, allowing administrators to troubleshoot failed tasks directly.

Adapters for external monitored systems and the REST API are inbound channels for collecting events from external sources. VCF Operations correlates a subset of events published by monitored systems and delivers them as faults. Fault symptoms can be defined based on these events and added to alert definitions, so failures in external log sources are surfaced within the central monitoring platform. For organizations that forward logs to external SIEM systems via syslog, any disruption in log forwarding is itself a monitorable condition, since VCF components generate alerts when syslog services stop running.

At the Kubernetes consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters include instrumentation to detect and report log processing failures in workload environments. The Kubernetes API server exposes the apiserver_audit_error_total metric, which counts audit events dropped due to export errors, and the companion apiserver_audit_event_total metric, which tracks total audit event volume; a sustained rise in the error count relative to the event count indicates a failure in the audit export pipeline. When Prometheus is installed as a standard package on a VKS cluster, Prometheus instances are configured to send alerts to Alertmanager in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace. Alertmanager is deployed as a StatefulSet with multiple replicas in that namespace, and administrators can configure alert rules targeting these metrics to route notifications to designated personnel when audit event exports fail. Prometheus discovers Alertmanager pods through Kubernetes service discovery, filtering by namespace, app label, and component label.

VKS clusters include Fluent Bit logging agents for cluster-wide log collection and forwarding. Fluent Bit supports output to Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk destinations, so disruptions in log forwarding are detectable through the health of the centralized log collection infrastructure. For workloads running in vSphere Namespaces, the LogAgentConfiguration Custom Resource streams logs to a remote Syslog server once applied to the namespace; each log message includes namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name as metadata fields, allowing operations teams to correlate forwarding gaps with specific workloads or nodes.

The Node Problem Detector provides a SystemLogMonitor daemon that monitors system logs and reports problems to the API server as NodeConditions or Events, according to predefined rules covering log sources such as filelog, kmsg, and kernel logs. This surfaces node-level log source failures as cluster events that can be queried or routed through alert management infrastructure. In Kubernetes audit logging batch mode, if the rate of incoming events overflows the buffer, events are dropped without notification from the logging subsystem itself, which makes metric-based monitoring of apiserver_audit_error_total the primary signal for detecting silent audit pipeline failures. VM Operator logs record reconciliation events for VirtualMachineService resources in VKS clusters, providing additional log sources for troubleshooting provisioning and service failures.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides event logging with configurable alarms that can alert administrators to operational failures across the disaster recovery environment. Each event type has a corresponding alarm that can be configured to send email notifications, SNMP traps, or execute a script when triggered, giving administrators multiple channels to reach appropriate personnel when problems occur. For email notifications, mail settings must be configured in the vCenter Settings menu before alarm-triggered notifications function. PNR also requires an administrator email address to be configured during appliance registration with vCenter.

The PNR Server logging level can be configured to one of five levels: error, warning, info, verbose, or trivia, by modifying the log configuration. Error and warning levels capture conditions that indicate problems in the environment. When recovery plan execution encounters failures, PNR logs diagnostic details in the recovery site server logs, including plan identifiers, plan names, and detailed error messages with fault codes.

The PNR Appliance can forward log files to a remote syslog server for centralized log analysis and monitoring. vSphere Replication maintains separate audit logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hms/hms-audit.log for tracking security-relevant events, including authorization errors with timestamps and component information.

The Clean Room Orchestrator provides an additional monitoring layer for ransomware recovery workflows. Administrators can configure email alerts for critical events in the clean room environment. The orchestrator can forward events to VCF Log Management, Microsoft Sentinel, or Splunk Cloud; event forwarding to VCF Log Management requires version 9.0 or above.

VCF Operations provides an alert system that notifies administrators when specific symptoms of monitored objects are detected, including health alerts for protection groups, recovery plans, cloud file systems, recovery SDDCs, and protected sites. In environments with more than one vCenter, PNR displays all events from PNR servers registered as extensions.

PNR logs when a protected consistency group has errors or warnings, recording this as an Error-level event. These mechanisms surface operational failures and log-related disruptions as part of broader recovery environment monitoring. Dedicated log pipeline health monitoring and log processing failure detection are provided by the underlying VCF platform monitoring stack.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports log processing failure detection and personnel notification through its audit record failure logging capability and event alerting framework.

Avi Controller and Avi Service Engine explicitly record audit record failures for three log destinations: Events, Syslog, and Splunk. The failure categories captured include initiation and termination of trusted channel connections, failure of trusted channel functions, unsuccessful certificate validation attempts, client authentication failures, SSH session establishment failures, and identification of the initiators and targets of failed trusted channel establishment attempts. This capability is configured in the Application Monitoring section of the Avi documentation under Audit Logging > Audit Record Failures.

When log processing failures or system events occur, Avi Controller can route notifications to four destinations: the local Controller UI (displayed as colored bell icons), email, syslog, and SNMP trap servers. These notification channels are configured under Operations > Notifications in the Controller UI. Avi supports both SNMPv2c and SNMPv3 protocols for SNMP trap notifications, and SNMP notification channels can be configured with TLS encryption and strict certificate verification for secure transport. The Avi Controller cluster leader issues SNMP trap notifications and supports multiple SNMP servers, providing redundancy for alert delivery. The Syslog-Audit-Persistence alert action template is available for configuring alert actions that stream events to external syslog servers, which can feed into a centralized log management or SIEM platform.

Auditors should be aware of two scoping boundaries when evaluating this control. First, Avi debug logs and Event Logs are not transmitted to remote syslog servers through the OS-level syslog configuration; Event Logs require the Syslog notification configuration within the Controller UI or API-based export. Second, configuring Syslog notifications in Avi Controller pushes alerts to syslog servers but does not export virtual service logs; virtual service logs must be pulled from an external logging system through the API or scripted to be pushed from the Controller to a remote destination.

Auditors verifying this control should confirm that Alert Actions are configured in the Operations section of the Avi Controller UI, that notification destinations (email addresses, SNMP trap server addresses, or syslog server targets) are defined, and that organizational runbooks or response procedures define who receives these alerts and what remediation steps follow.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control AU-05(01)

> Provide a warning to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel, roles, and/or locations] within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when allocated audit log storage volume reaches [Assignment: organization-defined percentage] of repository maximum audit log storage capacity.

**SCF Controls:** MON-05.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides automated alerting mechanisms that notify personnel when log and disk storage usage approaches capacity limits, with configurable thresholds that map directly to organization-defined percentages.

VCF Operations includes a Capacity Remaining policy element in the Policy Workspace that administrators configure to set the percentage threshold at which capacity remaining alerts fire. This applies across monitored storage resources, including datastores used for log retention. Capacity settings can be turned on and configured per policy, allowing administrators to override default thresholds for specific environments. Administrators can also configure different capacity calculations for production environments versus testing environments using custom groups.

VCF Operations supports capacity-specific alert types, with Alert Type and Subtype options that can be configured to select Application: Capacity. This allows capacity-related conditions, including log storage utilization, to be surfaced as distinct alert categories. VCF Operations also supports log-based alerts configured through the Symptom Definitions card in Infrastructure Operations, enabling administrators to define custom symptoms tied to log storage conditions and trigger alerts when those conditions are met.

VCF Log Management ships with pre-built content pack alerts for storage conditions. The SysDiskUsage alert triggers when disk usage for one or more partitions exceeds 89% for more than 10 minutes, providing out-of-the-box detection of log storage exhaustion on the logging infrastructure itself without requiring custom configuration. The SysMemUsage alert similarly triggers when memory usage exceeds 95% for more than 10 minutes, covering resource exhaustion conditions that could affect log processing. VCF Log Management can also be configured to pull tasks, events, and alerts from VMware vCenter instances and receive syslog feeds from VMware ESX hosts through Log Collections configuration. This centralized collection means that log storage capacity can be monitored from a single pane rather than requiring per-host monitoring.

For workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters in the VCF consumption layer, Prometheus and Alertmanager are available as standard packages installable on VKS clusters. The kubelet exposes kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes and kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes metrics labeled by namespace and PersistentVolumeClaim, enabling calculation of storage fill percentage per PVC. Prometheus scrapes these metrics and evaluates organization-defined alerting rules that fire when any volume approaches its configured capacity threshold. The Kubernetes API server exposes the apiserver_storage_db_total_size_in_bytes metric, which reports the physical size of the etcd storage database, allowing organizations to incorporate audit log backend storage into the same alerting framework. Alertmanager is deployed as a StatefulSet with three replicas in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace, backed by a 2 GiB persistent volume claim with ReadWriteOnce access, and routes alert notifications to configured receivers including email, Slack, and PagerDuty. Organizations must define the threshold percentages, write the Prometheus alerting rules, and configure Alertmanager routing and receivers; the platform provides the instrumentation and delivery infrastructure but does not ship pre-configured rules for event log storage thresholds.

Workload logs from vSphere Namespaces can be streamed to a remote Syslog server by applying the LogAgentConfiguration custom resource to a namespace; each log message includes metadata fields such as namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and VMware ESX host name, preserving context for capacity monitoring at the receiving system. Fluent Bit, available as a VKS standard package, can forward logs from cluster workloads to Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk destinations, enabling integration with external log storage and monitoring systems that perform their own capacity alerting. When configured for audit log collection, Fluent Bit reads kube-apiserver audit logs in JSON format from /var/log/kubernetes/kube-apiserver.log, with a configurable memory buffer limit of 50 MB per instance. The Kubernetes audit log backend supports configurable truncation through the audit-log-truncate-enabled flag and the audit-log-truncate-max-event-size parameter, which limit individual event sizes and batch sizes sent to the underlying backend and allow administrators to control the rate of audit log storage consumption. Audit event truncation is disabled by default and must be explicitly enabled via API server configuration before the size parameters take effect.

VCF Operations for Networks includes disk utilization alerts viewable on the platform dashboard, the collector dashboard, or in the Infrastructure and Support > Infrastructure and Updates page. These alerts surface storage pressure on the network monitoring components that collect and store network flow data and logs.

VCF Operations also publishes Capacity Analytics metrics for disk space, calculating capacity remaining in GB as the maximum point between usable capacity and projected utilization between now and three days into the future. This forward-looking analysis gives administrators advance warning before storage is actually exhausted, rather than only alerting after the fact. Datastore capacity metric trends can be checked to identify spikes or abnormal growth in storage usage. VCF Automation provides an Alerts page that surfaces capacity, performance, and availability issues, enabling cloud administrators to react proactively before resources are exhausted.

When capacity thresholds are reached and alerts fire, VCF provides multiple automated notification channels to reach appropriate personnel. vCenter can send SNMP traps and email notifications when alarms are triggered, with configurable recipient addresses. vCenter's SNMP agent supports v1 and v2c traps as well as v3 traps with all security levels. The vCenter SNMP agent also supports notification filtering, allowing administrators to suppress specific notifications that should not reach their SNMP management software. VCF PowerCLI provides the New-AlarmAction command to create SNMP alarm actions for alarm definitions programmatically, enabling infrastructure-as-code approaches to alarm notification configuration. VCF Operations can send alert notifications via SNMP trap to up to four SNMP trap destinations, with filtering available when defining notifications. These notification mechanisms allow organizations to route capacity alerts to the appropriate teams through their existing monitoring and incident management infrastructure.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) generates alarms when allocated resources exceed predetermined threshold values, alerting administrators to capacity conditions that may affect analytics and detection services. A separate alarm mechanism in the Distributed Firewall Security Overview Dashboard triggers and remains active when dropped events are detected, notifying administrators of data loss conditions at the event collection layer.

For organizations implementing threshold-based log storage capacity alerting at organization-defined percentages, vDefend provides multiple log forwarding integration points. VMware vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS can be configured to send IDS events to external syslog consumers via the ids_events_to_syslog setting (disabled by default). vDefend appliances emit RFC 5424-compliant syslog messages, and VMware ESX hosts emit RFC 3164-compliant messages, making vDefend log streams compatible with standard syslog infrastructure and SIEM platforms. SSP SIEM integration sends Network Detection and Response (NDR) campaign and event logs to the connected SIEM as they occur, including logs generated when new detections are created, when existing detections are updated, and when new campaigns are identified. Identity Firewall Event Log Sources can be integrated with VCF Log Management for centralized log collection and monitoring.

Native configurable alerting to designated personnel at an organization-defined percentage of log storage capacity is not a documented vDefend feature; this function is provided by an external monitoring platform or SIEM that ingests vDefend log streams and implements capacity-based threshold alerting.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

Protection and Recovery (PNR) monitors free disk space on the appliance host and raises a Warning event, logged as LowDiskSpaceEvent, when disk space falls below a configured threshold. PNR alarms can be configured to send email notifications, SNMP traps, or run scripts on the VMware vCenter host when threshold conditions are met. Configuring PNR alarms to deliver email notifications requires that Mail settings be configured in vCenter. PNR appliance registration requires that an administrator email address be provided, establishing the notification path for disk space and other system events.

PNR log storage is managed through a configurable file count limit. The maxFileNum parameter controls the maximum number of log files retained by the PNR Server, with a default of 20 files. When log files reach their configured size, older files are compressed to keep storage consumption bounded.

The PNR Appliance supports forwarding log files to a remote syslog server for centralized log analysis. PNR also supports forwarding events to VCF Log Management version 9.0 and above. In Live Cyber Recovery (LCR) deployments, the Clean Room Orchestrator can forward events to a Microsoft Azure Log Analytics Workspace using Data Collection Rules and Data Collection Endpoints, extending event visibility to external log management systems.

Percentage-based log storage capacity alerting with organization-configurable thresholds depends on the broader VCF platform monitoring stack. VCF Operations provides an advanced alert system for monitored objects that includes health alerts for protection groups, recovery plans, cloud file systems, recovery SDDCs, and protected sites.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) monitors storage capacity on the data disk and generates global alerts when usage reaches threshold values. A WARNING alert fires when storage on the data disk reaches 70% usage, and a CRITICAL alert fires when it reaches 90%. In DSM, both thresholds are exposed as named settings (data-disk-warning-threshold and data-disk-critical-threshold) in the Configure the Storage on a Data Disk section, giving administrators a documented location to review and adjust these values when sizing storage. The alerts are designed to notify administrators of capacity conditions that may affect backup and WAL archival operations, both of which depend on available storage to function correctly. The alert system operates automatically without administrator intervention and is visible through the Global Alerts Reference in the DSM interface.

DSM uses a three-level severity model for alerts: OK, WARNING, and CRITICAL. The DATA_DISK_HEALTH_ALERT type is associated with disk usage thresholds and is surfaced in the database monitoring view under Database Alerts and Metrics. When this alert fires, administrators should increase the data disk size to prevent data service outage or read-only mode conditions. DSM also documents the ability to change the storage amount directly when disk warning or critical alerts are observed, allowing remediation within DSM itself without external tooling.
