For alert delivery, DSM supports webhook-based notifications that can route alert events to email recipients or to external applications such as Slack or ServiceNow. This allows organizations to connect DSM storage capacity alerts to their existing incident management or on-call workflows. Configuring these notification targets is an administrative step performed by the DSM Provider administrator.

The organization is responsible for defining which personnel receive these alerts and for configuring the notification delivery targets. Organizations that require alerting at thresholds other than DSM's defaults should verify whether the named threshold settings meet their requirements, or supplement DSM's built-in alerts with external monitoring tools that observe DSM disk metrics.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) includes an automated disk utilization alerting mechanism through its configurable metric-based alert framework. The Alert feature processes system events and metrics against organization-defined threshold rules and generates alerts that initiate configured notification actions. When disk usage on a Service Engine exceeds a configured threshold percentage, the Controller generates an alert and delivers it to appropriate personnel. The threshold percentage is organization-defined, allowing teams to set the level at which alerting fires.

The Avi Controller supports multiple alert notification destinations: email, syslog, SNMP v2c and SNMPv3 traps, ControlScript automation, and Splunk. These destinations are configured in the Operations > Notifications page. The Syslog-Audit-Persistence template supports streaming audit events to external syslog servers, integrating Avi alerts into centralized log management systems. SNMP notifications support both v2c and v3 protocols, with optional TLS encryption and strict certificate verification for secure delivery to SNMP-based monitoring infrastructure. Multiple SNMP servers can be configured simultaneously, and the Avi Controller cluster leader issues SNMP trap notifications based on system events.

Service Engine log storage is configurable through SE runtime properties. The debug-logs-capacity is calculated as (SE Total Disk minus Maximum Storage not allocated for logs on SE) divided by 100. Client log capacity is derived from total SE disk space minus the debug log allocation, with further adjustments based on configured minimum storage and SE RAM. Increasing disk resources on Controllers and Service Engines extends the volume of logs that can be stored and the overall retention duration.

For deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator for VCF clusters, the AKO persistentVolumeClaimSpec.storage parameter allocates a default of 2Gi of storage for the log Persistent Volume Claim, which can be adjusted at deployment time to accommodate larger log volumes.

To activate disk utilization alerting, administrators must configure a metric-based alert with an appropriate threshold and assign at least one notification destination with the relevant recipient addresses, trap server endpoints, or syslog server details in the Operations > Notifications page. Avi system component debug logs and Event Logs are not forwarded to remote syslog servers through OS-level syslog configuration; they require Avi-specific alert action configuration to reach external logging systems. These organizational configuration steps are required before automated alerting is active.

### Control AU-05(02)

> Provide an alert within [Assignment: organization-defined real-time period] to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel, roles, and/or locations] when the following audit failure events occur: [Assignment: organization-defined audit logging failure events requiring real-time alerts].

**SCF Controls:** MON-05.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple mechanisms for near real-time alerting when event log processing encounters failures, spanning the VMware vCenter alarm system, VCF Operations symptom-based alerting, VCF Log Management centralized collection, SNMP trap notification infrastructure, syslog forwarding validation, and Kubernetes workload cluster log pipeline monitoring.

vCenter alarm system

vCenter includes a built-in alarm system that can trigger notifications when specific events or conditions occur across the managed environment. Administrators create alarm definitions with trigger conditions and associated actions; when the conditions defined for an alarm occur, the specified action starts automatically. Alarm actions include sending SNMP traps, sending email notifications with configurable recipient addresses and alarm subject information, and executing scripts through PowerCLI's New-AlarmAction -Script parameter. The vCenter SNMP agent sends traps when the vCenter system is started and when alarms fire, providing continuous notification capability. These alarm capabilities operate around the clock and can be configured to monitor system health conditions, including logging service status and log forwarding connectivity.

VCF Operations symptom-based alerting

VCF Operations extends vCenter's alarm framework with a symptom-based alert system that supports multiple signal types: metrics, properties, message events, faults, metric events, and log-based symptoms. Administrators can define symptoms that detect anomalies in log processing pipelines, such as unexpected drops in log volume, log collection service failures, or missing expected log entries. These symptoms can be combined into alert definitions that notify appropriate personnel when log processing disruptions are detected. Log-based alerts are configured through the Symptom Definitions card in Infrastructure Operations, accessible under Configurations. VCF Operations also supports log-based conditions as an alternative to log-based symptoms, providing flexibility in how log processing health is monitored.

When an alert fires, administrators can attach text instructions that describe precisely how the engineer or administrator should fix the problem to resolve the alert, giving responders clear remediation guidance. Alert definitions also include built-in Recommendations that provide remediation options for resolving the problems indicated by generated alerts. Intelligent alerting capabilities help administrators filter alert noise and focus on the most significant issues.

VCF Operations demonstrates self-monitoring capability by generating a critical alert when no SNMP targets are configured and by generating warning-severity alerts when services such as the NSX Management Node SNMP Service stop running. Within NSX, the NSX Manager Logging Monitor uses the System Health Agent framework to evaluate log generation rates against each log's rotation settings in runtime and raise log duration alarms when logs are at risk of being flushed before collection, surfacing logging-pipeline problems as they develop rather than after log data is lost. This pattern of monitoring its own notification infrastructure helps ensure that alerting pathways remain functional.

Alert delivery and notification channels

VCF Operations supports multiple notification channels for alert delivery. Outbound SNMP trap notifications can be sent to up to four SNMP trap destinations per instance. The vCenter SNMP agent supports v1, v2c, and v3 notifications, with v3 supported at all security levels, giving organizations flexibility to align SNMP trap delivery with their security requirements. SNMP trap notification filtering allows administrators to control which alerts are forwarded to external management systems. Alerts can also be logged to a file on each VCF Operations node using the Log File plug-in, which supports configurable notification rules. PowerCLI provides programmatic control over alarm actions through the New-AlarmAction -Snmp parameter, enabling automated configuration of SNMP-based alerting across the environment.

Centralized log collection and absence detection

VCF Log Management pulls tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances and receives syslog feeds from VMware ESX hosts. This centralized log collection creates a secondary monitoring path: if log feeds from specific sources stop arriving, the absence itself can be detected and alerted on through VCF Operations symptom definitions. Log-based alert definitions can monitor host systems for specific conditions such as cluster performance failures, and they generate alerts when those conditions are detected.

Syslog forwarding infrastructure and health validation

At the host level, ESX supports configurable syslog with remote syslog server forwarding using the esxcli system syslog config set command. The ESX host firewall ruleset for syslog must be explicitly enabled using the esxcli network firewall ruleset set command. These configurations establish the log forwarding infrastructure whose health can then be monitored by VCF Operations.

When audit record transmission is active on an ESX host, the syslog daemon (vmsyslogd) emits audit records for its own operational events, including daemon start, stop, and error conditions, allowing administrators and monitoring tools to detect syslog service failures through the log stream itself.

The vCenter Management API provides a syslog forwarding test method that validates the forwarding configuration and returns a ConnectionStatus object showing the status of the connection between the vCenter instance and a remote server. This allows administrators to programmatically verify that syslog forwarding pathways are operational, and the test can be incorporated into automated health checks to detect log delivery failures before they become prolonged outages.

For organizations that forward logs to external SIEM systems via syslog, any disruption in log forwarding is itself a monitorable condition. VCF components generate fault events when connectivity to configured syslog targets is interrupted, and VCF Operations symptom definitions can be configured to detect these faults and raise alerts.

VMware Kubernetes Service log pipeline monitoring

Within VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters, the Kubernetes API server exposes the `apiserver_audit_event_total` Prometheus metric, which tracks the total number of audit events exported. Organizations can configure Prometheus to scrape this metric and define AlertManager rules to fire alerts when audit event rates drop unexpectedly or stop entirely, providing a mechanism to detect audit logging pipeline failures within Kubernetes workload clusters. Prometheus with AlertManager is available as a standard package for VKS clusters from version 2.45.0. The Prometheus package deploys AlertManager in the `tanzu-system-monitoring` namespace and configures Prometheus instances to route alerts to AlertManager via Kubernetes service discovery, filtering by namespace and component labels.

VKS clusters deploy Fluent Bit logging agents in the `vmware-system-logging` namespace for cluster-level log collection and forwarding. Fluent Bit supports log delivery to external destinations including Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk. Fluent Bit exposes internal metrics that can indicate forwarding failures or buffer drops, which can be fed into Prometheus alerting pipelines to detect log delivery interruptions. For workloads running in a vSphere Namespace, operators apply the LogAgentConfiguration custom resource to stream pod logs to a remote syslog server; each forwarded message carries metadata fields including namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name, supporting downstream correlation and monitoring.

Node Problem Detector includes a SystemLogMonitor daemon that monitors system logs according to predefined rules for log sources such as filelog, kmsg, and kernel subsystems. Detected problems are surfaced as Kubernetes NodeConditions and Events, which Prometheus can scrape and route through AlertManager for notification. This provides node-level visibility into log subsystem failures that may not be captured by API server metrics alone.

The Kubernetes audit policy determines what events are recorded and which backend receives them, with log file and webhook backends supported. Because the 24x7x365 alerting requirement involves ongoing operational configuration, organizations must define Prometheus scrape intervals, write alert rules targeting audit metric behavior and Fluent Bit forwarding health, and integrate AlertManager with notification channels. VKS provides the instrumentation layer; the operational alert configuration is the organization's responsibility.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides a native alerting framework that processes system events and metrics against user-defined rules and generates near real-time notifications through multiple channels. When audit record failures occur across the Events, Syslog, or Splunk subsystems, the Avi Controller and Service Engine log those failures and route them to configured notification destinations.

The Avi Controller supports four alert notification channels: email, external syslog servers, SNMP trap receivers, and ControlScripts. These are configured in the Operations > Notifications page of the Controller UI. The Controller cluster leader issues SNMP trap notifications based on system events, and multiple SNMP servers can be configured for redundancy. Both SNMPv2c and SNMPv3 protocols are supported for SNMP trap delivery. SNMP notifications can be configured with TLS encryption and strict certificate verification for secure transport to monitoring infrastructure.

For syslog-based notification, Avi provides the Syslog-Audit-Persistence mechanism, which streams alert events to external syslog servers. Alert events can be delivered using the SYSLOG_RFC5425_ENHANCED format for structured notification delivery. Syslog notifications push alert events to syslog servers; virtual service logs must be separately retrieved from the Controller through the API or pushed to a remote destination using a ControlScript.

The Avi Controller and Service Engine log audit record failures for trusted channel initiation, termination, and function failures to the Events, Syslog, and Splunk subsystems. Custom alert configurations allow administrators to map specific system events to specific notification actions. The Controller also executes ControlScripts when alert conditions are triggered, generating detailed execution logs including operation status and error conditions. Avi Event Logs provide an audit trail that records system activities and configuration changes with information about who made them and when.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control AU-06

> a. Review and analyze system audit records [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] for indications of [Assignment: organization-defined inappropriate or unusual activity] and the potential impact of the inappropriate or unusual activity; b. Report findings to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]; and c. Adjust the level of audit record review, analysis, and reporting within the system when there is a change in risk based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other credible sources of information.

**SCF Controls:** MON-02, MON-02.6

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides configurable audit and monitoring capabilities through VCF Operations that support an organization's ability to adjust audit review, analysis, and reporting in response to changing threat conditions.

Event auditing within VCF Operations registers user actions and platform interactions, providing visibility into changes across the infrastructure as well as insights into suspicious access events and policy violations. Audit events capture interactions such as searches, logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications, creating records that facilitate security and compliance audits and provide details for conducting security forensics. VCF Operations generates audit events across categories including Access, Access_control, Account_management, Configuration, Data_access, Network, Notification, Permissions, Session, System, Policy, and Firewall. Administrators can adjust the audit events time range through a drop-down menu with available presets, enabling focused review of activity during periods of elevated concern, and can apply filters to concentrate review on specific activity types, users, or time periods.

VCF Operations generates alerts with severity levels including Critical, Immediate, Warning, and Information. Alert definitions require configuration of name, description, thresholds, criticality, and the number of wait cycles, and alerts trigger when defined threshold levels are violated. This allows administrators to tighten monitoring by lowering thresholds or adding new alert definitions when threat conditions change. Risk alerts in VCF Operations indicate problems that require near-term investigation, helping audit staff identify areas that warrant deeper analysis. The Compliance Alert List in VCF Operations displays alerts that triggered compliance violations, sortable by Alert ID, Criticality, Alert name, and trigger time, which allows audit staff to prioritize review based on severity. When investigating individual alerts, the Potential Evidence tab presents events, property changes, and anomalous metrics that may be relevant to the alert, supporting deeper analysis when threat conditions warrant it.

NSX Manager audit logs further contribute by capturing both old and new values for modified security policies, enabling detailed change tracking during heightened review periods. VCF Compliance monitoring based on the VCF Audit Guides can be enabled within VCF Operations, giving organizations baseline compliance checks that can inform decisions about where to increase audit scrutiny. A system audit report is also available to help administrators understand the scale of the VCF Operations environment, which is useful when determining the scope of an intensified audit review.

VCF Operations supports centralized log collection through configurable remote syslog host settings and log forwarding capabilities. VCF Log Management instances can be configured for log forwarding, where multiple instances forward logs to a single integrated instance. VMware vCenter supports syslog forwarding configuration through its REST API. These capabilities allow integration with a SIEM or security analytics platform that consumes threat intelligence feeds, enabling organizations to correlate VCF audit data with external threat indicators and adjust their review and analysis posture accordingly.

Within the Consumption layer of VCF, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) provides a configurable audit infrastructure for workload API activity through the Kubernetes API server audit policy system. An audit policy is defined as a set of ordered PolicyRule objects, each specifying which resources, verbs, namespaces, and users it applies to and at what level of detail. Four audit levels are available: None, which suppresses the event; Metadata, which records the requesting user, timestamp, resource, and verb without request or response bodies; Request, which adds the request body; and RequestResponse, which records the full request and response bodies. Rules are evaluated in order, with the first matching rule applied, and the default level is None unless overridden by a catch-all rule at the end of the policy. This structure allows administrators to write targeted rules that capture full request and response bodies for high-risk API calls while recording only metadata for routine operations, and to respond to new threat intelligence by inserting or reordering rules to capture specific API operations, resources, or user identities at a higher level of detail. The audit throttling level for the kube-apiserver is also configurable, controlling how many events are processed per second; during periods of elevated concern, reducing the throttle allows a higher volume of events to be retained without records being dropped. Audit logs can be forwarded to a webhook backend or written to a file, enabling integration with a SIEM or log aggregation platform that consumes threat intelligence feeds. Human and service account activity can be tracked separately within VKS audit policy rules, supporting differentiated audit strategies across different principal types.

Pod Security Admission (PSA) in VKS supports three operating modes per namespace: enforce, audit, and warn. In audit mode, pod security violations are recorded in the audit log as an annotation on the event record without rejecting the workload, adding audit coverage of namespace security posture without disrupting running applications. The podSecurityStandard.audit field in VKS can be set to privileged, baseline, or restricted per cluster, and the audit and warn modes can independently be set to a stricter standard than enforce, giving operators visibility into what future tighter enforcement would affect before committing to it. Administrators can adjust PSA mode settings on a per-namespace basis to widen or narrow the audit capture surface as threat conditions change. An Organization Auditor role in VKS cluster management provides read-only visibility into cluster management resources within the assigned organization scope, supporting audit review workflows without granting administrative access.

At the VMware ESX host level, audit record behavior is governed by settings in the Syslog.global.auditRecord namespace, which take effect immediately upon configuration. ESX audit records use Informational and Debug severity classifications and are not activated by default in all configurations; certified deployments typically require explicit activation of audit records.

VMware vDefend, available as a separate product that extends VCF's networking foundation, supports virtual patching with custom IDS/IPS signatures that can be deployed in response to new threat intelligence, and can integrate with threat intelligence sources for sector-specific threat data.

While VCF provides the technical mechanisms to conduct configurable audit review and compliance monitoring across the platform and Consumption layers, the platform does not natively consume threat intelligence feeds from law enforcement, industry associations, or other external sources. Adjusting audit levels in response to evolving threat information requires organizational processes that interpret external threat intelligence and translate it into changes in audit scope, frequency, and focus within VCF's monitoring tools. Organizations should establish procedures for receiving threat intelligence, evaluating its relevance to their VCF environment, and directing audit staff to use VCF Operations' configurable reporting, alerting, and threshold capabilities, as well as VKS audit policy and PSA mode adjustments, accordingly.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend does not provide a mechanism to ingest or act on external threat intelligence from law enforcement, industry associations, or other credible sources; that function requires organizational processes and external integrations such as a SIEM or threat intelligence platform. The Security Services Platform includes an Auditor role with read-only access to view information and track activity, and a dedicated local Audit account with full API access for system auditing, but neither can modify audit levels or respond to external threat feeds. Security Services Platform audit logs are enabled by default and cannot be disabled, providing a continuous audit record that supports any subsequent level adjustments made through external processes.

Where vDefend contributes is in the internal threat visibility it provides to help security teams assess whether audit intensity should be raised. Security Intelligence, hosted on the Security Services Platform, categorizes detected suspicious traffic events by impact type (Critical, High, Medium, and Low) to help prioritize response actions. Campaign Correlation Rules display an impact score from 0 to 100, with 100 indicating the highest severity of the identified threat, and the Campaign view tracks the total number of detections and threats identified under Malicious Activity. The blast radius view in Security Intelligence identifies lateral movement patterns and assesses the true scope of a security incident. The threat visualization canvas supports rapid threat containment, identifying compromised assets, and adjusting microsegmentation policy. Security Intelligence also provides the Security Segmentation Report, which assesses the effectiveness of segmentation implementation and gives an overall view of security posture. Security teams can investigate individual threat events and correlated campaigns through the Security Services Platform interface to assess impact, identify root causes, and take appropriate mitigation actions. These capabilities give security teams the internal situational awareness to decide whether audit scrutiny should be elevated for specific segments or workloads, even when the trigger for that decision originates from an external intelligence source.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) generates security-relevant event logs and supports forwarding them to centralized log collection and SIEM platforms through multiple native mechanisms.

The Protection and Recovery Appliance provides built-in syslog forwarding to remote syslog servers. The forwarding configuration supports a selectable transport protocol and a configurable destination port (default 514). PNR includes a test message capability to verify that a remote syslog server is receiving messages before relying on it operationally. REST API endpoints are available for managing syslog configuration, including endpoints to list all configured syslog servers, update syslog server settings, and send test messages programmatically.

PNR's event subsystem integrates with VMware vCenter, registering protection and recovery-specific events in the vCenter event infrastructure. Each event type has a corresponding alarm that PNR can trigger when the event occurs, enabling automated notification and downstream forwarding through vCenter's event pipeline to external SIEM platforms. PNR logs security-relevant events including login activity with timestamps, user identity, session keys, and locale information; authorization error messages with timestamps and component identification; and protection group configuration changes. The SrmDpxAgentUser role enables the DPX agent service to log events directly to vCenter Event Manager.

The Clean Room Orchestrator component supports native event forwarding to VCF Log Management (version 9.0 or later), Microsoft Sentinel, and Splunk Cloud. Integration with Microsoft Sentinel uses Data Collection Rules (DCR) and Data Collection Endpoints (DCE) within an Azure Log Analytics Workspace, with DCE and Log Analytics Workspace required in the same Azure subscription and resource group. Splunk Cloud integration is configured using a Splunk URL and authentication token. Clean Room Orchestrator logs events for protection group creation and deletion, replication tasks, and snapshot operations. Event records include timestamp, priority level, message content, component name, severity level, configuration ID, component ID, and event type.

vSphere Replication, as a PNR component, maintains audit logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hms/hms-audit.log and broker logs at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hbr on the Appliance and at /var/run/log on VMware ESX hosts. vSphere Replication generates typed audit events for host and virtual machine replication configuration and unconfiguration changes, using structured event type identifiers.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides Avi-native mechanisms for adjusting the granularity of audit review, analysis, and reporting at the application-delivery layer, operating separately from the platform-level audit infrastructure provided by VCF.

In Avi 32.1, the Avi Cloud Console includes a Live Security Threat Intelligence service that delivers security threat feeds for multiple attack vectors in real time. When enabled, this service automatically updates WAF signatures and the IP Reputation database on connected Avi controllers, allowing WAF policy and bot detection to reflect current threat intelligence without requiring manual rule updates. The service is opt-in and disabled by default; it is activated through Cloud Services configuration. The User Agent Database Sync capability can also be enabled separately to receive User-Agent updates that support bot threat detection.

At the Web Application Firewall (WAF) layer, Avi supports configurable logging levels per rule action through the WAF Profile. Administrators can set additional logging (`log`) and audit logging (`auditlog`) levels to control the verbosity of WAF rule evaluations. When threat intelligence indicates heightened risk from a specific attack category, administrators can tune these settings to capture more detail from matching rules without increasing noise across all traffic.

At the data-plane layer, Avi exposes configurable audit settings through the Service Engine time tracker properties (`se_time_tracker_properties`) within the Service Engine group configuration. The `conn_est_audit_mode` setting controls latency auditing for connection flows and defaults to `LATENCY_AUDIT_ON`. The `ingress_audit_mode` and `egress_audit_mode` settings default to `SE_TT_AUDIT_ON_WITH_EVENT`. The `fe_conn_est_audit_mode` setting defaults to `TT_AUDIT_ON_WITH_SIG`, capturing front-end connection establishment events with signal detail. Administrators can adjust these modes to gather deeper per-flow data during periods of elevated concern.

The Avi Application Security Report gives security teams and executives a view of overall security posture, including an executive summary, ranked lists of top attack types (such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting), lists of top attacking IP addresses, and trend summaries of total and flagged requests. The report is available as a downloadable PDF and is designed to support audit-ready documentation of application security posture. The WAF Evaluation Report, generated when WAF operates in Evaluation Policy Mode, provides observations about WAF findings and recommendations that can inform decisions about tightening WAF policy or adjusting audit scope.

The Avi Controller Config Audit Trail records user activity events and configuration changes over a selectable period, accessible via Operations > Events > Config Audit Trail. The Avi Controller can also stream audit-relevant alerts to external syslog servers through the Syslog-Audit-Persistence configuration, supporting integration with centralized monitoring systems.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control AU-06(01)

> Audit Record Review, Analysis, and Reporting | Automated Process Integration Integrate audit record review, analysis, and reporting processes using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].

**SCF Controls:** MON-03.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF protects sensitive and regulated data in log files through a layered approach that spans design philosophy, access controls, data masking, centralized log management, and cryptographic protections. The Consumption layer, including VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), vSphere namespaces, and the Kubernetes control plane, extends these protections to workload infrastructure running on the VCF platform.

At the hypervisor layer, VMware ESX log files are not encrypted at rest because they are designed not to contain sensitive data such as passwords or encryption keys. This design principle reduces the risk of sensitive data exposure in host-level logs by avoiding the problem at the source. The platform documentation acknowledges that core files and audit logs may contain sensitive information, which drives the design of access restrictions and handling procedures around log artifacts. When support bundles or core files are collected, administrators should be aware that these artifacts might contain sensitive information and handle them accordingly. vSphere also supports encrypting Fault Tolerance log traffic to reduce exposure of sensitive virtual machine state data during replication operations. This is configured via the Encrypted FT option in VM Options; placing Fault Tolerance logging traffic on a dedicated private network provides additional network-level isolation.

VCF Operations for Networks includes a control that allows Admin role users to view and enable or disable Personally Identifiable Information (PII) in audit logs. This capability gives administrators direct control over whether sensitive identity data is recorded in log output, reducing exposure of regulated data at the point of generation.

At the Kubernetes API server layer, VKS clusters support a configurable audit policy that determines what data appears in API server audit logs. Audit policy rules are defined in a configuration file and support four logging levels: None (no event recorded), Metadata (request metadata only, without request or response bodies), Request (request metadata and body), and RequestResponse (full metadata, request, and response bodies). Applying the Metadata level to sensitive resource types such as Secrets and ConfigMaps allows administrators to capture meaningful audit trails while keeping the contents of sensitive resources out of log records. Audit log files are written to a protected path on the control plane node using the --audit-log-path flag, and the Kubernetes audit logger records all actions taken by the API for later analysis in the event of a security incident.

Access to log data is controlled through role-based access at multiple levels. VCF Log Management requires permissions to be granted to VCF SSO users and groups before they can access the instance, restricting who can view and search log data. VCF Operations provides role-based access to audit data, restricting who can view users, objects, and collected information. Auditing capabilities are designed to meet audit requirements for business-critical applications that contain sensitive data. Audit data can be filtered by User ID, User Name, Auth Source, Session, Client IP, Category, and Message, allowing administrators to scope access to log content and limit exposure of sensitive fields. Event auditing registers each platform interaction, increasing user accountability by tracking who accessed or modified log-related configurations. At the Kubernetes layer, access to log data through the API, such as retrieving pod logs with kubectl logs or querying cluster events, is governed by Kubernetes RBAC. ClusterRoles and custom roles restrict which principals are permitted to retrieve log output, reducing the exposure of sensitive data that workloads write to standard output. Audit log files on control plane node filesystem paths are subject to host operating system file permissions.

Centralized log collection through VCF Operations reduces the number of locations where sensitive log data resides. Rather than leaving application log messages distributed across local file systems on individual components, VCF Log Management aggregates log sources into a managed environment. The VCF Log Management UI supports log source configuration and partitioning, which allows administrators to isolate sensitive log streams from general operational data. For workloads running in vSphere namespaces, the LogAgentConfiguration Custom Resource can be applied to a namespace to stream syslog messages from vSphere Pods to a remote syslog server; each forwarded message carries structured metadata fields including namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name. On VKS clusters, Fluent Bit logging agents deployed in the vmware-system-logging namespace collect and forward logs from cluster components. Fluent Bit tags Kubernetes API server audit logs separately from container logs and Linux daemon logs, which allows different forwarding, access control, and retention policies to be applied to audit data; Fluent Bit supports forwarding to VCF Log Management, Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk.

For log data in transit, VCF supports forwarding logs to external syslog targets using TLS encryption. NSX Manager syslog supports the TLS protocol and requires port 6514 to be open on both the NSX Manager and the remote log server for encrypted log transmission. Fluent Bit on VKS clusters supports TLS for log forwarding to syslog targets. When forwarding to VCF Log Management, Fluent Bit connects via CFAPI on port 9543 for TLS-encrypted connections or port 9000 for non-TLS connections. For environments that require validated cryptographic protections, VCF Log Management supports FIPS Compliance Mode as a deployment option. When enabled, this mode applies FIPS-validated cryptographic algorithms to data handling within the log management infrastructure. Kubernetes documentation recommends deploying cryptographic protections in high-assurance environments to help keep logs tamper-resistant and confidential.

Log retention and lifecycle management help reduce exposure of any inadvertently logged sensitive data. VCF Operations allows administrators to edit log file folders, limit retained log size, and set logging levels. The log.keepOld parameter controls how many log files are retained, and administrators can limit log file size on virtual machines. By default, ESX log files are stored in an in-memory file system and are lost when the host reboots; configuring persistent logging to a datastore preserves audit log continuity across host restarts and reduces the risk of losing sensitive audit data. These controls reduce the window during which sensitive data remains on disk.

NSX audit log messages include a structured data field (audit="true") that distinguishes audit records from operational logs, enabling targeted retention and access policies for audit-sensitive content.

Pod Security Admission (PSA) on VKS clusters provides an additional mechanism for generating security-relevant audit annotations. In PSA audit mode, security policy violations trigger an audit annotation added to the event recorded in the Kubernetes API server audit log without blocking the workload. This allows administrators to identify workloads that violate Pod security standards while maintaining operations, and those audit annotations can be captured and forwarded through the Fluent Bit pipeline for centralized review.

Kubernetes documentation recommends that applications running on VKS avoid logging Secret values in the clear after reading them from environment variables or mounted volumes, so that credentials and other regulated data do not appear in container standard output or standard error streams. This is design-time guidance rather than a platform enforcement mechanism; organizations deploying workloads on VKS are responsible for implementing log sanitization at the application level.

Defining what counts as sensitive log content in the organization's context, configuring the toggles per log destination, operating access policy on log management infrastructure, defining and enforcing Kubernetes audit policy rules for sensitive resource types, configuring application workloads to avoid logging credentials or regulated data, and verifying that log storage systems apply appropriate access controls and encryption at rest sit with the organization.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend protects sensitive and regulated data in its log files through TLS-encrypted log forwarding, structured log content, and integration with centralized log management.

For log data in transit, the Malware Prevention Service VM can be configured to forward log messages to a remote logging server using TLS with mutual certificate authentication. The configuration accepts logging server IP address, protocol, log level, server CA certificate, client CA certificate, client certificate, and client key. The Malware Prevention Service VM currently supports only the SECURITY message ID for log filtering when transmitting to a remote server, which limits the log categories forwarded.

Malware Prevention Service event logs record a detailed set of file analysis lifecycle events: file intercepted, verdict cache hit, file sent for local static analysis, file sent for cloud dynamic analysis, verdict obtained, and policy enforced. Each entry includes structured JSON attributes covering file hashes (SHA256, SHA1, MD5), file metadata (fileName, fileType, fileSize), malware classification, malware family, error codes, HTTP domain, HTTP method, user agent, file upload status, submission status, verdict classification (such as BENIGN), and analysis score. Given the sensitivity of this data, access to these logs at the receiving system should be restricted to authorized personnel.

VMware vDefend Distributed IDS/IPS can send events to external syslog consumers by enabling the ids_events_to_syslog setting on an IDS/IPS profile; the setting is disabled by default. IDS/IPS logs include the user ID whose traffic generated the event, providing identity context alongside detection metadata. vDefend components write to log files in /var/log, with syslog messages conforming to RFC 5424 on vDefend appliances and RFC 3164 on ESX hosts. Both NSX Manager and ESX hosts must be configured to forward syslog to the central log repository for complete IDS/IPS event coverage.

Network Detection and Response supports SSL verification for event logs sent over HTTPS, protecting detection event data in transit when logs are forwarded to an external receiving system.

Identity Firewall Event Log Sources can be configured to collect logs from Active Directory Event Log Sources or from VCF Log Management. VCF Log Management provides a centralized log management framework for vDefend; logs are forwarded by configuring VCF Log Management as a syslog server, where centralized access controls, retention policies, and enterprise-wide log governance can be applied.

While vDefend provides TLS transport protection for Malware Prevention logs, IDS/IPS event forwarding with identity context, SSL-verified transport in Network Detection and Response, and integration paths to centralized logging, the broader log protection framework including enterprise-wide retention policies, centralized access governance, and log access controls at the receiving system requires VCF platform components and organizational processes outside vDefend's scope.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) addresses this control through a combination of by-design log content restrictions, access controls, log storage options, and database-engine-level audit configurations that reduce the exposure of sensitive data in audit and operational log files.

DSM audit logs are structured around a defined set of fields: Component, Event Type, Event Details, Event Time (UTC), Status, and Source. The Source field records either the initiating user's email identifier or "System" for automated operations. Gateway audit log files contain standard Kubernetes audit events, and the structured field schema limits what data appears in log records by design. This design is documented under Audit Logs in Data Services Manager in the DSM documentation.

Access to audit log data within DSM is controlled through the role-based access model. The System Audit Events view in the Provider interface is the designated location for reviewing non-database-related operations and tasks; access is scoped to authenticated DSM sessions. Audit events can be exported in CSV or XLSX format through the ACTIONS drop-down in the System Audit Events view, and exports reflect events visible to the requesting user based on their role.

Gateway audit log files are stored on the Provider VM at /var/log/tdm/provider/audit/ with filenames in the format gateway-audit-timestamp.log. Access to these files is governed by the Provider VM's operating system controls. DSM can be configured to archive gateway audit log files periodically to an S3-compatible repository using the Provider Log Repo Url setting under Managing Storage Settings, which allows organizations to consolidate audit logs in a separately protected storage system. DSM also supports log forwarding, configurable through the log-forwarding setting in Managing Storage Settings, allowing logs to be sent to a central log aggregation system such as a syslog-compatible platform where additional access restrictions can be applied.

For SQL Server databases, DSM provides additional audit logging controls at the engine level. When creating a SQL Server cluster, administrators can configure Login Auditing to select the level of audit logging for system login events and set a log retention period. DSM uses server-level DDL triggers to restrict the file paths available for SQL Server audit logs, limiting where those logs can be written. The errorLog.numErrorLogs engine setting controls how many error log files are retained before recycling.

DSM restricts what is logged and provides mechanisms to forward and archive logs. Organizations are responsible for implementing access controls on the S3 log repository, configuring the receiving log aggregation system with appropriate restrictions, applying log retention and integrity policies, and reviewing log contents to confirm that no application-level data patterns create inadvertent sensitive data exposure in event detail fields.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer includes a Sensitive Log Profile (sensitive_log_profile) that masks or removes sensitive data from application-layer client logs on a per-virtual-service basis. The profile is configured through the Analytics Profile, under the Sensitive Log Information section of the Analytics tab in the Avi Controller UI, or via the CLI using the `configure analyticsprofile` command. Administrators define match criteria to identify and redact fields such as REQUEST_COOKIES, Args:Password, Basic Authorization tokens, OAuth credentials, and other personally identifiable information (PII) before those values are written to the log. Both masking (replacing the value with a placeholder) and removal (excluding the field from the log entry entirely) are supported, applied at the application delivery layer where Avi processes traffic. Sensitive log profile configurations can also be deployed and managed through Ansible and Terraform interfaces.

For traffic inspected by Avi's Web Application Firewall (WAF), the Sensitive Log Profile extends to WAF match logs and application log headers, restricting exposure of session tokens or credential data that would otherwise appear in WAF event records. OAuth-related sensitive fields, including Basic Authorization headers, can be masked before being written to the application log.

Avi's configuration audit log records administrative changes, capturing event type, object name, tenant, timestamp, and the identity of the user who performed the action. These audit events can be forwarded to external syslog servers using the Syslog-Audit-Persistence alert action template configured under Alert Actions. Avi Controller and Service Engine audit records for trusted channel initiation, termination, and failures can be delivered to Events, Syslog, and Splunk destinations. Event Logs are filterable by event type (for example, event_id=CONFIG_CREATE or event_id=CONFIG_UPDATE) to support targeted audit review. For assured retention of all virtual service logs, Avi documentation recommends streaming them directly to an external server such as Splunk.

In Kubernetes deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO), the log verbosity level (AKOSettings.logLevel) can be configured to INFO, DEBUG, WARN, or ERROR, controlling the detail captured in operator logs.

An operational note: Avi system component debug logs and Avi Event Logs are not delivered through the OS-level syslog configuration. Debug logs are available only through Tech Support bundle generation, and Event Logs require separate API-based or scripted export to external logging systems.

Protection of the underlying storage holding Avi Controller and Service Engine VM disks, including data-at-rest encryption and hypervisor integrity controls, is handled by the VCF platform layer rather than by Avi itself.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control AU-06(03)

> Analyze and correlate audit records across different repositories to gain organization-wide situational awareness.

**SCF Controls:** MON-02.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlates detection events into campaigns, comparing new detection data against existing events to identify related activity. Campaigns provide a view of a threat by correlating detection events to identify the different stages of an attack, and security analysts can view and triage threat campaigns through a campaign timeline. NDR supports SIEM integration, sending campaign and event logs to a connected SIEM in real time, allowing network security event data to be exported for broader correlation with other enterprise data sources.

Security Intelligence detects and categorizes suspicious events using MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques, recording event occurrences during selected time periods, and provides a View Full Event History capability that displays all events associated with a given signature ID and intrusion severity. Security Intelligence also correlates IDS events from the same source and destination hosts to connect related events and build a broader picture of an attack. This structured, MITRE-tagged event data can support SIEM-based analysis and enterprise-wide threat correlation.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) can be configured to forward log messages to external syslog servers for centralized log collection. SSP syslog configuration supports TCP, TLS, and UDP protocols and allows administrators to specify server address and port, integrating with existing SIEM ingestion pipelines. NDR Sensors report metrics covering health, traffic trends, and data processing activity, available as point-in-time measurements or aggregated data over time.

vDefend correlates only technical network security data and has no capability to ingest or correlate non-technical information such as HR records, physical access logs, or operational data. Satisfying the full scope of this control, which requires correlation of both technical and non-technical information across the enterprise, requires an external SIEM or equivalent platform.

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control AU-06(05)

> Integrate analysis of audit records with analysis of [Selection (one or more): vulnerability scanning information; performance data; system monitoring information; [Assignment: organization-defined data/information collected from other sources]] to further enhance the ability to identify inappropriate or unusual activity.

**SCF Controls:** MON-02.3

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides automated mechanisms to integrate audit record analysis with vulnerability scanning data, network performance monitoring, system monitoring, and other operational sources across the infrastructure stack. This integration is achieved through several interconnected components. VCF Operations is the centralized monitoring and analytics platform, and VCF Log Management aggregates log data from multiple VCF components into a unified collection point.

VCF Log Management can be configured to pull tasks, events, and alerts from VMware vCenter instances and receive syslog feeds from VMware ESX hosts through its Log Collections configuration. This brings audit records from across the infrastructure into a single platform where they can be analyzed alongside operational and performance data. VCF Log Management collects and aggregates log data from vCenter and ESX hosts, providing dashboards and analytics for centralized log analysis that spans the full infrastructure stack.

VCF Operations itself generates audit events across a broad set of categories, including Access, Access_control, Account_management, Configuration, Data_access, Network, Notification, Permissions, Session, System, Policy, and Firewall. Each audit event includes a detailed view containing information that supports analysis of events in the virtual infrastructure for suspicious user actions or system issues. The event auditing capability provides visibility into platform interaction changes across the VCF infrastructure and surfaces suspicious access events and policy violations. Audit events capture platform interactions such as user logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications.

The audit log in VCF Operations is integrated with the syslog subsystem, and the syslog collector can be configured to collect all audit logs. This integration allows audit records to flow through the same infrastructure as system and application logs, facilitating correlation. VCF Operations can also send user activity logs to an external syslog server for security auditing, enabling correlation with third-party SIEM or security analytics platforms. System audit reports can be downloaded in PDF or XLS format for offline analysis and compliance review.

VCF Operations provides several capabilities that support cross-source analysis and anomaly identification. Metric correlation runs on all metrics for a selected object to find metrics with similar or opposite behavioral change over the same time period, using both self-metrics and peer-object options. This allows administrators to correlate audit activity with performance deviations across the infrastructure. VCF Operations also supports dynamic thresholds for metrics, calculated based on historical and incoming data, which generate alerts when current values fall outside trended ranges. Dynamic threshold analysis uses a period of seven days of historical data before generating alerts. Alert definitions support metric event symptoms based on events from monitored systems where a selected metric violates a threshold, enabling automated responses when performance metrics indicate unusual activity.

VCF Operations collects OS and Application Monitoring Properties for operating systems, application services, remote checks, Linux processes, and Windows services, which can be used to create reports, views, and dashboards that combine audit data with system performance and health metrics. Self-monitoring metrics track internal health indicators such as CheckThresholdAndHealth outcome observations. Sensor metrics including temperature, fan speed, and fan health state are collected for host systems, broadening the scope of monitored data available for correlation with audit records.

At the host and network layer, ESX hosts can be configured to send syslog messages to a remote log server using the esxcli system syslog config set command, with configurable log levels to control the detail captured. The Syslog.global.auditRecord.remote parameter specifically enables forwarding of security audit records to the remote host defined by Syslog.global.logHost, providing a dedicated control for audit-record transmission that is separate from general syslog configuration. vCenter supports syslog forwarding to up to three remote servers using TCP, UDP, TLS, or RELP protocols, allowing audit records from across the VCF stack to be sent to a SIEM or log aggregation platform. Event streaming from vCenter to a remote syslog server can be enabled independently through the vpxd.event.syslog.enabled option, configured in the vCenter Management Interface. NSX audit logs are sent directly to the remote syslog server, and NSX logging and auditing capabilities can be integrated with logging and monitoring solutions to monitor for security events and anomalies. NSX Manager syslog supports TLS protocol on port 6514 for encrypted log transmission. NSX nodes can be configured to send syslog messages to multiple remote servers, each with independently specified port numbers and protocols.

VCF Operations for Networks provides its own audit log, which is integrated with syslog so that network monitoring data can be collected alongside infrastructure logs. VCF Operations for Networks can be configured to send problem alerts and platform or collector syslogs to remote syslog servers. Audit log data from VCF Operations for Networks can also be exported in CSV format for offline analysis.

The vCenter alarm infrastructure integrates with other server components such as events and performance counters, and supports metric-based alarm expressions that can trigger alerts when performance metrics indicate unusual activity. VCF PowerCLI supports creating metric-based alarm triggers with New-AlarmTrigger to define thresholds and conditions for CPU usage and other performance metrics. vCenter can also create alarms to monitor events on vSAN objects, including the cluster, ESX hosts, datastores, networks, and virtual machines.

Integration with external systems extends the correlation capabilities further. In integrated environments, VCF Automation insights and alerts for monitored objects can be accessed alongside VCF Operations data through the Alerts tab in the VCF Automation main menu, providing cross-product visibility. A ServiceNow Details dashboard provides status, relationships, health, alerts, and KPIs for monitored resources, connecting infrastructure monitoring data with IT service management workflows.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control AU-06(06)

> Correlate information from audit records with information obtained from monitoring physical access to further enhance the ability to identify suspicious, inappropriate, unusual, or malevolent activity.

**SCF Controls:** MON-02.4

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control AU-07

> Provide and implement an audit record reduction and report generation capability that: a. Supports on-demand audit record review, analysis, and reporting requirements and after- the-fact investigations of incidents; and b. Does not alter the original content or time ordering of audit records.

**SCF Controls:** MON-06

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides event log report generation capabilities through VCF Operations and its integrated logging infrastructure, allowing administrators and auditors to generate, schedule, and customize reports from event log data to support the detection and assessment of anomalous activities.

VCF Operations includes a reporting engine that generates scheduled snapshots of views and dashboards covering objects, metrics, and alerts. Administrators can create reports using predefined report templates or build custom reports tailored to their monitoring requirements. Reports can be scheduled to run at regular intervals, providing recurring visibility into resource utilization, operational health, and potential risks. The reporting functions capture details related to both current and predicted resource needs, giving operations teams a forward-looking view of the environment. VCF Operations also provides audit reports that offer traceability of objects and users across the environment.

For event-specific analysis, VCF Operations provides an Event Trends capability where administrators can construct and run queries against collected log events, apply filters, and view results in the Event Trends tab. This feature supports trend and anomaly analysis by allowing comparison of query results against the same time period immediately prior, making it straightforward to identify deviations from normal activity patterns. The Log Analysis Widget further allows customization of the visual representation of events through aggregation queries, enabling tailored views of event data for different reporting needs. The troubleshooting incident page highlights anomalies in collected metrics and supports filtering by metric name, entity name, and other parameters for targeted investigation.

Event auditing within VCF provides visibility into platform interaction changes across the infrastructure and surfaces suspicious access events and policy violations. The VCF Operations Audit Events framework captures interactions such as searches, logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources. Each action is registered as an event with relevant metadata, enabling detection of suspicious access patterns and policy violations while providing user accountability through detailed activity tracking. Audit logs across VCF components record security-related events with timestamps, source identifiers, results, and event descriptions. For Common Criteria evaluated configurations, all auditable events are logged for each applicable requirement.

VCF Log Management extends these capabilities by collecting tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances and receiving syslog feeds from ESX hosts through its Log Collections configuration. The Logging Monitor feature generates reports with detailed information about logging anomalies such as log spew problems, helping administrators identify unusual logging patterns that may indicate security events. The VCF Operations audit log is integrated with syslog, and the syslog collector can be configured to aggregate all audit logs centrally.

The VCF consumption layer, encompassing VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters running on vSphere Supervisor, provides Kubernetes-native audit logging and log collection capabilities that extend event reporting to containerized workloads. The Kubernetes API server on VKS clusters generates structured audit events in the audit.k8s.io API group; each event records the requesting user identity, timestamp, target resource, unique audit ID, and event stage. Administrators configure the scope and detail of captured events through an audit policy file, selecting from four detail levels: None, Metadata (recording user, timestamp, resource, and verb), Request (which adds the request body), and RequestResponse (which adds the full response body). Audit events can be written to a local log file through the --audit-log-path flag or forwarded to an external log aggregation or SIEM platform through the webhook backend. The kube-apiserver exposes the apiserver_audit_event_total Prometheus metric, allowing operators to monitor audit event volume and detect sudden changes in activity that may indicate anomalous behavior. The metric carries 'allowed' and 'denied' label dimensions that distinguish events by authorization decision; a companion apiserver_audit_level_total counter tracks the distribution of audit events across policy levels, with one increment per request. Fluent Bit logging agents, deployed as a standard package on VKS clusters, collect logs from across cluster nodes and can forward them to Syslog, HTTP, Elasticsearch, Kafka, and Splunk destinations, supporting integration with centralized log analysis and reporting platforms. Fluent Bit on VKS clusters also collects Kubernetes API audit logs in JSON format, Linux authentication logs, and container runtime interface logs through configurable parsers and filters. At the Supervisor control plane level, Fluent Bit separately collects the Supervisor's own kube-apiserver audit logs using the audit.apiserver.* tag from the path /var/log/vmware/audit/kube-apiserver.log and stores them for forwarding to external monitoring systems. Administrators can configure the Supervisor to preserve the source IP addresses of authentication and Kubernetes API server requests, making those addresses available in the resulting audit log entries to support more precise attribution of access events. For workloads running in vSphere Namespaces, the LogAgentConfiguration custom resource streams syslog messages from vSphere Pods to a remote syslog server, with each message carrying the namespace name, pod UID, pod name, container name, and ESX host name as metadata to support structured querying and reporting. The SystemLogMonitor component of the Node Problem Detector monitors file-based logs, journald, and kmsg sources on Linux nodes and surfaces node-level problems as Kubernetes conditions and events. Seccomp profiles on VKS workloads support the SCMP_ACT_LOG action, which logs all syscalls to syslog or auditd without restricting execution, providing syscall-level audit trails for workloads where that granularity is required. VKS support bundles collect logs from control-plane and infrastructure components including CAPI, CAPV, VM Operator, and VKS Controller Manager, as well as containerd journal logs from Linux nodes and Windows Event Log data from System, Security, and Application sources on Windows nodes.

VCF Automation maintains audit logs that display status values or messages for each reportable action type, including timestamps, user names, project names, and trace identifiers, providing granular audit trail data available for report generation.

At the infrastructure level, ESX hosts maintain local logging with configurable retention and rotation policies. ESX log files rotate at each power cycle by default or at a configured file size. ESX hosts support configurable syslog log levels that can be set and reloaded using esxcli commands, and can forward syslog messages to remote log servers for centralized collection. Audit records specifically can be directed to remote syslog hosts using the `Syslog.global.auditRecord.remote` parameter. VCF supports syslog forwarding from vCenter to up to three remote servers using TCP, UDP, TLS, and RELP protocols, with TLS encryption available for confidentiality in transit. Event streaming from vCenter is enabled via the `vpxd.event.syslog.enabled` setting; each forwarded event includes fields for event ID, event type, severity, user, target object, chain ID, and description, providing a structured record suitable for SIEM ingestion. This enables integration with external log management and SIEM platforms for consolidated report generation and correlation.

The vSphere Web Services API provides programmatic access to event data through custom event structures (EventEx) with metadata and event filtering through EventFilterSpec, which allows collection criteria based on user name, entity type, time, and state. EventHistoryCollector objects can be created with filters to collect events matching specific criteria, and the server automatically appends new events meeting the filter criteria to the collection as they occur. This API-level access supports automated report generation and integration with external monitoring tools.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) provides several mechanisms for collecting metrics, traces, and logs from AI workloads, giving organizations the data needed for event log report generation and anomaly detection on AI infrastructure.

The core observability mechanism is a managed Prometheus server pod that PAIS operates to collect metrics on Private AI Services components, including PAIS Controller activity, model endpoint behavior, and knowledge base indexing. Observability collection is activated by updating optional sections of the PAISConfiguration custom resource, which configures Prometheus metrics collection with a customer-specified storage class and retention period and configures LLM trace forwarding to an OpenTelemetry Collector. Trace forwarding is configurable for HTTP/protobuf or gRPC transport using the spec.observability.llmTraces section of the PAISConfiguration CR, allowing detailed traces from agents and knowledge base indexing operations to be examined for anomalous behavior patterns.

Metrics collected by the managed Prometheus server can be visualized in observability platforms such as Grafana, where MLOps engineers and application developers track AI application health, quality, and behavior. Grafana dashboards surface LLM completion API response times at the 95th percentile, inference engine tokens-per-second generation rate, and GPU compute and memory usage, providing visibility into operational patterns that can reveal anomalies. PAIS also integrates with VCF Operations for controller-level monitoring: PAIS Controller metrics are exposed at a Prometheus metrics endpoint on the pais-controller-manager service, and controller pod metrics streaming can be activated to view those metrics in VCF Operations. Collection is performed through Telegraf with namepass filters that capture controller-runtime workers, work queue depth, and similar reconciliation metrics, and VI administrators can use the VCF Consumption CLI to connect to the Supervisor endpoint and stream metrics from the PAIS controller pod. The PAIS Supervisor Service additionally exposes service-level metrics through pais.vmware.com for monitoring the health of the service itself.

At the model inference layer, the vLLM inference server logging level is configurable through the VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL environment variable, with DEBUG as a supported value for detailed trace capture. Deep Learning VMs record cloud-init script logs in /var/log/dl.log and GPU driver installation logs in /var/log/gpu-install.log for post-deployment review, and they expose vGPU performance metrics through Prometheus that can be collected and visualized for monitoring purposes. JupyterLab instances on Deep Learning VMs provide additional logs that can be examined to verify installation and operation of components.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides event log generation and monitoring capabilities across its security components in 9.1, directly supporting anomaly detection and assessment within the network security domain.

The IDS/IPS subsystem generates detailed security event logs for event analysis. Remote syslog must be configured on ESX hosts to receive IDS/IPS events exported directly from those hosts; the `ids_events_to_syslog` setting controls whether IDS/IPS events are forwarded to external syslog consumers (the default is disabled). vDefend components write log data to /var/log, with syslog messages conforming to RFC 5424 on vDefend appliances and RFC 3164 on ESX hosts, enabling consistent ingestion by syslog-compatible platforms. The Security Explorer Computes view in Security Intelligence provides a full record of all IDS/IPS events for analysis over a 30-day timeframe. Security Intelligence also provides a View Full Event History capability that displays all events associated with a given signature ID along with their intrusion severity. The Threat Event Monitoring dashboard in Security Services Platform displays the trend in intrusion events over time, categorized by Intrusion Severity, giving security teams visibility into detection activity patterns.

Malware Prevention Service generates structured event logs with detailed metadata per file analysis event. Event log fields include the verdict (BENIGN, malicious, or other classification status), a numeric risk score, malware classification, malware family, error codes and messages, HTTP domain, HTTP method, user agent, file upload status, submission status, analysis completion status, and gateway identification. File event statistics appear on the Monitor & Plan > Overview > Threat Monitoring dashboard in Security Services Platform, with graphs covering a configurable time period (defaulting to the last hour).

Network Detection and Response (NDR) generates and sends event logs to a SIEM endpoint when a detection event occurs, enabling integration with external correlation and reporting platforms.

The Security Services Platform Monitor & Plan Overview dashboard provides a view of security posture that includes the latest security segmentation report, flow trends, a summary of detected suspicious traffic events, pending actions, and current settings requiring attention. Security Intelligence generates bar graphs showing the number of anomalies detected, categorized by severity. The Policy Recommendations section tracks Security Intelligence recommendations with status indicators such as Ready to Publish, allowing security teams to act on recommended policy changes.

vDefend Identity Firewall Event Log Sources can be integrated with VCF Log Management for centralized logging and monitoring. VCF Log Management supports Top-N queries using Interactive Analytics, enabling report generation from the aggregated log streams that vDefend supplies. VCF Log Management handles organization-level scheduled report generation, while vDefend provides the network-security-specific event data and dashboards that feed those reporting workflows.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides event logging and report generation capabilities across its replication, recovery, and clean room components, scoped to disaster recovery operations.

The PNR Appliance maintains security audit logs for tracking security-relevant events, including authorization error messages with timestamps and component information. vSphere Replication logs are maintained at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hms/hms-audit.log on the Appliance and at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hbr on VMware ESX hosts. The PNR Log Manager records events at six severity levels: panic, error, warning, information, verbose, and trivia. The PNR Appliance can forward log files to a remote syslog server for centralized collection.

PNR generates structured audit events for significant replication and recovery operations. vSphere Replication produces events when virtual machines are configured or unconfigured for replication (com.vmware.vcHms.replicationConfiguredEvent, com.vmware.vcHms.replicationUnconfiguredEvent) and when hosts are added to or removed from the replication infrastructure (com.vmware.vcHms.hostConfiguredForHbrEvent, com.vmware.vcHms.hostUnconfiguredForHbrEvent). PNR monitors connections to remote sites by sending periodic ping requests and logs connection status events, triggering corresponding alarms to track system health and surface conditions such as unexpected replication failures or RPO violations. Protection groups log error- and info-level events when consistency groups have issues or are added to protection groups.

Report generation capabilities cover the full recovery workflow. PNR can generate and export reports about each run of a recovery plan, test of a recovery plan, or test cleanup for audit trail purposes. It maintains a history log for each recovery plan that records reprotect operations, which can be exported to verify whether errors occurred. The number of history reports exported is configurable. Snapshot Reports display total replication snapshots, snapshot summaries, and snapshots with issues. Health finding status history tracking covers customizable time periods, defaulting to 24 hours, to identify status changes over time. The health monitor service checks each finding every five minutes and updates the results with a last-checked timestamp.

The Clean Room Orchestrator maintains a running list of all clean room events including both system-generated events and user actions, which can be filtered for monitoring and audit purposes. It logs events related to protection groups (creating or deleting protection groups and replications), snapshot task completions, and alert notifications. Events from the Clean Room can be forwarded to VCF Log Management (version 9.0 and above), Microsoft Sentinel, Splunk Cloud, or Microsoft Azure Log Analytics via Data Collection Rules and Data Collection Endpoints. PNR dashboards in VCF Operations provide monitoring and reporting for vSAN snapshots, replication, and recovery plan status. The Recovery Plans Dashboard displays all alerts related to protection groups for monitoring and audit purposes.

Broader log analytics, anomaly detection, and cross-platform event correlation across the environment are provided by the underlying VCF platform's monitoring infrastructure.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides event log generation and reporting capabilities that support detection and assessment of anomalous activities within the database-as-a-service layer.

DSM's primary audit interface is the System Audit Events view, accessible through the System Audit section in the Provider UI. This view is the only location in the DSM interface where administrators can track non-database-related operations and tasks. Each audit entry records a Component field identifying the DSM component that performed the operation, an Event Type field identifying the nature of the audited event, Event Details providing operation-specific information, Event Time in UTC, and a Status field indicating the outcome of the event. The Source field records either a user identifier (Email ID) for user-initiated events or "System" for events initiated by DSM without user intervention, enabling attribution of each audit record to a specific actor. Standard Kubernetes audit events are also captured through the Gateway Audit Logs capability.

Audit events can be exported in CSV or XLSX format using the ACTIONS dropdown menu in the System Audit Events view. Exports include all events visible to the requesting user regardless of page size, covering the Component, Event Type, Event Details, Event Time (UTC), Status, and Source fields. These exports can be brought into external reporting and analysis tools to support audit review and anomaly assessment.

For persistent retention, DSM archives gateway audit log files periodically to an S3 repository subpath when a default S3 provider log repository is configured. Audit log files are also stored locally on the Provider VM at /var/log/tdm/provider/audit/ in files named with a gateway-audit-timestamp.log format, supporting direct access for investigation.
