Ansible can be integrated with CI/CD pipelines to trigger automated Controller deployments upon code changes. Terraform modules support automatic scaling of service pool members through Network Infrastructure Automation (NIA) integration with Consul backend state management.

In VCF deployments, Avi integrates with VCF Automation's Provider Management Portal, which enables provider administrators to define infrastructure boundaries, create regions and organizations, and set region quotas for capacity allocation. This integration extends Avi's configuration governance surface to include multi-tenant resource governance and capacity planning for organizations consuming load-balancing services through the VCF stack.

Defining the configuration baselines, scheduling continuous comparison across the broader environment, and reporting deviation findings to management are organizational responsibilities fulfilled outside Avi through tooling and processes that operate against Avi's API and audit data.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CM-02(03)

> Retain [Assignment: organization-defined number] of previous versions of baseline configurations of the system to support rollback.

**SCF Controls:** CFG-02.3

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple mechanisms for retaining previous versions of baseline configurations and supporting rollback across its component products.

VMware vCenter (vCenter) includes a configuration export and backup capability that retains configuration data for use as a rollback point. The backup retention policy defaults to retaining the last seven backups, providing a rolling history of configuration states. Administrators are advised to back up both configuration and state before making topology changes to a deployment, so that the environment can be reverted to a known-good configuration if a change introduces problems. The vCenter version from a backup must match the version recorded in VCF Operations, which helps prevent version mismatches during restoration. vCenter also includes a dedicated patching rollback capability that reverts to an automatically created snapshot when the revert option is selected during patching.

VCF Operations supports file-based and image-based backup methods for the platform. File-based restore for VCF Operations, vCenter, and NSX allows administrators to return each component to the configuration state captured in a backup. VCF Operations can also be configured to take a backup on state change, capturing configuration automatically when significant changes occur. Restoring VCF Operations from a file-based backup involves deploying a new appliance and then applying the backup to it, preserving the prior configuration.

VCF Operations orchestrator provides a version history system for orchestrator objects, including workflows and configuration templates. Previous version history records are preserved and can be used to track changes over time, compare different object versions side by side, and revert an object to a previous version by selecting the desired version from the Version History interface. When a revert operation is performed, the system creates a new version history record rather than discarding the current state, so the full change timeline is maintained for auditability.

Configuration drift management in VCF Operations, accessed through Fleet Management, uses configuration templates to define desired configuration values and tracks deviations from those templates. The system retains configuration drift history data for seven days, allowing administrators to review what changed and when. Configuration drift templates can be integrated with a source control repository, enabling version control over configuration baselines with change review workflows, auditing, and continuous integration support. Each configuration template must be stored in a unique folder in the source control repository, and templates sync to the repository after configuration.

When upgrading VCF Operations, the recommended practice is to clone, export, or create a backup of customized content before the upgrade to preserve customizations that would otherwise be overwritten. Regular backups of custom and out-of-the-box content can also be created for restoration or export to other environments.

VCF Automation blueprints support version history tracking. Administrators can inspect older versions in the canvas and code editor and restore a previous blueprint version. Restoring a previous version overwrites the current draft without removing any named versions, so the full version history of the blueprint remains intact.

VCF Operations for Networks supports selective restoration of configurations by setting data filter parameters to include or skip specific configuration types during a restore operation, giving administrators granular control over which configuration elements are rolled back.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager manages cluster images through base images, firmware add-ons, and vendor add-ons that can be added, replaced, removed, upgraded, or downgraded via PowerCLI. Fixed baselines contain only the patches that have been explicitly selected and do not change when new patches are downloaded, preserving a specific known-good configuration state. This contrasts with dynamic baselines, which update automatically based on criteria. The fixed baseline approach provides a stable reference point for rollback purposes, while dynamic baselines keep environments current. At the ESX host level, rollback is limited to one level, with only one previous build retained at a time; this scope should be factored into rollback planning for host software configurations.

VCF also supports VM-level snapshots that capture memory, disk, and settings state at a particular moment. Reverting to a snapshot returns all items to the state they were in when the snapshot was taken, providing a point-in-time rollback mechanism for individual virtual machines. PowerCLI provides the New-Snapshot command to capture state and the Set-VM cmdlet to revert to a specified snapshot.

At the Kubernetes workload layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor provide built-in revision history and rollback mechanisms for workload configurations, extending the platform's configuration retention capabilities to containerized workloads. Kubernetes Deployment objects retain revision history through their associated ReplicaSets; by default a Deployment retains the last 10 old ReplicaSets, each storing the configuration state of that revision. The retention limit is controlled by the `.spec.revisionHistoryLimit` field and can be increased to maintain a deeper rollback window. Setting this field to 0 removes all rollout history and eliminates rollback capability, so administrators should avoid that setting in environments where rollback is required. The `kubectl rollout undo` command rolls back a Deployment to the previous revision; the `--to-revision` flag allows targeting a specific earlier revision. StatefulSets provide analogous revision history through ControllerRevision objects, with the `.spec.revisionHistoryLimit` parameter controlling how many ControllerRevisions are retained and the `kubectl rollout undo` interface applying the Pod template from a chosen revision. DaemonSets share the same revisionHistoryLimit mechanism, also defaulting to 10 retained revisions, and support rollback through the same interface. Kubernetes records the last applied configuration as a JSON annotation on live objects, providing a baseline reference for declarative configuration management.

VKS clusters support rollback at the cluster upgrade level; if a cluster upgrade does not complete successfully, VKS supports reverting to the prior cluster configuration to restore stability. Kubernetes recommends storing object configuration files in a source control system such as Git to enable change review processes and audit trails. Argo CD, available as a Supervisor Service on vSphere Supervisor, applies this pattern at scale by maintaining a complete history of all changes in Git and supporting rollback to any prior configuration version. The Harness CI/CD integration available through VCF Services consumption incorporates Artifactory for artifact storage; Artifactory maintains version control for all stored artifacts, supporting rollback to prior artifact versions when needed.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides configuration retention and rollback capabilities across its Distributed Firewall, IDS/IPS signature management, NDR Sensor appliances, and Security Services Platform.

The VMware vDefend distributed firewall (DFW) uses a draft-and-publish model in which policy changes are saved as working configurations before being made active. Draft configurations can be saved as a new configuration or as an update to an existing one, and can be locked with an optional comment requirement that records the reason for a change. Saved drafts can be filtered by time period (last one day, one week, 30 days, or three months), by autodraft status, by creator, or by name, making it straightforward to locate and restore a prior configuration. When a draft is published, the system automatically creates an auto draft capturing the previously active policy state. Administrators can revert to that prior state by publishing this auto draft, which returns the firewall to its previous known-good policy. The View Draft Details window shows the differences between a saved draft and the last published configuration, providing a visible record of what changed between the current working state and the live policy.

vDefend also supports full rollback during workload migration operations. The migration tool captures the previous state and the new state of every rule. If a migration is unsuccessful or produces unexpected results, the system can revert the security configuration to its prior state by reversing the migration steps in order and removing the newly created policies.

For IDS/IPS signature management, vDefend includes a bulk edit Reset action that restores signatures to their default configuration. When working with custom signature bundles, documentation recommends exporting the existing bundle before updating it, which preserves the prior configuration as a recoverable reference in the event the updated bundle needs to be abandoned.

NDR Sensor appliances within the Security Services Platform (SSP) support rollback at the appliance level. The `rollback upgrade-bundle` CLI command restores the NDR Sensor to a previously saved OS state or snapshot and reboots the appliance into that prior state. Executing the rollback requires first enabling debug mode via the `set debug-mode` CLI command.

SSP supports backup and restore of its configuration data. Configuration changes made after a backup are overwritten when the platform is restored, which returns the SSP instance to the backed-up state. Documentation recommends backing up SSP before initiating an upgrade to preserve the current configuration as a restore point.

These rollback and version-retention capabilities cover vDefend's own firewall policies, IDS/IPS signatures, NDR Sensor appliances, and SSP configuration. Broader infrastructure configuration versioning for VMware ESX hosts and other VCF platform components is managed by other VCF capabilities.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides a Configuration Import/Export Tool that captures the full disaster recovery configuration, including inventory mappings, recovery plans, protection groups, and related objects, into XML files. These exported files are point-in-time configuration baselines that can be imported to restore a previous configuration state.

Multiple export methods are available. Administrators can export configuration data through the PNR user interface, through a shell script on the Protection and Recovery appliance, or through the PNR REST API. The export can also be embedded as a recovery plan step so that configuration data is automatically captured during recovery plan execution, providing a baseline snapshot tied to each recovery event.

PNR supports scheduled configuration exports through cron jobs with a configurable retention policy. The REST API endpoint PATCH /server/configuration/backup/settings allows administrators to enable or disable the scheduler, set the retention count, and define the schedule specification. This automated scheduling with built-in retention provides a rolling history of configuration baselines without manual intervention.

The vSphere Replication 9.1 Configuration Import/Export Tool imports configuration data from a previously exported XML file to recreate outgoing and incoming replications without modifying existing replications. vSphere Replication also supports a multi-point-in-time (MPIT) retention policy that stores more than one instance of a replicated virtual machine for recovery purposes. Setting vrReplication.preserveMpitImagesAsSnapshots to true in Advanced Settings retains older point-in-time snapshots during recovery, giving administrators a set of historical recovery points to choose from. Recovery point objective and MPIT retention count are configurable per replication.

For vSAN-based replication, PNR supports predefined snapshot retention templates, including Default, Ransomware recovery, Short-term retention, and Disaster recovery presets for quick configuration of RPO and snapshot retention parameters. Snapshot retention can be configured with daily, weekly, and monthly schedules, with a maximum retention duration of three years.

PNR's upgrade procedure preserves existing advanced settings and configurations from the previous release, carrying configuration state forward across version upgrades without requiring re-export.

To roll back, administrators import from any previously exported configuration file using the standalone Import/Export Tool, specifying the path to the target export file.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides patch rollback and configuration backup mechanisms that support recovery to prior software and configuration states.

For software patch rollback, Avi supports reverting the Controller and Service Engine (SE) layers to a previous patch version. The `rollbackpatch system` CLI command reverts the Controller and all SE groups to the previous patch version. The equivalent REST API operation uses `POST api/rollback` with `{"rollback_type": 2}`. For targeted rollback, individual SE groups can be reverted using `rollbackpatch segroup <se-group-name>`, and the Controller can be reverted independently. The `enable_rollback` setting on each Service Engine group controls rollback eligibility before an upgrade is applied, with separate controls for standard upgrade rollback and patch-specific rollback. The REST API also supports selective SE-group rollback, allowing all or some SE groups to revert to the previous patch version in a non-disruptive manner. The `rollback system` CLI command performs a patch-level rollback across the Controller and all SE groups. The Avi for VCF guide recommends staging a rollback image of the previous stable version prior to any upgrade and planning a remediation strategy before re-attempting an upgrade after a rollback.

One scope boundary applies: patch rollback reverts the system to the state without the applied patch but does not restore to a prior major software version. Rollback coverage is limited to recent patch-level changes rather than arbitrary historical baselines.

For configuration state retention, Avi supports automated configuration backup and restore. The backup configuration includes setting the frequency and retention policy for automated configuration exports via the Avi UI. Local backup retention is configurable by specifying the number of backups to preserve on the Controller. The Controller cluster continuously replicates configuration data across all nodes, supporting resilience against single-node failures.

Avi generates Config Audit Trail events that record configuration changes on the Controller, providing a time-stamped history of what was modified and by whom. This supports operational review of configuration history and helps identify the configuration state that existed prior to a change.

For retaining application delivery configuration baselines such as virtual service definitions, pool configurations, Web Application Firewall (WAF) policies, and certificate bindings, the supported practice is to export the Controller configuration snapshot via CLI or REST API before making changes and store those exports in an external location under organizational retention schedules. Avi does not natively version-control application delivery configuration objects in the manner of a dedicated configuration management system. Organizations are responsible for defining the schedule for configuration exports, the retention period and storage location for those exports, and the verification steps to confirm rollback readiness before applying production changes.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control CM-02(07)

> (a) Issue [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] with [Assignment: organization-defined configurations] to individuals traveling to locations that the organization deems to be of significant risk; and (b) Apply the following controls to the systems or components when the individuals return from travel: [Assignment: organization-defined controls].

**SCF Controls:** CFG-02.5

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CM-03

> a. Determine and document the types of changes to the system that are configuration- controlled; b. Review proposed configuration-controlled changes to the system and approve or disapprove such changes with explicit consideration for security and privacy impact analyses; c. Document configuration change decisions associated with the system; d. Implement approved configuration-controlled changes to the system; e. Retain records of configuration-controlled changes to the system for [Assignment: organization-defined time period]; f. Monitor and review activities associated with configuration-controlled changes to the system; and g. Coordinate and provide oversight for configuration change control activities through [Assignment: organization-defined configuration change control element] that convenes [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; when [Assignment: organization-defined configuration change conditions]].

**SCF Controls:** CHG-01, CHG-02

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides technical controls that govern configuration change processes across the infrastructure stack through VCF Operations, vSphere Lifecycle Manager, and VCF Automation.

VCF Operations includes a Configuration Drifts capability, accessible through Fleet Management > Configuration Drifts, that tracks changes in product configurations over time and allows administrators to compare current configurations against assigned desired values for VMware vCenter instances and vSphere clusters. When drift is detected, administrators are alerted so they can investigate and remediate. The config polling interval can be adjusted to control how frequently configuration data is collected from data sources, and drift detection can be scheduled at defined intervals. Together, these capabilities provide continuous monitoring of configuration state across the VCF environment.

Configuration templates in VCF Operations define a desired state for infrastructure configurations. Administrators create and manage these templates through the Fleet Management > Configuration Drifts interface. A configuration template represents a set of configurations that enables an administrator to define and monitor the intended configuration of managed resources. Templates can be assigned to policies, activated or deactivated as needed, and their attributes and properties can be overridden through the Policy Definition interface under Infrastructure Operations > Configurations.

For formal change control workflows, VCF Operations supports integrating configuration templates with a source control repository. This integration enables versioning, auditing, and centralized management of configuration templates. It also facilitates change control with reviews and supports continuous integration and continuous deployment pipelines for automated testing and validation of configuration changes before they are applied. This source control integration provides the traceability and approval workflows that configuration change control processes require.

VCF Operations provides policy management capabilities through its Infrastructure Operations Configurations module, where policies can be added, edited, and removed. Policy settings can be inherited from existing policies and modified with appropriate permissions, supporting a layered approach to configuration governance.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) enforces desired-state configuration for VMware ESX hosts, including drivers, firmware, and add-on software. Hosts are compared against approved baselines, and vLCM detects and can remediate deviations. vSphere Configuration Profiles provide an additional layer of periodic host configuration assessment, enabling management of the configuration of all hosts in a cluster collectively to help maintain consistency in host configuration at a cluster level. The cluster configuration in vSphere Configuration Profiles is represented as a JSON document that can be extracted from a reference host, imported, or drafted manually; the ClusterConfiguration.Modify privilege is required to create or modify this desired-state document.

VCF Operations governs lifecycle management for VCF components, including updates and patches. Before any update can be applied, VCF Operations requires a precheck to verify that the environment is ready for the change. This built-in gate helps prevent untested changes from being applied to production infrastructure. vSphere Lifecycle Manager synchronizes to its download sources, including VCF Operations, on a regular and automatic basis, providing a controlled channel for software updates. If out-of-band changes are made directly through vCenter or NSX Manager, VCF Operations can detect these changes and synchronize its inventory with the updated state, providing visibility into changes made outside the standard change control process.

VCF Automation includes approval policies that require review before changes to deployed catalog instances take effect. Approval policies enable project administrators to review all changes to deployed catalog items before resources are consumed or modifications are made, providing a gate for configuration changes in provisioned environments. Day 2 actions on deployments, such as changing project assignments, are controlled through the VCF Automation actions framework.

For workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor, the platform extends configuration change governance through Kubernetes-native mechanisms. All configuration changes to cluster resources are expressed as object specifications applied through the Kubernetes API server, which is the authoritative entry point for any modification to cluster state. Before applying changes, operators can use kubectl diff to preview the delta between the current cluster state and a proposed configuration, allowing review of what will change before it is committed. Kubernetes Deployment objects track the previously applied configuration through the kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration annotation, preserving the prior state and supporting rollback; StatefulSet controllers similarly use ControllerRevision objects to track historical configuration changes for version tracking and rollback of stateful workloads. Kubernetes documentation recommends storing object configuration in a source control system such as Git to enable change review processes and audit trails at the workload layer.

Access to the Kubernetes API server is governed by Kubernetes RBAC. Roles and ClusterRoles define what resources a subject can modify, and RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings assign those permissions to specific users or service accounts, restricting the identities that can alter cluster configuration. Admission controllers provide a further layer of change governance: validating and mutating admission webhooks intercept API requests before they are persisted, allowing organizations to enforce configuration standards or reject non-compliant configurations. Kubernetes MutatingAdmissionPolicy objects, declared using CEL expressions with structured merge diff, define desired configuration values that are enforced as resources are created or updated, with Parameter resources allowing policy configuration to be maintained separately from policy definitions. VCF Automation Kubernetes Management provides Mutation policies that can be assigned at the organization level to enforce policy-driven modifications to workloads across VKS clusters. For control plane configuration, kubeadm manages the ClusterConfiguration object stored in the kubeadm-config ConfigMap in the kube-system namespace, which is the authoritative source of control plane settings; individual control plane components can be reconfigured using patch files, with the recommended practice of updating one node at a time to maintain cluster availability during reconfiguration.

Standard packages deployed on VKS clusters, such as Harbor, Cert Manager, and Istio, are updated through the VCF CLI using the vcf package installed update command, which specifies the installed package name, target version, values file, and namespace, creating a structured and repeatable path for configuration changes to these components. For add-ons, vcf addon install update reconfigures an existing add-on with modified configuration parameters, separating configuration values from the deployment mechanism. VM class configurations in vSphere Supervisor are updated through the Data Center CLI command com vmware vcenter namespacemanagement virtualmachineclasses update, which allows administrators to modify instance storage volumes and storage policy assignments in a controlled manner. For environments using the Argo CD Supervisor Service, all configuration is managed exclusively through the argocd.spec field; direct modification of the underlying argocd-cm ConfigMap is not permitted, requiring all configuration changes to go through a declared, version-controlled specification rather than ad hoc in-place editing.

Role-based access control across VCF components restricts which users can initiate configuration changes. Many ESX configuration settings require use of specific command-line tools (configstorecli or esxcli), limiting ad-hoc modification. Audit logging across VCF components records configuration changes for accountability and review. VCF Operations tracks each configuration change to the authenticated user who initiated the change or scheduled the job that performed the change, allowing administrators to determine who changed what and when. User permission changes are also tracked to capture when such changes were made and who made them. The SystemConfiguration.Administrators group in VCF SSO provides designated administrators the ability to view and manage system configuration through the vCenter Management Interface on port 5480, including viewing and restarting services, while APIs that modify critical system configurations remain restricted even for members of this group. At the Kubernetes layer, all configuration changes processed through the API server are recorded in Kubernetes audit logs; the audit policy governs which API calls are logged and at what verbosity, producing a record of who changed what and when across the Consumption layer, and these logs can be forwarded to external log management or SIEM systems.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides configuration change control mechanisms within its network security policy domain, contributing to governance of technical configuration changes for firewall rules, IDS/IPS settings, and microsegmentation policies.

Security Services Platform includes a deployment wizard that conducts thorough validations before implementing any configuration changes, verifying that configurations are correct and compatible before they take effect. This pre-deployment validation reduces the risk of misconfigurations that could disrupt network security enforcement or create unintended policy gaps. Security Intelligence extends this with staged policy enforcement, where initial policy recommendations can be deployed while traffic is allowed but monitored. Administrators can verify that recommended rules do not disrupt legitimate operations before enabling full enforcement, providing a controlled transition path for firewall policy changes.

Security Intelligence includes a Monitoring capability that checks for changes in the scope of input entities every hour when enabled. This change detection helps administrators understand when underlying conditions have shifted and policies may need updating. Security Intelligence also tracks the number of new configurations per configuration type on an hourly basis, providing visibility into the rate and scope of policy changes across the security environment.

In multi-site deployments, vDefend enforces a hierarchical configuration model through the Global Manager and Local Manager architecture. In federated environments, the Global Manager centrally manages IDS/IPS signatures, profiles, Distributed Firewall (DFW) and Gateway Firewall (GFW) policies, oversubscription settings, and syslog settings across all mapped Local Managers. Settings configured on a Local Manager remain local to that site and are not propagated to other managers, supporting site-specific change control while preserving global policy consistency.

vDefend provides a draft-based publishing model that gives administrators explicit control over when policy changes take effect. Administrators can create manual drafts of firewall configurations, save them as new configurations or updates to existing ones, and lock them with a required comment before publishing. When a draft is published, vDefend automatically saves an auto draft that captures the configuration state immediately before the publish operation. This auto draft is a rollback mechanism, allowing administrators to restore the previous firewall policy state by republishing it if an applied change produces unintended results. Auto drafts and manual drafts can also be cloned and saved for additional change management flexibility.

vDefend IDS/IPS provides a bulk edit feature for system signatures that includes a Reset action, allowing administrators to restore default signature configurations when changes need to be reversed.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides technical mechanisms that support configuration change control governance within the disaster recovery environment.

The Configuration Import/Export Tool enables export and import of PNR server configuration data, including server version, build number, local and remote site names, inventory mappings, placeholder datastores, advanced settings, and array manager information with Storage Replication Adapter details. The tool includes schema support for programmatic configuration management, enabling automated or scripted export and import workflows. Configuration data can also be imported using a properties file (srm_configuration.properties), supporting automated imports from XML. These capabilities allow administrators to capture configuration baselines that can be compared against the current state after changes are made.

Advanced settings for the PNR server are accessible through Site Pair > Configure > Advanced Settings in the user interface. The underlying system configuration is defined in the va-configurator.xml file and is modifiable through the same interface. Changes to advanced settings require adequate user privileges, restricting higher-impact configuration modifications to authorized administrators. Some configuration changes require a PNR server restart to take effect. PNR preserves advanced settings and configurations during product upgrades, maintaining baseline continuity across version transitions.

PNR integrates with the VCF Operations orchestrator plugin, which provides structured, repeatable workflows for managing replication and recovery configurations. Provider Administrators manage access to PNR capabilities through the VCF Automation Provider Management UI, scoping which users can initiate configuration changes across organizations.

PNR configuration files contain settings that affect the security and proper functioning of the environment, and the product documentation notes that improper settings can impact operation. Role-based access controls restrict which users can modify these files, and custom settings that require direct file modification must be applied over an SSH connection to the appliance, providing an additional layer of process accountability.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides technical mechanisms that support configuration change control for managed database infrastructure. System-wide settings are accessible only to DSM administrators through the Settings view in the Provider interface and the Dashboard, which restricts who can initiate configuration changes. This role-based access model limits changes to infrastructure policies, VM class definitions, network settings, and directory service configuration to authorized personnel.

DSM supports controlled change scheduling through its Maintenance Policy, which allows administrators to configure a start date and duration window for applying software updates to DSM appliances and the DSM plug-in. The Maintenance Policy editor is accessible from the Version & Upgrade section under the DSM System tab in both the vSphere Client and the DSM UI. The policy cannot be modified while a Provider Appliance software update is in progress, preventing changes to the update schedule during active operations. For database minor-version upgrades, DSM provides a separate maintenance window setting with a default configuration of six hours beginning each Saturday at 23:59, giving administrators control over when updates are applied to individual database instances.

DSM's Operations tab provides visibility into the progress of database configuration changes as they are applied. System configuration is managed through a dsmsystemconfig Custom Resource within the gateway that can be inspected via SSH access to the appliance VM, allowing administrators to review applied settings and verify configuration state.

Certain configurations are intentionally protected from modification after initial deployment. The Management Network for the Provider VM cannot be changed after deployment. Infrastructure policies actively in use by a database cannot have existing settings modified, though new compute resources can be added to them. Policies not currently assigned to any database can have settings changed at any time. Certain MySQL parameters are restricted from user modification by design. System-level settings cannot be modified when applying DSM system settings fails, providing a safety constraint against partial configuration changes.

DSM does not provide a formal change management workflow with approval routing, change ticketing, or mandatory review steps. Satisfying this control fully requires organizations to establish governance procedures that operate alongside DSM's technical controls, including formal change authorization processes, documentation requirements, and post-change verification practices that DSM itself does not enforce.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Avi Load Balancer provides a Config Audit Trail that records every configuration change made to the Avi Controller. The trail captures user activity events and configuration changes over a selected time period and is accessible via Operations > Events > Config Audit Trail. Changes are categorized by event code: CONFIG_CREATE for new objects, CONFIG_UPDATE for modifications, and CONFIG_DELETE for removals. CONFIG_UPDATE events include the status, resource name, and old resource data, giving auditors a before-and-after record of each change.

At the object level, the Pool Config Events Overlay provides an expandable change history that visually displays the difference between previous and current configurations, with additions highlighted in green and removals in red. Administrators can also use the Config Events overlay on the Service Engine Analytics view to correlate traffic behavior changes with specific configuration modifications, such as changes to virtual service security settings.

When a configuration change is made through the web interface, CLI, or REST API, it is automatically propagated to all Avi Controller cluster members, so any node has a complete and current configuration state.

In Avi for VCF, the product supports Organization-Managed Mode, in which an organization administrator independently manages their own Service Engine Group configurations and infrastructure settings. Provider-Managed Mode allows the provider administrator to control Service Engine Group settings including scale-out, CPU, and memory configurations. This role separation means configuration responsibilities, and the audit trail for each scope, align with the organizational structure.

These mechanisms provide the technical record-keeping and observability that change management governance depends on. Governing those changes, including approval workflows, change review requirements before deployment, and formal change records, is an organizational process responsibility that falls outside Avi's scope.

### Control CM-03(01)

> PROHIBITION OF CHANGES Use [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms] to: (a) Document proposed changes to the system; (b) Notify [Assignment: organization-defined approval authorities] of proposed changes to the system and request change approval; (c) Highlight proposed changes to the system that have not been approved or disapproved within [Assignment: organization-defined time period]; (d) Prohibit changes to the system until designated approvals are received; (e) Document all changes to the system; and (f) Notify [Assignment: organization-defined personnel] when approved changes to the system are completed.

**SCF Controls:** CHG-02.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides mechanisms that enforce organization-defined change authorization through approval workflows, role-based access controls, policy hierarchies, allowlist-based action restrictions, and configuration drift detection. These mechanisms require organization-approved change requests before modifications take effect.

VCF Automation includes approval policies that require designated administrators to review and approve changes before they are applied. These policies can be configured to require project administrator review of all changes to deployed catalog items, particularly those used by multiple teams. When approval policies are active, change requests that remain unapproved after a defined period can be configured to be automatically rejected, blocking deployment or modification of cloud resources without explicit authorization. An enforced policy can also be set to automatically approve or reject a request if the approver does not take action within the configured timeframe. Custom user roles can be created to grant specific permissions, including the ability to review and respond to approval requests, allowing organizations to designate appropriate change approvers.

Day 2 action policies in VCF Automation further restrict what modifications users can make to existing deployments and their component resources. These policies operate by creating a list of permitted actions, so any change not explicitly listed is prohibited by default. Project administrators can selectively entitle project members to specific operations, such as snapshot and revert actions, without requiring administrator intervention for those particular operations while still restricting all other changes. This keeps the approval process proportionate to the risk of each action.

Role-based access controls restrict which users can initiate changes at multiple levels of the platform. NSX Manager applies role-based access control to restrict system access to authorized users by assigning roles with specific permissions. Organization administrators cannot edit global roles published to their organizations, and organization users can exercise only those rights from their roles that have been published to their organizations, limiting the scope of changes any individual can perform. Runtime defined entities can only be modified by users who hold both the appropriate Edit right and a change-level ACL entry. Access to specific entity types can be granted only to designated users and organizations. System administrators with the Manage General ACL right can create, modify, and remove specific access control lists through the accessControls API, providing granular control over who can make what changes. These authorization controls apply consistently regardless of operational context.

VCF enforces a policy hierarchy that restricts unauthorized weakening of security controls. A restrictive security policy created by an organization administrator at the organization level cannot be overwritten by a less restrictive policy at the project level. Inherited policies cannot be modified or deleted at lower levels, though direct policies for a given object can be edited or removed. This hierarchy maintains consistent change controls across the environment and restricts lower-level administrators from circumventing organization-wide restrictions.

The Consumption layer of VCF, which includes VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), vSphere Supervisor, and vSphere namespaces, extends these change controls into Kubernetes workload environments. The Kubernetes API server is the single supported path for all resource changes in a cluster, with every write operation requiring authentication followed by authorization. Kubernetes RBAC Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ClusterRoleBindings define precisely which subjects can perform which verbs on which resources, and the RBAC API includes built-in privilege escalation protection that helps prevent users from editing roles or role bindings to obtain permissions they do not already hold, enforced at the API level regardless of the authorizer mode in use. VKS Cluster Management Policies apply a strict hierarchical enforcement model where policies can only be modified or deleted at their source of origin; inherited policies are immutable at the tenant level, extending the platform-level change restriction hierarchy into Kubernetes workload management. Cluster administrators can deploy ValidatingAdmissionPolicy to declaratively restrict what changes can be made via the Kubernetes API, protecting platform namespaces from deletion and restricting write actions on specific resource types beyond what RBAC alone covers. When ValidatingAdmissionPolicy is configured with a match policy of Equivalent, it applies to requests regardless of which API group or version was used to access the resource, maintaining consistent enforcement as APIs evolve. For sensitive workloads, ValidatingAdmissionPolicy can further narrow the permitted change surface; ValidatingAdmissionPolicy is also the recommended mechanism for protecting specific resources such as ResourceQuota objects from unauthorized modification. Admission control modules, including admission webhooks, can reject or modify requests to create, modify, delete, or connect to objects at the API boundary before they take effect. VKS Cluster Management Mutation Policies can enforce pod specification conformance at admission time, and because mutation policies follow the same hierarchical enforcement model, inherited mutation policies cannot be modified at child levels. VKS Cluster Management Policy Templates are the source blueprints for policies across the cluster estate; they can only be created and managed through their full lifecycle by Organization Administrators. Project Administrators can apply these templates within their projects and clusters but cannot modify the original templates. VKS Cluster Management custom policies can only be edited or deleted by users assigned the Organization Administrator or Project Administrator role, restricting policy modification to designated roles. Pod security policies on system namespaces (kube-system, tkg-system, and vmware-system-cloud-provider) cannot be changed, establishing a baseline that tenant operations cannot override. VKS local content libraries support Security Policy enforcement that controls access to and usage of vSphere Kubernetes releases. Supervisor enforces vSphere Namespace RBAC to restrict connections to Supervisor and VKS clusters based on user permissions, applying consistent authorization at the Consumption layer boundary. The Consumption layer also supports immutable infrastructure patterns, where automated processes overwrite unauthorized changes or systems are configured to reject changes outright, making unauthorized modifications easier to detect and remediate. Where organizations deploy the Argo CD Supervisor Service for GitOps-based change delivery, Argo CD enforces access controls across all components and manages secrets securely outside of Git, providing a controlled delivery pipeline through which changes are tracked and deployed consistently. Organizations should separately restrict access to underlying cluster components such as etcd and node-level configurations, as direct access to those layers bypasses API server controls.

At the infrastructure level, VCF validates proposed changes against compliance requirements before applying them. vSAN rejects storage policy changes immediately when they violate policy compliance or when the underlying storage capabilities do not support the requested change, such as attempting a RAID-5 to RAID-6 change on a cluster that cannot support it. At the virtual networking layer, the default security policy of a distributed virtual switch is to reject MAC address changes. Both standard and distributed virtual switches can be configured to accept or reject promiscuous mode, MAC address changes, and forged transmits, giving administrators explicit control over which network-level configuration changes are permitted on virtual switch ports.

VCF Operations provides configuration drift detection that compares the current state of VMware vCenter and cluster objects against desired-state configuration templates. Drift detection can be scheduled to run automatic checks at a configurable interval, and drift reports can be viewed and downloaded for each vCenter instance. When configuration templates are managed in source control, VCF Operations tracks whether the current template reflects the desired state and detects divergence. This allows administrators to identify unauthorized or unintended configuration changes and take corrective action.

User permission changes are tracked to provide an audit trail of when such changes were made and who made them, supporting after-the-fact review of change authorization.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides several mechanisms that help prohibit unauthorized changes to network security policy within its domain, contributing to an organization's broader change control process.

The Distributed Firewall (DFW) includes a policy locking feature: when a security policy section is locked, no other user can modify the policy until the lock is released by the user who applied it. This restricts concurrent or unauthorized edits to active firewall policy sections and is configurable per section, allowing administrators to protect policies during change freezes or maintenance windows. Within project-based deployments, Organization Admin ownership of firewall rules prevents project members from accessing firewall rule modifications entirely, providing a role-based structural constraint on who can modify network security policy.

Security Services Platform (SSP) enforces immutability on published security policies: once initial policies have been published through SSP, they cannot be modified from the user interface. This requires administrators to follow a defined workflow for any policy updates rather than directly editing production policies. SSP also surfaces a change-detected indicator in Security Intelligence Policy Recommendations when input entities used to generate prior recommendations change, giving administrators visibility into modifications that could affect security posture.

DFW system-generated rules produced by applying a security strategy cannot be edited individually through the API or UI (except for logging settings and log labels), and new rules cannot be added to those sections. DFW policy numbers cannot be changed after initial creation, providing a stable reference for change tracking and audit purposes.

DFW and Gateway Firewall policy changes follow a realization workflow: after a create, update, or delete operation on a policy or rule, the change must be explicitly realized on the transport nodes where the policy applies. Changes are not propagated to enforcement points without deliberate action, providing an additional checkpoint between policy definition and enforcement.

For IDS/IPS, signature bundle updates can be configured in either automatic or manual mode. Manual mode gives administrators explicit control over which signature bundle versions are deployed to each site, supporting change approval workflows where updates require review before deployment. The Global Signature Management interface records signatures changed globally by users, including excluded signatures, providing an audit trail of signature-level changes.

These capabilities operate within vDefend's network security policy domain. Broader organizational change management processes, approval workflows, and integration with IT Service Management platforms are provided by other components of the VCF stack, such as VCF Automation.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to the prohibition of unauthorized changes through its role-based access control model, which restricts modification of disaster recovery configurations to personnel holding appropriate roles.

PNR organizes access across two integration tiers. In the VCF Automation tier, access is controlled through role-based permissions assigned to Provider Administrators, Organization Administrators, Project Administrators, and Project Advanced Users. VM replication is restricted to authorized Organization users, and access control is enforced based on user authorization within vSphere Supervisor namespaces. Administrative operations such as namespace unpairing and network mapping edits require Organization Administrator authorization. In the vSphere Replication tier, PNR implements role-based access control through predefined roles that can be cloned and customized by adding or removing privileges. The VRM Replication Viewer role restricts users from performing any modifications to configured replication or replication sites, providing a read-only access level for users who need visibility without modification rights. Users without appropriate privileges receive a permission denial when they attempt restricted operations.

PNR also enforces operational change locks at multiple points. Protection groups can only be edited on the source site; on the peer site, the group is in dormant or recovery state and cannot be edited until the data protection group is active. Protection groups cannot be edited while in the Reprotercing state. When the PNR server is updating a protection group, changes cannot be reverted or cancelled while that update is in progress, enforcing atomicity. PNR also logs Array Manager reconfiguration events when a user edits Array Manager properties, providing an audit trail of configuration changes.

These role-based restrictions and audit capabilities help organizations enforce change control over disaster recovery configurations. Formal change request approval workflows and configuration drift detection are handled at the broader platform level, outside PNR's scope.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

CHG-02.1 requires mechanisms to prohibit unauthorized changes unless organization-approved change requests have been received. VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to this control through role-based access controls, policy enforcement, and system-level immutability protections that restrict who can make changes and what changes are permissible.

DSM's access control model scopes configuration authority to specific roles. For databases created in the default namespace, only the database owner can modify compute resource allocations. DSM's internal management login (dsm_mgmt) restricts users from accessing or modifying system-level objects in SQL Server, keeping administrative operations within an isolated context. DSM grants the SQL Server cluster administrator login restricted read-only privileges and specific ALTER privileges for server management and auditing rather than full administrative access. For MySQL, DSM revokes the create and update privileges on objects in the mysql system schema for database users.

Data service policies provide a framework for defining and enforcing approved configurations across tenants. DSM requires review of policy violations before applying policies to databases, allowing administrators to assess the impact before committing changes. Policy names and the applicable data service cannot be edited after creation for MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQL Server policies, which limits the scope of post-deployment modifications. If an in-use policy is deleted, associated data services become non-compliant, making the effect of such actions visible.

Infrastructure policies actively used by a database cannot have their existing settings modified, though new compute resources with corresponding parameters can be added. VM classes associated with an infrastructure policy and actively used by a database cannot be edited or deleted. Infrastructure policy names cannot be changed after creation. These immutability constraints collectively reduce the surface area for unauthorized or accidental configuration drift. MySQL on DSM automatically overwrites user changes to non-editable advanced database settings back to their configured defaults, enforcing DSM-managed baseline configurations.

What DSM does not provide is a formal change management workflow with approval gates, ticketing integration, or change advisory board controls. Fully prohibiting unauthorized changes requires organizational processes: a change management policy, defined approval authorities, and integration with change management tooling. DSM's controls support this objective by scoping who can make which changes, but the approval workflow itself must be maintained outside the platform.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides role-based access control (RBAC) at the application delivery tier that restricts which users can make configuration changes to Avi-managed objects. The RBAC model supports granular, sub-resource-level access control, allowing administrators to grant a user write access to a specific aspect of an object while withholding the ability to modify the broader policy. For example, a role can be configured to allow updating the IP address list within an IP group without granting permission to alter the IP group policy itself, enabling separation of duties across administrative functions.

In deployments integrated with VCF, the RBAC model extends through organizational boundaries. Project Administrators can deploy and manage load balancing for their applications without needing organization administrator involvement, while being restricted from altering Service Engine Group properties such as capacity, placement, or scaling. In organization-managed mode, editing a Service Engine Group requires an organization administrator to be logged in, limiting who can make infrastructure-level changes. Configuration changes made through the Avi Controller's web interface, CLI, or REST API are automatically propagated to all cluster members, so the access control boundary applies consistently regardless of which cluster node or management interface is used.

The default system admin account on the Avi Controller is protected; changes to that account are not permitted, preventing modification of the most privileged credential.

These access control mechanisms restrict configuration changes to users holding the appropriate permissions but do not constitute a change management workflow with documented approval records. The organizational process for reviewing and approving changes before they are applied must come from an external change management system; Avi's RBAC provides the technical enforcement boundary that restricts configuration changes from being applied by users who lack the necessary permissions.

### Control CM-03(02)

> Test, validate, and document changes to the system before finalizing the implementation of the changes.

**SCF Controls:** CHG-06

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several technical mechanisms that support verification of cybersecurity and data protection control functionality following changes, though the organizational process of systematically defining when verification is required and documenting results remains an additional responsibility.

VCF Operations includes a Configuration Drifts capability that monitors vCenter instances and vSphere clusters for deviations from assigned configuration templates. When a change is made, scheduled drift detection compares current configuration values against the desired state defined in those templates. Any deviation is flagged, and administrators can view and download drift status reports from the Configuration Drifts dashboard. Configuration templates can be integrated with a source control repository for versioning and change control, supporting automated testing and validation of configuration updates after changes are applied.

At the host and cluster level, vSphere Configuration Profiles manage the configuration of all hosts in a cluster collectively, ensuring consistency and detecting when individual host configurations deviate from the cluster-level desired state. vSphere Lifecycle Manager extends this to software specifications, offering compliance check operations that return the compliance status of each host in a cluster against the committed desired state. Together, these mechanisms allow administrators to confirm that host-level security controls remain intact after patching, upgrades, or configuration changes.

VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure objects against configured compliance benchmarks and identifies non-compliant objects along with their specific violations. When non-compliance is detected, VCF Operations provides actionable remediation recommendations. Automated remediation actions can also be configured to bring objects back into compliance based on severity. Separately, VCF includes automated compliance assessment capabilities for vSphere security controls based on the VMware vSphere Security Configuration Guide. These assessments can be run after changes to confirm that host and virtual machine configurations remain aligned with the established security baseline, where drift from the baseline after a change would indicate that a control is no longer functioning as intended.

NSX Manager maintains detailed audit logs that capture both old and new values for modified security policies. When a security policy is changed, the audit log records the user who performed the operation, the security policy identifier, rule configurations, action types, and sequence numbers. This level of detail allows administrators to review exactly what changed in a security policy and compare the before-and-after state, which is a foundational step in verifying that security controls continue to operate as designed after modifications.

Lifecycle management operations include built-in precheck validation. VCF Operations requires a precheck validation step before management component upgrades can proceed, and NSX Manager's upgrade coordinator includes pre-checks to validate upgrade readiness. These prechecks help confirm that the environment is in a known-good state before changes are applied.

VCF Automation provides a Content and Policies interface that identifies custom forms containing actions that cannot be validated. The Form Actions tab in the custom form designer lists all actions used in a form, allowing administrators to review non-validatable actions and verify their input validation code. This provides a mechanism for verifying that automation-driven controls continue to enforce expected behavior after form or policy modifications. VCF Automation day 2 action policies control which users can run selected actions on deployed resources, with only users matching the policy criteria permitted to execute those actions. After changes to these policies, administrators can review the policy configuration to confirm that the intended access restrictions remain in place.

At the Kubernetes consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor provide additional mechanisms for verifying security control behavior after changes. Pod Security Admission supports three enforcement modes that can be set independently per namespace: enforce, audit, and warn. The enforce mode blocks pods that do not comply with the configured Pod Security Standard; the audit mode logs non-compliant pod creation attempts without blocking; and the warn mode returns a warning to the API client without blocking. VKS cluster management provides out-of-the-box security policy templates, including baseline and restricted profiles that mirror Pod Security Standard definitions, and custom policies can be created in Dry run mode to observe their impact before enabling full enforcement. After a change to Pod Security Admission configuration, operators can apply the restricted profile in audit or warn mode to observe which workloads would be affected, with the audit log capturing the results for review.

Kubernetes ValidatingAdmissionPolicy bindings support validationActions of Deny, Warn, and Audit to control how validation failures are handled during admission. When set to Audit, policy violations are recorded in the Kubernetes audit log without blocking traffic, allowing operators to observe policy match behavior after a policy change without disrupting production workloads. The FailurePolicy field governs behavior when a policy expression fails to evaluate, and bindings are scoped to namespaces so that namespace-level labels determine enforcement scope. After deploying or modifying a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, operators can submit representative test resources to verify that validation rules fire as intended.

Kubernetes RBAC provides a SelfSubjectAccessReview capability that allows operators to query whether a given subject holds a specific permission on a given resource, enabling direct confirmation that role bindings are applying as intended after changes to Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, or ClusterRoleBindings. Pod security contexts can also be reviewed after policy changes to verify that individual workloads are operating within the intended privilege boundaries.

At the Supervisor infrastructure level, core cluster component pods such as etcd and kube-apiserver record restart events and uptime duration. After configuration changes that affect the cluster control plane, operators can examine these health indicators to confirm that cluster infrastructure components remain continuously operational and that changes have not disrupted core control functionality.

For resilience control verification, VKS cluster management supports backup and restore of entire clusters, selected namespaces, or specific resources identified by a label, managed through the VCF Automation Tenant Portal under Manage & Govern > VCF Services > Kubernetes Management. The Velero Plugin for vSphere supports both stateless and stateful applications and allows restore operations to be initiated from the Data Protection tab, including restoring a backup from a source cluster to a different target cluster. VKS cluster node VMs are backed up and restored through Supervisor state backup using the vCenter backup feature. After changes to backup or storage configuration, performing a test restore from a recent backup confirms that the resilience control is functioning correctly.

Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) for Supervisor and VMware Kubernetes Service release nodes (VKrs) are available to guide hardening verification for the Kubernetes platform layer, providing a reference baseline against which post-change configuration compliance can be assessed.

These capabilities provide the technical building blocks for post-change control verification across the full VCF platform: configuration drift detection, compliance benchmarking, audit logging of security policy changes, upgrade prechecks, automation policy review, Pod Security Admission audit modes, admission policy verification, Kubernetes RBAC inspection, and VKS backup restore testing. However, they do not by themselves constitute a complete verification program. Organizations must establish procedures that define when functional verification is required, what controls to test after specific types of changes, how to perform functional testing of whether security controls are actively blocking, detecting, or enforcing as intended, and how to document verification results.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend and the Security Services Platform (SSP) provide several mechanisms that help organizations verify that network security controls continue to operate as intended after changes to firewall policies, segmentation rules, and threat protection configurations.

The Security Services Platform's Security Intelligence feature includes a Monitoring capability that checks for changes in the scope of input entities every hour when enabled. When a change is detected, affected policy recommendations are flagged with a "change-detected" status indicator. Administrators can review the flagged recommendations through the Policy Recommendations interface, accessible via Monitor & Plan, and rerun the analysis to confirm that segmentation policies and firewall rules remain consistent with the intended security posture after an infrastructure or policy change.

The Rule Analysis feature on the Security Services Platform provides a structured mechanism for verifying the efficiency and correctness of Distributed Firewall rules. When activated, Rule Analysis evaluates rule configurations and generates reports identifying redundant, shadowed, or misconfigured rules. After changes are made to firewall policies, running Rule Analysis helps confirm that the updated rule set operates as intended and that no unintended effects have been introduced. The feature's operational status is visible in the Security Services Platform UI at System > Platform & Features by checking the Rule Analysis feature card Status field.

The Security Services Platform extracts all IDS policies, rules, and security profiles to support multiple remediations, clean up stale policies, and suggest policies that do not conflict with one another. This supports a verification workflow where policy consistency across IDS and threat prevention configurations can be reviewed following changes. Before republishing policies, administrators can also review infrastructure and environment rules through the Review & Add Rules interface for the SSP-Infrastructure policy, confirming that rules are free of error conditions before propagating changes to the enforcement layer.

The Security Services Platform System > Overview page displays the current platform status, allowing administrators to assess stability before and after configuration changes. Feature-specific status indicators are available at System > Platform & Features, where the Rule Analysis and Malware Prevention feature cards each display a current operational status that administrators can check following updates or changes. The platform's deployment wizard also conducts validation checks before implementing configuration changes, reducing the risk that a change introduces a misconfiguration that could silently degrade security control effectiveness.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides several mechanisms that help verify data protection controls remain functional after changes to the environment.

Test recovery operations are the primary verification mechanism. Administrators can run test recoveries at any time to confirm that recovery plans execute correctly without affecting production workloads. Each test exercises the full recovery workflow, including virtual machine startup, network reconfiguration, and any custom steps defined in the plan. Recovery plans transition through defined states during testing, moving to a Test Complete state when the test finishes or a Test Interrupted state when a failure occurs during execution. This state tracking provides immediate visibility into whether data protection controls are operating as designed following any change.

Recovery plan history reports capture the result and timestamps for each plan execution and for individual steps within the plan. These records can be exported for documentation and compliance purposes, giving change management teams evidence that data protection controls were exercised and verified after changes. PNR explicitly requires that protection groups and recovery plans be verified following upgrades to the Protection and Recovery Appliance, and that connections between protected and recovery sites be validated for correctness after any upgrade.

Protection group state monitoring surfaces configuration problems that result from environmental changes. Protection and Recovery protection groups monitor the status of virtual machines and determine which operations are allowed in each protection state. Groups transition through well-defined states including Configuring Protection, Removing Protection, Restoring Placeholders, and Operations in Progress. When protection is disrupted by a change, the affected group transitions to a state that restricts test recovery and planned migration operations, surfacing the problem before a recovery attempt is made.

Alarms provide proactive notification when changes affect data protection controls. Alarms are generated when connectivity problems occur or when protection groups and recovery plans enter an error state, allowing administrators to detect and remediate issues promptly.

PNR introduces an Advanced Cyber Compliance (ACC) subscription that extends verification capabilities. With ACC enabled, Cyber Recovery can install security sensors on virtual machines during the validation phase to perform vulnerability analysis, behavioral analysis, and malware signature scanning. This adds an active security verification layer alongside the existing snapshot integrity and replication status checks. The ACC subscription also enables use of the Latest instances option in recovery plans, providing additional recovery point selection capabilities for verification testing.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Avi Load Balancer provides several mechanisms that support verification of security control configurations following changes. The Avi Controller CLI includes the `show networksecuritypolicy` command, which allows administrators to inspect the current state of Network Security Policies and confirm that changes have taken effect as intended. At the virtual service level, Avi WAF Policies attach a Positive Security group that applies learned application data and defines hit and miss actions, giving administrators a direct view of the rules actively enforced after a WAF policy modification. The `learned-data-promotion-location` setting controls which learned application data is promoted to Positive Security rules, allowing administrators to validate that the correct data has been incorporated following a change.

For correlating configuration changes with observed effects, the Service Engine Analytics Config Events overlay lets administrators identify whether a shift in traffic behavior was caused by a specific configuration modification, such as an update to virtual service security settings. This provides a post-change verification path that links configuration actions to traffic outcomes in a single view. Individual Layer 4 Policy Set rules provide a mechanism to control the behavior of Layer 4 virtual services by evaluating Layer 3 and Layer 4 attributes, allowing targeted policy modifications to be assessed in isolation.

ControlScripts are Python-based scripts that execute on Avi Load Balancer Controllers and are initiated by Alert Actions triggered by system events. They can alter Avi Load Balancer configuration or communicate with remote systems such as email, syslog, and SNMP servers, enabling automated post-change health checks and notification workflows. Alert Actions can also trigger ControlScripts to automatically add offending client IP addresses to a blocklist attached to a Network Security Policy, providing a feedback loop between detection and enforced policy state. The Application Security Report generates a downloadable PDF documenting security findings and pre-check results, supporting structured post-change documentation.

For deployments integrated with VCF, the Avi Load Balancer for VCF documentation specifies that Avi Management Plane alarms, alerts, and dashboards must be operational as part of post-upgrade validation, establishing explicit criteria that administrators can check following software changes.

The control's question asks whether the organization verifies control functionality as a deliberate process. Avi's observability features, CLI inspection tools, Config Events overlay, and ControlScript automation support that activity by making control state visible and queryable after a change. The organization must define acceptance criteria, expected baselines, and approval workflows. Avi does not automate the organizational verification decision itself.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control CM-03(04)

> Require [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy representatives] to be members of the [Assignment: organization-defined configuration change control element].

**SCF Controls:** CHG-02.3

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CM-03(06)

> Ensure that cryptographic mechanisms used to provide the following controls are under configuration management: [Assignment: organization-defined controls].

**SCF Controls:** CHG-02.5

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides integrated mechanisms for governing cryptographic assets across the platform, including dedicated APIs, certificate lifecycle management, key management infrastructure, role-based access controls, validated cryptographic modules, and configuration drift detection. These mechanisms extend through the platform's Kubernetes consumption layer, where declarative Kubernetes API objects expose cryptographic configurations that can be version-controlled, reviewed, and governed through standard change management workflows.

At the API level, the CryptoManager managed object in VMware vCenter is the central interface for cryptographic key management and cryptographic operations, including key management server integration via KMIP (Key Management Interoperability Protocol) on port 5696. The CryptoManager hierarchy, with CryptoManagerKmip as its child, gives administrators a single point of control for managing encryption keys, configuring key providers, and performing cryptographic operations across the environment. Storage policies for virtual machine encryption are managed through the PBM Profile Manager API, which associates encryption capability instances with policy rules, providing a structured and repeatable approach to encryption configuration.

For certificate management, VCF Operations provides centralized certificate lifecycle capabilities. Administrators can configure a Certificate Authority (either Microsoft CA or OpenSSL), generate certificate signing requests, replace component certificates with CA-signed certificates, and activate auto-renewal. VCF Operations monitors certificate expiration across the infrastructure, tracking days to expiration for platform, root, STS, and TLS certificates and alerting administrators when certificates are nearing expiration. The VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA) built into vCenter can manage certificates automatically in multiple modes, including full VMCA management and machine-SSL-only replacement with VMCA handling the rest. The vSphere Automation API can refresh both VMCA-issued and third-party certificates. NSX Manager provides a certificate management interface for viewing, importing, and replacing certificates for its services. VCF Operations exposes API endpoints for trusted certificate management. Within the Kubernetes consumption layer, cert-manager integrates with the Supervisor namespace model to manage certificate lifecycle for workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters, handling issuance, rotation, and revocation. VKS clusters include their own set of TLS certificates supporting cryptographic infrastructure for inter-component communications, and Harbor, when deployed as a Supervisor Service, accepts custom TLS certificates via a tlsCertificate configuration field.
