For encryption key management at the infrastructure layer, VCF offers two approaches. The vSphere Native Key Provider is included in all vSphere editions and does not require an external key server; vCenter generates the Key Encryption Key and pushes it to the VMware ESX hosts, which then generate Data Encryption Keys. For organizations that require an external key management infrastructure, vSAN encryption supports KMIP-compatible Key Management Servers. The Native Key Provider can coexist with an existing external key server infrastructure.

VCF Encryption Management extends key governance to the consumption layer by allowing provider administrators to publish key providers to tenant organizations. Organization administrators then gain the ability to use their own key providers for encryption of virtual machines and disks in their VCF environment. Configuration of key providers through this service requires either the Encryption Management Admin role or a custom role that grants the right to configure key providers, restricting this capability to authorized personnel.

Within the Kubernetes consumption layer, the Kubernetes EncryptionConfiguration API object provides declarative control over which resources are encrypted in etcd, which encryption providers are in use, and in what order. Supported resource types include Secrets, ConfigMaps, and custom resources, with per-resource encryption policies and wildcard patterns to target all resources within an API group. The first provider in the EncryptionConfiguration list governs encryption of resources written to storage; all configured providers are attempted in order when decrypting. Because EncryptionConfiguration is a declarative configuration file, it can be stored in version control, reviewed through change management tooling, and audited for drift. The Argo CD Supervisor Service provides a GitOps-based enforcement mechanism for this version-controlled governance: it continuously monitors configuration repositories, compares live Kubernetes cluster state against the desired state stored in Git, and automatically applies changes, reporting any configuration deviations as OutOfSync conditions. VKS clusters support a fips.enabled ClusterClass variable that activates FIPS-compliant cryptographic modules for the cluster, making FIPS enablement a versioned, governable cluster configuration decision. VKS cluster security configurations are immutable and persistent by design, which supports consistent configuration governance by preventing ad-hoc node-level modifications.

VCF enforces strict access control over cryptographic operations through a granular privilege model. Only users assigned Cryptographic Operations privileges can perform cryptographic operations in vCenter. The default Administrator system role includes all Cryptographic Operations privileges, while a dedicated No Cryptography Administrator role provides all Administrator privileges except Cryptographic Operations. This separation allows organizations to restrict who can modify cryptographic configurations, limiting cryptographic management to authorized personnel. Custom roles can be cloned from the No Cryptography Administrator role to grant specific subsets of cryptographic privileges, such as allowing encryption but not decryption. Key management operations (ManageEncryptionPolicy, ManageKeyServers, ManageKeys) require their own dedicated permissions. Within VKS workloads, the Secret Store Service applies role-based access control for administrators, DevOps engineers, and application tenants managing sensitive data, governing who can read, create, or modify cryptographic material stored as Kubernetes Secrets.

VCF components use validated cryptographic modules across the platform. NSX uses FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default, validated through the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program. VCF Automation uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, compliant with FIPS 140-3 (Certificate #4743). VCF Operations for Networks uses BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 with FIPS 140-2 Certificate #37009, and can restrict cryptographic operations to FIPS-validated modules for both internal and external connections. VCF Operations supports a FIPS Security Mode that enforces additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites when enabled. These validated modules provide a known, documented baseline for the cryptographic protections in use across the platform. The vCenter appliance also uses VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0, which provides FIPS-validated algorithms including AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, and SHA2 variants, and two versions of the VMware OpenSSL FIPS Object Module (versions 3.0 and 2.0.20-vmw) that cover additional algorithms including DRBG, DSA, KBKDF, and Triple-DES. Starting in vSphere 9.0, all TLS profiles for both vCenter and ESX are FIPS compliant, with the COMPATIBLE profile using modern FIPS-validated cipher suites.

Cryptographic protocol settings at the Kubernetes layer are exposed through standard configuration mechanisms that integrate with configuration management workflows. The kube-apiserver supports a --tls-cipher-suites parameter to restrict the set of permitted cipher suites and a --tls-min-version flag to set the minimum protocol version the API server accepts. Kubelet exposes equivalent tlsCipherSuites and tlsMinVersion fields in KubeletConfiguration. The Kube Scheduler similarly supports a --tls-cipher-suites parameter. For ingress traffic, Contour exposes tls.minimum-protocol-version and tls.maximum-protocol-version parameters in its package configuration. Service mesh deployments using Istio include a meshConfig.meshMTLS.minProtocolVersion setting that controls the minimum protocol version for mutual TLS across the mesh. These settings are Kubernetes API objects or configuration files that can be committed to version control and governed through standard change control workflows. Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) are available for Supervisor components and VKS node configurations, providing a hardening baseline that organizations can incorporate into their configuration management policy definitions.

VCF Operations provides unified configuration management with scheduled drift detection for vCenter and cluster objects. The Configuration Drifts feature compares current vCenter values against configuration template values, proactively detecting deviations from the desired state. When a configuration drift is detected, notifications alert administrators to any deviation from assigned template values. The Configuration Drift dashboard allows viewing and comparing drifts and identifying which settings have changed. Configuration drift templates can be integrated with a source control repository, enabling versioning, auditing, and structured management of configuration baselines, including those that govern cryptographic settings. This drift detection capability allows organizations to monitor cryptographic-related configurations for unauthorized or unintended changes as part of their broader configuration management process.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend Security Services Platform (SSP) provides cryptographic asset governance within its own infrastructure through FIPS-validated cryptographic modules and an immutable cryptographic configuration. SSP uses the VMware BouncyCastle Module version bc-fips-2.1.2 (NIST Certificate #4943) and VMware OpenSSL FIPS provider Object Module version 3.0.15 (NIST Certificate #4861) for cryptographic operations, with both modules meeting FIPS 140-2 and FIPS 140-3 standards. This FIPS coverage extends across SSP components, including SSP Installer and NDR Sensor for vDefend.

The protocol versions and cipher suites permitted on SSP Installer and SSP cannot be modified by administrators, placing the cryptographic configuration in a fixed state consistent with the product's security baseline. This immutable configuration supports governance by removing the possibility of unauthorized or non-compliant cryptographic configuration changes within the platform.

SSP also supports certificate import workflows for web proxy and License Hub components, providing a mechanism for managing the certificates used by vDefend's own infrastructure. Governance of cryptographic assets outside of vDefend's own infrastructure requires organizational configuration management processes beyond what vDefend provides.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides multiple mechanisms for governing assets involved in cryptographic protections, supporting an organization's configuration management processes for the cipher suites, certificates, keys, and cryptographic modes used in application traffic handling.

SSL/TLS Profile Templates are the primary configuration-managed objects for cryptographic governance in Avi. Found under Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile in the Controller UI, each profile specifies the accepted cipher suites and protocol versions with a prioritized ordering. Administrators define and maintain these profiles centrally, then apply them to virtual services. Changes to a profile propagate to all associated virtual services, giving the organization a single configuration object to manage under change control. In 9.1, SSL Profile Templates include support for post-quantum cryptography cipher suites, extending the cryptographic governance scope of each profile object.

Avi supports FIPS 140-3 mode as a system-wide compliance control for cryptographic operations. When FIPS mode is enabled on the Controller, the system uses the OpenSSL FIPS Provider version 3.1.2, which has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded Certificate #4985 by the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). Only FIPS-compliant algorithms are used when FIPS mode is active. This mode-level constraint on algorithm selection can be tracked and managed as a configuration item across the Avi deployment.

For cryptographic key storage, Avi Controller integrates with Thales Luna Hardware Security Modules (HSMs). Virtual services can be configured to reference an HSM Group for certificate and key management, keeping key material in hardware-bound storage. Service Engine Groups can be configured with HSM groups that define how Service Engines authenticate with and use the HSM for secure storage of SSL certificates and private keys.

Avi certificate management includes Certificate Management Profiles with certificate authority integration. A built-in Let's Encrypt Certificate Management Profile is available by default at Controller startup and can be configured for automated CSR issuance and lifecycle management, using Let's Encrypt Production as the default certificate authority with DNS-01 challenge-based auto-renewal. This allows certificate assets to be governed through a defined profile rather than through manual operations. Certificates can be shared across tenants through CLI configuration. PKI Profiles provide an additional mechanism for managing certificate and cipher settings in a structured, named configuration object. When integrating with external certificate lifecycle management platforms, Avi's App Transport Security feature allows a platform such as Thales Trust Protection Platform to use any administrator account that has write access to SSL/TLS certificates for the required tenants to manage certificate lifecycle.

For Kubernetes environments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) supports PKIProfile configuration that enables secure communication with backend services through certificate-based authentication and encryption. This extends cryptographic asset governance to Kubernetes-deployed workloads managed through Avi.

Access to cryptographic assets is governed through Avi's role-based access control (RBAC). The Controller restricts private SSL key export to the fewest administrators possible. Write access to Virtual Service, Application Profile, SSL/TLS Certificate, and Certificate Management Profile objects is scoped through role assignments. Configuration exports can be protected with passphrase encryption to secure sensitive configuration data, including cryptographic material, when configurations are exported as part of change management processes.

In Avi for VCF deployments, after upgrading each component, a drift check must be performed on VCF Operations, vCenter, and NSX Manager to confirm configuration consistency, which includes verifying cryptographic configuration objects such as SSL profiles and certificates remain in their intended state.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CM-04

> Analyze changes to the system to determine potential security and privacy impacts prior to change implementation.

**SCF Controls:** CHG-03

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CM-04(01)

> Analyze changes to the system in a separate test environment before implementation in an operational environment, looking for security and privacy impacts due to flaws, weaknesses, incompatibility, or intentional malice.

**SCF Controls:** TDA-08

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several technical capabilities that support the separation of development, testing, and operational environments, though the organizational policies and governance processes that define environment boundaries and enforce segregation must be established independently.

VCF workload domains provide infrastructure-level separation, allowing administrators to deploy distinct domains for development, testing, and production workloads with independent compute, storage, and networking configurations. Each workload domain operates with its own resource boundaries and management policies, and configuration settings appropriate for one environment tier may differ from those used in another. VCF documentation explicitly notes that configuration appropriate for development environments may not be suitable for production environments within the same deployment.

VCF Operations includes an environment classification feature that allows administrators to tag Business Application environments as Development, Production, Staging, Test, or DR. This classification enables differentiated monitoring and management across environment tiers. Administrators can configure custom groups with distinct alert policies and capacity calculations for production environments versus testing environments, allowing each tier to operate under monitoring rules appropriate to its purpose. The VM Performance and VM Contention dashboards note that development environments can tolerate a small amount of contention in CPU and memory, while production environments should not, reinforcing the need for distinct operational treatment per environment.

VCF Automation provides organizational and project-level isolation boundaries that map naturally to environment separation. Each organization in VCF Automation operates in an isolated environment with dedicated network setup and network isolation, where users can access only allocated services and resources. Within an organization, projects create additional isolation boundaries between teams, and namespace-level isolation adds a further layer of separation. Security groups and vDefend firewall rules support network isolation between project environments. When stronger isolation is required between environment tiers, multiple projects or organizations can be used; when isolation requirements are lower, fewer projects simplify the operating model. These constructs give administrators the building blocks to enforce separation between development, testing, and production workloads.

NSX Projects enable isolation of networking and security configurations for multiple departments or organizational units within a single NSX deployment, allowing network-level separation between environment tiers. NSX multi-tenancy isolates security and networking objects across tenants, supporting the use of separate tenant configurations for development, testing, and production environments.

vSphere provides workload isolation at multiple levels. Each VM runs as an isolated system protected from interference by other VMs or workloads. Resource pools isolate virtual machine performance from other users of the same pool. Namespaces provide additional isolation for containerized workloads, and administrators can assign different port groups as the network for each namespace, providing layer 2 isolation between namespaces. Cross-namespace communication is blocked unless explicitly permitted. vSphere Auto Deploy requires secure network separation between the production network and the management or deployment networks, making this separation a prerequisite for stateless or stateful ESX host provisioning workflows.

The VCF CLI supports context-based environment switching, enabling administrators to move between local development, staging, and production clusters without requiring constant configuration updates. This helps maintain clear boundaries between environments during day-to-day operations and reduces the risk of accidental changes to production systems.

VCF also supports a test-before-production discipline across several components. VCF Operations allows test runs before final deployment to verify that management packs collect correct data and accurately represent the target system. The documentation recommends testing newly created configuration files for the Management Pack for Aggregator with a test instance before applying them to production systems. For VM-based workloads on vSphere Supervisors, administrators are directed to create production images with the latest patches and required security settings that follow corporate security policies, establishing a controlled promotion path from development to production. Administrators can also configure more restrictive VIB acceptance levels for ESX hosts in production than for hosts in testing environments, limiting which software packages can be installed on production infrastructure.

vSphere administrators set guardrails for the policy and governance of VMs, delivering VM resources such as VM classes and VM templates to DevOps engineers. This separation of administrative and consumer roles restricts who can modify production infrastructure versus who can consume resources in development or testing namespaces.

Defining which environments are designated as development, testing, or production, establishing access policies for each, and managing the lifecycle of changes across those environments requires organizational processes beyond what VCF provides. VCF supplies the isolation mechanisms and classification tools; the organization must define the policies that govern how those mechanisms are used.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides network-level enforcement mechanisms that support separating development, testing, and operational environments, reducing the risk of unauthorized access or cross-environment changes.

The Distributed Firewall enables environment isolation through microsegmentation, dividing the data center into broader security zones such as production, development, testing, and PCI environments. Distributed Firewall rules are enforced at the vNIC level, so environment boundaries are maintained even when workloads from different environments share the same physical hosts or network segments. The Gateway Firewall in vDefend can enforce isolation between projects by denying all inter-project communication except for essential infrastructure protocols such as DHCP, DNS, NTP, and ICMP, while permitting internal team communication within isolated development virtual private clouds.

Security Services Platform provides purpose-built tools for defining environment hierarchies and configuring segmentation policies. Administrators define environments by specifying which virtual machines, IP addresses, distributed virtual port groups, and network segments belong to each environment using CSV files, which Security Intelligence uses to create groups and apply appropriate tags. When implementing environment pair controls, the recommended approach is to apply a broad block policy between sensitive pairs such as Testing and Production as an initial step, then identify all inter-environment flows and add specific policies before enforcing a strict block. Security Intelligence administrators can monitor traffic leakage between environments, establish permitted exceptions, and apply lockdown protection rules to control inter-environment traffic.

The Security Segmentation Score provides a quantitative view of segmentation coverage. The Environment Protection component evaluates whether workloads are protected by environment-specific segmentation policies such as Dev, Prod, and Test, contributing five points to the overall score. The security segmentation score breakdown report details total environments and pairs, protection rule actions, and highlights environment pairs that lack inter-environment policies. The Environments monitoring window shows the total number of inter-environment pairs and how many are without inter-environment policies. Segmentation Planning supports the design phase by allowing administrators to publish environments together with related applications to maintain proper correlation between them. Security Services Platform Inventory monitoring allows administrators to view the infrastructure hierarchy, including regions, zones, and virtual machines, to verify that the environment hierarchy is correctly defined before enforcement begins.

For Virtual Desktop Infrastructure deployments, Security Services Platform recommends isolating VDI servers from server-based traffic patterns and preventing separate VDI environments from communicating with one another.

Defining which workloads belong to which environment, determining which environment pairs may communicate, and managing the lifecycle of configuration changes across environments require organizational processes and decisions that vDefend does not govern. vDefend enforces the network boundaries once those decisions are made.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides mechanisms at the application delivery layer to support separation between development, testing, and operational environments. The primary isolation mechanism is Avi's multi-tenant architecture: a single Avi Controller cluster can host each environment in a dedicated tenant with RBAC-enforced access boundaries, or organizations can deploy separate Controller clusters per environment. Controller cluster topology supports this separation; each Controller cluster can be configured as a one-node cluster for development and testing, or a three-node cluster for production, giving organizations a clear infrastructure-level distinction between environment tiers.

Within a single-cluster deployment, Avi's tenant model provides administrative isolation, scoping visibility and access to objects within tenant boundaries. Service Engine Groups provide data-plane isolation: virtual services for test and production environments should be deployed on different Service Engine Groups, so application traffic from test workloads is handled by different data-plane VMs than production traffic, reducing the risk of cross-environment data flows. An administrator can deploy virtual services for the same application in both test and production environments on different Service Engine Groups within the same tenant, or across separate tenants for stronger isolation.

In 9.1, the Avi for VCF integration adds dedicated environment isolation constructs. Service Engine Group allocation within VCF Automation blueprints for Layer 4 and Layer 7 virtual services provides operational isolation, keeping each project within its designated boundaries. Namespace allocation in the Avi Controller similarly scopes each project's resources. In Organization-Managed Mode, organization administrators manage their own Service Engine Group configurations and infrastructure settings, reinforcing the organizational boundary between environment owners. VCF Automation integration supports infrastructure-as-code workflows, which can be used to apply consistent environment configurations across development, testing, and production tiers.

Service Engine deployments using dedicated management mode provide network isolation between data and management traffic, separating the management plane from application traffic paths.

Organizations should define which Avi tenants map to which environments, establish RBAC policies that restrict personnel from operating across environment boundaries, and configure distinct Service Engine Groups for each tier. The Avi Controller's configuration audit log supports review of whether environment boundaries have been respected during configuration changes.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CM-04(02)

> After system changes, verify that the impacted controls are implemented correctly, operating as intended, and producing the desired outcome with regard to meeting the security and privacy requirements for the system.

**SCF Controls:** IAO-06

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CM-05

> Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to the system.

**SCF Controls:** CHG-04, END-03.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF enforces access restrictions on infrastructure changes through layered role-based access control across all major components, enabling organizations to define, document, and enforce access restrictions associated with changes to the platform's technology assets, applications, and services.

At the infrastructure layer, VMware vCenter uses an AuthorizationManager that controls access to specific server objects based on the permissions associated with each object. Authorization verifies what objects principals have access to, restricting the ability to make configuration changes to only those users and groups with the appropriate roles and privileges assigned. This permission model applies to all managed objects in the vCenter inventory, including hosts, virtual machines, datastores, and networks, so that changes to any asset require explicit authorization. When a user belongs to a group with a role that grants broader access, a more restrictive role can be assigned directly to the individual user, with the direct user assignment taking precedence over the group role to enable per-user access restriction. The Modify permission privilege separately gates who can define or update permission rules on any managed entity, restricting the ability to alter access configurations to authorized administrators.

VCF Automation provides an extensive set of granular permissions covering operations across the platform, including rights for managing API tokens, blueprints, catalog items, certificates, content libraries, and approvals. VCF defines three levels of entity access rights (View, Edit, and Full Control) that can be assigned to specific user roles. Only a System Administrator can modify the rights in a predefined role, and organization users can exercise only those rights from their roles that are published to their organizations, providing an additional layer of restriction beyond role assignment.

Rights bundles organize rights into categories and subcategories (Access Control, Administration, Compute, Content Hubs, Extensions, Infrastructure) that control view or manage access to objects, allowing selection of rights individually, by subcategory, or globally. Administrators can clone existing rights bundles to create custom access control configurations. Custom user roles can be created to tailor permissions, such as expanding a project administrator's permissions to manage parts of the infrastructure or granting a service viewer the ability to review and respond to approval requests. The accessControls API allows System Administrators with the Manage General ACL right to create, modify, and remove specific access control lists programmatically, and access to runtime defined entities is controlled through rights bundles and ACL entries.

Access restrictions on entity types allow administrators to grant access to specific users and organizations and to set different restrictions for different versions of an entity type, providing version-level change control. Entitlements based on user criteria manage access to projects and catalog items in the Service Catalog, restricting who can request or modify specific resources.

VCF Automation enforces project-level access controls governed by pre-defined roles. Policy enforcement follows a hierarchical model: Policy as Code enables organization administrators to enforce instance-centric policies for Infrastructure as a Service resources from a central location, reducing the risk of unauthorized changes. A restrictive security policy created by an organization administrator at the organization level cannot be overwritten by a less restrictive policy at the project level, so higher-level access restrictions remain in effect regardless of lower-level configurations.

VCF Automation also integrates with ServiceNow ITSM, where administrators can configure read, create, and update permissions for application access, enabling change approval workflows through existing ITSM processes. The approver-user role in the ServiceNow ITSM Plug-in requires specific permissions, and system administrators can update approval group system properties to manage subworkflow approval groups.

VCF Operations implements its own role-based access control system where users can only perform the actions that their assigned permissions allow. Administrators manage permissions through the Roles tab, where expandable category trees allow granular selection or deselection of permissions under areas such as Infrastructure Operations. Out-of-the-box roles come with default permissions that can be customized, and the Apply Default Permissions option provides a baseline. Scopes further refine access control by associating defined scopes with user accounts and user groups, restricting which objects a given user can view or modify. Access groups define the specific actions available to members, such as the ability to add, edit, or delete dashboards, or to view, configure, or manage objects. VCF Operations also provides scheduled configuration drift detection for vCenter and cluster objects to proactively detect configuration deviations from the desired state, alerting administrators when changes occur.

NSX role-based access control defines permissions for system fabric operations including Edge Cluster Profiles, Node Profiles, and Transport Zones management, restricting which administrators can make changes to the network infrastructure components of the platform.

The Consumption layer of VCF, delivered through VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor, extends access restrictions to Kubernetes-managed technology assets and services. Kubernetes RBAC is the primary authorization mechanism at this layer: Roles and ClusterRoles specify permitted operations (create, update, delete, patch) on specific API resources and subresources, while RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings assign those roles to authenticated identities. Role objects can restrict permissions to specific resource names and subresources, providing access control at a granularity finer than resource type. Kubernetes guidance calls for least-privileged access even for administrators, reserving broader access for emergency break-glass scenarios only. Dynamic Resource Allocation APIs are controlled through RBAC to restrict access based on user persona, and software installed to support access control is isolated from general workloads given the elevated privileges it carries.

At the namespace level, administrators configure per-namespace RBAC, resource limits, and image libraries for each vSphere Namespace, scoping change authority so that teams can only modify resources in namespaces where they hold explicit roles. IAM role trust policies should be scoped to specific service accounts and namespaces to enforce least privilege when consuming VKS clusters. Kubernetes Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) policies provide an additional authorization mode, defining rules using user, namespace, resource, and apiGroup attributes to grant or restrict access; they can limit individual users to read-only operations on pods in a specific namespace. Different authorization rules can be applied per namespace to further enforce isolation between workloads.

Admission controllers enforce access restrictions at the Kubernetes API server level before any change is accepted. The ValidatingAdmissionPolicy resource allows administrators to define policy rules specifying which API groups, operations, and resources are in scope, validating or rejecting changes that violate those rules; for sensitive workloads, a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy can further restrict the permitted write actions beyond what RBAC alone provides. The OwnerReferencesPermissionEnforcement admission controller protects object ownership metadata so that only users with delete permission on an object can modify its ownerReferences, preventing unauthorized lifecycle manipulation. Cluster administrators should restrict access to delete or update ResourceQuota objects using ValidatingAdmissionPolicy to preserve resource governance integrity. Custom validating and mutating webhooks extend this enforcement chain for organization-specific requirements.

AppArmor provides mandatory access control on VKS cluster nodes running Ubuntu and Photon, restricting container capabilities, limiting file system access, controlling network access, and enforcing security policies on containerized processes through profiles that define the permitted operations. Pod Security Standards allow administrators to configure PSA enforcement at the namespace level; namespaces that permit privileged workloads should establish appropriate access controls aligned with least privilege, and per-pod security contexts should be reviewed to limit privileges beyond the baseline standard. Istio, available as a standard package on VKS clusters, provides a pluggable policy layer and configuration API supporting access controls, rate limits, and quotas at the service mesh level, adding an enforcement point for changes to service-to-service communication.

Additional services within the VKS ecosystem enforce their own access restrictions. Harbor Registry enforces role-based access control to restrict who can push, modify, or delete container image artifacts. The Secret Store service enforces RBAC for administrators, DevOps engineers, and application tenants managing sensitive data. The Argo CD Supervisor Service implements RBAC through policy rules that define permissions for accounts and role assignments, restricting who can apply GitOps-driven changes to cluster state; policy rules use (p) assignments for permissions and (g) assignments for role groups. Immutable infrastructure patterns supported by Kubernetes restrict unauthorized in-place modifications through automated processes that overwrite changes or systems that reject modifications entirely.

VCF Automation for VM Apps enforces permission-based access to the Resources tab, restricting deployment and resource management to catalog consumers with the necessary permissions, extending the access control model to the self-service consumption interface.

Supervisor and VKS supply the technical mechanisms that restrict who can make changes, including RBAC, admission control, and audit logging, but they do not provide a formal change-management workflow such as change proposal, approval routing, or change-record creation; organizations implement that process layer using their own change-management tooling.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend enforces configuration restrictions across its firewall, IDS/IPS, and TLS Inspection policy management lifecycle, restricting the ability of users to modify security policies outside approved workflows. Role-based access control governs who can create, modify, and publish security policies, while policy locking mechanisms block concurrent edits and restrict modification of locked configurations.

vDefend (DFW) supports a draft-based configuration management workflow where security policies are authored in draft form before being published to the enforcement plane. Drafts can be locked to prevent multiple users from simultaneously opening and editing a manual draft, and a comment is required when locking. Auto drafts and manual drafts can also be cloned and saved with the option to lock the configuration with a required comment. Manual drafts can be reverted to the original configuration, providing a rollback path when a draft change needs to be undone. Policy section locking is available from the Global Manager and restricts editing to specific portions of the firewall rule base while leaving other sections available for authorized changes. When a security policy is locked, no other user can modify it until the lock is released.

Gateway Firewall supports the same section-level locking model, with a required comment when locking a section. Gateway Firewall locking is off by default and must be explicitly enabled through Gateway Specific Settings. Organization Admin ownership of firewall rules prevents project members from accessing rule modifications.

vDefend IDS/IPS policies support section-level locking for both Distributed and Gateway deployments. Distributed IDS/IPS provides a Locked option that prevents multiple users from editing the same policy sections, with a required comment when locking. Gateway Firewall IDS/IPS policy sections support the same locking model with the same comment requirement.

TLS Inspection policies on the Gateway Firewall support an Advanced Configuration option to lock policies and prevent concurrent changes by multiple users.

Security Services Platform (SSP) enforces an additional restriction: initial security policies cannot be modified through the user interface after they have been published. This immutability requires administrators to follow a controlled change process rather than directly editing production policies. SSP role-based access control defines five distinct roles with differentiated permissions across Rule Analysis and other platform operations: Enterprise Admin, Auditor, Security Engineer, Security Operator, and Support Bundle Collector. The Auditor role grants read-only access to view information and track activity without the ability to make changes. Access to User Management and role assignment modifications requires Admin privileges.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) enforces configuration restrictions through a role-based access control (RBAC) model that restricts the ability of users to make unauthorized changes to disaster recovery configurations. PNR defines predefined roles with distinct privilege sets, and administrators can create custom roles by cloning existing ones and removing specific privileges to enforce least-privilege access.

PNR provides predefined roles including the Protection and Recovery Administrator and the Protection Groups Administrator, each granting a specific set of privileges over protection groups, recovery plans, and site configuration. The Protection and Recovery Administrator role is required to add or modify roles that restrict or grant user access; this role cannot edit inherited permissions, establishing a boundary on privilege escalation. Certain roles allow users to run commands on the Protection and Recovery server, and the product documentation recommends these roles be assigned only to trusted administrator-level users.

PNR also includes a Test Administrator role, a constrained role that allows users to run and monitor recovery plan tests without the ability to configure protection on virtual machines, create protection groups or recovery plans, or execute recovery or reprotect operations. This role supports separation of duties by enabling test-execution responsibilities to be delegated without granting configuration change privileges.

For operations that configure protection on virtual machines, users must hold explicit permissions on both the protected and recovery site VMware vCenter instances. Site-wide permission assignments must be added on a per-site basis with corresponding permissions required at both sites. In shared recovery site configurations, users must not be allowed to change roles or assign permissions for objects that are not dedicated to their own use. PNR restricts users from renaming, moving, or deleting data centers or hosts in shared recovery site configurations, adding a structural boundary on unauthorized infrastructure modification.

Protection groups in PNR can only be edited on the source site. On the peer site, the protection group is in dormant or recovery state and cannot be edited until the data protection group is active. Protection groups cannot be edited while in Reprotecting state. These state-based restrictions limit the window during which configurations can be changed.

Protection groups can be configured with immutability mode, which blocks modification or deletion of the protection group after immutability is enabled. This option provides a configuration-level lock that is independent of role assignments and restricts changes to protection definitions even by users with administrative access.

PNR Advanced Settings, accessible under Site Pair > Configure > Advanced Settings, are restricted to users with adequate privileges. Users without sufficient permissions cannot view or change custom settings that affect the operation of protection and recovery features.

PNR server access is restricted to administrators only to reduce the number of accounts available for unauthorized access. Users who have their permission removed on a protected site are blocked from continuing operations, and the product returns a permission denial error when users without appropriate privileges attempt restricted operations. Users must have permissions to perform IP customization and in-guest OS callout operations during the recovery process.

vSphere Replication supports similar restriction capabilities. The VRM Administrator role can be cloned and modified to remove specific privileges, creating a custom role with a narrower permission set. The VRM Replication Viewer role restricts users from performing any modifications to configured replication or replication sites, and users assigned this role receive a permission denial error when they attempt operations that require higher privileges.

When PNR is deployed with VCF Automation, access is controlled through role-based permissions assigned to Provider Administrators, Organization Administrators, Project Administrators, and Project Advanced Users, providing a tiered permission model for multi-tenant environments.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides role-based access control and policy mechanisms that govern who can make changes to database resources and configurations. DSM defines two primary roles: the DSM Admin role, which can create, monitor, and manage all data services in the environment, and the DSM User role, which is scoped to specific namespaces and can only manage data services within those assigned namespaces. This role separation restricts change authority to appropriate personnel by design.

For sensitive change operations, DSM applies ownership-based controls. Users with namespace access can change database VM resources; for databases created in the default namespace, only the database owner can change those resources. Only database owners can perform administrator password changes on managed databases. These restrictions scope who can initiate high-impact changes without requiring a separate approval workflow within DSM.

DSM's data service policies allow administrators to define configuration constraints that are enforced when databases are provisioned and modified. The DataServicePolicy custom resource specifies which infrastructure policies may be applied to database instances through the allowedInfrastructurePolicies setting. Administrators can configure these policies through the DSM UI or via VCF Automation, and they can be applied consistently across multiple tenants. Infrastructure policies set limits on the compute, storage, and network resources that database workloads can consume, creating boundaries that restrict the scope of changes users can make to infrastructure resource allocations. Infrastructure policies that are actively used by a database cannot have their existing settings modified, though new compute resources with corresponding parameters can be added.

For SQL Server data service policies, DSM supports an "Allow with a List" option that defines a specific allowlist of approved parameters users may select from when provisioning or modifying a database. DSM also restricts the SQL Server cluster administrator login to read-only privileges and specific ALTER privileges for server management and auditing, rather than full administrative access.

For PostgreSQL databases, applying host-based access restrictions requires creating a separate custom login rather than using the system-defined pgadmin user, enabling fine-grained control over which hosts can connect to specific databases.

Namespace-level isolation reinforces these controls. DSM namespaces enforce RBAC such that users assigned to a namespace can collaborate on data services within that namespace but cannot access or modify data services in other namespaces. A Cloud Administrator can also enforce namespace-specific restrictions to allow or disallow the use of specific infrastructure policies and backup locations in different namespaces.

DSM does not include a formal change management workflow. Defining the approval process for changes, documenting change requests, and obtaining sign-off before applying changes to production database services are organizational responsibilities that require process and tooling outside DSM.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer enforces access restrictions on configuration changes through a role-based access control (RBAC) system built into the Avi Controller. Each role assigns one of three access types to every resource: Write (full create, read, modify, and delete capability), Read (read-only without modification), or No Access (no visibility including inability to list the resource). Administrators create custom roles that configure these permissions independently for each system area, including Virtual Services, Application Profiles, SSL/TLS Certificates, Certificate Management Profiles, and Service Engine Groups.

The Extended Granular RBAC feature adds sub-resource scoping and marker-based filtering. Granular RBAC per field allows administrators to define roles with write access restricted to specific sub-resources within objects, limiting edit access to only designated sub-resources or excluding specific sub-resources from modification. Label-based permissions for cloud objects allow roles to be scoped to specific objects using markers, and label groups can be associated with tenants to enforce label-based access policies across a shared environment. Auth Mapping rules provide a complementary restriction mechanism: the Avi Controller can deny privileges to specific users, preventing them from obtaining any roles or tenants upon login.

Avi Cloud Console enforces role-based access control through two access levels: Full Access (User Administrator or Product Administrator role) and Read-Only (Site Access role), with differentiated permissions for license visibility, management, and administration. Within the Avi Controller itself, the Enterprise Admin role has Full Access to deploy and manage the security services platform, perform upgrades, and configure authentication and authorization.

For organizations using enterprise identity infrastructure, Avi integrates with TACACS+, LDAP, SAML, and OAuth/OIDC authentication profiles. TACACS+ authorization uses attribute value pairs to identify the host, and the TACACS+ server configures user-level authorization with attributes mapped to specific Avi roles and tenants, allowing access restriction policies for configuration changes to be defined centrally through an existing enterprise authorization server rather than managed locally on the Controller.

Administrators can restrict management access to the Avi Controller by specifying individual IP addresses, address ranges, subnets, or predefined IP groups in the Client Management Access section. In VCF-integrated deployments, provider-managed mode and organization-managed mode separate administrative responsibilities, with provider administrators controlling Service Engine Group assignments and application limits per organization. Administrators should restrict the ability to export certificates and private keys to the fewest necessary administrators using the role-based controls described above.

### Control CM-05(01)

> (a) Enforce access restrictions using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]; and (b) Automatically generate audit records of the enforcement actions.

**SCF Controls:** CHG-04.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple mechanisms for performing after-the-fact reviews of configuration change logs to identify unauthorized modifications. These capabilities span VMware vCenter, NSX, VCF Operations, VCF Operations for Networks, and the VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) consumption layer, giving administrators visibility into configuration changes across the full platform.

VCF Operations audit logging tracks each configuration change to the authenticated user who initiated the change or scheduled the job that performed it. This allows data center administrators to determine the principal identifier of the user who modified an object, including the specific time the change occurred. Audit logs capture administrative actions carried out through the API, the user interface, and the CLI, including regular CRUD operations and login/logout events. VCF Operations categorizes audit events across multiple domains, including Access, Access_control, Account_management, Configuration, Data_access, Network, Permissions, Session, System, Policy, and Firewall. This categorization allows reviewers to filter and focus on configuration-related changes specifically. vCenter also writes audit events to the VCF SSO audit log for changes to security configuration parameters, security policies, and user roles or permissions, providing an additional log trail for identity and access-related configuration changes.

NSX Manager maintains audit logs that provide detailed change tracking, recording both old and new values for modified configurations. When security policies or other NSX-managed objects are changed, the audit trail captures the username, module name, operation type, operation status, resource type, display name, path, and unique identifier. This level of detail enables reviewers to reconstruct exactly what changed, who changed it, and what the previous state was, which is the foundation for identifying unauthorized modifications. For gateway and domain resource modifications, NSX policy operations logging provides additional traceability through request identifiers.

VCF Operations includes a Configuration Drift dashboard that allows administrators to view and compare configuration drifts and identify settings that have changed from their expected baseline. The vSphere Daily Check Dashboard complements this by covering configuration changes and verifying that settings match authorized changes executed during the change window. It also monitors dynamic changes including VM state, location, and inventory changes, and covers compliance status for the past 12 to 24 hours. Together, these dashboards provide both broad drift detection and targeted verification against approved change records. Administrators can also configure alarms within vSphere Configuration Profiles that activate in response to configuration drift events and deliver notifications via email or SNMP traps, supporting timely detection when host configurations deviate from their approved baseline.

Event auditing within VCF Operations provides visibility into platform interaction changes across the infrastructure and surfaces suspicious access events and policy violations. VCF Operations also provides user permissions audit reports that display the access group associated with each local user and the access privileges granted. These reports, along with user activity audit logs and system audit reports, can be downloaded in PDF or XLS format for offline review. User activity logs can be forwarded to an external syslog server, enabling integration with SIEM platforms or other log management tools where after-the-fact analysis and correlation can be performed at scale.

VCF Operations for Networks captures audit logs from API, user interface, and CLI interactions, providing visibility into network configuration changes regardless of the interface used to make them. Audit log access on the Settings page is controlled by user role, so that review capabilities align with administrative responsibilities.

At the workload consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) provides audit logging through the Kubernetes API server's built-in subsystem, configured through the audit.k8s.io/v1 API. Kubernetes auditing produces a security-relevant, chronological set of records documenting actions taken by users, applications using the Kubernetes API, and the control plane itself. Audit policies are configured through PolicyRule definitions that map requests based on metadata to audit levels, giving administrators precise control over which API requests are recorded and at what level of detail. Each audit record can include authorization annotations that capture the authorization decision (authorization.k8s.io/decision) and the reasoning behind it (authorization.k8s.io/reason), providing the context needed to determine whether a configuration change was properly authorized. Audit logs can be written to log files on the API server host or forwarded to a webhook endpoint, supporting integration with centralized log management and SIEM platforms.

Pod Security Standards provide a complementary audit mechanism at the namespace level within VKS clusters. The pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit label enables audit mode, which logs policy violations to the audit log without blocking pod creation. This allows administrators to review deviations from security baselines as configuration changes are introduced, without disrupting running workloads. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy resources support auditAnnotations that produce audit annotations for API requests, extending audit visibility to custom admission policies. The VKS system also generates an Incompatibility insight on the Policy Insights page when a pre-existing Gatekeeper installation is detected that was not deployed through cluster management, flagging the conflict for administrative review.

For environments using GitOps practices, the Argo CD Supervisor Service maintains a complete history of all changes in Git, providing a durable, reviewable record of every declared configuration change. Kubernetes documentation recommends storing object configurations in source control to enable change review processes and audit trails, and notes that combining immutable infrastructure with version-controlled configuration creates an audit log of every authorized change to a system. Imperative kubectl commands applied directly to the cluster do not produce an equivalent trail, so environments that mix imperative and declarative management must rely on the Kubernetes API server audit log as the authoritative record for all changes regardless of how they were applied.

After-the-fact review of configuration change logs to discover unauthorized changes ultimately requires organizational processes that use the audit data VCF, vSphere, and VKS generate. The platform provides the raw audit records and supporting tooling; identifying and investigating unauthorized changes within that data requires log analysis tooling, alerting rules, and operational procedures that the organization must configure and maintain.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) provides several mechanisms that support after-the-fact review of security configuration changes within its own domain.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) implements role-based access control with five defined roles, including a dedicated Auditor role that grants read-only access to view information and track activity without the ability to make changes. SSP also includes a built-in local Audit account, configured during deployment, that has full access to APIs and services for system auditing. This separation of review from administrative functions allows designated personnel to examine platform configuration state without the ability to alter it.

The vDefend Threat Intelligence Service Portal includes a Bundle Change Log where users can review updates and modifications made to application IDs and IDS signatures over time, providing an after-the-fact record of changes to threat intelligence content.

The vDefend distributed firewall (DFW) supports per-rule and per-group logging configurations, including a Monitoring Mode that logs all traffic to an application group to enable audit of permitted connectivity. When Security Intelligence policy recommendations are published, logging is activated by default. DFW rules can also be configured to enable logging through Advanced Settings, including in VPC contexts. IDS/IPS event logs are accessible for troubleshooting and compliance review of intrusion detection and prevention activities. The Identity Firewall event log sources can be integrated with VCF Log Management for centralized log management.

Configuration change review extending beyond vDefend's own firewall and security policy domain requires an organizational SIEM or change management platform.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) generates structured audit events for configuration changes within the disaster recovery environment, providing the data needed for after-the-fact review of DR-related modifications.

The PNR appliance includes an auditd service that writes audit records to disk; this service is stopped by default and must be manually started to activate persistent audit record capture. vSphere Replication maintains a dedicated audit log at /opt/vmware/support/logs/hms/hms-audit.log that captures authorization error messages with timestamps and component identifiers, certificate verification errors, and authorization failures for remote site manager operations, recording the method name, fault type, object reference, and privilege ID for each failure.

PNR records configuration change events across its subsystems. When a user edits Array Manager properties, PNR logs a reconfiguration event. Consistency group additions to protection groups are recorded as Info-level events, and error conditions on protected consistency groups are captured at Error level. Virtual machine protection reconfiguration events are posted to both VMware vCenters upon completion of the Commit phase, and Automatic Protection operations generate a VmAutoprotectEvent entry. These events document changes to the DR topology and protection scope.

PNR captures audit events that record user actions, including configuration changes and recovery plan executions. Each event includes the event name, user identity, and source IP address from which the action originated. The Clean Room Orchestrator, used for isolated recovery and ransomware recovery workflows, generates events within this same audit framework. PNR provides a REST API to update configured syslog servers, enabling audit data to be forwarded to external log management systems for centralized analysis and correlation.

The logManager.RecoveryConfig setting, defaulting to verbose, controls the logging level for recovery configuration operations.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides a set of audit logging capabilities that support after-the-fact reviews of configuration changes. The primary mechanism is the Gateway Audit Logs feature, accessible from the DSM UI and the DSM Gateway Kubernetes interface. These logs record user read and write operations performed against DSM data services and infrastructure objects, with each entry including a Source column that identifies the initiating entity, either a user's email address or "System" when DSM initiates the action. Gateway audit log files contain standard Kubernetes audit events and are stored on the Provider VM at /var/log/tdm/provider/audit/ with filenames in the format gateway-audit-timestamp.log.

For platform-level configuration activity, the System Audit Events view in the DSM UI is the designated location for tracking nondatabase-related operations and tasks. Administrators can review these events to identify configuration changes that fall outside expected patterns.

DSM gateway audit log files are retained on the Provider VM for five days and are removed from local storage after being successfully uploaded to the configured S3 repository. When a default S3 provider log repository is configured, DSM periodically archives those log files to an S3 subpath named "gateway audit", extending the effective retention window. Audit events can be exported in CSV or XLSX formats to support offline review, reporting, or integration with analysis tools. DSM also supports log forwarding, configured under Managing Settings in the UI, which allows organizations to route logs from the provider VM and provisioned databases to a centralized aggregation and analysis platform for broader review.

At the database level, PostgreSQL instances deployed through DSM include the pgaudit extension, which provides audit logging of database operations across supported versions. The Operations tab in the database UI allows monitoring of the progress of database configuration changes.

DSM adds SQL Server as a supported database engine with dedicated audit logging capabilities. SQL Server clusters include an Enable Auditing setting to select the level of audit logging for system logins and to set the log retention period. Login Auditing can be enabled to automatically create an audit for login events. Administrators can also define custom audit configurations using the standard T-SQL CREATE AUDIT and CREATE AUDIT SPECIFICATION statements. Preconfigured server auditing presets for login tracking are available for deployments that do not require custom audit definitions.

DSM does not automate analysis of audit logs or pattern detection for unauthorized changes. The audit data, export options, and log forwarding capabilities support organizational review processes or external analysis tooling.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Avi Load Balancer provides built-in mechanisms for after-the-fact review of configuration changes through its Config Audit Trail, accessible at Operations > Events > Config Audit Trail in the Controller UI. This view presents a filtered audit trail of user activity events and changes to system configuration over a selectable time period. Each entry records the configuration object that was modified, the tenant affected, the timestamp of the change, and the user account that performed the update.

For more granular investigation, Avi Event Logs (accessible via Operations > Events) can be filtered by event type using search parameters such as event_id=CONFIG_CREATE or event_id=CONFIG_UPDATE to identify configuration changes. When a specific configuration event is selected, the Event Log displays the exact API fields that were changed, giving reviewers a detailed record of what was altered and by whom. CONFIG_CREATE and CONFIG_DELETE events include client_IP and user_agent fields, which allow reviewers to determine what client or automation tool initiated the change. The Avi documentation recommends reviewing event logs over a period of one year to maintain complete audit trails of configuration changes.

The Config Events overlay within the Service Engine analytics view allows administrators to correlate traffic changes with configuration modifications, helping identify whether a change in traffic behavior was caused by a specific configuration update such as modifications to virtual service security settings.

Configuration change events can be forwarded to external log management or SIEM platforms using Avi's syslog alert notification types: SYSLOG_JSON, SYSLOG_RFC5425_ENHANCED, and SYSLOG_LEGACY. Each notification type carries the event type (CONFIG_UPDATE), affected object name, tenant name, update status, timestamp, and the user account that performed the change, allowing the audit trail data to be ingested into centralized logging infrastructure for retention, correlation, and automated review workflows.

Avi Load Balancer includes a dedicated Audit local user account in the License Hub with the auditor role, providing full access to APIs and services for system auditing. The Admin user can reset the Audit account's password. This dedicated auditor role supports access-controlled review of configuration audit data by personnel who do not require administrative privileges.

For Web Application Firewall configuration, the Application Security Report (accessible via Operations > Reports) provides a downloadable PDF report documenting security findings and pre-check results that can be used to detect unauthorized modifications to WAF policy configuration.

After-the-fact review of these logs to identify unauthorized changes is an organizational process. Avi provides the log data, the filtering tools, the forwarding mechanisms, and a dedicated auditor account; the review cadence, alert thresholds, and escalation procedures must be defined and operated by the deploying organization.

### Control CM-06

> a. Establish and document configuration settings for components employed within the system that reflect the most restrictive mode consistent with operational requirements using [Assignment: organization-defined common secure configurations]; b. Implement the configuration settings; c. Identify, document, and approve any deviations from established configuration settings for [Assignment: organization-defined system components] based on [Assignment: organization- defined operational requirements]; and d. Monitor and control changes to the configuration settings in accordance with organizational policies and procedures.

**SCF Controls:** CFG-02, CFG-02.7

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides secure baseline configurations for all major platform components, consistent with industry-accepted hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline is a purpose-built document that builds on VCF security fundamentals to address frequently requested compliance standards, regulations, and frameworks. It is designed and validated to tailor security configurations without affecting VCF's ability to meet its design objectives. Organizations can use the VCF Security Baseline to evaluate both default and non-default configurations and to secure their environments in a compliance context.

VCF Operations includes a consolidated out-of-the-box benchmark called the VCF 9 Security Baseline, which provides a ready-to-use compliance profile for evaluating the security posture of a VCF environment. This benchmark enables automated assessment of configurations against the documented baseline, helping maintain a consistent configuration across VCF components. ISO Security Standards compliance benchmarks are also available from the VCF Operations repository as a native pack, giving organizations the ability to measure their environment against internationally recognized hardening standards. Broadcom also provides DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) for VCF components, including vSphere 7.0 and vSphere 8.0 STIGs, which provide detailed instructions for securing and configuring systems to meet specific security requirements.

Individual VCF components have their own security configuration guides that document hardening recommendations. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide provides best practices for securing VMware vCenter, including securing the installation, configuring access and authentication, defining roles and permissions, and enabling auditing and logging. VMware ESX host hardening covers authentication and authorization settings, disabling unnecessary services, enabling security features such as Secure Boot and Trusted Platform Module (TPM), and implementing network security measures. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide documents specific STIG-aligned values for the Security.PasswordQualityControlPwquality setting on ESX hosts, including parameters such as minlen=15, maxrepeat=3, maxclassrepeat=4, difok=4, and enforce_for_root. The NSX Security Configuration Guide provides recommendations for securing NSX components, including NSX nodes, NSX Controllers, and NSX Edge nodes, by configuring strong authentication, implementing role-based access control, and securing administrative access.

VCF Operations also provides a Configure Host Security Config Data workflow that allows selective enforcement of host security hardening rules. Beyond security settings, vSphere Lifecycle Manager maintains a desired-state cluster image that defines the ESX base build, vendor add-ons, and firmware for every host in a cluster, so the software baseline stays consistent across hosts, and VCF Operations runs scheduled drift detection that flags configuration deviations from the desired state for remediation. Administrators can configure individual configuration elements under Enforce Options, enabling granular control over which baseline settings are applied and enforced across the environment. This supports reducing organizational and business risk by maintaining configuration consistency. vSphere Host Profiles provide an additional mechanism for automated and centrally managed host configuration baseline enforcement, capturing a reference host's configuration and applying it consistently across ESX hosts, covering memory, storage, networking, and other configuration aspects.

For containerized workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) includes preconfigured security policy templates. The Baseline security template provides a set of constraints that restrict known privilege escalations while maintaining compatibility with common containerized workloads.

DISA STIGs for Supervisor and VKrs are available to guide hardening of the Supervisor control plane and individual VKS cluster releases, providing baseline security guidance at the same standard as the DISA STIGs applied to VCF infrastructure components. The VKS cluster management interface provides out-of-the-box security policy templates that mirror industry-standard Pod Security Standards profiles, including both Baseline and Restricted configurations. Custom VKS Cluster Management Security Policy templates are also available to fine-tune individual security context parameters and support governance controls tailored to specific organizational requirements.

Pod Security Admission (PSA), enabled in VKr 1.25 and later, enforces pod security standards at the namespace level. Three policy profiles are defined: Privileged (unrestricted, for system-level workloads), Baseline (helps prevent known privilege escalations while allowing the default minimally specified pod configuration), and Restricted (heavily restricted, following pod hardening best practices). Three enforcement modes are available: enforce, warn, and audit. The podSecurityStandard field in the ClusterClass API encodes the intended security posture in cluster definitions, accepting these modes with specific policy versions and namespace exemptions so that baseline security configurations are applied consistently across VKS deployments. System namespaces including kube-system, tkg-system, and vmware-system-cloudprovider are excluded from pod security enforcement. The PSA admission controller can also be statically configured with default enforce, audit, and warn levels for unlabeled namespaces, so namespaces created without explicit labels still receive a defined security baseline. To support gradual policy adoption, audit and warn modes can be set to a stricter level such as restricted while enforce remains at baseline, giving administrators visibility into policy violations before full enforcement is applied. VCF Automation IaaS Resource Policies provide an additional enforcement point at the management layer, supporting requirements that Kubernetes clusters operate at a minimum baseline pod security level.

Kubernetes-native kernel security mechanisms provide workload-level baseline hardening options. seccomp restricts the Linux kernel system call surface available inside containers using BPF-based profiles, and the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile provides a strong set of security defaults while preserving workload functionality. AppArmor and SELinux provide further kernel-level restrictions through default configurations that offer foundational protections. Kubernetes default RBAC role bindings should be reviewed as part of establishing a security baseline, as defaults may grant broader access than a hardened configuration requires. Isolating each workload in its own Kubernetes namespace allows tailored security policies to be applied per workload and restricts access to namespace-scoped resources such as ConfigMaps and Secrets.

Harbor Registry, available as a standard package for VKS clusters, supports a controlled baseline for container artifacts entering the environment. Harbor provides vulnerability scanning, content trust, and policy-based replication, along with role-based access control and artifact security policies, supporting controlled management of the container image supply chain. For stand-alone virtual machines deployed in vSphere Supervisor environments, Content Libraries can distribute VM images created with current patches and required security settings that follow corporate security policies.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) provides PAIS-specific declarative configuration mechanisms that organizations can use to define and apply baseline settings across the AI infrastructure stack on VCF.

The primary mechanism is the PAISConfiguration custom resource. Private AI Services settings are declared in a YAML file compliant with the PAISConfiguration schema and applied with kubectl, giving administrators a single resource that captures observability, identity, and trust configuration for an organization namespace. Observability includes the spec.observability.prometheusRuntime section for Prometheus metrics collection with a configurable storage class and metrics retention period, and an llmTraces section for routing large language model traces to an OpenTelemetry Collector. The PAISConfiguration custom resource can be patched with kubectl patch and verified with kubectl get paisconfiguration, supporting baseline change control and drift detection at the AI service layer.

Certificate trust is handled through the spec.clientTls.caBundleRefs section, which references ConfigMaps containing CA trust bundles. Private AI Services requires HTTPS trust bundles for the OIDC provider, the Harbor registry, content management systems hosting knowledge database data, and MCP servers that provide tools to models. Trust bundles can be organized per application, with periodic updates required before certificate expiration. Access scope is restricted through the authorizedGroups setting, which limits Private AI Services access to specified OIDC groups.

Harbor registry distribution carries baseline controls for model and image artifacts. Replication rules support configurable filters, target namespace, and trigger mode, including a manual trigger mode, so administrators can align artifact replication with organizational policy for connected and disconnected sites. The Model Gallery uses Harbor project access capabilities to restrict access to sensitive training and tuning data. Air-gapped deployments capture the self-hosted registry URL and registry login credentials as part of the activation flow.

Workload resource baselines are defined through SupervisorNamespaceClassConfig, which specifies per-zone CPU limits, CPU reservations, memory limits, and memory reservations for resource allocation across zones. GPU-capable VM classes can carry GPU reservations and be confined to a single zone or supervisor namespace, supporting isolation of GPU consumption to a specific tenant. A default storage policy selection in the Private AI Services quickstart defines storage placement and resources for the service database.

Deep Learning VM (DL VM) baselines are captured through VCF Automation self-service catalog items that parameterize VM class, data disk size, user password, and optional SSH public key authentication for remote access. The DL VM OVF property config-json accepts a base64-encoded configuration file that controls DCGM Exporter metrics visibility through export_dcgm_to_public and JupyterLab authentication through enable_jupyter_auth, giving operators a consistent surface to define DL VM hardening parameters.

Credentials supplied during Private AI Services activation, including the private container registry password and proxy password, are stored as secrets in VCF Automation rather than as plaintext configuration values, supporting credential hygiene as part of the deployment baseline.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to secure baseline configurations within its network security domain by providing structured configuration management capabilities, predefined security profiles and strategies, and configuration integrity controls that align with industry-accepted hardening practices.
