When VKS clusters span multiple availability zones, Kubernetes distributes workloads across zones so that a zone-level failure affects only a subset of running pods; administrators configure topology spread constraints and pod anti-affinity rules to align workload distribution with their RTO targets for individual services.

For backup and restore of containerized workloads, VKS Cluster Management provides data protection capabilities through Velero and native management tooling. Velero backup schedules can be configured to run at defined intervals, allowing organizations to align backup frequency with RPO targets. Restore operations support file system-based backup by default, with optional CSI volume snapshot restoration; restore scope can be specified to select which Kubernetes resources to recover from a backup. Cross-cluster restore allows a backup from one VKS cluster to be applied to a different cluster, accessible through the Data Protection tab in VKS Cluster Management or through the VCF Automation Tenant Portal. VKS Backup and Restore Hooks allow execution of pre- and post-operation scripts to achieve application-consistent backups and automate steps during recovery. Restore progress is tracked through the Data Protection tab, and the CloneFromSnapshot custom resource exposes a status.phase field that reports progress through each phase of a volume restore operation. The Velero backupRepository.config.fullMaintenanceInterval parameter controls repository maintenance behavior. ControllerRevision provides immutable snapshots of state data for DaemonSet and StatefulSet controllers, supporting rollback to a prior configuration. Third-party backup tools can capture point-in-time volume snapshots through the Kubernetes CSI snapshot API, and periodic snapshots of API server backing storage provide a recovery path for storage loss scenarios.

VCF Operations monitors infrastructure health and reports capacity and availability metrics through dashboards, including capacity remaining widgets and capacity summary views across all monitored environments. These give operations teams visibility into whether the environment is meeting its availability targets. Organizations should plan and test their recovery strategies regularly to confirm that actual recovery performance aligns with their stated objectives.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to secure recovery and reconstitution through backup and restore capabilities for its own security infrastructure, and through disaster recovery features that maintain security policy continuity across site failover events.

The Security Services Platform provides backup and restore functionality, accessible through the System > Backup and Restore interface, that allows recovery of a platform instance to its previous configuration when the current instance is in a non-recoverable state. The backup scope includes the full system inventory: tokens, Bare Metal servers, and Bare Metal server interfaces are captured as part of inventory backup, and required certificates from the backup server are restored automatically during the restore process. The platform must be in a healthy state to perform backup operations; backup and restore cannot proceed when the platform is unhealthy. Certificate consistency between backup and restored Security Services Platform environments must be maintained to avoid NDR Sensor disruptions. Backup and restore operations enter a failed state after 30 minutes if internal services are restarted manually or due to failure. Administrators should back up the Security Services Platform before performing scale-out operations, and Bare Metal Service operations (onboarding, offboarding, or upgrading Bare Metal servers) should not be performed while backup or restore is in progress.

The Security Services Platform can be deployed on vSAN stretched configurations, which supports recovery from site failures and reduces recovery time at the infrastructure level.

For disaster recovery scenarios, NSX Federation maintains security posture during workload migration and site failover by propagating Distributed Firewall rules, security groups, and microsegmentation policies across multiple sites. When workloads fail over from a protected site to a recovery site, the vDefend security policies governing east-west traffic at the destination site are already in place. vSphere HA can be configured to handle host-level failures affecting Security Services Platform appliances.

The broader recovery of compute infrastructure, storage, virtual machines, and application workloads to a known state is handled by other VCF components such as Protection and Recovery, vSAN Data Protection, vSphere Replication, and vCenter backup and restore.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Meets)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides mechanisms for secure recovery and reconstitution of virtual machine workloads to a known state after a disruption, compromise, or failure.

Recovery is organized through protection groups and recovery plans. Protection groups define which virtual machines are protected, while recovery plans orchestrate the sequence of steps required to recover those workloads at the recovery site. The state of a recovery plan is determined by the states of the protection groups within it, and the plan state governs which operations are permitted at each stage of the DR lifecycle. Recovery plans support two primary modes: planned migration for orderly, scheduled failover, and disaster recovery for unplanned site failures. A single recovery plan can also be configured to serve both disaster recovery and cyber recovery operations, allowing organizations to maintain consistent recovery procedures across different incident types.

Recovery plan testing is a built-in capability. Recovery plans should be tested before use for planned migration or disaster recovery to validate their functionality. This confirms that the recovery process will bring protected virtual machines online at the recovery site and that inventory mappings correctly place workloads on the appropriate networks, storage, and compute resources.

Cyber Recovery extends reconstitution capabilities with an Isolated Recovery Environment (IRE) designed for compromise scenarios such as ransomware incidents. The IRE provides a dedicated, isolated segment of the recovery environment that blocks direct private network connections from production, allowing compromised workloads to be analyzed and validated before being returned to production. Cyber Recovery transitions virtual machines through a controlled sequence of states, including Entering validated, Validated, Staging, and Staged, to prepare them for recovery. This state-based workflow supports data integrity validation prior to production restoration.

Recovery plan compliance checks verify that protection groups are actively replicating, that sufficient point-in-time snapshots are available for each virtual machine in the plan, and that all workflow steps can execute without conflict. Continuous compliance monitoring provides warnings when VM configuration status deviates from expected state.

The Configuration Import/Export Tool enables administrators to export PNR server configuration data, including inventory mappings, placeholder datastores, advanced settings, array manager configurations, and protection group definitions. This capability supports configuration backup and reconstitution of the PNR environment itself after a failure or migration.

PNR integrates with VCF Operations for recovery plan lifecycle visibility. The Recovery Plans Details dashboard shows the current phase of the DR lifecycle, including whether a plan is ready, whether a test is in progress, and whether incomplete recovery or cleanup operations require attention. PNR also supports recovery of VCF Automation and VCF Operations services: when a disaster event affects the management domain containing these services, PNR enables service recovery in another management domain.

All communications between PNR and VMware vCenter instances use TLS certificates and private keys. The PNR Appliance supports importing server certificates signed by a certificate authority to establish secure communications across both protected and recovery sites.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides technical mechanisms that support an organization's ability to meet defined Recovery Time Objectives and Recovery Point Objectives for database workloads.

Point-in-time recovery. DSM supports point-in-time recovery (PITR) for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server databases, allowing administrators to restore data to a specific moment within the configured backup retention period. When initiating a restore, administrators identify a restore point and choose either the most recent timestamp or a user-specified date and time, and the restored database is created as a new database instance. The granularity of recovery is bounded by the configured backup schedule and retention settings, and PITR can only restore data from within the configured retention period. PITR requires the source database to be operational, the required backup to be available, and connectivity to the S3 storage repository. SQL Server PITR can restore onto the same or a different SQL Server cluster. PostgreSQL restore can also target a different DSM control plane by specifying a time value; if the time field is omitted, DSM restores the latest available state. For SQL Server, DSM automatically manages the recovery model based on backup status, using Full Recovery Model when automated backups are enabled and Simple Recovery Model otherwise. PITR may fail or be incomplete if binary log files are missing from the source database, which administrators should account for in operational procedures.

Log backup frequency and RPO. DSM exposes a Log Backup Frequency setting in Advanced Settings with a maximum value of 30 minutes. The log backup frequency is directly related to the achievable RPO for both PostgreSQL and SQL Server databases, and administrators should configure this value based on their RPO requirements.

Database cloning. DSM can clone a MySQL or PostgreSQL database to create an identical database. Cloning is functionally equivalent to PITR to the latest available time and can act as a recovery mechanism when the most recent consistent state is the desired recovery target.

Provider VM recovery and restore duration. DSM documents a recovery procedure for a single Provider VM failure, and the Provider VM restoration must complete fully before proceeding to subsequent recovery steps. This is a relevant consideration for control plane RTO planning. Database restoration duration depends on data volume and underlying infrastructure performance, and administrators should account for this when planning restore operations against an RTO target.

Defining and formally documenting RTO and RPO targets is an organizational process outside DSM's scope. DSM provides the backup, restore, and cloning mechanisms that technical teams configure and test against those targets. Organizations must establish their RTO and RPO requirements, configure DSM backup policies accordingly, test recovery procedures, and verify operational results align with those targets.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides application-layer recovery and reconstitution mechanisms at the load balancing and application delivery tier.

The Avi Controller cluster can be deployed across two availability zones, with defined failure scenarios and documented recovery actions for each zone-loss case. Availability zones should be configured based on fault domains associated with shared power supplies, shared networking devices, or other common infrastructure to reduce the risk of virtual service unavailability. When the cluster enters manual recovery state following a failure or compromise, Avi automatically revokes all Service Engine secure channel credentials and causes Service Engines to reboot themselves, halting all application traffic delivery. This returns the data plane to a controlled, known state. Service Engines that subsequently re-establish connectivity to a recovered Controller node reconfigure themselves automatically to connect only to that node.

If all three Controller cluster nodes are permanently unavailable, recovery must be performed using a backup and restore procedure. When a Controller node fails but is not permanently lost, it can be recovered by bringing the existing node back online, which is transparent to existing data traffic, or by rebuilding a new Controller node, which is disruptive to existing connections. Factory reset recovery is irreversible and deletes all Controller configuration, requiring the database to be recreated from the configuration archive. When a Service Engine is compromised or rebooted independently, all configurations including private keys and public certificates are wiped from the Service Engine, providing a clean starting state for reconstitution.

Elastic High Availability (Elastic HA) provides fast recovery for individual virtual services following the failure of a Service Engine, redistributing workloads to surviving Service Engines without requiring a full cluster-level recovery event. In vSphere DRS-enabled clusters, virtual service recovery occurs before vSphere HA brings up a replacement Service Engine when virtual service slots are available in other Service Engines, so application services are restored in a defined sequence.

Avi GSLB (Global Server Load Balancing) supports an Active disaster recovery deployment mode, enabling global-level recovery and reconstitution for applications served across multiple sites.

Avi provides configuration backup and restore capabilities for the Controller. A remote backup destination should be specified to protect against non-recoverable Controller cluster failures. The Controller checks and obtains user consent prior to performing a restore operation. After a storage failure requiring a full restore, the Controller is restored from the configuration archive. The restore process follows a defined sequence: cloud discovery, post-restore validation, configuration unblocking, and placement resumption. Post-restore validation verifies the count of restored configuration objects, including clouds and Service Engine groups, confirming that the reconstituted state matches the backup. Restore operations report state information including the operation type, image version, and cluster node name. The Controller logs restore completion events with timestamps, node IP addresses, task status, and success or failure messages for audit trail purposes.

Avi's recovery mechanisms address the application delivery tier. VM-level backup and restore for the Controller and Service Engine VMs, storage-level recovery, and infrastructure reconstitution depend on the underlying VCF platform. See VCF Coverage for those mechanisms.

### Control CP-10(02)

> Implement transaction recovery for systems that are transaction-based.

**SCF Controls:** BCD-12.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides backup, snapshot, and recovery mechanisms at both the management plane and workload layers that contribute to transaction recovery in accordance with Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs). These capabilities supply the infrastructure-layer recovery functions that underpin an organization's broader transaction recovery strategy, though application-level transaction recovery requires additional tooling and organizational processes.

At the VMware vCenter layer, a transaction manager loads workflow journals from the database and flags corresponding managed entities as in recovery, preventing those entities from participating in conflicting operations while recovery is in progress. Recovery operations reconcile interrupted transactions and publish any issues encountered through events and tasks. After a file-based restore, a reconciliation job can be created and monitored through the vCenter appliance recovery API to verify that the restored state is consistent. This journal-based mechanism provides transaction-aware recovery behavior within the vCenter management plane, helping maintain consistency of in-flight operations during the restore process.

VCF's management plane components each support file-based or image-based backup methods. vCenter file-based daily backups are configured through the vCenter Management Interface of each vCenter instance. File-based backups are written to an external server (vCenter does not retain backup files locally) and support FTP, FTPS, HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP, NFS, or SMB as the backup transport; backup files can optionally be encrypted with a password that must be provided during the restore procedure. VKS Supervisor backup and restore is also activated through the vCenter backup feature available in the vCenter Management Interface, with VKS cluster node VMs backed up and restored through Supervisor state backup. VCF Operations configures the NSX Manager backup during the bring-up process, while file-based backup jobs for VCF Operations and vCenter must be configured separately. VCF Operations for Networks and VCF Identity Broker also support backup and restore of their respective deployment data, and VCF Identity Broker Appliance Cluster supports on-demand backup and restore operations using SFTP. Scheduled backups should be implemented to prepare for critical failure of a management component, an upgrade of a management component, or a certificate update of a management component.

A key consideration for transaction-based recovery is logical consistency across components. VCF's backup guidance calls for creating a series of backup jobs that capture the state of a set of related components at a common point in time. Simultaneous backups of component nodes help restore the component to a state where the nodes are logically consistent with each other, reducing the need for further logical integrity remediation after restore. This coordinated approach to backup timing is directly relevant to achieving transaction-consistent recovery points.

To restore a vCenter instance from file-based backup, a valid file-based backup of the same version as the vCenter appliance is required. VCF Operations must be available before proceeding with the restore of vCenter, and vCenter must be available before proceeding with the restore of VCF Operations. Understanding these dependency relationships is important when planning the recovery sequence for transaction-based workloads that depend on the management plane.

At the workload layer, vSAN Data Protection provides snapshot-based point-in-time recovery with configurable scheduling intervals and retention policies through protection groups. Protection groups contain snapshot policies that define retention duration and snapshot scheduling intervals, with configurable time units for both parameters. vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA) delivers native snapshots that use a B-tree structure instead of a chain structure, making snapshot creation more efficient and snapshot deletion up to 100x faster than previous implementations. Because ESA native snapshots are faster, they allow more frequent backups and faster recovery, supporting tighter RPO targets for transaction-based workloads.

Changed Block Tracking (CBT) tracks modified disk blocks to enable incremental backups through VADP-integrated solutions. By capturing only changed blocks rather than full disk images, CBT reduces backup windows for transaction-heavy workloads and supports more aggressive RPO targets. VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) integrate with third-party backup solutions that can automatically perform backup, full restore, and registration of VMs, including application-aware backup capabilities for transaction-based applications. Quiesced snapshots, which pause application I/O and flush in-memory data via VMware Tools before the snapshot is captured, produce application-consistent recovery points appropriate for transaction-based workloads and are the recommended snapshot type for automated, periodic VADP backups.

For workloads deployed on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), VCF provides a layered backup and restore framework managed through the VCF Automation Tenant Portal. VKS cluster backup and restore operations are accessed under Manage & Govern > VCF Services > Kubernetes Management and support backup and restore of an entire cluster, selected namespaces, or specific resources identified by a label. Backups can also be restored to a different cluster using the restore-from-another-cluster function in the Data Protection tab of VKS Cluster Management. At the workload level, VKS supports three backup methods via Velero: vSphere Plugin Snapshot, which performs block-level backup written directly to object storage and is faster for workloads with large numbers of small files; CSI Snapshot, which provides volume-level atomic-consistent backups with standard CSI driver permissions; and File System Backup, which provides file-level crash-consistent backups. CSI Snapshot and File System Backup both support file system-level incremental backup, where files unchanged since the last backup are not re-backed up, and both support deduplication, compression, and encryption of backup data, enabling shorter backup windows consistent with tighter RPO targets. CSI-compliant backups are recommended for production databases and high-IOPS applications; File System Backup is appropriate for legacy volumes with non-CSI storage. Third-party backup software for VKS clusters integrates directly via CSI snapshot functionality for volume backup and restore operations. Beyond the Velero-based workflow, VKS workloads can also recover individual persistent volumes through the native Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot API. VolumeSnapshot objects reference a PersistentVolumeClaim as their source and a VolumeSnapshotClass that defines the snapshot behavior and deletion policy. A new PersistentVolumeClaim can be provisioned directly from a VolumeSnapshot by specifying the snapshot as the dataSource, enabling point-in-time recovery of individual volumes without orchestrating a full cluster or namespace restore. On Supervisor clusters, this provisioning path creates a volume bound to a backing virtual disk that satisfies the new PVC's storage requirements. VKS Backup and Restore Hooks allow the execution of pre- and post-operation scripts that can quiesce applications and flush in-flight data before a snapshot is captured, providing application-consistent recovery points for transaction-based workloads running on VKS. The Velero Plugin for vSphere can back up and restore both stateless and stateful applications running on vSphere Pods in the Supervisor.

For Kubernetes cluster control-plane state, etcd supports built-in snapshot backup through the etcdctl snapshot save command, specifying the endpoint, CA certificate, client certificate, and client key. An etcd cluster can be restored from either a previous snapshot file or a remaining data directory. Kubernetes documentation notes that clusters should have a dedicated backup plan for etcd data; StatefulSet revision history is not a substitute for dedicated backup tooling.

For workload-level disaster recovery, VCF supports Application Disaster Recovery with data replication, which replicates data to a secondary site and provides recovery options in the event of catastrophic failure. Organizations should be aware that Application Disaster Recovery may result in some data loss during failover depending on the RPO. The documentation recommends that organizations plan and test disaster recovery strategies regularly to meet both recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO). Test recovery should be performed to validate that recovery plans work as expected.

VCF directly provides transaction recovery mechanisms through vCenter's journal-based recovery, snapshot-based point-in-time recovery with configurable RPO scheduling, incremental backup capabilities through CBT and VADP, coordinated management component backups, VKS Supervisor state backup through the vCenter Management Interface, and a Velero-based workload backup framework with pre/post hooks for application consistency. Application-level transaction recovery for workloads running on VCF requires third-party backup solutions with application-aware capabilities and organizational processes for backup scheduling, testing, and RPO/RTO planning.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides specialized replication mechanisms with configurable recovery point objectives (RPOs) that support transaction recovery for protected workloads.

PNR replication classes are preconfigured with an RPO that defines the acceptable amount of data loss after a disaster, allowing administrators to match each workload's protection profile to its transaction sensitivity. vSphere Replication allows configuration at the individual virtual machine level, aligning replication frequency with the data currency requirements of each protected application. Array-based replication, managed through Storage Replication Adapters (SRA) installed on the PNR server, provides an additional protection path at the datastore level and can be deployed on both protected and recovery sites. Virtual Volumes protection groups extend these RPO-aligned capabilities to policy-driven storage environments. vSAN snapshots and replication offer a native, integrated snapshot-based protection mechanism for virtual machines running on vSAN datastores, managed through the vSphere Client. These multiple replication approaches allow organizations to align the recovery mechanism to the transaction characteristics of each workload.

Recovery plans orchestrate the recovery process through sequenced steps. PNR supports two types of custom recovery steps: command recovery steps and message prompt steps. Command recovery steps can execute scripts on recovered virtual machines, enabling application-specific transaction recovery operations such as database consistency checks or transaction log replay as part of the automated recovery workflow. Understanding recovery steps, their order, and the context in which they run is important when customizing a recovery plan for transaction-aware recovery.

Recovery plans support both planned migration and disaster recovery execution types. During disaster recovery, the PNR server first attempts a storage synchronization, and if it succeeds, uses the synchronized storage state to recover virtual machines on the recovery site to their most recent available state according to the configured RPO. The reprotect process reverses the direction of protection and restores bidirectional replication, maintaining recovery capability after a failover event. PNR reprotect operations support array-based replication protection groups, Virtual Volumes protection groups, and standard protection groups. PNR also supports concurrent recoveries from multiple sites to a shared recovery site.

PNR records recovery plan execution details including recovery plan name, status, folder location, and mode of operation. Completion of the reprotect workflow triggers a notification that includes site name, recovery plan name, recovery type, and execution state, supporting operational awareness of RPO restoration after failover. Recovery plan steps can be exported in various formats for documentation and audit purposes, providing a record of the transaction recovery procedures configured for each protected workload. Integration with VCF Operations through a management pack enables additional visibility into recovery plan metrics and status across the environment.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides automated backup and recovery mechanisms for the database engines it manages, including PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server. These mechanisms help organizations align database recovery capabilities with Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs).

Administrators can enable automated backups for each database, configuring both a backup location and a retention period. Backups can be enabled at database creation time or added afterward through the database settings editor. DSM supports both full and incremental backup types, and a balanced schedule of both is recommended to maintain backup chain integrity. Backup storage is configurable, and the platform supports storing backups on local or S3-compatible cloud storage. Allowed backup locations can be controlled administratively through the allowedBackupLocations parameter, giving operators the ability to restrict where database backups are written. For SQL Server databases, DSM uses integrated Microsoft native backup technologies and automatically selects the recovery model based on backup status: Full Recovery Model is used when automated backups are enabled, and Simple Recovery Model is used otherwise. SQL Server High Availability clusters require automated backups to be enabled in order to provision databases. The Log Backup Frequency advanced setting accepts a maximum value of 30 minutes and is directly related to the achievable RPO for SQL Server databases.

DSM supports point-in-time recovery (PITR) for database restoration. When restoring a database, users specify a custom restore point within the backup retention period or restore to the latest available time. The PITR process creates a new database VM, and the user supplies the database name, datastore location, and management configuration during the restore. PITR can also be initiated through the VCF Automation UI for tenants consuming DSM, restoring a live or archived database to a specific moment within the retention window. For PostgreSQL, the time field in the restore specification selects the PITR target; if omitted, DSM restores the latest available state, and a PostgreSQL cluster can be restored to a different DSM control plane. SQL Server PITR can restore any database with automated backups onto the same or a different SQL Server cluster. A successful PITR restore requires the source database to be operational, the required backup to be available, and connectivity to the S3 storage repository. If a restore operation fails, the database VM must be manually deleted before retrying. Restore task progress can be monitored from the Operations tab or Operations view.

DSM retains automated backups of deleted databases until the configured retention period expires. The retention period can be modified after deletion, and deleted databases and their retained backups are accessible through the Archives tab, from which restore operations can be initiated.

A restore from an incremental backup chain requires the last full backup and all incremental backups following it in the chain. If an incremental backup in the chain is deleted, DSM cannot restore data beyond that point, which makes backup storage management and retention policy configuration important for sustaining RPO alignment over time. For MySQL databases, the most recently committed transactions may be lost in the event of a total cluster failure or unplanned restart, and for SQL Server PITR, restores may fail or be incomplete if binary log files are missing from the source database. Organizations should account for these limitations when establishing RPOs for these workloads.

For Provider VM recovery, DSM requires a Provider Backup Repo to be configured during installation. The restore-provider command with the -c flag and a pgbackrest.conf path restores the DSM Provider VM to the latest timestamp after a terminal failure during non-upgrade operations. Backup-related alert resolution procedures recommend validating the connection and operational status of the backup storage repository, supporting auditability of recovery operations.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CP-10(04)

> Provide the capability to restore system components within [Assignment: organization- defined restoration time periods] from configuration-controlled and integrity-protected information representing a known, operational state for the components.

**SCF Controls:** BCD-12.4

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides backup and restore capabilities across its management components and workload infrastructure that support restoration to a known, operational state. The platform requires that a defined backup and retention policy aligned with best practices or organizational requirements be established before performing backup and restore operations.

VCF's restore architecture covers the core management stack. VCF Operations, VMware vCenter, and NSX Manager each support backup and restore operations, with guidance to create coordinated backup jobs that capture the state of related components at a common point in time. VCF Operations file-based restoration is performed on a newly deployed appliance using an OVA file, providing a clean recovery path from a valid backup of the same version. After a file-based or image-based restore, the vCenter reconciliation API checks whether vCenter partners in embedded linked mode are available, synchronizes vCenter data and services with the partners, and runs vCenter services. Operators should verify that no running or failed reconciliation job exists before initiating a new restore.

Specific retention guidance is documented for each component. vCenter file-based backups are recommended to be performed daily with a seven-day retention period. VCF Operations recommends a minimum three-day backup retention, with a configurable retention policy that specifies how many backups to keep. NSX treats backup and restore operations as specific to each appliance, whether Global Manager or Local Manager, and restoring a backup restores the network state as it existed at the time of the backup. When restoring both Global Manager and Local Manager for NSX, backup timestamps should be selected as close to each other as possible to maintain consistency. HCX Manager and VCF Operations HCX backups must be no older than two days at the time of restoration, as inventory changes beyond that window may cause inconsistencies.

VCF Operations supports backup and restore of custom and out-of-the-box content, including policies, custom groups, super metrics, compliance scorecards, authentication sources, users, roles, and integration accounts. The platform provides CLI-based restore tooling through VCF Operations, with commands supporting actions to add, retrieve, delete, and check status of restore configurations. The restore method follows a defined sequence of operations: retrieving the restore profile, then executing snapshot, start-access, disk restore, and end-access functions in order. VCF Operations for Networks supports backup and restore of deployment data to enable recovery when the existing setup is unrecoverable.

For workload-level recovery, vSAN provides snapshot capabilities with configurable retention and scheduling policies through protection groups. Native snapshots in vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA) are faster than traditional vSphere snapshots, allowing more frequent backups and faster recovery. In ESA, a virtual disk and its snapshots are contained in one vSAN object rather than creating separate objects. vSAN Data Protection uses native snapshots stored locally on an ESA cluster to help recover virtual machines from operational failure or ransomware attacks. vSAN replication of virtual machines protects workloads by providing data backup and recovery in case of a site failure. Virtual machine snapshots support an optional quiesce setting that pauses in-memory processes to capture an application-consistent state, helping restored workloads return to a known, operational condition.

Changed Block Tracking (CBT) tracks modified disk blocks for incremental backups through VADP-integrated solutions, reducing backup windows. VADP-based backup partner solutions can automatically perform backup, full restore, and registration of VM Service VMs on a Supervisor. VCF Operations HCX provides workload mobility capabilities for moving workloads to recovery sites through migration waves and mobility groups.

For Kubernetes workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters on vSphere Supervisor, VCF supports two complementary backup and restore paths. VKS clusters can use Velero, available as a Supervisor Service, to back up and restore workloads from named backups using the `velero restore create <restore-name> --from-backup <backup-name> --wait` command; backups can be targeted at specific namespaces, label selectors, or resource types, allowing granular or full-cluster restoration. VKS Cluster Management provides an integrated backup and restore capability for entire VKS clusters and their namespaces: restore operations include file system backups by default, with CSI volume snapshots available as an optional selection. Backup schedules managed through VKS Cluster Management appear in the Data Protection tab and can be edited, paused, or deleted after creation.

The data-layer complement to Velero's resource-state restores is provided by CSI snapshots on vSphere Supervisor. Both the CSI Snapshot and File System Backup methods support file system level incremental backup, where files unchanged since the last backup are not backed up again. CSI snapshots support crash-consistent backups, block mode backup, NFS volume support, deduplication, and encryption, enabling Kubernetes object state and persistent volume data to be restored to a consistent point in time. VKS Backup and Restore Hooks extend these capabilities by enabling the execution of pre- and post-operation scripts, providing granular control over the data protection lifecycle and supporting custom automation steps during recovery. During a Velero restore of vSphere Pods, a CloneFromSnapshot custom resource is created in the same namespace as the original PVC, and its `status.phase` field tracks the restore operation through multiple phases. The platform documentation notes that snapshots should not be used as the sole backup solution or as a long-term backup strategy.

The Kubernetes API server state is stored in etcd, which supports restoration from snapshot files taken from previous backup operations. etcd accepts snapshots from the same major.minor version and supports cross-patch-version restores; a version-compatibility check rejects snapshots from a mismatched major version, reducing the risk of restoring to a structurally inconsistent state. Restoring from an etcd snapshot recovers all cluster objects, including Deployments, ConfigMaps, Secrets, and RBAC policies, to the state captured at backup time. For shorter-window recovery, Kubernetes Deployments maintain a configurable revision history tracked through immutable ControllerRevision objects; administrators can roll back a Deployment to a prior known-good state without requiring a full backup restore. Kubernetes configurations stored in version control create a durable audit log of authorized changes, supporting immutable infrastructure patterns that allow clusters to be rebuilt to a known, version-controlled state.

The platform documents specific scenarios where restore operations should be avoided, such as cases that might cause orphaned resources, version mismatches, or unstable cluster states, which helps organizations plan restoration procedures that result in a known, operational state. Restore operations require that in-place infrastructure and access to the most recent backup files be available.

VCF provides the recovery mechanisms, backup infrastructure, and documented restoration procedures that support restoration within defined time periods. Defining specific restoration time-period targets, testing recovery against those targets, maintaining off-cluster backup storage, and maintaining operational runbooks are organizational responsibilities.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

The Security Services Platform (SSP) component of VMware vDefend provides backup and restore capabilities for the vDefend security infrastructure, contributing to an organization's ability to restore the security policy layer to a known, operational state.

SSP backup and restore covers vDefend configuration data, including Distributed Firewall policy, Security Intelligence analytics, Network Detection and Response (NDR) settings, and Malware Prevention Service configuration when those features are activated. The backup also includes tokens, Bare Metal servers, and Bare Metal server interfaces as part of the full inventory backup. Backup data can be used to restore the system after hardware malfunctions, software malfunctions, data corruption, malware incidents, or unintentional data deletion. Restoration can be performed to the same SSP instance or to a new instance, providing flexibility in recovery scenarios. The backup and restore interface is accessible through System > Backup and Restore in the SSP management interface.

Backup scheduling is configurable to support organizational recovery point objectives. Recurring backups can be scheduled on a weekly basis by selecting the day and time, or at frequency-based intervals ranging from a minimum of 6 hours to a maximum of 720 hours (30 days). This scheduling flexibility allows organizations to align backup frequency with their restoration time-period requirements and acceptable data loss windows.

Restore operations have defined prerequisites that support predictable recovery: the SSP service and messaging FQDNs must match those at the time of backup, the same SSP package version that is in the backup bundle must be uploaded before restore, the same NSX instance that was onboarded at the time of backup must be used, and the SSP must be in a healthy state. Backup and restore operations enter a failed state after 30 minutes if internal services are restarted manually or due to failure, which provides a bounded failure window for recovery planning. Certificate consistency between backup and restored environments should be maintained to avoid NDR Sensor disruptions during recovery.

SSP maintains a Restore History section that displays restore operation status, showing Completed when an operation is successful. This provides verification that restoration to a known operational state has been achieved.

Defining restoration time-period targets, testing recovery against those targets, and integrating vDefend recovery into broader disaster recovery plans are organizational responsibilities. vDefend's backup and restore capabilities cover the security policy infrastructure layer but do not address the broader scope of technology asset, application, service, and data recovery that the control requires.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides technical mechanisms that support restoring virtual machine workloads within defined time periods from protected replicas.

Recovery plans automate the restore process across protection groups, with replication operating according to configurable recovery point objectives. vSphere Replication and vSAN snapshots support retention schedules for hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly snapshots, with a maximum retention duration of three years, allowing organizations to select a recovery point that meets their restoration requirements. Cyber Recovery snapshot schedules define a retention policy that determines how long each snapshot is retained, with retention duration configurable for hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly intervals up to a maximum of three years.

PNR retains archived recovery settings for a configurable duration, defaulting to 30 days, and executes the cleanup task for those settings at a configurable interval that defaults to 1440 minutes. Recovery plan synchronization has a default expiration of 60 minutes. The Protection and Recovery Configuration Import/Export Tool captures protection and recovery server version, build number, local and remote site names, inventory mappings, placeholder datastores, advanced settings, array managers with Storage Replication Adapter information, protection groups, and recovery plans in an XML file that can be reimported to restore a known configuration state. Configuration data can also be exported using a top-level recovery step within the recovery plan itself. Regular backups of PNR services are recommended so that they can be restored into another management domain if needed.

PNR supports service recovery in another management domain when a disaster event affects the domain containing VCF Automation and VCF Operations, allowing users to recover workloads from an alternate site. Recovery plan history reports can be exported containing information about each run, test, or cleanup operation, including the result and start and end times for the whole plan and for each step. PNR documentation recommends executing recovery plans at least once every three months to maintain operational readiness and validate recovery procedures.

Defining specific restoration time-period targets, testing recovery against those targets, and maintaining operational procedures to meet those targets are organizational responsibilities. PNR provides the configurable recovery infrastructure that these organizational processes rely on.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) supports point-in-time recovery (PITR) for managed databases, allowing administrators to restore a database to a specific date and time within the configured backup retention period. Restore operations create a new database instance; the user selects either the most recent timestamp or a user-specified date and time as the restore point. In DSM, PITR is available for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server databases. Databases can be restored through the DSM UI or, for tenant-managed databases, through the VCF Automation UI. When restoring through the VCF Automation UI, users can select the datastore location and configure management policies for the restored database; the resulting database is owned by the user who initiated the restore.

For PostgreSQL clusters, PITR is also supported when restoring to a different DSM control plane: specifying a value in the time field targets a specific point in time, and if omitted, DSM restores the latest available state. When recovering from a failed major version upgrade, the custom restore timestamp must correspond to a time when the cluster was running with the specified database version.

Deleted databases with retained backups remain accessible through the Archives tab, where restore operations can be initiated and archive retention periods can be edited. Backup retention is configurable per database through the backup-retention-period setting; PITR can only recover data from within the configured retention window. Restoration duration depends on data volume and underlying infrastructure performance, which should be factored into recovery time objective planning.

DSM provides the technical mechanisms needed to recover databases to a known, consistent state. The organization is responsible for defining restoration time-period objectives, aligning backup schedules and retention settings to satisfy those objectives, and periodically validating that databases can be recovered within the defined windows. DSM supplies the restore mechanism but not the organizational governance that determines whether recovery meets the defined time targets.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides a native backup and restore capability for the Avi Controller configuration. The backup scheduler supports configurable frequency units of minutes, hours, days, weeks, or months, with days as the default, enabling organizations to align backup cadence to their recovery point objectives.

Before initiating a restore, Avi runs a pre-restore validation sequence that checks backup configuration validity, verifies Service Engine attachment, validates system configuration, and performs patch verification. The Controller also obtains user consent prior to beginning a restore operation. These checks confirm that the backup and target environment are in a compatible state before the restore begins.

The restore process executes through defined sequential phases: the RestorePreRebootOps task syncs data from the database to configuration prior to reboot; after reboot, cloud discovery is invoked; a PostRestoreValidation task verifies the count of restored objects including Clouds and Service Engine Groups; and configuration unblocking and placement resumption complete the sequence. State information including operation type, image version, and cluster node name is reported throughout. Administrators can track restore progress at any point using the "show upgrade status" CLI command, the GET API endpoint at /api/upgradestatusinfo, or the VIEW PROGRESS option in Administration > Controller > Update in the Avi UI.

Every step of a restore operation is captured in the event log with timestamps, task names, node IP addresses, status, duration, and detailed validation messages. System reports record restore duration, completion status, image reference, total checks performed, and checks completed. This audit trail gives organizations the data needed to evaluate whether a restore completed within a defined time period. For longer-term audit lookups, the CONFIG_DELETE API supports a duration parameter of up to one year, enabling review of configuration deletion events over an extended period.

Note that factory reset recovery, which may be required for a non-operational Controller cluster, is irreversible and deletes all configuration, requiring additional time to recreate the database. This scenario should be accounted for in recovery time planning.

### Control CP-10(06)

> Protect system components used for recovery and reconstitution.

**SCF Controls:** BCD-13

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides built-in backup and restoration capabilities for its management components, with access controls, encryption, integrity checks, and platform-level protections that help protect the security of backup operations. Physical hardware protection, environmental controls for backup storage locations, offsite media handling, and network-level controls for backup storage remain organizational responsibilities.

VCF supports file-based and image-based backup methods for its core management components, including VMware vCenter, VCF Operations, and NSX Manager. NSX Manager backup is automatically configured during the VCF bring-up process, while file-based backup jobs for VCF Operations and vCenter must be configured separately. vCenter file-based daily backups are configured through the vCenter Management Interface of each vCenter instance. vCenter file-based backup and restore supports FTP, FTPS, HTTP, HTTPS, SFTP, NFS, or SMB protocols to transfer backup files to and from backup servers. NSX treats backup and restore operations as specific to each appliance, whether it is the Global Manager or the Local Manager, providing isolation between backup datasets. Scheduled backups should be configured to prepare for critical failure of a management component, an upgrade, or a certificate update.

File-based backups of management components, including VCF Operations, vCenter, and VCF Identity Broker, are restored onto newly deployed appliances, providing a clean recovery path. It is advisable to perform a backup of the VCF Identity Broker appliance following any substantial modifications to the identity source configuration, such as when resetting VCF SSO. VCF Operations supports backup and restore of custom and out-of-the-box content, and administrators should use recent backups to restore content or import content to a different environment if incorrect edits occur or data recovery is needed. VCF Operations for Networks supports backup and restore of deployment data for recovery when an existing setup is unrecoverable.

VCF enforces several integrity protections for backup and restoration operations. When restoring a vCenter instance from file-based backup, the administrator must have a valid backup file that matches the same version as the vCenter appliance being restored, and the version retrieved from VCF Operations must be verified against the backup file. Proper sequencing and verification of component restoration is required to maintain dependencies and system integrity. For example, vCenter must be available before proceeding with a restore of VCF Operations, and VCF Operations must be available before restoring vCenter. These dependency checks help protect against incomplete or corrupted restorations.

Role-based access control restricts who can perform backup and recovery operations. Only Admin and Console users can configure backup and restore through public APIs and CLI commands respectively. The No Cryptography Administrator role supports all Administrator privileges except for Cryptographic Operations privileges, which restricts unauthorized changes to encryption settings that protect backup data. The No Cryptography Administrator role can be cloned to create custom roles with only specific Cryptographic Operations privileges, providing granular control over encryption-related backup operations.

VCF provides backup encryption to protect backup data at rest. When backup encryption is enabled, a password must be supplied that is then required to decrypt the file during restoration. The backup process offers the option to encrypt the compressed file before transmission to the backup storage location. vCenter file-based backup files contain the Key Derivation Key, so access controls on the backup storage location are critical to protect both backup integrity and encryption key material. vSAN data-at-rest encryption encrypts everything in the vSAN datastore, including all files, virtual machines, and their corresponding data, which protects data stored in snapshots and replicated copies. vSAN data-in-transit encryption protects data as it moves across hosts and file service inter-host connections in the cluster, securing replication and backup traffic between sites and components.

vSAN Data Protection provides snapshot-based data protection with configurable retention policies and scheduling intervals. Protection groups contain snapshot policies that define retention duration and snapshot scheduling, keeping backup data managed with automated lifecycle policies.

For workload-level backup protection, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) includes a data protection capability that installs the data protection extension, selected plugins, and Velero on clusters to enable backup and restore operations. Disabling data protection on a cluster does not remove backups that have already been created, which helps protect against accidental deletion of backup data. For virtual machine workloads, partners and customers can develop backup applications using the Virtual Disk Development Kit (VDDK), and VM Service VMs can be backed up through partner solutions that use VMware vSphere Storage APIs for Data Protection.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to protecting backup and restoration software through access controls, configuration backup, encrypted communications, and data separation capabilities.

Role-based access control restricts who can manage protection groups, recovery plans, and replication configurations. Predefined roles limit the operations each user can perform on the backup and recovery infrastructure. Certain roles that allow running commands on the PNR Server should be assigned only to trusted administrator-level users.

All communications between PNR and VMware vCenter instances are secured over TLS connections. The appliance management interface verifies certificates during configuration, protecting the recovery infrastructure from unauthorized access and man-in-the-middle attacks during management operations.

PNR backup configuration supports setting a datastore path, enabling or disabling the backup scheduler, configuring the retention period, and scheduling backup frequency and time. Administrators should take regular backups of PNR services so that the service can be restored into another management domain if needed. The PNR Configuration Import/Export Tool backs up and transfers protection and recovery settings including protection groups, recovery plans, and site mappings. For configuration import and export, PNR supports service account authentication as an alternative to remote vCenter credentials, with the service account path stored at a configurable file location such as /opt/vmware/etc/dr-backup/secrets/file.

vSAN snapshots and replication provides a native, integrated snapshot-based protection solution for VMs running on vSAN datastores, managed through the vSphere Client. Snapshot-based protection enables recovery from operational failures or ransomware attacks using locally stored snapshots, while replication to a peer site through PNR protects against site-level failures. PNR allows administrators to manage protection groups, view available snapshots, and perform VM operations including restore and clone from existing snapshots. vSphere Replication can be configured to create and retain point-in-time snapshots on the recovery site, maintaining protected copies separate from the source environment.

Physical protection of backup and restoration hardware, environmental controls for storage locations, and offsite media handling are organizational responsibilities outside PNR's scope.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Avi Load Balancer provides mechanisms to protect both its backup software and the configuration backup data produced for the Avi Controller.

Backup data is protected through passphrase-based encryption. The backup_passphrase parameter in the Controller's backup configuration encrypts all sensitive fields in the backup file. The Controller also stores sensitive information encrypted in a local database, with encryption keys protected during backups when a passphrase is included. The passphrase is required at restore time to decrypt the file. Avi documentation recommends storing backups in a safe external location and labeling them clearly with version and date.

Backup files transferred to remote storage use Secure Copy Protocol (SCP), providing encrypted transport for data in transit. Remote server authentication uses SSH user credentials. Backups can also be sent to S3-compatible object storage, with authentication via access key and secret key credentials or an attached IAM role. The Controller supports independent configuration for local backups, stored on the Controller with a configurable retention count, and remote server backups. For single-node Controller deployments and for recovery from complete VM or disk failure, enabling periodic external configuration backups is mandatory. The documentation recommends designating a remote backup destination to protect against non-recoverable Controller cluster failures.

In VCF-integrated deployments, VCF Operations is used to configure the external backup server and storage path for Avi Load Balancer backups. By default, Avi backups are stored on the same server where VCF Operations appliance backups are stored. When restoring a Controller cluster from backup, the new instance must remain synchronized with the VCF Operations lifecycle.

The Controller's backup scheduler automates backup operations. On-demand backups are available via the CLI export command or the REST API. The CLI show backups command lists available backup files for verification. Prior to any upgrade operation, the Controller verifies that a backup has been taken.

Before applying a configuration backup, the Avi Controller performs pre-restore checks: validation of the backup configuration, verification of Service Engine attachment, system configuration validation, patch verification, backup configuration version compatibility with the current Controller version, confirmation that all file objects referenced in the backup are present on the Controller, FIPS mode verification (the FIPS mode of the backup configuration must match the controller environment), and available cluster disk space. The Controller also obtains user consent before proceeding with a restore operation. Both CLI (import command with keep_uuid option) and REST API restore methods are available, each requiring the backup passphrase. After restore, the Controller performs post-restore validation that verifies the successful restoration of configuration objects, including counts of restored Clouds and ServiceEngineGroups.

The hardware infrastructure hosting the Avi Controller VMs and Service Engine VMs is protected by VCF platform mechanisms, including vSphere High Availability, which provides failover protection against hardware and operating system outages. Protection of the underlying compute, storage, and network hardware is addressed by VCF.

Organizations must define backup storage policy, establish off-site or remote custody arrangements for backup media, and conduct periodic restore testing. These organizational governance processes are outside Avi's scope.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control CP-12

> Safe Mode When [Assignment: organization-defined conditions] are detected, enter a safe mode of operation with [Assignment: organization-defined restrictions of safe mode of operation].

**SCF Controls:** SEA-07.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple fail-secure mechanisms across its component stack that enable systems to transition to a known, predictable state during failures while preserving data integrity and system state information.

At the compute layer, vSphere Fault Tolerance (FT) provides continuous availability for protected virtual machines by maintaining a synchronized secondary VM running in lockstep on a separate host. If the primary host fails, the secondary VM takes over immediately with no data loss and no downtime, maintaining the application's in-memory state and active connections. This is a direct fail-to-known-state capability where the system transitions to a pre-established secondary instance. When Fault Tolerance encryption mode is set to Required, the platform restricts secondary VM placement to only those hosts that support FT encryption, so that failover can occur only on hosts that maintain the encryption posture of the protected VM.

vSphere High Availability (HA) automatically restarts VMs on surviving hosts following a physical host failure. HA uses network heartbeat monitoring to detect host failures, with a 15-second heartbeat timeout triggering failure detection. Admission control policies reserve cluster capacity so that sufficient resources are available for VM restart even after a host failure. For stretched cluster deployments, HA admission control can reserve 50% of the cluster's aggregate CPU and memory resources to allow all VMs on one site to restart on the other site during a site failure. NSX Manager nodes are similarly protected through vSphere HA with high restart priority, so that management plane components recover quickly after a host failure. For hosts using vSphere Distributed Services Engine with data processing units (DPUs), lifecycle operations are isolated within each DPU's boundaries, so that errors on one DPU do not affect the state of another; the overall operation result is still marked as failed to preserve visibility into the event.

At the storage layer, vSAN fault domains distribute data objects across independent failure boundaries based on storage policy. Each object is stored across at least two nodes (primary and replica) in separate fault domains, providing redundancy against localized failures. During a component failure, write operations for the failed component are redirected to a durability component, so the system continues operating in a degraded but data-safe state. When vSAN detects a permanent component failure, it begins rebuilding affected components immediately if adequate cluster resources exist. For transient failures where a component enters an absent state, vSAN waits 60 minutes before initiating a rebuild, preserving the opportunity for the original component to return. These behaviors return storage objects to their policy-defined state after failures. For stretched cluster deployments, the vSAN site disaster tolerance setting on the vSAN configuration page defines the data redundancy method used to handle a site failure, allowing administrators to configure the system's fail-secure behavior for site-level disruptions.

VCF Operations clusters are designed with fault domain awareness. Each object within the VCF Operations data store is replicated across fault domains. The cluster can tolerate the loss of one fault domain or individual nodes without data loss, continuing to operate in a degraded state until the issue is resolved. This preserves all monitoring and analytics state information through a failure event. VCF Operations recommends configuring host failure settings to restart VMs as part of vSphere HA setup, applying the same fail-secure approach to monitoring infrastructure.

At the infrastructure orchestration level, Cluster Availability associates each node with a fault domain, enabling the platform to track which components are affected by a failure and to coordinate the transition to a degraded-but-functional state. vSphere Supervisor failure domains allow each zone to be treated as an independent failure domain, extending this model to Kubernetes workloads. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters require at least one control plane instance per failure zone to provide fault tolerance, and each VKS failure domain maps to a vSphere Zone associated with a single vSphere cluster. The Cluster API enables sets of worker node machines to be defined across multiple failure domains with rules to automatically heal the cluster in the case of whole-zone service disruption.

At the Kubernetes workload layer, the admission control framework enforces fail-closed policy behavior for VKS clusters running on vSphere Supervisor. Both MutatingAdmissionPolicy and ValidatingAdmissionPolicy resources support a failurePolicy field that controls how the API server responds when a policy cannot be evaluated due to CEL expression parse errors, type check errors, runtime errors, or misconfigured policy definitions. The default value is Fail, causing the API server to deny the request rather than allow it through when evaluation fails. When a ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding is configured with failurePolicy set to Fail, the validationActions field defines how failures are enforced; setting parameterNotFoundAction to Deny causes the API server to reject requests when a referenced policy parameter is absent. Pod Security Admission applies a fail-closed model at the pod level: if any container fails to meet the securityContext requirements of the active Pod Security Standard profile (Baseline, Restricted, or Privileged), the entire pod is rejected, and partial enforcement is not permitted. For workload scheduling across failure boundaries, VKS validates that each MachineDeployment includes a failureDomain specification when multiple vSphere Zones are configured and denies requests that omit this field.

For batch workloads, the Kubernetes Job resource supports a Pod Failure Policy that uses custom conditions on pod status to distinguish transient failures from permanent misconfigurations. Transient failures can trigger retry behavior while permanent misconfigurations cause the job to terminate in a defined state rather than looping indefinitely. VKS cluster management provides structured visibility into failure states: a VKS cluster reports Unknown status when one or more control plane nodes or core system components are in an undetermined state, and the VKS Controller reports phase and component readiness conditions with Info, Warning, and Error severity classifications. VKS Cluster Management Policy Insights include Sync signals that indicate when policy definitions fail to propagate from the management plane to the targeted VKS cluster estate, making policy divergence visible rather than silent. For post-failure state preservation and analysis, VKS Linux nodes maintain system state information including cloud-init logs, network socket status, kernel parameters, and process information, all of which can be collected via support bundles.

In the networking layer, NSX maintains a desired state model where the platform continuously tracks the difference between the intended configuration (desired state) and the actual configuration (realized state). When an error occurs and the realized state diverges from the desired state, this divergence is surfaced so that administrators can identify and remediate the inconsistency. This model supports fail-secure operations by making configuration drift visible rather than silently allowing degraded state to persist.

For broader disruption scenarios, Application Disaster Recovery enables failover to a secondary site to maintain operations during major disruptions, providing a defined recovery target and a known-state transition path for site-level failures.

VCF provides fault detection and alerting capabilities that support fail-secure operations. VCF Operations correlates events published by monitored systems and delivers them as faults, signifying events that affect the availability of objects in the environment. Administrators can define custom fault symptoms based on these events and incorporate them into alert definitions for automated notification. At the host level, VMware ESX generates problem observations when storage devices enter a permanent error state, providing early warning of hardware degradation that could affect system availability.

VMware ESX hosts support host encryption safe mode, which means the host can encrypt virtual machines and is designed to keep encryption keys protected during failure scenarios. Once host encryption mode is enabled, it cannot be disabled easily, which limits accidental exposure of encrypted data during host recovery operations.

Authentication subsystems in VCF implement authentication failure handling, so that repeated authentication failures result in account lockout rather than continued access attempts. The VCF SSO lockout policy is the specific mechanism that governs this behavior: administrators can configure the maximum number of failed login attempts and the time interval between failures before lockout is applied. This is a fail-secure pattern at the security boundary, where the system transitions to a more restrictive state when anomalous behavior is detected.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager supports recovery from configuration drift by detecting when a host's current state differs from its desired specification. Administrators can check the compliance of a cluster or host against the desired state and remediate it back to the defined configuration, returning the host to its known-good baseline after localized failures or configuration changes.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) contributes to fail-secure system behavior through mechanisms in the Security Services Platform and the IDS/IPS subsystem.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) provides backup and restore capabilities that preserve platform configuration and security policy state. Backup and restore operations are gated on platform health; when the platform is not in a healthy state, these operations are prevented rather than allowed to proceed against a degraded system. When a scheduled backup fails, SSP triggers a Scheduled Backup Error alarm to alert administrators. The platform also provides continuous monitoring of deployment status through an alarms interface that surfaces errors requiring attention. If internal services are restarted during a backup or restore, the operation enters a failed state after 30 minutes, establishing a defined failure boundary rather than an indefinite hang. SSP deployments should use vSphere High Availability to support host-level failure recovery; deploying on vSAN stretched configurations provides additional resilience against multiple simultaneous host failures within a site.

The vDefend distributed firewall follows a Zero Trust staged deployment model that prioritizes securing common infrastructure services, establishing controlled east-west traffic postures as part of a progressive fail-secure architecture.

For IDS/IPS policy objects, the policy realization state must reach REALIZED with a runtime_status of SUCCESS to confirm that the policy has reached its intended known state on the enforcement plane. When high volumes of IDS/IPS events and PCAP data create out-of-memory conditions that could destabilize the system, vDefend applies an automated protective mechanism that selectively drops lower-priority events and PCAP data to maintain system stability, prioritizing operational continuity over complete event capture under load.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) is designed to transition to known, deterministic states when unexpected conditions arise during disaster recovery operations. Recovery plan state is determined by the states of the protection groups within the plan, and the plan state in turn determines which operations are allowed, so the system progresses through defined states rather than entering undefined conditions. When a recovery is canceled or datastore errors occur, the plan enters an Incomplete recovery state and requires administrators to either resolve the errors and rerun the recovery or remove protection for the affected virtual machines. Reprotect operations have a corresponding state machine with a Ready state on success and an Incomplete Reprotect state when failures occur during reverse replication or placeholder virtual machine creation.

PNR handles split-brain scenarios deterministically. The Protection and Recovery dashboards in VCF Operations generate critical warnings when a plan was running while two sites were disconnected, when a recovery plan is in an error state, or when reprotect fails for a plan, alerting administrators that the condition must be investigated before further operations proceed. During disaster recovery, if the protected site goes offline or errors occur on it, the failure is reported but the recovery operation continues to the recovery site without aborting, allowing recovery to proceed even when the protected site is partially or fully unreachable.

System state information is preserved through several mechanisms in 9.1. The Protection and Recovery Global Console alerts users to expired snapshot schedules, replication errors such as missing virtual machines, connectivity issues with protected or destination sites, and site misconfigurations that require investigation. The Protection and Recovery dashboards in VCF Operations expose the current phase of the disaster recovery lifecycle for each plan, including whether a plan is ready, a test is in progress, or incomplete recovery or cleanup operations are pending, and which protection groups are in which state. When errors are resolved and recovery is rerun, operations that succeeded previously are skipped and successfully recovered virtual machines continue running, supporting partial-success failure recovery without restarting the entire plan.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides application-delivery-specific fail-secure behavior through defined failure states for its data-plane components. When a Service Engine host fails, Service Engines automatically transition to one of two known operational states: OPER_PARTITIONED when virtual services on that host remain operational, and OPER_DOWN when no operational virtual services exist. These discrete, named states give operators and the platform a deterministic view of failure conditions within the application delivery layer, supporting the organization's ability to define and respond to known failure outcomes. When a Service Engine is unable to register with an unavailable Controller, it comes up stateless and ignores data-plane heartbeats from other Service Engines, providing bounded behavior in a degraded-Controller scenario.

The Avi Controller cluster documents failure scenarios and recovery actions for deployments across two availability zones. Fault domains are mapped to Availability Zones, and within each Service Engine Group operators can specify two or three Availability Zones across which Service Engines and virtual services are placed to improve fault tolerance, so that a zone failure produces predictable, bounded degradation. When a virtual service enters a failed state due to invalid configuration or resource constraints, the failed state reflects the state of the virtual service at a specific moment in time; operators can disable the virtual service in that state and re-enable it after running corrective actions.

The ICAP Profile provides configurable failure action handling for ICAP server errors, with two explicit options: Fail Closed, which sends an HTTP 503 to the client on error, and Fail Open, which allows traffic to pass. This mechanism gives operators direct control over the known state Avi enters when a content inspection dependency is unavailable.

System state information is preserved at failure through the inventoryfaultconfig mechanism. The Avi Controller tracks and generates alerts across independently configurable fault categories, including license faults, migration faults, backup scheduler faults, SSL profile faults, deprecated API version faults, and Service Engine faults. The Avi Controller and Service Engine log audit record failures to Events, Syslog, and Splunk, covering initiation and termination of trusted channels, failure of trusted channel functions, and unsuccessful certificate validation attempts, including the ObjectDoesNotExist condition when an SSL certificate does not exist during trusted channel establishment.

Infrastructure-level fail-secure capabilities for compute, storage, and network are provided by VCF rather than Avi (see VCF Coverage). The organization must also define the specific known failure states, recovery procedures, and failure scope that this control requires.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control IA-01

> a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]: 1.
