The platform documentation recommends that the Password grant type be avoided whenever possible, especially for third-party application authentication, in favor of token-based flows that do not require credentials to traverse the network in cleartext form. API clients can configure token-based authentication to communicate with the server, and the LoginByToken method authenticates clients using holder-of-key security tokens that embed security artifacts for authentication without exposing credentials. Both approaches avoid password transmission after initial token issuance.

Session identifiers are used to maintain authenticated state after initial login, so credentials are not re-transmitted with each request. Valid session identifiers establish trust with API endpoints without requiring repeated credential entry. TLS encryption protects all authentication exchanges in transit, so even the masked credential data transmitted during initial login is encrypted on the wire.

At the workload consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor extend these protections into the Kubernetes platform. The Kubernetes API server uses JWT and OIDC-based authentication flows in which the actual credential exchange occurs at the identity provider, such as VCF SSO or an external IdP, and the API server receives and validates signed tokens rather than handling plaintext passwords directly. JWTAuthenticator configuration specifies a unique issuer URL per authenticator and applies claim validation rules to incoming tokens; at most one JWT authenticator cryptographically validates a token per request, so authentication decisions are made on signed assertions without requiring credentials to traverse the API layer. Audience-aware token authenticators validate that a token was intended for the specific server it was presented to by verifying it against the server's audience identifier, further scoping token validity without additional password transmission.

Kubernetes Secrets provide the primary mechanism for storing authentication credentials within VKS clusters, including registry passwords, service account tokens, and other sensitive values. Secret data fields are stored in base64-encoded form and are not returned as human-readable text by default in API responses; when etcd encryption at rest is configured, Secret values are encrypted in the datastore. The Kubernetes platform requires that applications protect confidential values after reading them from environment variables or volumes by not logging secret data in cleartext and not transmitting it to untrusted parties. The Harbor registry Supervisor Service stores its authentication credentials, including admin passwords, secret keys, and job service secrets, as Kubernetes Secrets rather than in plaintext configuration files, keeping sensitive material out of environment variables visible to other processes. Registry secrets in Kubernetes store authentication credentials for private container image registries within the Secrets API rather than in plaintext manifests.

The Supervisor Secret Store service enforces role-based access control for administrators, DevOps engineers, and application tenants to manage and distribute sensitive authentication credentials through a controlled interface. For CI/CD pipelines on VKS, Argo CD's OIDC integration supports Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE) authentication through the enablePKCEAuthentication field, reducing the risk of authorization code interception in OIDC flows by requiring a verifier that cannot be replayed. HashiCorp Vault can generate dynamic secrets with limited lifespans for services, reducing the risk of long-lived credentials being exposed through logs or process inspection. The Kubernetes hardening guidance recommends configuring short token lifespans via the TokenRequest API to limit the exposure window if tokens are captured.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to authenticator feedback protection through several mechanisms that operate at different layers of the authentication stack.

The DSM administrative and user interface presents a web-based login screen where users supply an email address and password. Standard web browser behavior renders password input fields with character masking, so entered characters are not displayed in plaintext. DSM authenticates local users by matching the email address and validating the submitted password before any directory service processing occurs. For environments using LDAPS, DSM matches the email domain against the registered LDAP server and validates credentials over an encrypted channel, so the password is not transmitted or displayed in cleartext during the authentication exchange. DSM can be configured to use an LDAP server for both authentication and authorization and falls back to local user authentication when directory service credentials are non-functional.

At the database layer, DSM supports host-based authentication (HBA) configuration for database instances. Custom HBA entries support the scram-sha-256, reject, and cert authentication methods. The scram-sha-256 method is a challenge-response protocol that does not transmit the password itself over the connection, limiting exposure of credential material during the authentication handshake. DSM also supports enabling or disabling directory service authentication per database instance through the Enable Directory Service Authentication toggle, allowing administrators to enforce LDAPS-backed authentication for database access rather than relying on simpler mechanisms.

In 9.1, DSM extends LDAPS-encrypted authentication to MySQL databases. MySQL database users can be configured with LDAPS authentication, including Active Directory Secure/SSL Global Catalog configurations for multi-domain environments, carrying credentials over an encrypted channel. SQL Server databases support Windows Principal authentication through Kerberos-based Active Directory domain resources, which is a challenge-response mechanism that does not transmit passwords in plaintext. SQL Server data service policies also support SQL User authentication for standard SQL Server logins.

DSM's LDAPS integration uses certificate-based validation of directory service server connections, supporting protection of the credential exchange channel. What DSM does not provide is explicit product-level configuration for UI feedback behavior, such as a setting that controls whether password fields mask characters. That behavior is inherited from the client browser and web framework conventions rather than being a DSM-configurable control. Organizations should also account for any programmatic or API-based access patterns where credential handling falls outside the DSM UI and must be governed by application-level controls.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides authenticator feedback protection through log-level credential masking for application proxy authentication flows and through its administrative authentication interface.

For application-layer authentication, the sensitive_log_profile can be configured to mask sensitive fields, including Basic Authorization credentials and OAuth tokens, from virtual service application logs. This setting is available in the OAuth and OIDC client and resource server configuration. Organizations deploying Avi as a proxy for OAuth-authenticated applications should enable this profile to restrict credential material from appearing in operational log output. The masking applies to credentials that Avi processes at the proxy boundary, not to credentials transmitted between clients and backend servers outside Avi's inspection scope.

Avi's log protection capabilities extend masking to additional credential-bearing HTTP content. HTTP headers containing sensitive user-related information, including cookies and authorization headers, can be masked or removed from application logs. A sensitive log policy can be applied to mask cookies in both WAF logs and application log headers, which keeps session and credential tokens out of operational log storage.

For LDAP-based basic authentication exposed through a virtual service, Avi supports configurable realm indication to clients during authentication requests. This identifies the authentication realm to the client without exposing credential content in the response.

For administrative access, the Avi Controller web administration interface is the credential entry point for all administrator logins, including local accounts and accounts authenticated through federated sources such as LDAP, TACACS+, or SAML identity providers. When Avi acts as a proxy for federated authentication flows in which end users authenticate interactively against an external identity provider, such as a SAML identity provider, an LDAP directory, or an OAuth/OIDC authorization server, the credential entry experience and feedback masking for those user-facing flows are rendered and governed by the identity provider. Avi validates the resulting token or session assertion at the virtual service boundary but does not control what the end user sees during the interactive login step with the external provider.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IA-07

> Implement mechanisms for authentication to a cryptographic module that meet the requirements of applicable laws, executive orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidelines for such authentication.

**SCF Controls:** CRY-02, IAC-12

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF ships with FIPS-validated cryptographic modules across its core components, providing a foundation for meeting statutory, regulatory, and contractual requirements for cryptographic security strength.

At the platform level, VCF supports Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) 140-2, which specify security requirements for cryptographic modules. VCF Operations provides a FIPS Security Mode that, when enabled, enforces additional algorithm and cipher prerequisites across the environment. Administrators should be aware that self-signed certificates generated using OpenSSL are not FIPS-compatible, so certificate management practices must account for this when operating in FIPS mode.

NSX, the networking component of VCF, uses FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules by default. These modules are validated through the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). NSX Manager uses FIPS 140 approved algorithms for authentication to cryptographic modules. For network connectivity, the NSX L2 VPN offers a FIPS compliance suite that uses approved encryption algorithms with Perfect Forward Secrecy.

The VMware vCenter appliance uses multiple FIPS-validated cryptographic modules, including VMware's Boring Crypto Module version 6.0 and VMware OpenSSL FIPS Object Module versions 2.0.20-vmw and 3.0, which collectively support algorithms including AES, ECDSA, HMAC, RSA, SHA-3, and key derivation functions. VMware ESX also uses several FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules. Starting in vSphere 9.0, all TLS profiles are FIPS compliant, and FIPS-compliant deployments should use certificate key sizes of 2048 or 3072 bits, as key sizes greater than 3072 bits have not been tested with FIPS.

VCF Automation uses the BouncyCastle FIPS 2.0.0 cryptographic module, which is compliant with FIPS 140-3 (Certificate #4743). VCF Operations for Networks uses BC-FJA (Bouncy Castle FIPS Java API) version 2.0.0 with FIPS 140-2 Certificate #37009. VCF Operations for Networks can also restrict cryptographic module usage to FIPS-validated modules only by enabling FIPS mode for external connections.

The Consumption layer of VCF, which provides VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor capabilities, extends cryptographic module controls into workload clusters. Administrators can activate FIPS mode at the cluster level using the ClusterClass variable `fips.enabled`, which restricts encryption algorithm selection to approved algorithms. Individual node pools in TanzuKubernetesCluster resources can reference FIPS-specific Kubernetes release images that bundle operating system and runtime components compiled against FIPS-validated cryptographic modules. The Istio service mesh, available as a standard package on VKS clusters, provides FIPS compliance out of the box for deployments with FIPS requirements. Security Technical Implementation Guides for Supervisor and VKr components are available to guide hardening of the Kubernetes layer, including cryptographic configuration.

Across VKS cluster components, administrators can configure permitted cipher suites and minimum protocol version restrictions at multiple layers. The kube-apiserver `--tls-cipher-suites` and `--tls-min-version` parameters control which algorithms and protocol versions are permitted for API server communication. Kubelet nodes support corresponding `tlsCipherSuites` and `tlsMinVersion` settings in their KubeletConfiguration, governing HTTPS connections to node agents. The kube-scheduler similarly accepts a `--tls-cipher-suites` parameter. Clusters using the Antrea CNI plug-in expose a `tlsCipherSuites` setting governing the plug-in's internal communications, and the vSphere Cloud Provider Interface supports configurable cipher suites as well. Ingress traffic handled by the Contour package can be bounded through `tls.minimum-protocol-version` and `tls.maximum-protocol-version` parameters in the Contour configuration file. The Istio service mesh provides `meshConfig.meshMTLS.minProtocolVersion` to restrict the minimum protocol version used for mutual TLS traffic between services. VKS clusters include TLS certificates supporting the cryptographic infrastructure for inter-component communications, and the Supervisor secret injection agent automatically configures mutual TLS with auto-generated certificates for vault integration services.

VCF provides multiple certificate management options to support FIPS-compliant deployments. The VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA) is an internal certificate authority built into vSphere that handles the certificate needs of the environment. Administrators can use VMCA-issued certificates, integrate with an enterprise certificate authority such as Microsoft CA, or install custom certificates. VCF Operations supports replacing default certificates with enterprise CA certificates and provides CA configuration and CSR generation capabilities. VCF Automation provides certificate management functions including import, download, edit, and delete operations for certificate PEM data.

Cryptographic operations are governed by fine-grained privilege sets in vCenter. An extensive number of Cryptographic Operations privileges allow granular control over encryption tasks. Only users assigned these privileges can perform cryptographic operations. The No Cryptography Administrator role supports all Administrator privileges except for the Cryptographic Operations privileges, enabling standard VM and infrastructure management while restricting unauthorized encryption changes. This role can be cloned to create custom roles with only specific Cryptographic Operations privileges, supporting separation of duties for cryptographic management.

Auditors evaluating a VCF deployment should verify that FIPS Security Mode is enabled in VCF Operations, confirm that FIPS mode for external connections is enabled in VCF Operations for Networks where required, confirm that certificates in use are FIPS-compatible rather than self-signed via OpenSSL, and verify that VKS clusters requiring FIPS compliance have `fips.enabled` set in their ClusterClass configuration with FIPS-specific node pool images referenced. The FIPS validation certificates referenced above (Certificate #4743 for VCF Automation, Certificate #37009 for VCF Operations for Networks) can be verified through the NIST CMVP certificate database.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) contributes to cryptographic module authentication requirements through two primary mechanisms: a required FIPS-enabled Kubernetes build for GPU-accelerated AI workload clusters and a CA trust bundle model that governs TLS communication with every external integration point.

GPU-accelerated VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters provisioned through PAIS use a FIPS-enabled VMware Kubernetes Release (VKR) for both control plane and worker nodes, running on the Ubuntu 24.04 OS image. The FIPS-enabled VKR designation is specified at the cluster topology level in the PAIS provisioning workflow and applies in both connected and disconnected (air-gapped) environments hosting Deep Learning VMs and GPU workloads.

PAIS configures TLS for external communications through the PAISConfiguration custom resource, which accepts spec.clientTls.caBundleRefs entries that reference ConfigMap resources containing CA certificates. Trust bundles are required for each external integration point: OIDC authentication providers, Harbor container registries, content management systems hosting knowledge base data, and MCP servers that connect over HTTPS. The issuer certificate for an MCP server must be added as a CA trust bundle when connecting over HTTPS, and CA trust bundles for the OIDC provider and database server can be imported after PAIS activation if not supplied during activation. Administrators prepare trust bundles as YAML-formatted ConfigMap resources and apply them via kubectl apply. PAIS documentation recommends organizing CA trust bundles per application and monitoring them for certificate expiration to support timely renewal.

For authentication endpoints, PAIS is configured through OIDC with an issuer URL, client ID (pais-authentication-id), and scopes including openid, groups, and offline_access, and HTTPS-based communication is required for OIDC provider integration. JWT authentication for VKS cluster login to PAIS is enabled through the vcf cluster register-vcfa-jwt-authenticator command. Grafana for PAIS observability supports PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) and refresh token enablement for OIDC authentication. PAIS instances serve TLS using a self-signed certificate stored in the pais-ingress-default Kubernetes Secret; administrators retrieve the certificate using kubectl get secrets/pais-ingress-default -o yaml for client-side trust configuration. The PAIS Supervisor endpoint communicates over HTTPS on port 6443 with certificate-based authentication, and LLM trace transmission to the OpenTelemetry Collector authenticates through a Kubernetes Secret containing Authorization Bearer tokens and custom headers.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) uses FIPS-validated cryptographic modules for SSP Installer and NDR Sensor operations: the VMware BouncyCastle Module version bc-fips-2.1.2 (CMVP Certificate #4943) and the VMware OpenSSL FIPS Provider Object Module version 3.0.15 (CMVP Certificate #4861). These modules implement FIPS 140-2 and FIPS 140-3 NIST-recommended standards, providing a documented cryptographic compliance baseline for SSP components.

SSP enforces cryptographic strength at the connection level by rejecting certificates and TLS configurations that rely on deprecated hashing algorithms (SHA-1, MD5), 1024-bit keys, or older protocol versions. Validation failures produce explicit error messages, helping administrators identify and correct non-compliant configurations.

NDR Sensors authenticate to SSP using mutual TLS (mTLS), with a unique certificate generated per sensor and signed by an intermediate certificate authority hosted on the SSP. SSP also requires an LDAP certificate for directory service connections and validates it using the OpenSSL tool. NDR Sensor registration uses a time-limited unique token generated by SSP to secure the initial onboarding connection.

For traffic inspection, vDefend's TLS Inspection Decryption Action Profiles include a Crypto Enforcement setting that controls the minimum and maximum protocol versions and cipher suites permitted for client and server connections. The Enforce mode requires compliance with those bounds; the Transparent mode allows bypass. These profiles apply to both internal and gateway decryption scenarios.

SSP Installer and SSP ship with a current TLS version as the default. The supported TLS versions and cipher suites on SSP Installer and SSP cannot be modified after deployment, constraining all SSP component communications to the documented secure baseline.

vDefend's documented cryptographic module compliance is specific to SSP components. FIPS validation status for other vDefend subcomponents, including IDS/IPS signature update channels and ATP sandboxing integrations, is not established in the 9.1 documentation and should be assessed separately.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Meets)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides automated mechanisms for authenticating to cryptographic modules and services as part of replication and disaster recovery operations.

PNR establishes authenticated communication channels to remote services through token-based authentication. The server automatically acquires a holder-of-key SAML token from VCF SSO and sends this token in a cryptographically signed request to the remote service, allowing PNR to authenticate to the cryptographic services provided by the platform without manual credential entry. When connecting sites on peer VMware vCenter instances in the same or different VCF SSO domains, PNR supports credential-based authentication during site pairing. The configuration import/export tool supports authentication using either local vCenter credentials or a dedicated service account at a configurable path, enabling non-interactive authentication for operational tooling.

For all TLS connections to the PNR server, an SSL/TLS endpoint certificate is required. PNR defaults to a strong TLS protocol version, and administrators can configure the minimum TLS version through the envoy proxy settings by editing the tls_minimum_protocol_version parameter in envoy-proxy-config.json. When the minimum version is modified, all occurrences of this setting must be changed consistently across the system. PNR supports custom SSL/TLS certificates signed by a certificate authority as an alternative to self-signed certificates. Certificates used as endpoint certificates must be server certificates with x509v3 Extended Key Usage indicating TLS Web Server Authentication. The Configuration REST API provides an endpoint to generate a new key and certificate signing request (CSR) for signing, supporting automated certificate lifecycle operations. Remote site configuration workflows, including those executed through the VCF Operations orchestrator plug-in, require certificate import to establish authenticated login to the remote site.

The cyber recovery connector uses OAuth 2.0 for programmatic authentication with recovery services. Broadcom automatically rotates the connector's OAuth app secret every three months, and the connector authenticates using the rotating credential without administrator intervention.

PNR uses certificates and keys as foundational components of its security infrastructure. The PNR Appliance Management Interface requires admin authentication to access reconfiguration, service management, and network configuration functions. The appliance supports importing server certificates signed by a certificate authority to establish secure communications.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides mechanisms to align cryptographic operations with applicable requirements for cryptographic module security strength through its FIPS 140-3 mode, configurable SSL/TLS profiles, and Hardware Security Module (HSM) integration.

FIPS mode is available as an explicit deployment option for the Avi Controller and Service Engines. When FIPS mode is activated, Avi restricts all cryptographic operations to FIPS-approved algorithms, using the OpenSSL FIPS Provider version 3.1.2, which has been validated at FIPS 140-3 Security Level 1 and awarded Certificate #4985 by the Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP). FIPS 140-3 is the successor to FIPS 140-2 and aligns U.S. and Canadian cryptographic module standards with the international standards ISO/IEC 19790:2012 and ISO/IEC 24759:2017, broadening its applicability across jurisdictions. FIPS 140-3 introduces stronger protections for sensitive security parameters, including stricter handling of keys, authentication data, and random number generation, and tighter controls over module integrity and module updates. FIPS mode is enabled per Controller cluster deployment and applies to both the Controller and Service Engines.

For deployments requiring hardware-backed cryptographic key management, Avi integrates with Thales Luna Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), including the Thales Luna Network HSM. The Controller uses mutual certificate authentication with the HSM device during registration, and Service Engines support dedicated HSM interfaces for cryptographic key management and certificate operations. HSM groups are configured through Templates > Security > HSM Groups in the Avi Controller UI, allowing Service Engines to authenticate with HSM devices for secure storage of SSL certificates and keys. Cryptographic key material is stored and processed in the HSM rather than in software, providing a hardware root of trust for key protection.

SSL/TLS Profiles, found at Templates > Security > SSL/TLS Profile in the Avi Controller UI, allow administrators to define the accepted protocol versions and prioritized cipher suite list for virtual services, covering both classic and modern TLS versions including support for the latest protocol versions. Avi includes a curated list of ciphers considered reasonably strong for SSL/TLS configuration. The profile design explicitly acknowledges the trade-off between expanding the accepted cipher and version set for broader client compatibility and the potential reduction in security posture, giving organizations direct control over that trade-off. Avi Controller SSL certificates use SHA-256 with RSA encryption as the signature algorithm.

In Kubernetes-based deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator, PKIProfile Custom Resource Definitions enable certificate-based authentication and encryption for secure communication with backend services. The System-Standard SSL Profile supports SSL/TLS re-encryption for traffic to backend servers using modern cipher suites.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control IA-08

> Uniquely identify and authenticate non-organizational users or processes acting on behalf of non-organizational users.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-03

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides a centralized identity federation architecture that supports authentication, authorization, and auditing of both organizational and non-organizational users. Third-party users and service accounts can be uniquely identified and managed through integration with external identity providers, giving administrators a single point of control over who accesses the platform and what they can do.

VCF SSO is the central authentication service for the platform. All user authentication flows through VCF SSO, which issues tokens that establish identity and trust with API endpoints. VCF SSO supports federation with enterprise identity providers through OIDC, OAuth 2.0, and Generic SAML 2.0, so organizations can connect their existing directory services and enforce their own authentication policies, including multifactor authentication. Federating VMware vCenter to an enterprise identity provider alleviates the burden of identity management and provides flexible options such as multifactor authentication and automatic registration and termination of users across services. This federation model allows organizations to manage third-party user identities in their existing identity provider and have those identities flow through to VCF without creating local accounts. The VCF Identity Broker provisions users and groups from the configured identity provider, enabling centralized access control across the platform. vCenter also supports the System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM) protocol for automating user and group synchronization from external identity providers, with explicitly documented federation configurations for Okta and PingFederate as named OIDC partners.

Multiple VCF components support external identity provider integration. NSX Manager can be configured to authenticate users from identity management providers and integrates with LDAP, vIDM, and OIDC to verify user identities and determine access to services based on user group membership. NSX Manager also exposes API endpoints for authentication policy settings, allowing administrators to validate authentication configuration across nodes programmatically. The Supervisor component supports registration of external identity providers using OAuth 2.0 and OIDC protocols, and uses the Pinniped Supervisor and Concierge components to facilitate federated authentication for users and groups when an external identity provider is configured. Supervisor can be configured with any OIDC-compliant identity provider such as Okta, Workspace ONE Access, or Dex to authenticate users connecting to VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters, and custom ClusterClass configurations can authenticate developers using their existing corporate identity systems. Once authenticated via OIDC, third-party users are subject to Kubernetes RBAC; ClusterRoleBindings grant roles to OIDC-authenticated users via identifiers in the format oidc:user@example.com, giving administrators precisely scoped access to specific namespaces or resources without extending broader platform privileges. Kubernetes OIDC authentication produces a UserInfo object derived from the token subject and roles claims, and userValidationRules can enforce constraints on mapped identities, such as preventing the reserved system: prefix in usernames. For production clusters with multiple users accessing the Kubernetes API directly, OIDC is the recommended authentication mechanism; OIDC tokens are short-lived and subject to the identity provider's revocation mechanisms, so deprovisioning a user in the upstream identity provider removes their ability to authenticate to the cluster. VCF Automation allows each organization to define an identity provider that it shares with other applications, so that users authenticate to the identity provider to obtain a token they can use to access the organization. VCF Automation also supports importing provider users from SAML, OIDC, or VCF SSO identity providers, with configurable authentication mechanisms including redirections to external identity providers. Imported users log in with the credentials established in their OIDC identity provider. The vCenter Client Credentials grant type allows application-to-identity-provider authentication without a human user present, supporting machine-to-machine authentication scenarios.

For environments using Microsoft Active Directory, organizations can use user identities contained in a Windows Server domain controller or Active Directory service across their virtual infrastructure, providing another path for centrally managing third-party user identities through an established directory service. VCF SSO also supports user and group import for access control from configured identity sources. VMware ESX and vCenter validate user credentials against configured identity stores.

For programmatic and service-to-service access, Principal Identity users authenticate using certificates, providing unique identification and auditability for automated processes. A Principal Identity user's certificate must be SHA256 based. NSX Federation creates dedicated Principal Identity users with specific roles (such as Auditor) on each Global Manager when a Local Manager is registered, demonstrating how even automated inter-component communication uses uniquely identifiable accounts. The VCF Automation API supports endpoints for retrieving organizational context for authenticated users, and the Identity API authenticates and manages the authorization provider, giving programmatic control over identity and access management. Valid session identifiers establish trust with API endpoints, providing traceability for each authenticated session.

For non-human processes running inside VKS clusters, Kubernetes ServiceAccounts provide a distinct, namespaced identity separate from human user accounts. Each ServiceAccount authenticates with the username system:serviceaccount:(NAMESPACE):(SERVICEACCOUNT) and is assigned to namespace-scoped groups, enabling RBAC policies to authorize specific API operations without sharing human user credentials. Scoping IAM role trust policies to specific ServiceAccounts and namespaces enforces least privilege for workloads that access external services. Where VKS clusters integrate with AWS cloud services, IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) replaces long-lived static access keys with an OIDC-based identity federation model that provides workloads with short-lived, least-privileged credentials on demand. Separating ServiceAccount creation from human user onboarding supports differentiated audit strategies, since audit events carry the ServiceAccount identity rather than a generic shared credential. Third-party security software running in the cluster can use ServiceAccount identities to group pods into distinct security contexts for access control purposes. ArgoCD, deployable as a VCF Service on VKS clusters, supports OIDC authentication with global RBAC policies for federated identity and role-based access control, with both OIDC and local account authentication available depending on deployment requirements.

On the authorization side, VCF enforces role-based access control where authenticated users can view objects or invoke operations on the server depending on the permissions associated with their account. The vCenter authorization model provides consistent security control regardless of how users connect to the environment. Organization owners and Enterprise Admins can add, manage, and remove users, controlling what level of access each user receives. NSX Manager local user accounts are created with a default Auditor role, and role assignments can be changed as needed. NSX authentication best practices include defining access controls based on user roles and permissions and integrating with identity providers.

Each organization within VCF Automation has its own identity source and organization portal where users log in to consume resources and services. This multi-tenant isolation helps organizations maintain separation between internal and third-party user populations while still leveraging centralized identity infrastructure.

Audit logging captures authentication and authorization events across VCF components, providing a record of third-party user activity for review and compliance purposes. All API calls to NSX Manager are subject to authentication and authorization and generate audit logs. NSX generates audit logs for events such as logon, logoff, and access to resources, with each audit log containing the timestamp, source, result, and a description of the event. NSX Manager and NSX Edge both include a dedicated audit role with separate credentials; the NSX Manager audit user has read-only privileges to the NSX environment and is not active by default, requiring explicit activation after installation by an admin through the CLI. This separation of the audit role from administrative roles supports segregation of duties for audit functions. VCF Operations generates audit events across categories including access, access control, account management, configuration, data access, network, notification, permissions, session, system, policy, and firewall actions, which can be filtered, searched, and reviewed in detail. VCF Operations Orchestrator tracks the user who initiated each workflow run, providing audit accountability for automated management tasks executed against the platform. At the Kubernetes layer, the cluster API server records authentication events, API requests, and authorization decisions for all users and ServiceAccounts. Kubernetes audit annotations include authorization.k8s.io/decision to record each authorization decision; for OIDC-authenticated third-party users, audit entries capture the username and groups asserted by the identity provider, enabling traceability back to the individual; for ServiceAccounts, entries capture the service account name and namespace. Organizations can configure the Kubernetes audit policy to define which request types and API verbs are logged and at what verbosity, and route those events to a centralized log collector.

While VCF provides the technical infrastructure for centralized AAA of third-party users, the organization is responsible for defining policies that govern which third-party users receive access, establishing onboarding and offboarding procedures, conducting periodic access reviews, configuring the external identity provider with appropriate authentication strength requirements, and determining which AAA solutions to approve and how to handle AAA for external service providers.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) supports identification and access management for third-party and non-organizational users of AI services through OpenID Connect (OIDC) integration. PAIS activation requires coordination with the OIDC provider administrator, and the PAISConfiguration resource specifies an issuer URL, an OIDC client ID (the pais-authentication-id value provided by the OIDC administrator), and the scopes openid, groups, and offline_access. The OIDC client ID, the groups claim name, and additional accepted OAuth 2.0 audiences are individually configurable so that PAIS can interoperate with an external provider's existing tenant model rather than requiring a PAIS-specific identity store.

Authorization for PAIS is enforced through OIDC group membership. The authorizedGroups setting in the PAISConfiguration restricts access to user accounts whose OIDC Access Token includes membership in one or more designated groups, with example group names such as group-for-pais-access-01. The groups claim is included in the Access Token, and a corresponding groups scope is created in the OIDC configuration. This is the primary PAIS-native mechanism for uniquely identifying non-organizational users by OIDC identity claims and authorizing their access based on group membership in the external provider.

The Grafana observability interface for PAIS authenticates through the same OIDC provider, so visualization of PAIS metrics for monitoring and audit purposes is gated by the same identity and group controls. PAIS metrics can be exported to observability platforms to support monitoring and audit review of third-party user activity. Audit recording of authentication and authorization events at the platform layer is provided by the underlying VCF infrastructure rather than by PAIS-native audit trails dedicated to non-organizational user activity.

For third-party data sources and registries, PAIS Knowledge Bases authenticate to Confluence using either a user name and password or a personal access token, and to Microsoft SharePoint On-Premises using a user name and password. PAIS integrates with Harbor registries as a model storage backend, requiring authentication and certificate-based trust for model retrieval, and the oras login command authenticates to a private Harbor registry using user name and password credentials for catalog and image operations. These mechanisms address the identification and access control dimensions of the control for the third-party services that PAIS itself reaches out to.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) supports authentication and authorization of users who access database services, including third-party users and service accounts that connect to managed databases. DSM's identity mechanisms apply to all user types, and authentication controls can be configured to restrict access based on identity, source address, and authentication method.

For administrative access to the DSM management plane, DSM supports both local user accounts and LDAPS integration with an enterprise directory service (LDAP or Active Directory). An installation can choose to use one or both identity sources, and DSM manages them independently. DSM can be configured to use an LDAP server for both authentication and authorization. When Directory Service credentials are unavailable, DSM falls back to local user authentication.

For PostgreSQL database instances, DSM provides host-based authentication (HBA) configuration. The pg_hba.conf file controls which clients may connect to a database by specifying connection type, client address, requested database, and user name. Each connection attempt is matched against an ordered list of rules, and the first matching record determines the authentication method applied. Default entries use SCRAM-SHA-256 for local accounts including the pgadmin and postgres_exporter service accounts, and administrators can add LDAP entries to authenticate database users through Active Directory. Database instances can also be configured to use directory service authentication after initial creation using the directory-service-authentication setting. MySQL instances similarly support Active Directory authentication via LDAPS.

DSM adds SQL Server as a managed database type with its own authentication model. SQL Server clusters use contained database users, where users authenticate directly against the database and identity information resides within the database rather than at a server level. SQL Server clusters can be integrated with Active Directory, enabling Kerberos-based authentication and Windows Authentication for server-level logins. Windows Principals can be authorized as cluster administrators through T-SQL or the DSM API. Active Directory integration also supports single sign-on for clients that use Kerberos authentication, such as SQL Server Management Studio running on a Windows machine joined to the same domain. SQL Server Authentication logins can be created for clusters that do not require Windows Authentication. DSM supports two primary administrator user identities for SQL Server: SQL User or Windows Principal, with Windows Principal available for clusters integrated with Active Directory.

Third-party processes and service integrations that receive DSM event notifications through webhooks are authenticated using credentials. DSM webhooks support basic authentication with an Authorization User and Authorization Password, restricting webhook delivery to authorized endpoints.

Metrics forwarding to Prometheus-compatible endpoints supports configurable authentication, including certificate-only, basic authentication, or a combination, allowing organizations to control which external monitoring systems can pull DSM metrics.

The DSM Consumption Operator, which enables self-service database provisioning through Kubernetes-native interfaces, authenticates with DSM infrastructure using an authentication secret named dsm-auth-creds.

DSM audit logs track user actions performed through the UI and API, recording the source of each operation as either a user identifier (Email ID) for user-initiated events or System for events initiated by DSM without user intervention. For SQL Server clusters, DSM provides configurable server auditing with preset audits for login tracking and log retention, and can create a simple login event audit based on the Login Auditing option selection.

DSM does not itself categorize users as organizational versus non-organizational. Meeting this control fully requires organizational processes beyond what DSM provides: identifying which users or processes are third-party, ensuring they receive appropriately scoped accounts or directory entries, and reviewing audit logs to confirm that third-party access remains within authorized boundaries. DSM provides the authentication and access control mechanisms that support those organizational processes.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Avi Load Balancer provides authentication and authorization mechanisms across three planes: the administrative control plane, the License Hub component, and the application data plane.

For the administrative control plane, Avi supports federated authentication by delegating identity verification to external AAA servers rather than managing credentials locally. The Controller's Authentication Profile can be configured with LDAP or SAML providers, allowing remote users to authenticate against a central directory or identity service. Authorization Mapping Profiles provide dynamic tenant and role assignment during login using group membership evaluation, so group memberships retrieved from an LDAP server translate directly into Controller permissions without manual per-user assignment. HTTP authentication on virtual service policies supports a Required User Group Membership configuration that restricts access to members of specific groups.

The Avi License Hub includes its own authentication layer supporting Active Directory and OpenLDAP as identity sources for user authentication. License Hub also maintains a dedicated local Audit account with the auditor role, which has full access to APIs and services for system auditing, and a local Admin account responsible for user creation, authorization, and tenant management.

For the application data plane, Avi virtual services can enforce client authentication before traffic reaches back-end workloads. OAuth 2.0 provides secure delegated access where an authorization server issues tokens to clients on behalf of resource owners; Avi validates identity by consulting the UserInfo endpoint or reading claims from the ID token when configured for OIDC. OAuth is the recommended approach when third-party applications need access, as it avoids storing resource owner credentials in clear text on those applications. SAML securely verifies user identity without storing credentials on multiple untrusted devices. DataScript can check whether a user ID appears in an approved group list and redirect unauthenticated users to a login page, enabling identity-based access control at the proxy layer.

In Kubernetes environments managed through the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO), the SSORule custom resource definition configures either OAuth/OIDC properties or SAML service provider properties along with the SSO policy, extending centralized authentication to Kubernetes ingress virtual services. Avi GSLB supports creation of non-admin users with assigned roles, providing granular access control for global server load balancing operations.

Together these mechanisms support the authentication and authorization dimensions the control requires. Authentication is delegated to a central identity provider; authorization is enforced through role and tenant mapping at login; and Avi's audit and event logging captures administrative and configuration activity. For the organizational dimension of this control, the external identity providers must be deployed and operated by the organization, role mapping rules must be defined for each population of third-party users, and the governance program determining which third parties are authorized to access services must be maintained separately.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IA-08(01)

> CREDENTIALS FROM OTHER AGENCIES Accept and electronically verify Personal Identity Verification-compliant credentials from other federal agencies.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-03.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credential acceptance requires mechanisms that validate X.509 certificates issued by government or third-party PKI infrastructures, including certificate path validation and revocation checking against the issuing authority's Certificate Revocation Lists.

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides a PKI Application Profile that enables client certificate authentication at the TLS termination layer for protected application workloads. The PKI Application Profile allows administrators to configure trusted CA certificates, which can include external PIV issuing authorities and intermediate CAs from third-party organizations. The profile supports Certificate Revocation List (CRL) checking, which verifies that a presented PIV credential has not been revoked by the issuing authority. Client certificate authentication can be enforced in REQUIRE mode, where certificate presentation is mandatory, or REQUEST mode with a PKI profile, where the certificate is validated when presented. This flexibility allows administrators to tune enforcement posture at the virtual service layer based on the sensitivity of the protected workload. Configuration is available through the Avi Controller web interface, REST API, or CLI under Certificate Management. Avi also supports a CertAuth Service for pure TLS mutual authentication, providing an alternative enforcement point for client certificate presentation at the proxy layer.

Where organizations use federated authentication, Avi supports SAML-based client authentication in scenarios where a PIV-capable identity provider issues SAML assertions following smart card authentication. Avi operates as a SAML Service Provider and supports SP-initiated Single Sign-On with third-party identity providers. After successful user credential validation at the identity provider level, the identity provider's SSO service returns a SAML Response that Avi consumes on behalf of the protected workload. Avi has verified interoperability with identity providers including Google, Okta, and OneLogin for SSO authentication. This federated path allows PIV-based authentication flows to reach protected workloads through Avi without requiring the application to handle certificate validation directly.

Organizations should configure the PKI Application Profile with the CA trust chain corresponding to the third-party PIV PKI and enable CRL checking to maintain revocation validation. For federated scenarios, a SAML identity provider capable of performing PIV smart card authentication is required upstream of the Avi deployment.

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IA-08(02)

> AUTHENTICATORS (a) Accept only external authenticators that are NIST-compliant; and (b) Document and maintain a list of accepted external authenticators.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-03.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides application-layer authentication mechanisms that allow virtual services to accept credentials from third-party identity providers, including those operating under FICAM-approved frameworks.

For SAML-based authentication, Avi acts as a SAML Service Provider and supports SP-initiated Single Sign-On (SSO) with external identity providers. Virtual services are attached to an authentication policy that redirects authentication requests to the configured identity provider and validates the resulting SAML assertion. This enables standards-based assertion processing without requiring the application itself to implement identity provider interaction.

For OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) flows, Avi Controller supports OIDC-based identity verification for client authentication. OAuth addresses the limitations of direct credential sharing by allowing an identity provider to issue access tokens to third-party applications with user approval, without requiring credential sharing. Authorization Policies or DataScript can apply per-request authorization logic within the OAuth/OIDC flow, and virtual services can be configured with OAuth and OIDC authentication policies under Access Policy settings.

Avi supports LDAP authentication through SSO policy templates that can be created and attached to individual virtual services via an Access Policy binding. The LDAP SSO policy gives operators fine-grained control over which virtual services require LDAP-backed authentication. Avi's documentation recommends using federated authentication to centralize the authentication process by delegating to an identity provider rather than managing authentication locally.

Organizations that operate FICAM-approved identity services can integrate those providers with Avi's SAML, OIDC, or LDAP authentication profiles to accept FICAM-approved credentials at the application delivery layer.

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IA-08(04)

> Conform to the following profiles for identity management [Assignment: organization- defined identity management profiles].

**SCF Controls:** IAC-03.3

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IA-11

> Re-authentication Require users to re-authenticate when [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances or situations requiring re-authentication].

**SCF Controls:** IAC-14

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several technical mechanisms that enforce re-authentication under defined conditions, though the organization must establish the specific policies governing when and how often re-authentication is required.

VCF SSO manages session state across the platform. In a single sign-on environment, a user provides credentials once, and components perform operations based on the original authentication. When a user's browser detects an existing session, the authentication flow provides an SSO experience without requiring additional user interaction. When the session expires or is invalidated, the user is required to re-authenticate. VCF SSO supports modern or directory-based authentication through a configurable identity provider, which allows organizations to delegate re-authentication policy enforcement to their identity provider. Organizations using external identity providers can configure session timeouts, conditional access policies, and step-up authentication requirements that force re-authentication based on risk signals, time elapsed, or resource sensitivity.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters provisioned on the Supervisor platform support two authentication methods for cluster access: VCF SSO and an OIDC-compliant external identity provider. When an external provider is used, VKS clusters integrate with it through the Pinniped authentication service, enabling the external identity provider to enforce its own session management policies, including step-up authentication, inactivity timeouts, and forced re-authentication under organization-defined circumstances such as role changes or location changes. Kubernetes OIDC authentication uses an id_token with a defined expiry; once the token expires, the client must obtain a new id_token using a refresh_token and client_secret, requiring re-authentication at an interval determined by the token lifetime configured in the identity provider. The Kubernetes hardening guidance recommends using authentication providers that can control how long issued tokens are available and setting short lifetimes where possible. The guidance also recommends enabling as few authentication mechanisms as possible, to prevent users from retaining cluster access after it is no longer needed. TokenRequest API tokens are not recommended for user authentication because no revocation method is available; for scenarios where revocation-based forced re-authentication is a requirement, OIDC-based tokens or other provider-managed token types are the appropriate choice. Organizations using custom ClusterClass definitions can authenticate developers against existing corporate identity systems, inheriting whatever session and re-authentication policies those systems enforce.

Argo CD, available as a standard package on VKS clusters through the Argo CD Operator, supports OIDC authentication with global RBAC policies for federated identity management, as well as local account authentication with role-based access control defined through policy rules and group mappings. When Argo CD is configured with an external OIDC identity provider, session expiration and re-authentication follow that provider's token lifetime settings, extending the Supervisor platform's OIDC-based re-authentication model to GitOps workflows running on VKS clusters.

VCF SSO also supports holder-of-key tokens that embed security artifacts tied to the authenticated entity. These tokens have defined lifetimes after which re-authentication is required. The holder-of-key mechanism uses public key cryptography with RSA-SHA256 signatures, so that token-based authentication is bound to the original credential holder. An Issue method in the VCF SSO API allows clients to acquire these tokens, and the LoginByToken method authenticates clients using them.

VCF's token-based authentication model enforces re-authentication through token expiration for API and service account access. Service accounts authenticate using API access tokens that rotate after each use. Access tokens normally last one hour, after which the client must re-authenticate. These access tokens are based on a refresh token that normally lasts 90 days but can be revoked earlier and optionally rotated with each access token request. When a refresh token expires or is revoked, the service or user must perform a full re-authentication to obtain new credentials. VCF Operations provides a dedicated API endpoint (/v1/tokens/refresh-token) for token refresh operations, allowing programmatic credential rotation. The VCF Consumption CLI provides a `vcf context refresh` command that re-authenticates the user for a given context when existing tokens or credentials have expired; the CLI also supports automatic token renewal using a refresh token so that brief expirations do not interrupt interactive sessions.

The Kubernetes API server on the Supervisor platform automatically reloads JWT authenticators when the authentication configuration file is modified, allowing authentication policy changes such as revoking authenticators or adjusting session parameters to take effect on incoming requests without requiring a server restart. The `apiserver_authentication_config_controller_automatic_reloads_total` metric tracks these reloads by status and API server identity, supporting observability of authentication policy changes. A companion metric, `apiserver_authentication_config_controller_automatic_reload_last_timestamp_seconds`, records the timestamp of the most recent reload, enabling operators to confirm that authentication configuration updates have taken effect.

Session timeout settings are configurable across VCF components and trigger re-authentication. VCF Operations for Networks provides a configurable user session timeout, set to 15 minutes by default. ESX provides a Host Client Session Timeout setting that controls the duration before an inactive Host Client session is terminated. When any of these session timeouts expire, the user must re-authenticate to continue.

VCF Automation's SSO integration checks for existing user sessions before granting access. If no valid session exists, the user is directed through the full authentication flow, with the identity provider shared with other applications for token-based login. VCF Operations similarly supports single sign-on authentication, where session validity determines whether re-authentication is needed. When the single sign-on source is configured correctly, users can log in without entering credentials, but expiration of the underlying session forces re-authentication.

Password expiration policies provide another re-authentication trigger. VCF Operations defaults to passwords expiring every 90 days, with users receiving notification five days before expiration. NSX Manager passwords also expire 90 days after the last change by default, with warning messages seven days before expiration. NSX supports configurable password expiration settings with a valid range of 1 to 9,999 days, and expiration can be changed or deactivated for each user by the admin user or any user with the Enterprise Admin role. When a password expires, the user must set a new password, which constitutes a forced re-authentication event.

Password resets also trigger re-authentication. When a password reset is completed for a local user account in NSX Manager, the user is prompted to change their password during login. Resetting a password for any local user on one NSX Manager in a cluster automatically resets the password for the other managers in the cluster, forcing re-authentication on all nodes.

NSX Manager provides configurable authentication policies that affect re-authentication behavior. The API authentication policy defaults to a maximum of five authentication failures before account lockout. The lockout period settings can be modified to change the default lockout and reset periods from 900 seconds to alternative values. After lockout, the user must wait for the lockout period to expire before re-authenticating, or an administrator must manually unlock the account.

On VKS cluster nodes, the `requirePasswordOnSudo` setting in `osConfiguration.user` can be configured to require password re-authentication when performing administrative operations through sudo, providing a re-authentication checkpoint for privilege escalation on cluster nodes.

Organizations deploying VCF should define their re-authentication requirements and configure VCF's session timeouts, token lifetimes, password expiration policies, and identity provider settings accordingly. The configurable identity provider integration is the primary mechanism for enforcing organization-specific re-authentication policies, as external identity providers can evaluate context such as device posture, location, and time since last authentication to determine when re-authentication is necessary. For Kubernetes workloads on the Supervisor platform, the choice of OIDC token lifetimes in the identity provider and the use of short-lived service account tokens are the primary levers for re-authentication frequency. The overall re-authentication policy, including what events or intervals should require it, is an organizational decision that VCF's mechanisms enforce but do not define.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

Within VMware vDefend's own management interface, the Security Services Platform (SSP) enforces password policies for local user accounts, including password expiration, mandatory password change, and on-demand password reset. Both the SSP UI and the SSP Installer UI support resetting passwords for the Admin and Audit user accounts; when credentials are reset or expire, users must re-authenticate on their next login. The Admin user can reset the Audit user's password through the User Management interface, which requires Admin privileges accessed via HTTPS to the platform FQDN.

Changes to SSP authentication provider configuration, such as toggling LDAP authentication status, cause the Authelia server and Authserver to reboot, during which users cannot log in to the platform. This reboot prevents logins during the service restart, requiring users to re-authenticate once the platform returns to service following the authentication configuration change.

When vDefend Identity Firewall (IDFW) is deployed with Active Directory integration, AD administrators can force user logouts when group membership is modified so that updated IDFW policies take effect immediately. IDFW detects user logins using Event Log Scraping, which monitors login events from domain controller event log servers, and can apply security policies to virtual machines and physical devices based on the Active Directory group membership of the logged-in user. When a forced logout is issued following a group membership change, users must re-authenticate to re-establish their session context, and the updated IDFW rules apply to their subsequent traffic.

Re-authentication controls for end users, networked devices, and applications outside of vDefend's management plane are outside its scope and must be governed by broader identity and access management solutions.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) supports several authentication management capabilities that contribute to re-authentication requirements.

DSM provides the `force_password_change` flag for local user accounts, which administrators can configure to require users to complete a password change before regaining access. This flag is managed through DSM's local user administration and allows administrators to mandate credential rotation when circumstances require it, such as after a suspected compromise or during routine credential lifecycle management. User accounts also track `failed_login_count`, `last_failed_login_time`, and `locked_state_start_time` alongside the forced password change flag, giving administrators visibility into authentication state when deciding whether to trigger a forced change.

For local user accounts, DSM offers password reset capabilities through the Forgot Password link on the login screen, which initiates a reset workflow via email to the user's registered address. Password reset links are active for 20 minutes. When an SMTP server is not configured, administrators can reset user passwords directly through the DSM UI or API, with new credentials communicated to the user separately. To prohibit user access while keeping databases running, administrators can reset the user password and delegate management to a different user account without deleting the user.

DSM integrates with enterprise directory services through LDAPS, supporting LDAP and Active Directory authentication for user login. When directory service credentials are non-functional, DSM falls back to local user authentication, so login precedence must be considered when managing user accounts across both sources. The Enable Directory Service Authentication toggle on database instances can be activated or deactivated to control which authentication pathway is in use. When LDAPS is configured, the session management and re-authentication policies of the directory service apply to DSM-authenticated users, extending organizational re-authentication requirements to DSM access without requiring DSM-specific configuration. LDAPS users who need a password reset contact the LDAPS administrator rather than using the local reset mechanism.

DSM adds SQL Server as a supported database engine, with Kerberos-based authentication and single sign-on through Active Directory domain resources. SQL Server data service policies can be configured to allow Windows Principal user type authentication, enabling users to authenticate using their Active Directory credentials. This extends directory-service-managed authentication, and the re-authentication policies of the connected Active Directory, to SQL Server database instances alongside the MySQL and PostgreSQL LDAPS path. DSM can also automatically create an audit for login events based on the Login Auditing option during SQL Server cluster creation, providing a mechanism to track authentication activity.

Organizations should note that DSM does not provide a native mechanism for defining re-authentication intervals or triggers based on specific conditions such as elapsed session time or privilege escalation events. The `force_password_change` flag and administrator-initiated resets address circumstance-driven re-authentication for local accounts, but broader re-authentication orchestration, particularly for time-based or event-based triggers, requires organizational processes and policies implemented at the identity provider or organizational level. Administrators with responsibilities for DSM should establish procedures that use the forced password change and reset capabilities in response to the organization-defined circumstances that require re-authentication.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides re-authentication mechanisms at two layers: the application traffic layer, where it acts as an authentication proxy for workload-facing virtual services, and the Controller management plane, where it enforces session control for administrative access.

For application-layer re-authentication, Avi virtual services support JWT-based authentication policies. When a JWT token expires, the client must re-authenticate or use a refresh token to obtain a new JWT. Token expiry is the primary enforcement boundary for re-authentication under this model. Avi Controller authentication policies allow configuration of a default authentication profile reference and named authentication rules with path-based matching criteria, controlled through the authentication_policy setting. Individual authentication rules can be enabled with match conditions targeting specific request paths, giving operators granular control over where and under what conditions authentication policies apply.

For SAML-based application authentication, Avi virtual services can be attached to an SSO policy via the sso_policy_ref setting to enforce authentication and authorization. SSO policies are configured with a default authentication profile reference (default_auth_profile_ref) that specifies the SAML profile to use. SAML authentication rules support two actions: Skip Authentication, which bypasses authentication for matched paths, and Default Authentication, which enforces SAML authentication for matched paths. This policy model allows operators to implement organization-defined re-authentication requirements at the application layer with path-level granularity. OAuth/OIDC-type SSO policies similarly enforce single sign-on authentication for client and resource server interactions, and the same path-based policy framework applies.

For Kubernetes workloads, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) manages authtoken refresh automatically and does not require a reboot after authtoken refresh, maintaining continuous authentication state for ingress and load balancing services deployed on Kubernetes clusters.

For Controller management-plane sessions, Avi LDAP basic authentication supports a configurable reconnect timeout (reconnect-timeout) enforced on connections to LDAP servers. This setting governs how quickly the Controller re-establishes its LDAP binding. Avi Controller also enforces credential expiry for locally authenticated users; once credentials expire, users can re-authenticate through API calls, user account password change, or the Forgot Password workflow if an email address is configured. Organizations that integrate Avi with VCF SSO also inherit session timeout controls from the VCF SSO layer for administrative users.

Organizations define the specific circumstances requiring re-authentication, including token lifetime durations, session idle timeouts, and the set of application paths subject to mandatory authentication enforcement. Avi provides the policy framework and enforcement mechanisms; the scope and timing of re-authentication events are determined by how those policies are configured.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control IA-12

> Identity Proofing a. Identity proof users that require accounts for logical access to systems based on appropriate identity assurance level requirements as specified in applicable standards and guidelines;  b. Resolve user identities to a unique individual; and  c. Collect, validate, and verify identity evidence.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-28

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IA-12(01)

> Require that the registration process to receive an account for logical access includes supervisor or sponsor authorization.

**SCF Controls:** IAC-28.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several technical controls that support management approval workflows for new accounts and permission changes, though the formal approval process itself requires organizational procedures and may benefit from integration with external identity governance or ITSM tools.

Account creation across VCF components is restricted to highly privileged administrators. In NSX Manager, only users with the Enterprise Admin role can create new local user accounts or guest user accounts. In VCF Automation, only a System Administrator can create other system administrator and user accounts in the provider organization. Provider management service accounts are created by administrators who hold specific "View Service Accounts" and "Manage Service Accounts" privileges. Service accounts authenticate using API access tokens that rotate after each use per RFC 6749 section 6, with access tokens normally lasting one hour and based on a refresh token that normally lasts 90 days but can be revoked earlier. When authenticating with a service account, applications cannot perform user management tasks, create API refresh tokens, or manage other service accounts, limiting the blast radius of programmatic access. For user group management in VCF Operations for Networks, the user performing management operations must have admin-level permissions. User accounts must be created and activated in the system before role assignments can be made, establishing a clear two-step process where account provisioning and privilege assignment are distinct actions. These restrictions concentrate account creation authority in a small number of administrators, supporting a controlled process where account requests flow through authorized personnel.

VCF Automation includes a configurable approval policy framework that organizations can use for account-related workflows. Approval policies can be scoped at the organization level with configurable criteria and approval types, such as user-based approval with specific approver assignment by email address. When multiple valid approval policies apply, all designated approvers are added to the approval chain, creating a multi-level review process. The approval mode can be set to require all designated approvers to approve before an action executes. Approvers must hold at least the Organization User role, and the Organization Administrator role can create and manage policies. The Manage Association permission controls who can update policy associations for objects, allowing granular delegation of administrative responsibilities. Organizations can also create custom user roles that include the ability to review and respond to approval requests, allowing designated managers to participate in the approval workflow without granting broader administrative access.

Service accounts in VCF Automation are granted access using the Request Service Account Authorization process, which restricts token access so that only the application that must use the token has sole access and no other actor can retrieve it. After access is granted, the authenticated client application receives an OAuth refresh token and an access token representing its first VCF Automation session for immediate use.

VCF Automation integrates with ServiceNow ITSM through a plug-in that supports sub-workflows containing approval group activities. The system administrator creates a sub-workflow with approval group activity and approval group information, and by default three approval activities are added to the sub-workflow. Custom workflows in ServiceNow can route approval requests to different approval groups based on user entitlement membership, enabling organizations to route account and permission change requests through their standard change management process.

NSX Manager provides audit logging for account lifecycle events. Any user account change in NSX Manager is audited, and the audit trail captures the username, operation type, operation status, and new values for changes including role assignments. Changes to local user accounts are logged separately, providing visibility into both account creation and permission modifications. This audit capability allows organizations to verify after the fact that proper approvals were obtained before accounts were created or permissions changed.

Organizations should apply the principle of least privilege when granting account permissions. User accounts should be given the minimal number of privileges on the system required for their job functions. While VCF provides the technical enforcement layer, approval workflow tooling, and audit trail, organizations need to define and document the formal management approval process, designate approvers for different account types, and periodically review that account provisioning follows the established workflow. Organizations that require a dedicated identity governance console typically integrate VCF's identity infrastructure with external IAM or ITSM platforms to implement the full management approval lifecycle.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) structures account and permission management through an administrative role model that requires a privileged administrator to create accounts and assign roles, providing a technical control point where organizational approval workflows can be enforced.

DSM supports two identity sources for user accounts: a local user database and a centrally managed LDAPS directory. Local user creation is an administrative action performed by a DSM Admin, who must specify each user's role (Admin or User), email address, and namespace-level access controls at the time of account creation. No new DSM account can be created without explicit action from an authorized administrator.
