These audit records are forwarded to Events, Syslog, and Splunk, providing integration points for an organization's centralized incident monitoring and log aggregation infrastructure.

Avi does not provide an end-to-end incident management and tracking system. Defining incident categories, assigning ownership, tracking status through resolution, communicating updates to internal stakeholders at each stage, and maintaining incident records beyond Avi's application-layer logs and reports are organizational responsibilities. Avi's reporting and alerting capabilities feed the organization's incident management process as inputs alongside other tools and workflows.

### Control IR-05(01)

> Incident Monitoring | Automated Tracking, Data Collection, and Analysis Track incidents and collect and analyze incident information using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].

**SCF Controls:** IRO-09.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides automated mechanisms through VCF Operations and its associated components that support the tracking, collection, and analysis of information related to cybersecurity and data protection incidents. These capabilities address significant portions of this control, though a complete incident tracking and analysis program requires additional organizational tooling and processes.

VCF Operations includes a dedicated incident tracking system that captures structured metadata for each incident, including state, resolution code, resolution notes, impact based on business criticality, urgency based on resolution timeframe, priority for resolution sequencing, severity level, and approval workflow documentation. The Troubleshooting Incidents page allows operators to manage incidents, start new ones, and view summaries of existing incidents. This structured approach allows operations teams to systematically track incidents from detection through resolution while maintaining an auditable record of each step. For security-specific visibility, VCF Operations provides a Security Operations dashboard that presents a consolidated view of the security posture across the VCF environment, giving security teams a starting point for identifying and investigating potential incidents.

The alerting and symptom detection capabilities in VCF Operations form the automated detection layer. Administrators can define metric event symptoms based on reported threshold violations from monitored systems and attach those symptoms to alert definitions. The Alerts data grid presents generated alerts for troubleshooting, providing operations teams with a real-time view of conditions that may indicate security events. Administrators can also configure continuous monitoring of user security activity to detect risks early and respond to potential threats.

The data collection infrastructure in VCF Operations is extensive. The Collection Status view shows an overview of data being collected at the cluster level, and the platform tracks collectors within a cluster along with the number of objects, metrics, adapter instances, and threads each collector handles. Application monitoring adapters receive metrics and organize inventory automatically. Service discovery dashboards monitor services and display metrics based on the services being tracked. VCF Operations Aggregator supports remote collectors for distributed data collection across the environment. Custom groups can be configured with automatic membership criteria, allowing the monitoring strategy to adapt dynamically as the environment changes.

VCF Operations supports proactive monitoring to maintain a stable and secure environment. The Data and Site Resiliency Monitoring capability provides centralized visibility into data protection metrics across private clouds, which is relevant for tracking data protection incidents specifically. At the vSphere layer, the platform collects historical performance data at configurable intervals, producing aggregated data from counter instances that can be used to identify anomalous patterns.

Audit tracking in VCF Operations identifies who made changes to the data center during a specific range of time, which is valuable for post-incident forensic analysis and for detecting unauthorized changes that may indicate a security event. For policy and automation tracking, VCF Automation's Policy Enforcement tab audits when policies are applied, listing enforcement date, policy name, and policy status. Automation Central tracks recurring jobs, provides reports, and offers data visualization with configurable time intervals and transformations, supporting analysis of collected data across different time horizons.

The VCF Operations Analytics Service provides metrics analysis capabilities, including analyzable metrics counts, that support automated pattern detection across the environment. Log Analytics queries can retrieve records matching specific criteria, helping identify trends and patterns that may indicate security incidents or data protection concerns. VCF Log Management provides centralized log aggregation, collecting logs from all VCF components including VMware vCenter, VMware ESX, NSX, vSAN, VCF Automation, VCF Operations, VCF Identity Broker, VCF Operations for Networks, and VCF Operations HCX. ESX supports remote syslog to a central host, which facilitates monitoring and helps reveal information about coordinated attacks on multiple hosts. ESX audit records include event-based traceability information when such data is available, providing additional detail for forensic investigation of incidents. VCF Operations and VCF Log Management must be integrated to implement the centralized log collection architecture.

For programmatic access and integration, the vSphere Web Services API exposes a HistoryCollector managed object that provides operations for managing the lifecycle and scrollable view of historical event and task collections, enabling integration with external analysis tools. VCF Operations supports syslog configuration including enabling and disabling syslog, adding syslog servers, and editing or deleting syslog server entries, allowing collected data to be forwarded to external SIEM platforms for broader incident analysis workflows. VCF Operations also uses a configurable port to send audit information to an external syslog server.

At the Kubernetes consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters running on vSphere Supervisor contribute an additional audit and telemetry infrastructure. The Kubernetes API server generates a structured audit record for every API request, capturing the requesting user or service account, the target resource type and name, the operation performed, the response code, and a timestamp. Because VKS namespaces integrate with VCF SSO for identity resolution, these records carry real user identities rather than anonymous service principals, aiding attribution during incident investigations. Administrators configure audit policies to select which events are captured at the Request, RequestResponse, or Metadata level, and whether records are delivered through log file backends or webhook backends that forward events to external SIEM platforms in near real-time. Audit rules can target specific high-risk access patterns, such as concurrent reading of multiple Secrets by a single user, providing focused detection for suspicious credential access. The API server also generates Kubernetes Event objects tracking operational conditions including pod scheduling decisions, container restarts, image pull failures, volume attachment errors, and resource limit violations, offering a second event stream relevant to incident investigation. StatefulSet controllers use ControllerRevision objects to maintain a history of configuration changes, supporting forensic analysis and rollback after incidents.

Prometheus, available as a standard package on VKS clusters, scrapes and stores metrics from cluster components at configured intervals, evaluates rule expressions, and feeds Grafana dashboards with real-time alerts and health insights. Kubernetes metrics pipelines connect these time series data stores to dashboards and alerting systems and can trigger automated actions when thresholds are exceeded. The Istio service mesh standard package automatically generates metrics, logs, and traces for all traffic within a cluster, including ingress and egress, and supports OpenTelemetry tracing through Jaeger and Zipkin for distributed trace collection and visualization. For data protection incident tracking, Velero records backup and restore operations with a machine-readable history that documents what data existed at each point in time and whether operations succeeded or failed, which is directly relevant to investigations that require establishing pre-event data state.

While these capabilities provide a strong foundation for automated incident tracking and data collection, organizations will need to integrate VCF Operations and Kubernetes audit streams with a dedicated SIEM platform, threat intelligence feeds, and formal incident response workflows to fully satisfy this control. VCF collects and presents operational and security telemetry at both the platform and Kubernetes workload layers, but correlation of events across the broader enterprise, automated threat detection using behavioral analytics, and orchestrated incident response playbooks typically require additional tooling. VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with network-level threat detection and traffic inspection for organizations that deploy it alongside VCF.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides automated mechanisms that assist in tracking, collecting, and analyzing information from actual and potential cybersecurity incidents at the network layer. Several vDefend components contribute to this control through automated data collection, threat detection, and event correlation.

Security Intelligence continuously collects network traffic flow data and categorizes detected suspicious events by MITRE ATT&CK adversarial tactics and techniques, recording the number of occurrences during the selected time period. The Monitor and Plan Overview page displays the status of data collection activities, a summary of suspicious traffic, flow trends, and insights into network traffic flow data collected, giving operators a real-time view of the security posture across the environment. Security Intelligence combines information from administrator-provided CSV files containing lists of infrastructure servers with its own automatic detections to generate security rules, associating observed traffic patterns with known infrastructure for more accurate policy and event analysis.

The IDS/IPS subsystem provides a timeline graph visualization of intrusion events based on severity ratings over a selected time span, with colored dots indicating unique event types and dot size indicating event frequency. Operators can examine granular IDS/IPS event data in a visualization canvas that highlights affected connections in a blast radius view filterable by specific signature ID. The View Full Event History capability displays all events associated with a signature ID along with intrusion severity. Both IDS/IPS and Network Detection and Response (NDR) support date range filtering for event detail review, enabling temporal analysis of security events and intrusion attempts.

The Malware Prevention service within the Security Services Platform monitors file-based threats and allows filtering of file event statistics by time period on the Threat Monitoring dashboard, enabling operators to track trends in malicious, suspicious, and suppressed file events. Security teams can investigate both individual threat events and correlated campaigns through the Security Services Platform interface to assess impact, identify root causes, and take appropriate mitigation actions. The Campaign Blueprint Triage and Investigate feature filters detections by threat, affected workload, or MITRE tactics through an interactive graph interface, supporting structured incident analysis.

The Security Explorer Asset Collections View displays information about Asset Collection groups, including traffic flow data for the selected time period, providing visibility into communication patterns between asset groups that can reveal anomalous behavior. VCF Log Management integrates with vDefend's unified security log and supports querying for Top-N information from a specified number of hours using Interactive Analytics, extending the analytical capabilities available to security teams.

vDefend's automated data collection and analysis capabilities contribute to the incident tracking and analysis required by this control. However, vDefend does not provide end-to-end incident lifecycle management, case tracking, or SOAR-style workflow automation. Organizations should integrate vDefend's detection and event data with external SIEM and incident management platforms for end-to-end incident tracking.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides automated mechanisms for tracking, collecting, and analyzing information related to data protection incidents and ransomware activity. Cyber Recovery integrates with two Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) technologies, Carbon Black and CrowdStrike, to analyze snapshot data and detect suspicious activity by comparing change rates and entropy rates against baseline metrics for the same time of day or similar virtual machines not under attack. The analysis identifies likely ransomware encryption events from normal workload patterns by recognizing higher percentages of encrypted data and elevated entropy rates in malicious snapshots.

The CrowdStrike integration logs detection information including date, time, duration, status, affected process or executable, user who performed the action, and threat tactic and technique, and assigns each detection a CrowdScore between 0 and 100 calculated from frequency, severity, and correlation of security alerts. Vulnerability analysis uses the CrowdStrike Exploitability and Predictive Risk Technology (ExPRT) rating to assess risk based on exploitability likelihood and predicted potential impact. The Carbon Black integration displays malware scan and behavior analysis findings on the Analysis > Alerts tab and ranks findings by severity score, with categorization across risk levels for port scans, malware installation, system configuration changes, and execution and behavioral activity.

PNR forwards events from the Clean Room Orchestrator to Microsoft Sentinel and to an Azure Log Analytics Workspace through a Data Collection Rule (DCR) and Data Collection Endpoint (DCE) that direct data into a Log Analytics table for centralized monitoring and event collection. A management pack integrates PNR with VCF Operations through the Administration > Integrations > Repository interface to enable data collection from PNR cloud-managed instances, surfacing protection and recovery metrics in VCF Operations dashboards. Cyber Recovery maintains a history log for reprotect operations that can be exported as a report to verify that no errors occurred during the reprotection process, and a log collector tool simplifies the collection of PNR log files for diagnostic analysis.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides several automated mechanisms that support the collection and tracking of information relevant to cybersecurity and data protection incidents, contributing to this control's requirements.

DSM maintains a System Audit Events log, accessible through the System Audit section of the UI, that records system-level administrative and operational events tracked via the DSM UI and API. The System Audit Events view is the only DSM UI location for tracking non-database-related operations and tasks. Separately, Gateway Audit Logs capture gateway-level activity. Both audit trails can be exported in CSV or XLSX format through the ACTIONS menu. Gateway Audit Logs include structured fields for Component, Event Type, Event Details, Event Time (UTC), Status, and Source. The Source field identifies whether an event was user-initiated, recorded as the user's email address, or system-initiated, recorded as "System," supporting attribution during post-incident review.

For real-time incident awareness, DSM provides an Alerts pane in the Monitoring tab that surfaces active alerts across managed data services. Alerts cover database health conditions, data service operation failures, and scheduled backup failures. When a backup operation fails, DSM automatically generates an alert. Administrators can activate or deactivate alert categories, including database-level alerts and operation failure alerts, through the Activate Data Services settings.

DSM supports webhook integrations that automatically transmit alert information to external endpoints. Webhook payloads include the alert name, alert level (CRITICAL or WARNING), alert description, trigger timestamp, database cluster identifier, and cluster name, providing structured event data that external systems can ingest for automated tracking and correlation. This allows DSM alerts to feed into organizational SIEM platforms, incident ticketing systems, or notification pipelines without manual intervention.

DSM collects continuous metric data for each database and triggers alerts when connectivity or resource problems arise. Database metrics can be forwarded to Prometheus or to VCF Operations for centralized monitoring and trend analysis. For SQL Server databases, DSM provides built-in monitoring through an integrated InfluxDB instance and Telegraf agents deployed across the provider appliance and SQL Server workload clusters. SQL Server clusters also support a Login Auditing option that automatically creates an audit for login events.

DSM logs can be forwarded via syslog to external log management systems, where they can be aggregated with other platform logs for trend analysis. This log forwarding capability, combined with structured webhook alerts and exportable audit trails, gives organizations the data needed for automated incident tracking workflows when integrated with appropriate external tooling.

DSM generates and surfaces the data needed for automated incident tracking but does not itself provide an incident management system. Organizations must deploy and configure an external SIEM, log aggregation platform, or incident response system, and configure DSM's webhook and log forwarding integrations to connect those systems. Automated analysis and correlation of incident data requires tooling outside DSM's scope.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides automated mechanisms that collect, classify, and surface security-relevant data at the application delivery layer, supporting the tracking and analysis of potential cybersecurity incidents.

The Application Security Report gives security teams a consolidated view of security posture across managed applications. It is designed to help security teams and executives monitor threats and maintain audit-ready records. The report includes ranked lists of top attack types such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting, lists of top attacking IP addresses, and trend summaries showing total and flagged requests. It is available as a downloadable PDF documenting security findings and pre-check results, making it suitable for incident documentation workflows.

At the network and application layer, Avi monitors over 500 real-time metrics through its alerting and reporting tools, supporting detection and analysis of DDoS and other attacks. The Avi Service Engine generates DOS_ATTACK alert events that include attack type, source IP addresses, virtual service UUID and name, and report timestamp, providing structured event data for audit and forensic analysis. Alert Actions can trigger ControlScripts in response to system events, enabling automated remediation actions without requiring manual administrator intervention.

DataScript logging records security events such as blocklisted IP access attempts using the log() function. Client logs can be streamed to external servers through Analytics Profile configuration, with IPv6 destination support, enabling integration with centralized logging and security event management platforms. Avi integrates with external monitoring tools such as Prometheus for metrics collection and analysis, and supports user-defined metrics for tracking data specific to individual applications or environments.

Avi Bot Management provides consolidated logs viewable in the Controller UI and Bot analytics that display the types of bots detected over a given period. HTTP session tracking supports Client Behavior Analysis for bot classification. The Live Security Threat Intelligence IP Reputation Service is used for bot detection and classification, and the User Agent Database Sync capability can be enabled to receive updated User-Agent threat intelligence.

Additional visibility mechanisms include the DDoS Insights view, which allows exporting logs and filtering for metrics beyond those displayed in the UI, and ICAP log analytics, which provides an activity overview when content adaptation inspection is enabled. Avi Kubernetes Operator analytics policies enable real-time metrics collection on virtual services, extending incident-relevant telemetry to Kubernetes-hosted workloads.

### Control IR-06

> a. Require personnel to report suspected incidents to the organizational incident response capability within [Assignment: organization-defined time period]; and b. Report incident information to [Assignment: organization-defined authorities].

**SCF Controls:** GOV-06, IRO-10, IRO-14

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IR-06(01)

> Report incidents using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].

**SCF Controls:** IRO-10.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides automated reporting, alerting, and log aggregation capabilities that assist in identifying and reporting cybersecurity-relevant conditions. VCF Operations, VCF Log Management, VMware vCenter, and NSX supply the technical mechanisms for automated detection, notification, and log capture across the platform.

VCF Operations includes a reporting engine that generates scheduled snapshots of views and dashboards, capturing details related to current and predicted resource needs, health status, and operational metrics. Reports can be scheduled to run at regular intervals, and VCF Operations sends an email with the generated report each time one is produced. This automated delivery allows security and operations teams to receive regular status updates without manual intervention. Reports can be downloaded in PDF or CSV format, with the format defined in the report template. VCF Operations offers several predefined report templates that administrators can use based on their monitoring requirements, reducing the effort needed to set up recurring reports.

VCF Operations provides a configurable alerting framework based on metric event symptoms and threshold violations. Administrators can define alert definitions with symptom parameters that trigger when monitored systems report metric threshold violations, and alert automation rules can be configured to trigger notifications when specified conditions are met. For outbound alert delivery, VCF Operations supports multiple notification plug-in types: Log File Plugin, REST Notification Plugin, Standard Email Plugin, SNMP Trap Plugin, Webhook Notification Plugin, Slack Plugin, and ServiceNow Notification Plugin. Payload templates can be customized for each outbound plug-in type. This range of notification channels allows alerts to be forwarded to external SIEM, SOAR, or ticketing systems for incident tracking.

VMware vCenter alarms provide another layer of automated notification. vCenter can send SNMP traps and email notifications when alarms are triggered, with configurable recipient addresses and alarm subject information. The vCenter reports API supports downloading reports generated by interoperability and pre-check operations, which can be retrieved programmatically using an authenticated session.

VCF Log Management provides centralized log aggregation that collects logs from all VCF components, including vCenter, ESX, NSX, vSAN, VCF Automation, VCF Operations, VCF Identity Broker, VCF Operations for Networks, and VCF Operations HCX. Multiple VCF Log Management instances can be configured to forward logs to a single integrated instance. The Log Data transfer functionality enables forwarding of ingested logs to external platforms for broader incident analysis.

For log-related anomaly detection, the Logging Monitor feature generates reports with details about log spew problems, which can indicate abnormal system behavior that may warrant investigation. Log Assist extends this by generating log bundles and automatically attaching property-based and log-based diagnostic findings to accelerate the resolution of support cases. When an issue is detected, Log Assist adds both property and log-based findings that are active at the time of downloading the diagnostic report to the automatically generated log bundles, providing a consolidated view of relevant diagnostic data.

VCF Operations also supports custom groups with automatic membership criteria, allowing administrators to designate groups that update dynamically based on defined criteria. This enables automated categorization and tracking of resources that can feed into broader incident detection and reporting workflows. Automation Central displays a calendar of all scheduled jobs and provides history and job tabs for tracking execution status, giving operators visibility into recurring automated tasks.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides automated detection, analysis, and reporting capabilities that feed into an organization's incident reporting workflows. While vDefend does not independently produce or distribute formal incident reports, it generates the security event data, threat intelligence, and exportable reports that incident reporting processes consume.

The Security Services Platform Rule Analysis feature generates downloadable CSV reports of the last successful analysis, with separate CSV files for each detected anomaly type. These structured reports can be consumed by external SIEM, SOAR, or incident management platforms. The Security Services Platform also generates Security Segmentation Reports and Blast Radius Reports that break down at-risk workloads, communication chains, and flows, providing structured artifacts for incident documentation and escalation workflows.

Security teams can investigate individual threat events and correlated campaigns through the Security Services Platform interface to assess impact, identify root causes, and take appropriate mitigation actions. Security Intelligence detects and categorizes suspicious events using MITRE tactics and techniques, recording the number of occurrences during the selected time period. Security Intelligence also provides a visualization canvas where users can examine granular IDS/IPS event data, including affected connections highlighted in a blast radius view that can be filtered by specific signature ID.

The Security Overview Dashboard Threat Event Monitoring capability provides insights about the current state of security issues in the data center, displaying malicious IP statistics including Top Blocked IPs, Top VMs Accessing Malicious IPs, and Top Blocked Categories, helping security teams understand network activity and identify areas requiring investigation.

Malware Prevention monitors file inspection events and reports details on the Malware Prevention dashboard. File Analysis Reports document the types of activities detected during artifact analysis, including Autostart, Disable, Evasion, File, Memory, Network, Reputation, Settings, Signature, Steal, Stealth, and Silenced classifications, and include an ATT&CK TECHNIQUES field that records the observed actions or tools a malicious actor might use. The Process Analysis Report tab includes links to artifact reports on the VirusTotal portal. Process Analysis Reports can be exported in XML, JSON, and PDF formats, providing structured evidence for incident documentation.

These automated reporting and export capabilities reduce the manual effort required to gather evidence and generate the data feeds that incident reporting systems consume. However, the formal incident reporting lifecycle, including classification, notification routing, regulatory reporting, and stakeholder communication, remains an organizational responsibility handled by external incident management platforms that consume vDefend's alerts, detections, and exportable reports.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides automated reporting mechanisms across disaster recovery operations and ransomware recovery analysis. PNR generates and exports reports for each recovery plan run, test execution, and test cleanup operation for audit trail purposes, capturing per-step execution data. The recovery plan history log also records reprotect operations and can be exported as a report to verify whether errors occurred during the protection cycle.

PNR records operational information into log files on the appliance and triggers alarms tied to system health, including monitoring of connections between protected and recovery sites. Health finding status history tracking maintains a configurable time window, defaulting to 24 hours, for visibility into ongoing system health findings. Diagnostic information can be collected and compressed into a log bundle written to a user-specified location, supporting hand-off to engineering or audit teams during incident response. Configuration import operations also generate a CSV report file that documents any problems encountered during the import stages.

Cyber Recovery within PNR includes Integrated Security and Vulnerability Analysis with Carbon Black and CrowdStrike. The Carbon Black integration analyzes snapshot data against baseline metrics for change rate and entropy to detect suspicious activity, displays behavior analysis results on the Alerts tab, and ranks findings by severity score. The CrowdStrike integration logs detection information including date, time, duration, status, affected process or executable, the user who performed the action, and the threat tactic and technique. Vulnerability records carry Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) identifiers with links to public vulnerability articles, the CrowdScore for Incident Detections measures real-time threat level on a 0 to 100 scale based on frequency, severity, and correlation of security alerts, and the CrowdStrike ExPRT rating assesses vulnerabilities by exploitability likelihood and predicted impact.

The 9.1 release introduces the VCF Protection and Recovery Cloud Management Pack for VCF Operations. The management pack is installed through the Administration > Integrations > Repository interface and collects telemetry from a registered VCF Protection and Recovery Cloud account. Protection and Recovery dashboards in VCF Operations consolidate views across vCenters with vSAN snapshots and replication, recovery site pairs, and Live Recovery Cloud recovery regions, providing centralized incident-relevant reporting beyond what the PNR appliance produces on its own.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides automated mechanisms that support the reporting of cybersecurity incidents at the application delivery layer.

The Application Security Report feature allows security teams to generate PDF-format reports documenting application security findings, WAF statistics, threat monitoring data, and pre-check results. In 9.1, these reports include ranked lists of top attack types such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting, lists of top attacking IP addresses, and trend summaries showing total and flagged requests over time. Reports are accessible through both the Controller UI (under Application Monitoring > Application Security Report) and the CLI. The `generate report` command creates a report for a specified virtual service with an optional duration parameter to define the reporting period. Existing reports can be listed with `show report` and exported in PDF format using `show report <REPORT NAME> export`. The reports are designed to support audit-ready documentation of security posture and threat data for security teams and executives. Virtual Service analytics reports cover periods up to seven days.

Avi ControlScripts provide automated event capture and reporting for DDoS and network security events. The Avi Service Engine generates DOS_ATTACK alert events that include attack type, source IP addresses, virtual service UUID and name, and report timestamp for audit and forensic purposes. ControlScripts can enable logging on network security policy rules to record traffic denied based on DDoS attack source IP detection, and can retrieve structured DDoS attack event details through the `dos_params` event structure. Alert Actions can invoke ControlScripts to automatically add offending client IP addresses to a blocklist IP group, supporting automated response workflows. Avi's alerting and reporting tools monitor over 500 real-time metrics for DDoS and attack mitigation.

WAF Custom Rules capture matched data in audit logs with severity classification and OWASP attack taxonomy tags, providing structured logging for application-layer attack events. DataScript supports logging of security events such as blocklisted IP access attempts using the `log()` function. The Live Security Threat Intelligence service, available through the Avi Cloud Console, delivers real-time threat feeds for various attack vectors and provides an IP Reputation Service used as a source for bot detection and classification, with User Agent Database Sync capability for ongoing threat intelligence updates.

These reporting mechanisms are scoped to application-layer security events observed by Avi at the load balancing and WAF boundary. Broader incident management, SIEM integration, and platform-level security event aggregation are provided by the underlying VCF platform components. Organizations integrating Avi with a SIEM or incident management platform should configure Avi's syslog forwarding alongside Application Security Report generation to support automated, end-to-end incident reporting workflows.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control IR-06(03)

> Provide incident information to the provider of the product or service and other organizations involved in the supply chain or supply chain governance for systems or system components related to the incident.

**SCF Controls:** IRO-10.4

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IR-07

> Provide an incident response support resource, integral to the organizational incident response capability, that offers advice and assistance to users of the system for the handling and reporting of incidents.

**SCF Controls:** IRO-11

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control IR-07(01)

> SUPPORT Increase the availability of incident response information and support using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].

**SCF Controls:** IRO-11.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides native automated mechanisms that increase the availability of incident response-related information and support through VCF Operations, VCF Automation integration capabilities, and syslog forwarding to external systems.

VCF Operations includes a Troubleshooting Incident Page that displays information related to incidents, including status, root cause metrics, closing remarks, and timestamps for when incidents were created and by whom. The platform maintains a list of all incidents with their status and provides Edit, Share, and Delete actions for incident management. Incident tracking fields include state, resolution code, resolution notes, impact (business criticality), urgency (resolution timeframe), priority (resolution sequence), severity level, and approval workflow documentation. Alerts can be configured with impact and criticality metadata to prioritize response efforts. This structured incident data is available through the VCF Operations console without manual collection.

VCF Operations integrates with ServiceNow through the Service-Now Notifications plug-in, which can be configured to automatically create incidents in the ServiceNow ticketing system when alerts fire. The Management Pack for ServiceNow provides alert-sync functionality that synchronizes alerts between VCF Operations and ServiceNow based on incident configurations. Notification rules allow configuration of detailed incident fields including incident state, resolution code, resolution notes, on-hold reason, impact, urgency, priority, assignment group, assigned-to person, severity, approval actions, related problems, and change cause. ServiceNow incidents created by VCF Operations include caller, category, subcategory, business service, and other alert-related attributes, giving responders immediate context. These integrations keep incident response information flowing to ticketing systems without manual intervention.

VCF Automation extends this integration through extensibility actions that connect to enterprise ITSM platforms such as ServiceNow. System administrators can configure email notification and support ticket properties for failures in Day-2 actions and RITM requests through the ServiceNow ITSM Plug-in for VCF Automation. ServiceNow users can be added to the Automation-SupportGroup to handle incidents created by the failure of VCF Automation catalog request submissions. VCF Automation also provides out-of-the-box workflows for common infrastructure tasks and supports extensibility actions for broader enterprise ITSM integration.

VCF Operations provides automated remediation and diagnostic capabilities that support incident resolution. Alert definitions include recommendations with effective solutions, and the Management Pack for VCF Operations Orchestrator ships with out-of-the-box workflows mapped to these recommendations for automated remediation. Alert definition instructions can be configured to describe precisely how an engineer or administrator should fix the problem to resolve the alert. Using dynamic actions, the Orchestrator management pack automatically converts custom VMware vCenter package workflows to alert actions and can be configured to automatically remediate alerts by executing mapped recommendations in the next collection cycle.

The Diagnostic Findings and VCF Health capabilities work together to detect, predict, and remediate issues, reducing the number of support tickets and mean time to resolution. Log Assist can generate log bundles and automatically attach property-based diagnostic findings data to accelerate resolution of support cases. Log Assist also handles intermittent network or infrastructure issues automatically before requiring user intervention, and can link log bundles to support cases with diagnostic findings attached. The vCenter appliance also provides a vc-support.sh script that generates a support bundle containing installation logs and diagnostic information, offering a CLI-based mechanism for collecting vCenter diagnostic data independently of the Log Assist workflow.

Syslog forwarding from VCF components to external SIEM systems provides continuous availability of security event data for incident response teams. vCenter syslog forwarding can be configured through the vCenter Management API or HTTP requests, and VMware ESX hosts emit syslog messages conforming to RFC 3164. ESX system logging supports eight severity levels as specified in RFC 5424: Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notice, Informational, and Debug. vSphere Supervisor control plane logs can be forwarded to external syslog servers using Fluent Bit in RFC 5424 format. This layered forwarding capability keeps incident-relevant log data available in centralized monitoring and analysis platforms.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides several automated mechanisms that increase the availability of incident response-related information within its network security domain, though the broader incident response program (ticketing integration, centralized log management, staffing, and escalation procedures) relies on other VCF components and organizational processes.

The IDS/IPS Security Dashboard provides automated tracking and management of intrusion events. An automated protective mechanism maintains availability of incident response data by selectively dropping lower-priority event data (Low and Informational severity) first when high IDS/IPS event volumes or PCAP data threaten system stability, keeping higher-severity events accessible to responders. The Security Overview Dashboard provides centralized visibility into threat event activity across the environment.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) automatically calculates impact scores for detection events from Confidence and Severity values, and provides filtering of the unified event list by impact score so that responders can prioritize investigations. Security teams can investigate individual threat events and correlated campaigns through the Security Services Platform interface to assess impact, identify root causes, and take appropriate mitigation actions. Security Intelligence categorizes detected suspicious traffic events by impact type (Critical, High, Medium, and Low) to help prioritize response actions. Security Intelligence Policy Recommendations includes a Monitoring capability that checks for changes in the scope of input entities every hour when enabled, providing automated ongoing visibility into policy coverage.

Intelligent Assist is an AI-assisted analysis feature accessible through the NSX Manager UI and the Security Services Platform UI on the IDS/IPS, Detections, and Campaigns pages. When security events are detected, Intelligent Assist provides analysis support and recommends policies to address attacks, such as restricting lateral propagation, with options to enable the policy immediately or hand off recommended actions to another team for review. For Malware events, Targeted remediation enables all IDS signatures for the affected workload, while Comprehensive remediation creates vDefend policies. For Network Anomaly events, Intelligent Assist produces vDefend policies regardless of which strategy is selected. All remediation actions go through human review and approval before enforcement. Responders can mark events as Ignored, Resolved, or In-progress to track investigation status. Intelligent Assist enforces a usage limit of two messages per licensed core per month; organizations should factor this limit into incident response workflow planning. Not all Network Anomalies can be remediated; Intelligent Assist automatically determines when remediation is not possible.

The Security Services Platform has high availability (HA) capabilities by default, supporting continuous access to detection and analysis services during incident response operations.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides automation support for disaster recovery and ransomware recovery operations through its Events and Alarms subsystem, Clean Room Orchestrator monitoring, integrated security analysis with CrowdStrike, and scheduled configuration export.

The PNR Events and Alarms subsystem logs Site Status Events that report the status of protected and recovery sites and the connection between them, along with protection group changes and replication or failover task progress. Each event includes severity, timestamp, and target system information. Alarms can be configured to send email notifications, SNMP traps, or run a script when triggered by specified event conditions, giving administrators a programmable channel for surfacing recovery-relevant information. PNR also monitors connections to remote sites by sending periodic ping requests, detects when monitored connections have broken, and logs connection check events so disruptions are visible before a recovery event is invoked.

The Clean Room Orchestrator extends this with its own monitoring layer for isolated recovery operations. Email alerts can be configured for critical events in the clean room orchestrator to deliver timely notification of recovery operations and system events. The Clean Room Tasks and Alarms view maintains an Alarms list showing a history of all notifications that require attention, triggered when events do not occur as planned, with search and filtering by severity. The Clean Room Events panel exposes event severity, timestamp, target system information for replication or failover tasks, and event description.

Cyber Recovery integrated with CrowdStrike provides incident information for detected security threats, including date, time, duration, status, and recommended remediation actions. CrowdScore for incident detections measures the real-time threat level to an endpoint on a 0 to 100 scale, calculated from frequency, severity, and correlation of security alerts, and updates dynamically based on real-time data so responders have current threat context when triaging a ransomware event.

PNR configuration data export can be automated by scheduling a cron job on the PNR appliance host virtual machine, keeping the recovery configuration available for inspection or import without manual intervention. The Protection and Recovery Reports view makes health findings visible for ongoing inspection of the protection state, and individual findings can be silenced when they have been reviewed.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer contributes to automated incident response information availability through its event, alerting, and ControlScript framework at the application delivery layer.

Avi Load Balancer events form the building blocks of configuration automation and are available for reactive troubleshooting and proactive notification. The Alert feature processes system events and metrics against user-defined rules and generates alerts that initiate configured actions such as sending an email, a syslog message, or an SNMP trap. Alert configurations are customizable: administrators define conditions based on system events or metric thresholds, then attach alert actions to notify personnel or invoke automated workflows.

ControlScripts extend the alerting framework by enabling automated responses at alert time. When an alert fires, a ControlScript can modify pool priority, trigger pool failover operations, or send custom messages to external incident management and monitoring platforms, making application-layer operational and security information available to response teams as conditions occur.

Avi's Ansible integration provides additional automation for operational management: Avi Ansible modules automatically handle error reporting and retries, so failures are logged and managed within automation workflows. All automation tooling operates through Avi APIs and SDKs, which provide consistent programmatic access to controller state and configuration for incident documentation and response workflows.

Support bundles consolidate diagnostic data, logs, and configuration information from an Avi system to assist troubleshooting and incident resolution.

Incident response coordination at the platform level is handled by VCF (see VCF Coverage). Avi's contribution is scoped to the application delivery layer: its event system, configurable alerting, ControlScripts, and API integrations provide automated mechanisms for making application-level incident information available.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control IR-08

> a. Develop an incident response plan that: 1. Provides the organization with a roadmap for implementing its incident response capability; 2. Describes the structure and organization of the incident response capability; 3. Provides a high-level approach for how the incident response capability fits into the overall organization; 4. Meets the unique requirements of the organization, which relate to mission, size, structure, and functions; 5. Defines reportable incidents; 6. Provides metrics for measuring the incident response capability within the organization; 7. Defines the resources and management support needed to effectively maintain and mature an incident response capability; 8. Addresses the sharing of incident information; 9. Is reviewed and approved by [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; and 10. Explicitly designates responsibility for incident response to [Assignment: organization- defined entities, personnel, or roles]. b. Distribute copies of the incident response plan to [Assignment: organization-defined incident response personnel (identified by name and/or by role) and organizational elements]; c. Update the incident response plan to address system and organizational changes or problems encountered during plan implementation, execution, or testing; d. Communicate incident response plan changes to [Assignment: organization-defined incident response personnel (identified by name and/or by role) and organizational elements]; and e. Protect the incident response plan from unauthorized disclosure and modification.

**SCF Controls:** IRO-04

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control MA-01

> a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]: 1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System- level] maintenance policy that: (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and 2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the maintenance policy and the associated maintenance controls; b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the maintenance policy and procedures; and c. Review and update the current maintenance: 1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and 2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

**SCF Controls:** GOV-02, GOV-03, MNT-01, MNT-05.1, MNT-05.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control MA-02

> a. Schedule, document, and review records of maintenance, repair, and replacement on system components in accordance with manufacturer or vendor specifications and/or organizational requirements; b. Approve and monitor all maintenance activities, whether performed on site or remotely and whether the system or system components are serviced on site or removed to another location; c. Require that [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] explicitly approve the removal of the system or system components from organizational facilities for off-site maintenance, repair, or replacement; d. Sanitize equipment to remove the following information from associated media prior to removal from organizational facilities for off-site maintenance, repair, or replacement: [Assignment: organization-defined information]; e. Check all potentially impacted controls to verify that the controls are still functioning properly following maintenance, repair, or replacement actions; and f. Include the following information in organizational maintenance records: [Assignment: organization-defined information].

**SCF Controls:** MNT-02

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides integrated lifecycle management capabilities for conducting controlled maintenance activities across the full infrastructure stack, spanning host servicing, component updates, and operational monitoring.

VCF Operations includes a maintenance mode capability that allows administrators to place managed objects into maintenance mode either manually or on a defined schedule. Manual maintenance mode can be applied indefinitely or for a specified duration in minutes, and it can be started or stopped independently of any assigned recurring schedule. For recurring maintenance windows, administrators can create maintenance schedules that place objects in maintenance mode at fixed intervals, such as from midnight until 3:00 a.m. on a specific night each week. These schedules are configured per policy through the Policy Definition interface in VCF Operations. When objects are in maintenance mode on schedule, VCF Operations suppresses alerts and status indicators for those objects, preventing misleading data that would otherwise result from objects being offline or in an unusual state during planned maintenance.

VCF Operations also supports automation of maintenance tasks through Automation Central, which allows administrators to create jobs that schedule actions to run automatically. Jobs can be configured with daily, weekly, or monthly recurrence and set to end after a specific date or number of occurrences. The VCF Operations orchestrator further supports scheduling of workflow runs at specified dates, times, and intervals. These capabilities allow maintenance tasks to run during defined windows without manual supervision.

Lifecycle management in VCF provides tools for planning and executing updates across the software stack. Starting with VCF 9, component lifecycle management is performed through the VCF Operations UI, which centralizes update and patch deployment for all VCF components. Administrators can schedule maintenance windows and coordinate changes to reduce disruption to services. Configuration updates are required to be performed during a maintenance window. VCF lifecycle management automates the process of applying updates and patches to software components within the stack, and it manages version compatibility across VCF components to maintain stability during and after updates.

VCF delivers maintenance releases as scheduled, cumulative updates focused on platform stability and support. These releases are synchronized among VCF components through a new Bill of Materials, which coordinates versioning across the stack so that components remain compatible after each update cycle. Patch releases, by contrast, are not synchronized among all components and do not require a new Bill of Materials.

For workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor, the platform provides structured lifecycle management procedures at both the cluster and workload levels. Cluster-level lifecycle operations are performed through the Cluster API resource, which manages provisioning, scaling, and upgrades of VKS clusters. Node maintenance follows a defined sequence: operators cordon a node to prevent new workload scheduling, then drain it using kubectl drain to safely evict running pods before performing maintenance. Maintenance should not begin until the drain command completes, and operators should select worker nodes rather than control plane nodes when performing tasks such as checking for updates, preserving control-plane stability during the maintenance window. On shared clusters, operators should verify that cordoning and draining a node will not adversely affect other tenants before executing the operation.

For application updates within VKS clusters, Kubernetes rolling update strategies on Deployments, DaemonSets, and StatefulSets allow workloads to be updated with maintained availability. The kubectl rollout command manages the rollout lifecycle, supporting pausing, resuming, and rolling back changes. StatefulSet controllers use ControllerRevision objects to track historical configuration changes and maintain a revision history that enables rollback when updates need to be reversed. StatefulSet pods retain their identity and persistent storage when rescheduled to available nodes during maintenance, allowing stateful applications to survive node maintenance events without losing state. The DaemonSetUpdateSurge feature gate enables DaemonSet workloads to maintain per-node availability during updates. Declarative object management using kubectl apply with version-controlled configuration files provides a controlled change mechanism that supports tracking what changed and restoring prior state if needed.

Kubernetes Operators running on Supervisor extend these capabilities by automating lifecycle procedures for stateful applications, including snapshot creation, StatefulSet and volume management, backup scheduling, and version upgrade orchestration. Operators allow complex, application-specific lifecycle procedures to be codified and applied consistently across environments. The Kubernetes TTL controller automatically removes finished Jobs based on configurable time-to-live settings, supporting lifecycle hygiene for batch workloads without manual intervention. Finalizers provide an additional mechanism for controlling the lifecycle and cleanup of resources owned by deleted objects.

The VCF Automation self-service catalog allows consumers to deploy IT resources and independently manage workloads through day-2 actions including updates and patches. Catalog items are scoped to projects, and lifecycle and day-2 actions for custom resources in a deployment must be shared with all relevant projects to support continued management after a deployment moves between projects. VCF building-block services have independent lifecycles, enabling individual components to be updated or maintained without requiring a complete platform maintenance window.

For backup data lifecycle maintenance, Velero on VKS clusters includes a configurable maintenance job system through the backupRepository.maintenanceJobConfig setting, which supports both global configuration and per-backup-repository maintenance job configuration. The fullMaintenanceInterval defaults to normalGC, and resource limits for maintenance job pods are tunable.

Supervisor system components, including the vmware-system-tkg controller manager, state metrics, and webhook components, are deployed across multiple replicas for high availability. This design allows component-level updates to proceed without requiring a complete platform maintenance window. Cluster health visibility is available through Kubernetes cluster conditions, including a Remediating condition that surfaces details about ongoing remediation of controlled machines. Readiness probes run throughout a container's lifecycle and allow the platform to detect and respond to recovery from temporary faults during or after maintenance events.

For host-level maintenance, VMware ESX hosts support a formal maintenance mode with dedicated API methods (EnterMaintenanceMode_Task and ExitMaintenanceMode_Task) that allow hosts to be taken offline for servicing in a controlled manner. Maintenance mode can also be managed through the ESXCLI interface. Administrators can verify whether a host is in maintenance mode using `esxcli --server=<server_name> system maintenanceMode get`, and access to maintenance mode operations is governed by the `Host.Config.Maintenance` privilege, which controls the ability to place a host in and out of maintenance mode and to shut down and restart the host. When remediating hosts through Update Manager, administrators can configure maintenance mode settings to control how hosts enter and exit maintenance mode during patching operations. For vSAN clusters, placing a host in maintenance mode requires selecting a data evacuation mode, such as Ensure accessibility, Full data migration, or No data migration.

At the network layer, routine maintenance tasks for NSX Manager appliances include running backups, adding licenses and certificates, and changing passwords, providing a defined set of maintenance activities for the networking infrastructure.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides lifecycle management and operational visibility capabilities through its Security Services Platform (SSP) that support controlled maintenance activities for the vDefend security infrastructure.

SSP's Platform Inventory Monitor gives administrators status visibility across environments, regions, zones, and virtual machines, identifying assets in success, timeout, or out-of-sync states. Filtering by name and status allows administrators to assess operational health before and after maintenance activities. The View details of external rollouts option in SSP displays progress details for Refresh operations covering Bare Metal servers, NDR Sensors, and Service VMs at both the individual and overall feature status levels, providing granular visibility into rollout state.

SSP enforces sequencing constraints on maintenance operations: Bare Metal Service operations such as onboarding, offboarding, and upgrading Bare Metal servers should not be performed while a backup or restore is in progress. After restoring a backup in either a disaster recovery or in-place scenario, agent health status must be confirmed as Up on Bare Metal servers before proceeding. The vDefend CLI provides a command to display the health of all services and agents running on a Bare Metal server, giving administrators a mechanism to verify service state before and after servicing. SSP also includes Depot and Registry services that run as systemd-managed services and can be restarted and monitored via systemctl commands.

SSP proactively manages its Platform CA certificate lifecycle. The certificate manager and security intelligence services within SSP can be queried for operational status to diagnose service availability issues during and after maintenance events.

NDR Sensor management operations, including activating or deactivating features and offboarding sensors, are restricted to the Enterprise Admin or Security Engineer role, limiting who can execute maintenance actions on detection infrastructure. Tokens, Bare Metal servers, and Bare Metal server interfaces are included in the full inventory backup, supporting recovery after maintenance activities.

When using vDefend Intelligent Assist or Network Detection and Response remediation workflows, objects that encounter errors during remediation require manual cleanup, which is a constraint administrators should account for in maintenance planning.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) supports controlled maintenance through two configurable policy mechanisms: a maintenance policy for the DSM plug-in software itself, and per-database maintenance windows for database engine upgrades.

For plug-in software updates, administrators configure a maintenance policy that specifies a start date, start time (in hours and minutes), and a permitted duration for applying updates. The policy is accessible from the Version & Upgrade section under the DSM System tab in the DSM UI, as well as from the vSphere Client. This policy governs automatic upgrades of the DSM Provider Appliance plug-in, allowing organizations to schedule maintenance during off-peak hours and bound the duration of any resulting downtime. The policy can be edited during the 10-minute interval between automatic update attempts, which cancels the next scheduled attempt and applies the revised settings. If an automatic upgrade fails, administrators can reconfigure the maintenance policy to reschedule or perform a manual update.

For database engines, each database supports an individually configurable maintenance window that controls when minor version upgrades are applied. The default schedule is Saturday at 23:59 UTC with a six-hour duration, adjustable through the Edit Maintenance Window dialog. MySQL databases include an Enable Maintenance Window toggle with the same default schedule. SQL Server databases support configurable maintenance windows for both software version upgrades and operating system patching. In 9.1, DSM adds a Monthly Occurrences setting that refines the maintenance schedule from a standard weekly cadence to specific weeks within a month, allowing selection of a particular day and one or more specific occurrences of that day. If no specific occurrence is selected, the maintenance window runs every week. Maintenance window settings, including Monthly Occurrences, are also configurable when provisioning databases through VCF Automation. Administrators are prompted to define maintenance window schedules during database creation alongside backup policies.

The DSM controller manages component dependencies through status monitoring and automated remediation. DSM also provides operational visibility into maintenance activity through the database Status indicator and the Operations tab, which display in-progress operations, configuration changes, disk extension tasks, and restore operations. This allows administrators to confirm that maintenance actions have been applied and to identify those that have failed.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides several mechanisms that support controlled maintenance activities across its application delivery infrastructure.

For back-end server pools, Avi's health monitoring framework includes maintenance mode detection. Health monitors can be configured so that when a back-end server enters maintenance mode (signaled by returning a designated response code or response string), Avi automatically changes the server's health status to "down for maintenance" and stops routing traffic to it. This behavior is supported across TCP, UDP, HTTP, HTTPS, HTTP/2, and POP3/POP3S health monitor types. TCP and UDP monitors use the tcp-udp-maintenance-mode-filters setting; HTTP monitors use the maintenance_response and maintenance_code CLI parameters; HTTP/2 monitors use the maintenance-server-response-data setting; POP3/POP3S monitors match a configured string such as MAINTENANCE_ACTIVE against the health-check response body. The maintenance mode detection monitor must be associated with the pool the server belongs to. Administrators and application developers can inspect health-check responses directly to validate server maintenance state. When the maintenance string or code is no longer returned, subsequent health checks restore the server to active rotation.

For Global Server Load Balancing (GSLB) operations, Avi enforces a controlled pre-maintenance step before a manual leader change. The CLI command gslb maintenancemode enabled must be run in GSLB configuration mode to place the leader Controller into maintenance mode before the leader change proceeds; the corresponding gslb no maintenancemode command exits maintenance mode. Avi GSLB can also direct traffic to alternative endpoints during planned maintenance, supporting service continuity while primary endpoints are offline.

For cloud-level maintenance, the Avi Controller provides a CLI workflow to place an entire cloud into maintenance mode using configure cloud <name> followed by the maintenance_mode directive. This is used during Controller upgrades and patch operations. The cloud exits maintenance mode via the no maintenance_mode directive.

For Kubernetes deployments using the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO), maintenance mode detection is available through the HealthMonitor custom resource definition (CRD). AKO HealthMonitor TCP monitors support a maintenance_response value, enabling Kubernetes-native maintenance workflows where health-check responses trigger traffic drain before maintenance begins.

For bare-metal Linux Server Cloud deployments hosting both Controller and Service Engine components, Avi defines a sequential maintenance procedure: Service Engines must be deactivated before hardware maintenance, host OS upgrades, security patching, or kernel updates on the host. Controller cluster nodes must be serviced one at a time to preserve cluster quorum and availability throughout the maintenance window.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control MA-02(02)

> (a) Schedule, conduct, and document maintenance, repair, and replacement actions for the system using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]; and (b) Produce up-to date, accurate, and complete records of all maintenance, repair, and replacement actions requested, scheduled, in process, and completed.

**SCF Controls:** MNT-02.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides automated mechanisms for scheduling, conducting, and documenting maintenance activities across the infrastructure stack, including the VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and VCF Services consumption layers.

VCF Operations includes a dedicated maintenance scheduling capability that allows administrators to create and assign maintenance schedules to managed objects. Schedules can be configured to place objects into maintenance mode at recurring intervals, with recurrence options including once, daily, weekly, or monthly. For example, a server can be placed into maintenance mode from midnight until 3 a.m. on a specific night each week. Maintenance mode can end automatically after a specified number of minutes, or it can be applied indefinitely and ended manually. These scheduling options are available through the Policy Definition interface, where each policy can have its own maintenance window configured. Maintenance schedules can be attached to policies to transition infrastructure into maintenance mode at defined intervals so that planned downtime proceeds consistently without manual intervention each time. VCF Operations Automation Central allows administrators to schedule and run maintenance jobs during defined windows without manual supervision. Automation jobs support configurable recurrence options including one-time and daily schedules with configurable day intervals, and end conditions can be set based on a specific date or a specified number of occurrences. At the workload layer, Kubernetes CronJob objects provide a native scheduling mechanism for automated tasks within VKS clusters, using standard cron syntax to define execution intervals; the `.spec.concurrencyPolicy` field governs concurrent execution behavior for overlapping schedule windows. Finished Job objects created by CronJobs are cleaned up automatically based on a configurable capacity-based policy, keeping cluster state manageable without manual intervention. Velero, available for VKS cluster workloads, supports the `velero schedule create` command to create automated backup schedules using cron expressions, and the `backupRepository.maintenanceJobConfig` setting supports both global and per-repository maintenance job configuration independently of individual backup schedules.

For conducting maintenance tasks, vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages the life cycle of hosts and clusters using a single image-based approach. vLCM images can include firmware add-ons that can be added, replaced, removed, upgraded, or downgraded across a cluster. Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins, registered with VMware vCenter, integrate vendor-specific hardware management for proactive hardware monitoring and firmware updates. VMware ESX hosts support programmatic maintenance mode transitions through dedicated API methods, enabling automation tooling to orchestrate host servicing workflows. Hosts in a fully automated DRS cluster are placed into maintenance mode automatically during remediation, with virtual machines evacuated to other hosts before the transition occurs. The current maintenance mode status of a host can be queried programmatically using `esxcli --server=<server_name> system maintenanceMode get`, supporting scripted verification workflows. VCF Automation provides an extensible workflow engine with out-of-the-box workflows for common operations such as host configuration and infrastructure management. Infrastructure operations can also be automated through integration with tools such as Terraform and Ansible, enabling repeatable and consistent maintenance procedures. VCF Automation's self-service catalog allows consumers to independently manage workloads and run day-2 actions such as updates and patches through a governed interface, extending maintenance automation to application teams without requiring direct platform access. VCF's lifecycle management capabilities provide tools for planning updates so that administrators can schedule maintenance windows and apply changes with minimal disruption to running services. At the VKS layer, Kubernetes Operators encode repeatable operational procedures as code, automating maintenance tasks such as StatefulSet and volume management, backup scheduling, and version upgrades in response to defined conditions or schedules. Deployment controllers continuously monitor running application instances and automatically replace instances on failed or unavailable nodes, providing a self-healing mechanism that conducts node-level recovery without human intervention. The Cluster API supports defining machine sets across multiple failure domains with rules to automatically heal the cluster in the event of node or zone failures. VKS clusters also support planned maintenance through node cordoning and draining, which gracefully relocates workloads away from targeted nodes before maintenance begins, allowing the node to be serviced while maintaining application availability across the cluster.

Pre-update validation automates health checks and compatibility verification before updates are applied. For example, NSX upgrade pre-checks verify component connectivity, version compatibility, and component status, and Supervisor update pre-checks include health checks, resource checks, and dependency checks to confirm that a successful update is possible. VCF Operations Configuration Drifts provides a proactive method to monitor configuration changes and assess whether vCenter instances and vSphere clusters are configured consistently, reducing potential issues from configuration drift. Image-based lifecycle management periodically assesses host configuration against a desired-state image, and upon remediation the image is installed across all hosts in the cluster. The kube-apiserver performs periodic maintenance of FlowSchema configuration objects automatically, running an initial maintenance pass at startup and then once per minute thereafter, keeping API flow control configuration consistent without administrator intervention.

On the documentation side, VCF Operations maintenance schedules have a dual purpose: they automate the transition of objects into and out of maintenance mode, and they identify which objects are in maintenance mode at any given time. This identification is used to suppress misleading monitoring data that would otherwise appear when objects are intentionally offline for maintenance. VCF Operations daily checks cover changes in settings and verify that the settings match the authorized changes executed during the change window, providing an automated documentation trail for maintenance activities. VCF Operations audit events track configuration changes with timestamps, displayed by default for the last 24 hours with the option to extend the time range. Scheduled automated reports can be generated at regular intervals to track current resource status and identify potential risks, providing an ongoing record of environment health before and after maintenance activities. Custom groups can be configured with automatic membership criteria to organize objects for monitoring and reporting, supporting a structured approach to maintenance documentation. GitOps tooling, including Argo CD available as a Supervisor Service, automates the synchronization of desired state from Git repositories to VKS cluster environments; infrastructure as code stored in version control combined with immutable Kubernetes resources creates a durable record of every authorized change, satisfying the documentation requirement for the workload layer. CronJob-scheduled Job resources within VKS clusters can be monitored in real time using `kubectl get jobs --watch`, which streams execution status updates as jobs are scheduled and complete, providing an on-demand audit view of automated maintenance task execution. VCF Automation for VM Apps maintains a history of deployments that can be reviewed to retrieve information about deleted machines and diagnose deployment failures, and deployment cards display Last Request Status filters that track the last operation on each deployment with Create, Update, and Delete operations in Successful, In Progress, or Failed states.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides automated mechanisms for scheduling and conducting database-layer maintenance activities, contributing to this control's requirements within the scope of managed database services.

DSM's maintenance window feature allows administrators to define when automatic software updates are applied to database instances. During database creation, or at any time through the Edit Maintenance Window dialog, administrators set a start day, start time, and duration. The default schedule runs each Saturday at 23:59 UTC for six hours.
