Outbound plug-in instances can be activated or deactivated to start or stop sending messages without removing the configuration from the environment.

These capabilities provide the technical foundation for monitoring inbound and outbound communications traffic across the infrastructure, networking, and workload layers of VCF. However, continuously monitoring for unusual or unauthorized activities requires the organization to define traffic baselines, configure alert thresholds, establish monitoring procedures, and integrate VCF telemetry with a security information and event management (SIEM) platform or similar analytics tool that can correlate traffic data and detect anomalies over time. This telemetry spans NSX flow records and IPFIX exports, VCF Operations network metrics, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy enforcement statistics, and Istio service mesh traces. VMware vDefend, a separate product that extends NSX with security functions, adds distributed firewall connection and packet logging, stateful flow tracking with IPFIX export, deep packet inspection, IDS/IPS, TLS inspection, and microsegmentation, which together provide the security-oriented detection of unusual or unauthorized traffic that VCF alone does not.

#### VMware vDefend (Meets)

VMware vDefend provides multiple capabilities for continuously monitoring inbound and outbound communications traffic for unusual or unauthorized activities.

Security Intelligence continuously monitors for new flows between environments and alerts administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic, enabling ongoing policy adjustments. The Environments Monitoring dashboard provides traffic flow analysis with protection status summaries for selected environment pairs within a configurable time range. Private IP range definitions establish the boundary between internal and external workloads, so traffic crossing that boundary is treated as external and subject to additional scrutiny. The Secure External Communications security project identifies workloads with inbound or outbound connections to external IP addresses, helping address exposure to external threats. The Monitor and Plan Overview page provides a consolidated view of pending actions, data collection status, network security posture, flow trends, and a summary of suspicious traffic. Network traffic flows in Security Intelligence are based on the distributed firewall rules in effect and on traffic flows observed during the selected time period, including traffic that matches stateful Layer 4 DFW rules.

Network Traffic Analysis (NTA) for Suspicious Traffic continuously monitors and analyzes network traffic and flow records using advanced machine learning models and statistical techniques to establish a baseline of normal network behavior and identify deviations such as protocol misuse and unusual traffic patterns. NTA detects anomalies in the time series profile of individual hosts, identifying unusual network traffic patterns that may indicate compromise or policy violation. Suspicious traffic detectors report execution status after each run, with possible values of NOT_STARTED, SUCCESS, NOT_ENOUGH_BASELINE, or FAILURE, giving administrators visibility into detector health and data availability. Suspicious traffic events are surfaced through the Suspicious Traffic Events dashboard and the Threat Monitoring tab within Security Services Platform, providing a centralized view for security analysts.

vDefend IDS/IPS inspects communications traffic for known attack signatures and suspicious behavior across both distributed and gateway deployments. IDS/IPS event monitoring supports filtering by traffic type, allowing administrators to focus on distributed traffic, gateway traffic, or both. Threshold and rate filters allow event triggering based on traffic behavior patterns, enabling administrators to tune detection sensitivity to their environment.

The vDefend distributed firewall supports Monitoring Mode, which enables logging for all connectivity strategies to audit traffic flowing through the environment. DFW rules support per-rule logging of both allowed and dropped traffic. The Security Overview Dashboard provides centralized visibility into threat events, with filtering by All Traffic, Distributed Only, or Gateway Only traffic types.

The vDefend gateway firewall secures north-south traffic at the environment boundary, providing a control point for monitoring and filtering inbound and outbound communications. Geo-Fencing capabilities allow administrators to create rules that monitor and control traffic based on geographic origin or destination, down to the country level. In multi-tenant environments, the Centralized Transit Gateway Firewall enables project administrators and providers to configure firewall rules on Transit Gateway nodes for managing incoming and outgoing traffic to external networks.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides several mechanisms for continuous monitoring of application-layer communications traffic flowing through its configured virtual services.

At the analytics layer, Avi Analytics continuously evaluates traffic against statistical baselines for each virtual service, detecting anomalies when traffic falls outside the statistical norm for the time period. The system assigns anomaly penalties for deviations and surfaces them in virtual service and pool analytics dashboards. Attack Visibility tools track over 500 real-time metrics across virtual services, supporting DDoS detection and response workflows.

DataScripts extend the monitoring and mitigation capabilities by allowing custom identification of suspicious traffic and execution of appropriate mitigation actions at both L4 and L7 layers. Port-scan detection allows Avi Load Balancer to report or block sources performing port scans as part of DDoS mitigation.

Bot Management via Avi WAF bot detection can classify traffic as dangerous bots and apply HTTP security actions including closing connections. In Cisco ACI-based deployments, integrated WAF (iWAF) provides granular security insights on traffic flows and rule matches, supporting precise policy enforcement based on observed traffic patterns.

For integration with external monitoring infrastructure, the Virtual Service Sideband Profile replicates incoming HTTP(S) traffic at the protocol level to designated logging or sideband servers. This enables deep traffic inspection by external SIEM platforms or inspection appliances. Organizations should configure log forwarding to a centralized syslog or SIEM target to aggregate Avi's monitoring output with broader network telemetry and to satisfy continuous monitoring requirements across all communications paths, including those not routed through Avi virtual services.

Avi's monitoring scope is bounded to traffic flowing through its configured virtual services. Communications that bypass Avi, such as east-west workload traffic not routed through the load balancer, require separate monitoring tooling at the platform level.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-04(05)

> Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] when the following system- generated indications of compromise or potential compromise occur: [Assignment: organization-defined compromise indicators].

**SCF Controls:** MON-01.4

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides a layered alerting infrastructure across VMware vCenter and VCF Operations that generates system alerts based on defined conditions, correlates related alerts through intelligent grouping, and forwards them to external management systems through multiple outbound channels. These capabilities address the cybersecurity and data protection dimensions of this control, though the physical and supply chain alert domains require organizational processes and tooling outside of VCF.

At the vCenter layer, the built-in alarm system monitors objects in the virtual infrastructure and triggers alerts when defined conditions are met. vCenter includes an SNMP agent that sends traps when the system starts or when alarms fire. The SNMP agent supports SNMP v1 and v2c traps as well as SNMP v3 traps with all security levels; when configured for v1 or v2c, the agent also handles incoming GET requests, supporting both push-based alerting and poll-based monitoring from management systems. Alarm notifications can also be delivered via email with configurable recipient addresses and subject lines. Alarm rules are configured per event, so administrators must create individual rules for each event type they wish to monitor. VCF PowerCLI supports programmatic creation of SNMP alarm actions using the New-AlarmAction -Snmp parameter, enabling automation of alert configuration across managed vCenter instances. VMware vCenter High Availability can also be configured to send SNMP trap notifications for vCenter HA cluster events, extending alert coverage to vCenter availability and failover scenarios.

VCF Operations provides a more extensive alerting framework built on symptom-based alert definitions. Alerts are generated when collected metric or property data is compared against alert definitions and the defined symptom conditions evaluate as true. Each alert definition includes configurable impact and criticality metadata so administrators can prioritize responses. When an alert triggers, it presents the triggering symptoms alongside recommendations for resolution, giving operators the context they need to act. Administrators can attach recommendations to alert definitions so that engineers have documented remediation steps when alerts fire. Alert definitions support best practices such as consistent naming, actionable recommendations, and appropriate severity levels. The Wait Cycle setting, which defaults to 1, controls how many collection cycles a symptom condition must persist before an alert is generated, and should be adjusted based on the environment's tolerance for repeated alerts on recurring conditions. Administrators can create alert definitions with tighter symptom parameters to track alerts and identify problems before users encounter further issues.

The Alerts data grid in VCF Operations lists all generated alerts and their status. Generated alerts feed the top-level Health, Risk, and Efficiency badges, providing a consolidated operational view across the monitored environment. Alerts can be added by adapters, users, or the VCF Operations system itself. The system supports self-monitoring through internal metrics such as analytics operation counts, threshold check durations, and incoming observation sizes, allowing administrators to verify that the monitoring platform is functioning correctly.

For alert correlation, VCF Operations includes intelligent alert clustering, which groups related alerts together based on their creation time and topology distance. Each alert cluster includes a graphical timeline and an object-relationship chart, helping administrators trace root causes across related infrastructure objects rather than investigating each alert in isolation. This reduces alert noise and helps operators identify underlying root causes.

VCF Operations supports alert definitions based on log events from monitored systems. VCF Log Management collects logs from all VCF components and provides pre-built content packs with dashboards, alerts, and queries for common operational and security scenarios. An NSX-specific content pack includes alerts for NSX environment monitoring. Log-based alert definitions allow administrators to trigger notifications when specific log patterns appear, connecting log analysis with the broader alerting framework. VCF Operations and VCF Log Management must be integrated to implement the centralized log collection architecture.

For alert distribution and external integration, VCF Operations supports multiple outbound notification plug-in types. Notification options can be configured to specify which alerts are sent through each channel. Supported outbound plug-in types include Standard Email, SNMP Trap, Webhook Notification (for REST-based integration with external systems), Slack, Log File, and ServiceNow Notification. Payload templates are available for each plug-in type, allowing administrators to customize the alert content delivered to each destination. At least one instance of the relevant outbound plug-in must be configured and running before notifications can be sent.

SNMP trap notifications can be sent to up to four configurable destinations, and notification rules support filtering to control which alerts are forwarded to which destinations. VCF Operations for Networks also supports SNMP trap forwarding to up to four destinations. VCF Operations can log alerts to an existing SNMP Trap server in the environment. The Management Pack for SNMP plug-in can receive events from vCenter over the SNMP protocol, enabling centralized collection of alerts from across the VCF stack. VCF Operations generates a critical alert when no SNMP targets are configured, helping ensure that outbound notification paths remain active.

The Management Pack for ServiceNow provides alert-sync functionality that synchronizes alerts between VCF Operations and ServiceNow. It creates ServiceNow alerts for VCF Operations alerts and associates them with their Configuration Item if present in the CMDB. Sample configurations allow alerts to be sent to ServiceNow as alerts, events, or incidents without applying filters, and each alerting configuration supports a callerId parameter that identifies the caller for ServiceNow integration. The Slack plugin supports forwarding alerts to Slack channels for notification and troubleshooting purposes.

Cloud proxy instances can be configured to generate their own alerts and notifications. It is recommended to create notification rules for cloud proxy alerts so that remediation steps can be taken promptly when connectivity or service health issues arise. An alert is displayed in VCF Operations when one or more services on a cloud proxy are down.

Out-of-the-box customizable alerts and reports are included with predefined dashboards to assist with operational awareness from initial deployment. Administrators can also create custom dashboards with alert-related widgets to monitor alerts for specific object groups.

At the VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) layer, the Prometheus standard package provides a built-in alerting stack for containerized workloads. When installed on a VKS cluster, it deploys Prometheus AlertManager as a StatefulSet with multiple replicas in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace. Prometheus instances on VKS clusters send alerts to AlertManager via HTTP on the configured web port. The AlertManagerConfig custom resource definition specifies how alerts are grouped, inhibited, and forwarded to external notification systems; the default-receiver routing configuration applies a group wait of 10 seconds, group interval of 5 minutes, and a repeat interval of 3 hours. Administrators can extend these notifications to external incident management systems through AlertManager receiver configurations. AlertManager instances are deployed with a securityContext that enforces non-root execution, running as user and group 65534. VCF Automation provides an Alerts tab on deployment resources that surfaces active alerts sourced from VCF Operations, and Grafana Operator is available alongside Prometheus for visualization of cluster-level monitoring data.

For supply chain integrity alerting, VKS supports configuring Kubernetes admission controllers to verify container image signatures at deploy time. The sigstore policy-controller admission controller validates image authenticity and integrity before workloads are scheduled, blocking or flagging images that fail signature checks. Harbor Registry, available as a standard package on VKS clusters, signs images as trusted to provide image authenticity verification. When copying VKS images to a private registry, the imgpkg copy command supports the --cosign-signatures flag to verify and transfer Cosign signatures alongside images, preserving supply chain traceability. The VCF Consumption CLI binary integrity can be verified using a SHA256 checksum file available for download from the VCF Automation Tenant Portal. Admission policy violations are recorded in the Kubernetes audit log, generating traceable security events that can be forwarded to external monitoring or SIEM platforms.

To achieve the integrated situational awareness described in this control, organizations should configure outbound notification plug-ins from vCenter, VCF Operations, and AlertManager on VKS clusters to forward alerts to a centralized security information and event management (SIEM) or network management platform. Alert definitions should be tuned following the built-in best practices guidance, outbound plug-in instances should be configured and verified as active, and container image signature verification should be enabled on VKS admission controllers to surface supply chain integrity events. The physical security domain of this control and the full cross-domain correlation function require processes and systems outside of VCF's scope.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to cybersecurity alert generation and monitoring within its network security domain but does not cover physical security, supply chain, or data protection alerting.

The Distributed IDS/IPS generates alerts based on configurable threshold conditions, tracking events by source with a count threshold and time period so that alerts fire only after a minimum number of matching events occur within a defined window. Alert logs capture source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port, IP protocol, and timestamp for each triggered event. Signature severity levels are adjustable, allowing tuning of which events generate alerts based on organizational security requirements. To manage alert volume, IDS/IPS supports a Limit option that caps alert generation per host within a specified time frame and a Both threshold type that applies thresholding and rate limiting simultaneously to reduce flooding from high-volume signature matches.

The Threat Event Monitoring dashboard in vDefend provides visibility into the current state of security issues across the data center, helping security teams understand network activity and prioritize focus areas. An alarm triggers and remains active until dropped events are no longer detected, keeping administrators aware of ongoing event-drop conditions beyond the initial notification banner.

Security Services Platform (SSP) generates alarms when allocated resources exceed predetermined threshold values, with deployment status and platform alarms visible in the System Dashboard. SSP alarm records include detailed descriptions, recommended actions, and the date and time each alarm triggered. Alarms can be acknowledged, suppressed, or resolved to manage the alert lifecycle, and the entity ID that caused each alarm is available for correlation. The License Hub dashboard within SSP highlights platform-related alarms displaying system alerts and critical errors requiring attention. SSP also generates alarms on the Bare Metal Server page when certificate updates fail, providing operational awareness of certificate lifecycle issues on bare metal infrastructure.

Security Intelligence continuously monitors for new traffic flows between environments and alerts administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic, supporting ongoing policy adjustment. The Monitor and Plan Overview dashboard displays high-level information about the most recent state of network activities managed by Security Intelligence, while the Environments Monitoring view provides traffic flow analysis with protection status summaries for selected environment pairs across configurable time ranges.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) integrates with external SIEM systems to send security-related event logs about network infrastructure, generating and forwarding logs both when a detection event occurs and when an existing detection is updated. Security teams can investigate individual threat events and correlated campaigns through the SSP interface to assess impact, identify root causes, and take appropriate mitigation actions.

Physical security, supply chain monitoring, and data protection alerts are outside vDefend's scope and require complementary controls to achieve integrated situational awareness.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides alert generation and notification capabilities for data protection activities, integrating with the VMware vCenter alarm framework and supporting outbound notification to external response systems.

PNR extends vCenter's alarm system with recovery-specific alarm types. Alarms are generated automatically when connectivity issues are encountered or when protection groups and recovery plans enter an error state. PNR logs events and triggers corresponding alarms to track system health and address potential issues before they affect protection. Administrators can define and configure the action taken by each alarm type.

Alarm notifications can be configured to send email, SNMP traps, or run scripts on the vCenter host when an alarm triggers. For email notifications to function, Mail settings must be configured in vCenter Settings. PNR requires an administrator email address to be configured during appliance registration with vCenter. Licensing event alerts can also be configured so that licensing administrators receive email notifications when licensing compliance issues occur.

vSphere Replication supports configurable alarms for replication events. Administrators can define and edit alarms so that specific replication events, such as after configuring a virtual machine for replication, trigger notifications.

In 9.1, the unified PNR appliance includes the Clean Room Orchestrator, which adds a distinct alerting surface for cyber recovery operations. The Clean Room Orchestrator sends email alerts to configured recipients when the status of recovery environment components changes, such as when the Orchestrator or connector moves from good to warning status. PNR maintains an Alarms list for the clean room showing a history of all notifications requiring attention, with search and filtering by severity. PNR also supports configuration of email alerts for critical events to notify users of important recovery operations and system events.

The Protection and Recovery Overview Dashboard displays alerts with severity levels of Critical, Immediate, and Warning for snapshots, protection groups, recovery plans, and deployment settings. Through the VCF Protection and Recovery Cloud Management Pack integration with VCF Operations, administrators receive health alerts for protection groups, recovery plans, cloud file systems, recovery SDDCs, and protected sites.

For environments using PNR's Cyber Recovery capability, PNR supports integration with CrowdStrike, which provides CrowdScore for Incident Detections. CrowdScore measures the real-time threat level to recovery endpoints on a scale of 0 to 100, calculated using factors including frequency, severity, and correlation of security alerts, giving administrators a data protection-scoped view of security conditions relevant to recovery readiness.

These PNR-native alerting mechanisms contribute to situational awareness for data protection activities. Physical infrastructure alerting, network security alerting, and supply chain alerting fall outside PNR's scope and depend on the broader VCF platform and supplementary tools.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) generates system alerts for conditions within its operational scope, covering database engine health, infrastructure connectivity, cybersecurity events, and data protection activities. The Dashboard displays Global Alerts at a glance, and a dedicated Global Alerts page, accessible from both the plug-in UI within vSphere Client and the standalone UI, provides a consolidated view of non-catastrophic alerts and overall system status. An Alerts pane in the Monitoring tab gives administrators a database-scoped view of active conditions for individual instances.

DSM categorizes alert conditions at three severity levels: OK, WARNING, and CRITICAL. Named alert types include INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_VC_ALARMS, triggered when one or more infrastructure resource alarms found in VMware vCenter (vCenter) are active; VCENTER_CONNECTIVITY, triggered when vCenter connectivity fails; DATABASE_ENGINE_HEALTH_ALERT, triggered when the database engine service is experiencing problems or disruptions; and alerts for vCenterBinding API status and LicensingNotUpdated conditions. DSM also generates alerts for authentication failures, backup and restore corruptions, and certificate errors. When critical alarms are active for vCenter hosts, DSM surfaces guidance to avoid create, upgrade, and scale operations to reduce the risk of failures during degraded infrastructure states. Each alert includes resolution suggestions that give operators actionable steps to resolve conditions without disrupting database services. Alert notification settings and threshold values for database alerts are configurable.

To support integration with broader monitoring and incident response workflows, DSM can forward alerts as email notifications or as webhook notifications to external systems. Named integration targets include Slack channels and ServiceNow. Webhooks support customizable payload templates and AlertLevel filtering, restricting delivery to CRITICAL or WARNING conditions as appropriate for the operational model. Alert payloads include the alert name, alert level, alert description, trigger timestamp, alert message, database cluster identifier, cluster name, triggered user email, task status, previous status, and task type. At the individual database level, administrators can configure alert notifications to route to Email, Slack, or ServiceNow endpoints. This mechanism supports routing DSM-generated alerts into enterprise monitoring platforms, SIEM systems, or ticketing workflows.

DSM integrates with VCF Operations to surface monitoring data for database services, providing an additional visibility channel for triggered or active alerts scoped to individual nodes or the overall cluster within the broader VCF observability stack.

DSM's alert coverage is scoped to the database-as-a-service layer: database instance health, infrastructure policy compliance, connectivity to platform components, authentication events, and backup operation outcomes. Physical security, network-level cybersecurity events, and supply chain activities are outside DSM's scope and require monitoring through the broader VCF platform and organizational tooling. Correlating DSM alerts with alerts from other platform components, and implementing a coordinated response workflow that spans physical, cybersecurity, data protection, and supply chain domains, requires organizational processes and enterprise monitoring capabilities beyond what DSM provides on its own.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer has a native alerting subsystem that generates, routes, and responds to alerts from application delivery and cybersecurity events. Alert Configurations define the conditions under which alerts are generated, drawing from system events or metrics as the configurable source, with parameters specifying when an alert should fire and what actions to take. When conditions are met, Avi executes the configured Action Groups that trigger notifications for new alerts. The Alert Manager captures alert details across the system, including information for Service Engines, Virtual Services, and Pools, along with event identifiers and cloud-specific metadata such as Service Engine VM UUIDs.

For alert routing, the Avi Controller supports four notification destinations: the local Controller UI (visible under Operations > Alerts > All Alerts, categorized by configured Alert Level), email, syslog, and SNMP traps. SNMP trap support covers both SNMPv2c and SNMPv3 protocols. In 9.1, SNMP notifications can additionally be configured with TLS encryption and strict certificate verification for secure transport to external SNMP servers, and multiple SNMP servers can be specified for notification delivery to support redundancy. Syslog Audit Persistence forwards alert events to remote syslog servers for centralized monitoring and audit trail collection. All destinations are configured through Operations > Notifications in the Controller UI. The Controller cluster leader issues SNMP trap notifications based on system events, and event-based traps can include status-change events such as virtual-service-down conditions. Local alert notifications are generated by default in addition to any configured external alert actions.

For automated response, Avi Alert Actions support ControlScripts: Python scripts executed on the Controller that receive the triggering alert's EVENT_DESCRIPTION as an environment variable and full alert details as JSON. Alert Actions can be associated with specific system events and can trigger email, Syslog, SNMP Trap, or ControlScript responses. Avi logs configured alerts and their execution status in the Alert Actions tab under Operations > Alerts. Custom Alert Actions can be created alongside or in place of the default system alert actions (such as System-Alert-Level-High), allowing organizations to tailor alerting and automated response to their operational and security requirements.

For VCF-integrated deployments in 9.1, the Avi Load Balancer Observability Management Pack provides unified alert reporting across multiple Avi instances, giving VCF platform operators a consolidated view of alerting state across the Avi deployment.

Auditors should verify that Alert Configurations and Alert Actions are configured under Operations in the Avi Controller UI, that syslog or SNMP trap destinations are set to route events to the organization's centralized monitoring platform, and that alert thresholds reflect the cybersecurity and application availability events within the Avi scope. The organization is responsible for routing Avi alerts into the broader monitoring stack and for establishing the cross-domain correlation required to achieve integrated situational awareness across physical, data protection, and supply chain domains.

### Control SI-04(10)

> System Monitoring | Visibility of Encrypted Communications Make provisions so that [Assignment: organization-defined encrypted communications traffic] is visible to [Assignment: organization-defined system monitoring tools and mechanisms].

**SCF Controls:** NET-18.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend includes TLS Inspection capability that intercepts, decrypts, and re-encrypts traffic transparently via a TLS proxy, making encrypted communications visible to downstream security enforcement mechanisms including IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention, and URL Filtering. Administrators configure TLS Inspection through External Decryption Action Profiles, which support predefined and custom decryption settings to target specific traffic categories for inspection. vDefend TLS Inspection supports importing or generating trusted and untrusted proxy Certificate Authorities, giving administrators control over the CA infrastructure required for transparent interception.

Without TLS Inspection enabled, advanced security features cannot enforce policy or gain visibility into encrypted traffic. Full URL Filtering of encrypted traffic requires TLS Inspection to be enabled so that traffic is decrypted before category evaluation. The Security Services Platform's Security Segmentation Report identifies Communication Chains as groups of workloads communicating with each other and flags chains that include at least one at-risk unprotected member, giving administrators visibility into where TLS Inspection coverage should be extended. The Security Services Platform also routes and monitors internet-bound HTTP/HTTPS traffic through a configurable proxy, providing an additional point of visibility for outbound encrypted communications.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Meets)

Avi Load Balancer is the TLS-terminating application proxy in VCF deployments, and it provides native mechanisms for making encrypted communications visible to monitoring tools.

The foundational mechanism is TLS termination. Avi Service Engines terminate incoming encrypted connections at the virtual service boundary, decrypting application traffic before forwarding it to back-end servers. This gives Avi's own analytics and logging infrastructure full visibility into the decrypted request and response stream. When back-end pool encryption is required, a server-side SSL profile can be applied to the pool to re-encrypt traffic between the Service Engine and origin servers, maintaining end-to-end encryption while preserving proxy-level visibility into application content.

A second mechanism is SSL/TLS intercept mode. In this mode, client SSL traffic is terminated at the Service Engine, the decrypted payload is sent unencrypted over the internal wire so that network taps can intercept and inspect it, and the traffic is then re-encrypted before transmission to the destination server. This makes application-layer content available to network monitoring infrastructure without altering the traffic's final encrypted form as seen by the back-end.

A third mechanism is traffic cloning. Virtual services can be configured to clone incoming traffic and forward a copy of the decrypted stream, with the original client IP preserved, to a designated pool of monitoring or inspection servers. External security tools or logging infrastructure can inspect decrypted content without affecting live application traffic.

In Kubernetes environments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) supports TLS termination through its HTTPRule custom resource definition (CRD), which also provides an option to re-encrypt traffic between the Service Engine and back-end application servers after client-side TLS has been terminated. This preserves the same proxy-level visibility model in containerized workload deployments.

Organizations configure these capabilities through the Avi Controller UI or REST API. SSL/TLS intercept mode and traffic cloning require deliberate per-virtual-service configuration; TLS termination is the standard operating mode for HTTPS virtual services.

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-04(12)

> System Monitoring | Automated Organization-generated Alerts Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms] when the following indications of inappropriate or unusual activities with security or privacy implications occur: [Assignment: organization-defined activities that trigger alerts].

**SCF Controls:** MON-01.12

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple automated alerting mechanisms across its management stack that can notify incident response personnel when anomalous or potentially security-relevant activities occur.

VCF Operations is the primary alerting platform. Administrators create alert definitions that specify symptom parameters and conditions; when those conditions evaluate as true, VCF Operations generates an alert. Each alert definition can be configured with an Impact setting (such as Risk, indicating a potential problem requiring near-term attention) and associated recommendations that describe how to resolve the issue. Symptom parameters can be tightened to detect problems earlier, before they affect users or escalate into incidents. Anomalies are highlighted automatically when metrics cross dynamic or static thresholds.

VCF Operations supports multiple outbound notification plug-ins for delivering alerts to response teams. The Standard Email plug-in sends alert notifications to configured email addresses via SMTP. The SNMP Trap plug-in forwards alerts to up to four trap destinations per instance, with notification filtering available to control which alert types are forwarded to which destinations. The Webhook Notification plug-in delivers alerts in JSON or XML format to any REST API endpoint, enabling integration with ticketing systems, incident management platforms, and custom automation. The Slack plug-in posts pre-formatted alert details directly to Slack channels, with support for multiple notification rules targeting different channels. If no SNMP targets are configured, VCF Operations generates a critical alert to flag the gap in notification coverage. Alerts can also be logged on an existing SNMP Trap server.

Notification rules act as filters, controlling which alerts are sent through which channels, so teams receive only the alerts relevant to their responsibilities. Each alert notification includes the rule name, alert ID, and server identification for triage.

VCF Operations Intelligent Alerts reduce noise by clustering related alerts based on creation time and topology proximity. Rather than flooding response teams with thousands of individual alerts, this capability groups related alerts together based on their correlation to the same underlying issue, helping personnel focus on actionable problems without being overwhelmed by duplicate or cascading notifications from a single root cause.

VCF Operations also supports alert automation through policies. When the highest-priority recommendation for an alert definition has an associated action, that action can be automated, enabling the platform to take predefined remediation steps or escalation actions without manual intervention. Alert definitions can be activated with automated actions in policies.

VCF Operations for Networks provides its own alert framework with notifications sent via email, SNMP trap (to up to four destinations), or both. Search-based alerts allow administrators to define custom alerting criteria, with all user-created alerts categorized as search-based alerts. These support configurable severity levels (Critical, Moderate, Warning, and Info) and can send email notifications at configurable frequencies to specified addresses. Out-of-the-box alerts are also available but require explicit enablement to trigger notifications.

At the VMware vCenter layer, the alarm system sends notifications through two channels when alarm conditions are triggered: SNMP traps and email. The SNMP agent included with vCenter sends traps when alarms fire, allowing integration with enterprise SNMP management platforms. The vCenter SNMP agent supports SNMPv1, SNMPv2c, and SNMPv3 with all security levels, allowing organizations to select the appropriate version and authentication strength for their environment. Email notifications can be configured with specific recipient addresses and customizable alarm subject lines, enabling direct notification of incident response personnel. Administrators create alarm definitions with triggers for specific state transitions across hosts, VMs, storage, and networking objects. vSphere Configuration Profiles can also trigger alarms that send email or SNMP trap notifications when configuration drift is detected on cluster hosts, supporting automated alerting when host configurations deviate from the desired baseline. PowerCLI supports programmatic creation of SNMP alarm actions through the New-AlarmAction -Snmp parameter and script-based alarm actions through the New-AlarmAction -Script parameter, enabling bulk configuration of alarm-to-notification mappings and automated script execution when alarms fire. Alarms can also be managed programmatically through the vSphere Web Services API.

For workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters within the VCF platform, the Prometheus standard package provides an integrated alerting stack. Prometheus Alertmanager is deployed as a high-availability StatefulSet with multiple replicas in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace, with Prometheus instances configured to send alerts to Alertmanager via HTTP. The AlertmanagerConfig custom resource definition (alertmanagerconfigs.monitoring.coreos.com) controls how alerts are grouped, inhibited, and delivered to external notification targets; the default routing configuration applies a group wait of 10 seconds, group interval of 5 minutes, and repeat interval of 3 hours. The kube-state-metrics component exposes cluster state metrics that Prometheus alert rules can target to detect anomalous conditions such as pod failures, resource exhaustion, and unexpected workload state changes. Audit policies can be configured to generate alerts on security-relevant Kubernetes API server events, such as concurrent reads of multiple Secrets by a single user, and the resulting audit event records carry response codes and timestamps that alerting and SIEM pipelines can consume.

Pod Security Admission (PSA) on VKS clusters supports three enforcement modes on a per-namespace basis: enforce, audit, and warn. In audit mode, security policy violations generate an audit annotation in the API server event record without blocking the workload, producing a stream of security events that alert pipelines can consume. In warn mode, violations generate a user-facing warning. Organizations deploying ArgoCD as a Supervisor service can enable the notification controller via the notification.enabled field in the ArgoCD custom resource, which delivers alerts on GitOps pipeline events including deployment failures and configuration drift. The VCF Automation self-service catalog surfaces active resource alerts in its Alerts tab, drawing data from VCF Operations, providing consumption-layer visibility into operational and security conditions on deployed resources.

All VCF components support syslog forwarding to external SIEM platforms, enabling centralized alert correlation and automated incident notification through the organization's existing security operations tooling. vCenter provides REST API endpoints for configuring syslog forwarding, and vSphere Supervisor can forward control plane VM system logs to external syslog servers via Fluent Bit using RFC 5424 format. VCF Log Management collects and indexes log data from across the stack. Its content packs include pre-built dashboards, alerts, and queries for VCF components, supporting security use cases such as failed login attempts, privilege escalations, and suspicious administrative activity.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides automated alerting across several security capabilities in 9.1. Security Intelligence continuously monitors for new network flows between environments and alerts administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic, categorizing detected suspicious traffic events by impact type (Critical, High, Medium, and Low) to help prioritize response actions. Security Intelligence also detects and displays MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques, recording the number of suspicious events associated with each tactic or technique during the selected time period.

The Threat Event Monitoring dashboard, accessible from the Security Overview Dashboard in the vDefend UI, provides security teams with visibility into the current state of security issues across the data center, helping them understand network activity and identify focus areas. When dropped events are detected, an alarm is triggered and remains active until no dropped events are observed, providing persistent notification until the condition clears.

IDS/IPS generates alerts with configurable per-source threshold and rate filtering, allowing administrators to specify both a count threshold and time period before an alert fires. IDS/IPS events are written to the nsx-idps-events.log file on ESX hosts, capturing all alerts, drops, and rejects with metadata including source port, destination IP, IP protocol, and timestamp.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlates related threat events into campaigns and organizes them on a timeline available for analyst triage. NDR automatically sends event logs to a configured SIEM when detection events occur, when events are updated, when new campaigns are created, and when campaigns are updated. NDR also manages exclusion lists and threshold values for detector definitions to reduce alert volume. The Security Services Platform SIEM integration sends campaign and event logs to connected SIEM platforms, including Splunk and VCF Log Management, as they occur.

The Security Services Platform alarm management interface classifies alarms by four severity levels (Critical, High, Medium, Informational), provides detailed alarm descriptions with recommended actions and timestamps, and allows administrators to acknowledge or suppress open alarms.

The actual delivery of alerts to incident response personnel via email, ticketing systems, or paging depends on integration with an external SIEM or alerting tool. vDefend produces, classifies, and forwards security events; the notification delivery chain beyond the SIEM requires external configuration.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) includes an automated alerting system that covers operational anomalies across database infrastructure, supporting the detection and notification requirements of this control.

DSM maintains a Global Alerts page accessible in both the plug-in UI and the standalone UI, which displays non-catastrophic alerts and system status in real time. The DSM Dashboard also surfaces Global Alerts for immediate visibility. Alerts are categorized at three severity levels: OK, WARNING, and CRITICAL. Administrators can configure threshold values for triggering each level across monitored resources including CPU health, data disk health, system disk health, and maximum connections. These thresholds can be tuned to match the organization's tolerance for operational deviation.

Specific alert types cover a range of conditions with potential security or availability implications. DSM generates an INFRASTRUCTURE_POLICY_VC_ALARMS alert when one or more infrastructure resource alarms from VMware vCenter are active, and a VCENTER_NOT_REACHABLE alert when vCenter becomes unreachable from the Provider appliance using the configured FQDN. It generates alerts when orphaned VMs are detected in vCenter. A VCENTER_CONNECTIVITY alert fires when vCenter connectivity fails; the associated guidance directs administrators to verify vCenter authentication logs and confirm that the DSM service account is not locked, making this alert relevant to authentication-related incident detection. A VCENTER_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NOT_CREATED alert fires when vCenter service accounts fail to be created, indicating that DSM cannot authenticate to vCenter for ongoing operations. DSM also generates alerts for vCenterBinding API status and LicensingNotUpdated conditions, and a VCENTER_LICENSE_NOT_FOUND alert when it cannot retrieve vCenter license information.

Alert routing to incident response personnel is handled through the Webhook settings. Administrators configure webhooks that deliver alert notifications to external endpoints including email, Slack channels, and platforms such as ServiceNow. Webhook payloads include the alert name, alert level, alert description, trigger timestamp, alert message, and database cluster identifier. The AlertLevel parameter on each webhook can be scoped to CRITICAL or WARNING, allowing administrators to route high-severity alerts to on-call channels while sending lower-severity notifications to broader audiences. Webhooks can be associated and dissociated per data service, so alert routing can be tuned at the service level. Alerts for database operation failures and database health conditions can be independently enabled or disabled per data service policy. DSM also raises metrics-based alerts when collected metrics from a database cluster cross configured thresholds, and provides resolution suggestions alongside triggered alerts to help administrators address issues without disrupting database services. Active alerts for individual nodes or the overall cluster can be viewed through VCF Operations.

DSM's alerting is designed around database infrastructure health and operational continuity. It does not include behavioral anomaly detection, access pattern analysis, or threat intelligence capabilities. Organizations that require alerting on security-specific events such as unusual access patterns, privilege escalation, or policy violations need to complement DSM's operational alerts with a security information and event management platform or equivalent tooling that consumes DSM's syslog output and audit event stream.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer includes a dedicated alerting framework in the Avi Controller that generates automatic notifications when system and security events occur. Alert configurations are created in the Operations > Alerts > Alert Configurations section of the Controller UI, and notification destinations are configured in the Operations > Notifications page. When an alert condition is triggered, the Controller can deliver notifications to the local Controller UI (displayed as colored bell icons), email, syslog, and SNMP v2c and SNMPv3 traps. In DDoS mitigation contexts, Avi can proactively send alerts to custom APIs in addition to the standard notification channels.

Alert Actions define how the Controller responds to triggered conditions. Each Alert Action can be configured to send an email notification, forward a syslog event to an external syslog server using Syslog-Audit-Persistence, generate an SNMP trap to an SNMP trap server, or invoke a ControlScript for automated response. Alert events carry configurable severity classifications, with defined levels including ALERT_LOW, allowing administrators to tune notification thresholds. Multiple SNMP servers are supported for redundancy. In 9.1, SNMP notification transport can be configured with TLS encryption and strict certificate verification, which helps protect alert traffic in transit. Alert Actions are configured through the Operations > Alerts > Alert Actions interface.

The integration of alerting with automated response is illustrated by the SYN flood detection capability. When a SYN flood is detected, an Alert Action can invoke a ControlScript that automatically adds the offending client IP address to a blocklist IP group attached to a network security policy, combining detection and response in a single workflow. ControlScripts generate detailed execution logs, including operation status, results, and error conditions, supporting audit of alert-driven infrastructure changes.

Metric-based alert configurations support multiple events combined with logical operators, enabling threshold-based triggers that go beyond simple event matching. The Alert Manager captures alerts across the system, covering Service Engines, Virtual Services, and Pools, so alert coverage extends across the full data path.

For WAF deployments, Avi WAF policy in detection mode logs alerts during an attack without taking blocking action, providing visibility into application-layer threats before enforcement is enabled.

To meet this control, organizations must configure notification recipients, define which event categories trigger alerts, and integrate Avi's syslog or SNMP output with their SIEM or incident response platforms to route alerts to the appropriate incident response personnel.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control SI-04(14)

> System Monitoring | Wireless Intrusion Detection Employ a wireless intrusion detection system to identify rogue wireless devices and to detect attack attempts and potential compromises or breaches to the system.

**SCF Controls:** MON-01.5

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-04(20)

> System Monitoring | Privileged Users Implement the following additional monitoring of privileged users: [Assignment: organization-defined additional monitoring].

**SCF Controls:** MON-01.15

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple mechanisms for monitoring privileged user activity across VMware vCenter, VCF Operations, and the VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) consumption layer, enabling organizations to maintain oversight of administrative actions throughout the full platform stack.

vCenter includes a privilege checks recorder that captures authorization decisions as they occur during operations. This feature records which privileges were checked, against which managed objects, by which users and sessions, along with the associated operation IDs. Administrators can query these recorded privilege checks through the vCenter Management API, using the Get Latest Privilege Check operation to retrieve all privilege checks up to or after a specified point in time. The PrivilegeChecks service interface supports filtering by managed object IDs, request operation IDs, principal names, privilege names, and session identifiers through the AuthorizationPrivilegeChecksFilterSpec structure, allowing targeted review of specific privileged user activity. The Privilege Recorder is available only as an API with no corresponding UI; organizations using it will need scripted query workflows to retrieve and review the recorded data.

VCF Operations provides a dedicated user activity audit capability, accessible from the Administration menu under Control Panel by selecting the Audit tile. This audit interface displays the activities that users performed in the environment and provides visibility into user permissions, associated access groups, and access privileges. Activity records can be filtered by User ID, User Name, Auth Source, Session, Client IP, Category, and Message, allowing focused review of privileged account behavior. VCF Operations can generate User Activity Audit reports that provide traceability of privileged user actions including logging in, actions on clusters and nodes, changes to system passwords, activating certificates, and logging out. Administrators can also track how extensions and automation operate by verifying the state of defined entities they create and modify, providing an additional layer of oversight for automated privileged operations.

VCF Operations enforces privilege-based access control that restricts users without appropriate privileges from viewing collected data, so monitoring data itself is protected from unauthorized access. This access control scopes visibility of audit trails and monitoring outputs to users with appropriate privileges.

The platform's role-based access control model supports this monitoring by defining privileged users through roles. vCenter allows creation of custom roles with granular privilege assignment and permission propagation to vSphere objects. Privileges are fine-grained access controls that can be grouped into roles and mapped to users or groups. VCF Operations provides predefined roles to assign privileges, with guidance that the administrators role should be assigned only to specific users who must access objects and perform actions across the entire environment. This clear delineation of privileged accounts makes it straightforward to identify privileged users and to scope audit queries accordingly.

For centralized analysis and long-term retention, VCF Operations can forward user activity logs to an external syslog server, supporting integration with SIEM platforms for correlation, alerting, and forensic investigation of privileged user actions across the environment.

VKS clusters extend privileged user monitoring through the Kubernetes API server audit logging infrastructure. The API server records every API request, capturing the requesting user identity, any impersonated user, source IP address, request verb, resource kind, namespace, and response status code. Audit policy rules can be configured to target specific resource types, API groups, and user groups at higher verbosity, enabling more detailed capture of cluster-admin and other high-privilege account activity. The audit log can be directed to a file backend or forwarded via webhook to an external logging or SIEM platform, where it integrates with the broader VCF audit trail for correlation and alerting. Audit rules can be configured to alert on specific privileged-access patterns, such as concurrent reading of multiple Secrets by a single user, providing targeted detection of suspicious activity by privileged accounts.

The Kubernetes RBAC model within VKS defines which accounts hold cluster-level privileges and structurally limits how that privilege can be obtained or extended. The RBAC API blocks users from escalating their own privileges by editing roles or role bindings, with this enforcement applied at the API level regardless of authorization mode. Creating or updating a RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding requires the bind verb on the target role, and such operations are captured in the Kubernetes audit log, providing a record of every privilege grant or change. Administrators can use kubectl auth can-i with user impersonation to determine what actions a specific account can perform without executing those actions, supporting periodic review of privileged account scope across namespaces and cluster-level resources.

For workload-level privilege controls, VKS enforces Pod Security Admission (PSA) on clusters running VKS release 1.25 and later, and Pod Security Policies (PSP) on clusters running VKS release 1.24 and earlier. The privileged PSA profile and vmware-system-privileged PSP provide unrestricted permissions and allow known privilege escalations; cluster administrators must explicitly bind these policies to authenticated users before those users can create privileged pods, so the set of accounts able to run privileged workloads is explicitly controlled. The Baseline pod security policy template is available for workloads that do not require elevated capabilities, mitigating common privilege escalation risks while maintaining compatibility with standard containerized workloads. VKS documentation recommends that administrators use least-privileged kubeconfig profiles for routine work and reserve cluster-admin credentials for break-glass scenarios, which narrows the set of accounts whose activity warrants enhanced monitoring and makes the boundary between routine and elevated access explicit.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) defines two distinct roles: the DSM Admin role, which has privileges to view and manage all objects in the DSM environment, and the DSM User role, which is restricted to objects the user owns. Organizations can also configure directory service groups with custom role assignments through LDAPS integration, providing more granular control over who holds monitored administrative access. This role structure identifies which DSM accounts hold privileged access, making admin-role actions distinguishable in the audit trail.

DSM generates system audit events that record user actions performed through both the DSM UI and API. These events are accessible through the System Audit Events view, which is the only DSM UI location where non-database-related operations and tasks can be tracked. Because admin-role actions including configuration changes, user management, namespace access modifications, and policy changes all flow through the DSM UI and API, they are captured in this audit trail alongside standard user actions. Administrators can review these events directly in the System Audit Events view.

DSM provides Gateway Audit Logs that record user read and write operations to DSM data services or infrastructure objects performed in the DSM UI or the DSM Gateway Kubernetes interface. The Permissions view in the DSM administrator UI supports creation, monitoring, and management of configured users, giving administrators visibility into the current user population and their role assignments. Role assignments and namespace access for existing users can be reviewed and modified through the user management interface.

For SQL Server databases managed by DSM, the platform assigns the cluster administrator login restricted server-level privileges rather than full sysadmin access. DSM grants the cluster administrator specific capabilities such as ALTER ANY LOGIN and VIEW SERVER STATE for server management and auditing, limiting the effective privilege footprint of database-level admin accounts.

For organizations seeking monitoring that targets privileged-user activity specifically, DSM's audit event data supports that goal when combined with organizational processes for log review and alerting. DSM supports syslog forwarding, which allows audit events to be shipped to a centralized logging system where filtering and alerting rules can be applied to admin-role actions.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports privileged user oversight at the application delivery controller layer through an explicit privileged account model, role-based access control (RBAC), and security event forwarding.

Avi defines privileged users through its Super User account model. The admin account created during Avi Controller installation automatically has Super User access enabled. Additional accounts can hold Super User status through the is_superuser flag, which grants the account access to all tenants with every role; disabling the flag reverts the account to standard access. Once a user is assigned Super User privileges, no other tenant or role mapping assignment affects the access level, making Super User accounts a well-defined and enumerable privileged tier within the Controller. The admin user is authorized and responsible for the highest-level configuration operations, including user creation and authorization, tenant creation, and all infrastructure associated with the admin tenant.

A second privileged account type is the avidebuguser account. A non-admin user configured as a super-user can be associated with the Avi Controller using the attach command, which grants the user access to the Controller container with sudo privileges. Organizations should include accounts with avidebuguser access in their privileged user inventory and apply the same enhanced oversight policies as for Super User accounts.

Privileged access can also be assigned through Auth Mapping Profile rules. A Super User Rule in the Auth Mapping Profile assigns Super User privileges to a matched user, granting access to all tenants with the most privileged role. Authorization mapping rules can elevate a user to super user status based on group membership, such as membership in the Service Operators group. When a user has multiple LDAP or TACACS+ vantageRole attribute values, the Avi Controller assigns the most privileged role to take effect in the session. This structured assignment means the population of privileged users is defined through explicit configuration, making it possible to identify which accounts require enhanced monitoring.

Beyond user classification, the Avi Controller supports Extended Granular RBAC using markers. Marker-based label matching scopes access to specific resources, so privilege levels are explicit and tied to named roles with defined permission sets. Roles carry Write, Read, No Access, or Assorted privilege levels per resource type; the Assorted level allows a user to hold a mixture of different privileges for different resources within a system area. Granular RBAC includes distinct permission categories for alert operations, including PERMISSION_ALERT, PERMISSION_ALERTCONFIG, PERMISSION_ALERTEMAILCONFIG, and PERMISSION_ALERTSYSLOGCONFIG, allowing organizations to control which roles can configure the alert forwarding mechanisms used for activity monitoring. In Kubernetes environments, markers on Avi Load Balancer objects can provide user access permissions through the Granular RBAC feature on the Avi Controller.

For activity visibility, the Avi Load Balancer can proactively send alerts to email, syslog, SNMP, or custom APIs as security events occur. This forwarding mechanism supports integration with a centralized security monitoring or SIEM platform, where administrators can define enhanced alerting policies targeting activity by privileged accounts. Configuration changes made through the Controller are associated with the authenticated user, so forwarded events carry the context needed to attribute actions to specific privileged accounts.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-04(22)

> System Monitoring | Unauthorized Network Services (a) Detect network services that have not been authorized or approved by [Assignment: organization-defined authorization or approval processes]; and  (b) [Selection (one or more): Audit; Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]   ] when detected.

**SCF Controls:** MON-11.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides automated mechanisms for detecting unauthorized network services and alerting incident response personnel through VCF Operations for Networks, NSX alarm capabilities, VCF Operations alerting infrastructure, configurable notification delivery channels, and Kubernetes-native controls in VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) for containerized workloads.

VCF Operations for Networks includes intent-based networking verification, which models all possible network-wide behavior and continuously checks whether defined network intents are satisfied. When an intent is violated, the system triggers an alert automatically. This capability allows administrators to define expected network service baselines and receive automated notification when the observed network state deviates from those baselines. The Network Operations Dashboard provides visibility into failed intents and unprotected flows, giving operators a consolidated view of network anomalies that may indicate unauthorized services.

VCF Operations for Networks supports discovery alerts, delete alerts, and change alerts, which can be filtered by username to track who made modifications. Discovery alerts notify administrators when new network entities appear in the environment, while change alerts flag modifications to existing network configurations. These alert types provide automated detection of changes that could represent unauthorized network services being introduced into the managed infrastructure. The Network Alerts view includes a metric overview of all alerts in the environment with search capability and the ability to drill into alert details and related alerts, giving administrators a consolidated view of network activity and anomalies. Search-based alerts extend this capability by letting administrators define custom alert rules for metrics and conditions not covered by built-in thresholds, enabling detection of specific unauthorized service patterns. Alert definitions support custom search queries to include or exclude entities in alert definition rules.

VCF Operations for Networks captures administrative actions in audit logs, including regular CRUD operations and login/logout events. Access to audit logs is controlled by user role. When threshold violations occur, administrators can use flow search functionality to view network routing details and flow information, and trace the network path to investigate the source of unauthorized services. NSX Traceflow allows inspection of the transport node-level path of a packet to examine routing and forwarding behavior, and Advanced Path Search supports filtering by path status to view reachable or blocked paths.

For physical and virtual network device monitoring, the Management Pack for Network Devices pulls alerts and events from discovered network devices and displays them within VCF Operations. The Network Devices Alerts view shows all operational alerts specific to resources discovered by adapter instances, and selecting an alert displays relationships and health data to help determine the origin and cause of the alert. Alert severity for threshold violations is configurable, allowing organizations to tune alerting sensitivity to their requirements.

NSX provides configurable alarm definitions that allow administrators to enable or disable detection of specific alarm conditions. Alarms can be configured to be reported in the API, the user interface, or both, and administrators can control whether an SNMP trap is emitted when an alarm is detected or resolved. This flexibility lets teams tailor alerting to their operational requirements and integrate with existing incident response workflows.

For Kubernetes workloads running on VKS clusters, the platform provides controls that restrict unauthorized network service exposure and surface policy violations. Antrea, the network plugin integrated with VKS, enforces Kubernetes NetworkPolicy objects that define permitted ingress and egress traffic per namespace and workload; the Antrea NetworkPolicyStats feature provides enforcement statistics that surface policy-blocked connection attempts, giving administrators visibility into traffic that deviates from declared policy baselines. The Kubernetes DenyServiceExternalIPs admission controller, active in VKS clusters, rejects any new Service object that sets spec.externalIPs, which blocks workloads from using this mechanism to expose services to external networks outside the cluster's standard ingress paths. Cluster administrators can restrict the IP address ranges that Kubernetes Services may use through ServiceCIDR resources, enforced via ValidatingAdmissionPolicy that intercepts CREATE and UPDATE operations on the servicecidrs resource in the networking.k8s.io API group; this configuration limits service assignments to approved address ranges. Kubernetes RBAC controls who may create or modify Service and NetworkPolicy objects, reducing the likelihood that unauthorized services are introduced without oversight. One important limitation is that standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources cannot log individual network security events such as connections that were blocked or accepted; detecting unauthorized services through traffic flow analysis requires additional network observability tooling, such as Antrea flow export capabilities, that organizations must deploy and configure independently.

Prometheus with Alertmanager is available as a standard package for VKS clusters, deployed by Prometheus Operator into the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace as a StatefulSet with multiple replicas for high availability. Prometheus instances on VKS clusters are configured to send alerts to Alertmanager instances in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace via HTTP, and administrators configure the AlertmanagerConfig custom resource to specify how alerts are grouped, inhibited, and routed to external notification channels such as email, webhook endpoints, or ticketing systems. The Prometheus Operator includes an optional admission webhook that validates PrometheusRule and AlertmanagerConfig custom resources to reduce misconfiguration errors. Organizations can configure Prometheus alerting rules against network-related metrics and route the resulting alerts through Alertmanager to incident response channels. The Kubernetes API server's audit logging records all operations on Service, NetworkPolicy, and Endpoints objects, including user identity, source IP, and timestamp; forwarding these audit logs to a security event management platform supports detection of unauthorized service creation or NetworkPolicy modifications outside of approved change windows.

VCF provides multiple channels for delivering alerts to incident response personnel. VMware vCenter can send SNMP traps when alarms are triggered, and the built-in SNMP agent sends traps when the vCenter system starts or when alarms fire. vCenter supports SNMP v1, v2c, and v3 notifications; SNMPv3 supports all security levels including authentication and encryption, enabling secure trap delivery to management systems. vSphere Configuration Profiles can also trigger SNMP trap notifications when configuration changes to clusters or hosts are detected, extending automated alerting to configuration drift scenarios that could indicate unauthorized network services being introduced. VCF Operations supports outbound SNMP trap notifications to up to four SNMP trap destinations, with filtering available when defining notification rules. vCenter also supports email notifications with configurable recipient addresses when alarms are triggered. VCF PowerCLI supports creating SNMP alarm actions programmatically using the New-AlarmAction -Snmp parameter, enabling automated configuration of alert delivery across the environment. VCF supports syslog forwarding from components to external platforms, including vCenter syslog forwarding configurable via the vCenter Management API, Supervisor control plane log forwarding through Fluent Bit, and VCF Log Management pulling tasks, events, and alerts from vCenter instances and receiving syslog feeds from VMware ESX hosts. These mechanisms enable correlation of network service detection alerts with broader security monitoring for incident response.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides multiple complementary network-layer detection capabilities that contribute to identifying unauthorized network services.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) collects detection events from every activated event source in the environment, including IDS signature matches, suspicious traffic detectors, and malware event data. NDR assigns a threat classification to every detection event and aggregates and correlates events to produce visualizations of specific threats mapped to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques. Security analysts can triage detections, filter affected workloads by IP address or CIDR range to identify suspicious or compromised hosts, and create remediation policies directly within NDR. NDR integrates with SIEMs, forwarding campaign and event logs as they occur, which provides the mechanism for routing detection events to incident response personnel.

Security Intelligence continuously monitors for new flows between environments and alerts administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic. Administrators can supplement automatic detection by providing CSV files listing known infrastructure servers and selecting which detected servers to include in security rules.

vDefend (DFW) and Gateway Firewall (GFW) rules can enforce allowlists of permitted services and ports. Administrators should modify the GFW default ANY ANY ALLOW rule to DROP or enable logging to capture traffic from unauthorized network services and meet compliance requirements.

vDefend IDS/IPS detects network intrusion events through signature matches against traffic in the protected network and maintains detailed event logs on ESX hosts, capturing alerts, drops, and rejects with metadata including source port and timestamp.

Detection is limited to the network layer. Unauthorized services running as host processes or within managed workloads require endpoint or host-based controls outside vDefend's scope. Alerting of incident response personnel depends on integration with an external SIEM or notification system.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-05

> a. Receive system security alerts, advisories, and directives from [Assignment: organization- defined external organizations] on an ongoing basis; b. Generate internal security alerts, advisories, and directives as deemed necessary; c. Disseminate security alerts, advisories, and directives to: [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]; [Assignment: organization-defined elements within the organization]; [Assignment: organization-defined external organizations]]; and d. Implement security directives in accordance with established time frames, or notify the issuing organization of the degree of noncompliance.

**SCF Controls:** NET-12, TDA-18, THR-03

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-06

> a. Verify the correct operation of [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy functions]; b. Perform the verification of the functions specified in SI-6a [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined system transitional states]; upon command by user with appropriate privilege; [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]]; c. Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] to failed security and privacy verification tests; and d. [Selection (one or more): Shut the system down; Restart the system; [Assignment: organization-defined alternative action(s)]] when anomalies are discovered.

**SCF Controls:** CHG-06

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several technical mechanisms that support verification of cybersecurity and data protection control functionality following changes, though the organizational process of systematically defining when verification is required and documenting results remains an additional responsibility.

VCF Operations includes a Configuration Drifts capability that monitors vCenter instances and vSphere clusters for deviations from assigned configuration templates. When a change is made, scheduled drift detection compares current configuration values against the desired state defined in those templates. Any deviation is flagged, and administrators can view and download drift status reports from the Configuration Drifts dashboard. Configuration templates can be integrated with a source control repository for versioning and change control, supporting automated testing and validation of configuration updates after changes are applied.
