At the host and cluster level, vSphere Configuration Profiles manage the configuration of all hosts in a cluster collectively, ensuring consistency and detecting when individual host configurations deviate from the cluster-level desired state. vSphere Lifecycle Manager extends this to software specifications, offering compliance check operations that return the compliance status of each host in a cluster against the committed desired state. Together, these mechanisms allow administrators to confirm that host-level security controls remain intact after patching, upgrades, or configuration changes.

VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure objects against configured compliance benchmarks and identifies non-compliant objects along with their specific violations. When non-compliance is detected, VCF Operations provides actionable remediation recommendations. Automated remediation actions can also be configured to bring objects back into compliance based on severity. Separately, VCF includes automated compliance assessment capabilities for vSphere security controls based on the VMware vSphere Security Configuration Guide. These assessments can be run after changes to confirm that host and virtual machine configurations remain aligned with the established security baseline, where drift from the baseline after a change would indicate that a control is no longer functioning as intended.

NSX Manager maintains detailed audit logs that capture both old and new values for modified security policies. When a security policy is changed, the audit log records the user who performed the operation, the security policy identifier, rule configurations, action types, and sequence numbers. This level of detail allows administrators to review exactly what changed in a security policy and compare the before-and-after state, which is a foundational step in verifying that security controls continue to operate as designed after modifications.

Lifecycle management operations include built-in precheck validation. VCF Operations requires a precheck validation step before management component upgrades can proceed, and NSX Manager's upgrade coordinator includes pre-checks to validate upgrade readiness. These prechecks help confirm that the environment is in a known-good state before changes are applied.

VCF Automation provides a Content and Policies interface that identifies custom forms containing actions that cannot be validated. The Form Actions tab in the custom form designer lists all actions used in a form, allowing administrators to review non-validatable actions and verify their input validation code. This provides a mechanism for verifying that automation-driven controls continue to enforce expected behavior after form or policy modifications. VCF Automation day 2 action policies control which users can run selected actions on deployed resources, with only users matching the policy criteria permitted to execute those actions. After changes to these policies, administrators can review the policy configuration to confirm that the intended access restrictions remain in place.

At the Kubernetes consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor provide additional mechanisms for verifying security control behavior after changes. Pod Security Admission supports three enforcement modes that can be set independently per namespace: enforce, audit, and warn. The enforce mode blocks pods that do not comply with the configured Pod Security Standard; the audit mode logs non-compliant pod creation attempts without blocking; and the warn mode returns a warning to the API client without blocking. VKS cluster management provides out-of-the-box security policy templates, including baseline and restricted profiles that mirror Pod Security Standard definitions, and custom policies can be created in Dry run mode to observe their impact before enabling full enforcement. After a change to Pod Security Admission configuration, operators can apply the restricted profile in audit or warn mode to observe which workloads would be affected, with the audit log capturing the results for review.

Kubernetes ValidatingAdmissionPolicy bindings support validationActions of Deny, Warn, and Audit to control how validation failures are handled during admission. When set to Audit, policy violations are recorded in the Kubernetes audit log without blocking traffic, allowing operators to observe policy match behavior after a policy change without disrupting production workloads. The FailurePolicy field governs behavior when a policy expression fails to evaluate, and bindings are scoped to namespaces so that namespace-level labels determine enforcement scope. After deploying or modifying a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, operators can submit representative test resources to verify that validation rules fire as intended.

Kubernetes RBAC provides a SelfSubjectAccessReview capability that allows operators to query whether a given subject holds a specific permission on a given resource, enabling direct confirmation that role bindings are applying as intended after changes to Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, or ClusterRoleBindings. Pod security contexts can also be reviewed after policy changes to verify that individual workloads are operating within the intended privilege boundaries.

At the Supervisor infrastructure level, core cluster component pods such as etcd and kube-apiserver record restart events and uptime duration. After configuration changes that affect the cluster control plane, operators can examine these health indicators to confirm that cluster infrastructure components remain continuously operational and that changes have not disrupted core control functionality.

For resilience control verification, VKS cluster management supports backup and restore of entire clusters, selected namespaces, or specific resources identified by a label, managed through the VCF Automation Tenant Portal under Manage & Govern > VCF Services > Kubernetes Management. The Velero Plugin for vSphere supports both stateless and stateful applications and allows restore operations to be initiated from the Data Protection tab, including restoring a backup from a source cluster to a different target cluster. VKS cluster node VMs are backed up and restored through Supervisor state backup using the vCenter backup feature. After changes to backup or storage configuration, performing a test restore from a recent backup confirms that the resilience control is functioning correctly.

Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) for Supervisor and VMware Kubernetes Service release nodes (VKrs) are available to guide hardening verification for the Kubernetes platform layer, providing a reference baseline against which post-change configuration compliance can be assessed.

These capabilities provide the technical building blocks for post-change control verification across the full VCF platform: configuration drift detection, compliance benchmarking, audit logging of security policy changes, upgrade prechecks, automation policy review, Pod Security Admission audit modes, admission policy verification, Kubernetes RBAC inspection, and VKS backup restore testing. However, they do not by themselves constitute a complete verification program. Organizations must establish procedures that define when functional verification is required, what controls to test after specific types of changes, how to perform functional testing of whether security controls are actively blocking, detecting, or enforcing as intended, and how to document verification results.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend and the Security Services Platform (SSP) provide several mechanisms that help organizations verify that network security controls continue to operate as intended after changes to firewall policies, segmentation rules, and threat protection configurations.

The Security Services Platform's Security Intelligence feature includes a Monitoring capability that checks for changes in the scope of input entities every hour when enabled. When a change is detected, affected policy recommendations are flagged with a "change-detected" status indicator. Administrators can review the flagged recommendations through the Policy Recommendations interface, accessible via Monitor & Plan, and rerun the analysis to confirm that segmentation policies and firewall rules remain consistent with the intended security posture after an infrastructure or policy change.

The Rule Analysis feature on the Security Services Platform provides a structured mechanism for verifying the efficiency and correctness of Distributed Firewall rules. When activated, Rule Analysis evaluates rule configurations and generates reports identifying redundant, shadowed, or misconfigured rules. After changes are made to firewall policies, running Rule Analysis helps confirm that the updated rule set operates as intended and that no unintended effects have been introduced. The feature's operational status is visible in the Security Services Platform UI at System > Platform & Features by checking the Rule Analysis feature card Status field.

The Security Services Platform extracts all IDS policies, rules, and security profiles to support multiple remediations, clean up stale policies, and suggest policies that do not conflict with one another. This supports a verification workflow where policy consistency across IDS and threat prevention configurations can be reviewed following changes. Before republishing policies, administrators can also review infrastructure and environment rules through the Review & Add Rules interface for the SSP-Infrastructure policy, confirming that rules are free of error conditions before propagating changes to the enforcement layer.

The Security Services Platform System > Overview page displays the current platform status, allowing administrators to assess stability before and after configuration changes. Feature-specific status indicators are available at System > Platform & Features, where the Rule Analysis and Malware Prevention feature cards each display a current operational status that administrators can check following updates or changes. The platform's deployment wizard also conducts validation checks before implementing configuration changes, reducing the risk that a change introduces a misconfiguration that could silently degrade security control effectiveness.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides several mechanisms that help verify data protection controls remain functional after changes to the environment.

Test recovery operations are the primary verification mechanism. Administrators can run test recoveries at any time to confirm that recovery plans execute correctly without affecting production workloads. Each test exercises the full recovery workflow, including virtual machine startup, network reconfiguration, and any custom steps defined in the plan. Recovery plans transition through defined states during testing, moving to a Test Complete state when the test finishes or a Test Interrupted state when a failure occurs during execution. This state tracking provides immediate visibility into whether data protection controls are operating as designed following any change.

Recovery plan history reports capture the result and timestamps for each plan execution and for individual steps within the plan. These records can be exported for documentation and compliance purposes, giving change management teams evidence that data protection controls were exercised and verified after changes. PNR explicitly requires that protection groups and recovery plans be verified following upgrades to the Protection and Recovery Appliance, and that connections between protected and recovery sites be validated for correctness after any upgrade.

Protection group state monitoring surfaces configuration problems that result from environmental changes. Protection and Recovery protection groups monitor the status of virtual machines and determine which operations are allowed in each protection state. Groups transition through well-defined states including Configuring Protection, Removing Protection, Restoring Placeholders, and Operations in Progress. When protection is disrupted by a change, the affected group transitions to a state that restricts test recovery and planned migration operations, surfacing the problem before a recovery attempt is made.

Alarms provide proactive notification when changes affect data protection controls. Alarms are generated when connectivity problems occur or when protection groups and recovery plans enter an error state, allowing administrators to detect and remediate issues promptly.

PNR introduces an Advanced Cyber Compliance (ACC) subscription that extends verification capabilities. With ACC enabled, Cyber Recovery can install security sensors on virtual machines during the validation phase to perform vulnerability analysis, behavioral analysis, and malware signature scanning. This adds an active security verification layer alongside the existing snapshot integrity and replication status checks. The ACC subscription also enables use of the Latest instances option in recovery plans, providing additional recovery point selection capabilities for verification testing.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

Avi Load Balancer provides several mechanisms that support verification of security control configurations following changes. The Avi Controller CLI includes the `show networksecuritypolicy` command, which allows administrators to inspect the current state of Network Security Policies and confirm that changes have taken effect as intended. At the virtual service level, Avi WAF Policies attach a Positive Security group that applies learned application data and defines hit and miss actions, giving administrators a direct view of the rules actively enforced after a WAF policy modification. The `learned-data-promotion-location` setting controls which learned application data is promoted to Positive Security rules, allowing administrators to validate that the correct data has been incorporated following a change.

For correlating configuration changes with observed effects, the Service Engine Analytics Config Events overlay lets administrators identify whether a shift in traffic behavior was caused by a specific configuration modification, such as an update to virtual service security settings. This provides a post-change verification path that links configuration actions to traffic outcomes in a single view. Individual Layer 4 Policy Set rules provide a mechanism to control the behavior of Layer 4 virtual services by evaluating Layer 3 and Layer 4 attributes, allowing targeted policy modifications to be assessed in isolation.

ControlScripts are Python-based scripts that execute on Avi Load Balancer Controllers and are initiated by Alert Actions triggered by system events. They can alter Avi Load Balancer configuration or communicate with remote systems such as email, syslog, and SNMP servers, enabling automated post-change health checks and notification workflows. Alert Actions can also trigger ControlScripts to automatically add offending client IP addresses to a blocklist attached to a Network Security Policy, providing a feedback loop between detection and enforced policy state. The Application Security Report generates a downloadable PDF documenting security findings and pre-check results, supporting structured post-change documentation.

For deployments integrated with VCF, the Avi Load Balancer for VCF documentation specifies that Avi Management Plane alarms, alerts, and dashboards must be operational as part of post-upgrade validation, establishing explicit criteria that administrators can check following software changes.

The control's question asks whether the organization verifies control functionality as a deliberate process. Avi's observability features, CLI inspection tools, Config Events overlay, and ControlScript automation support that activity by making control state visible and queryable after a change. The organization must define acceptance criteria, expected baselines, and approval workflows. Avi does not automate the organizational verification decision itself.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control SI-07

> a. Employ integrity verification tools to detect unauthorized changes to the following software, firmware, and information: [Assignment: organization-defined software, firmware, and information]; and b. Take the following actions when unauthorized changes to the software, firmware, and information are detected: [Assignment: organization-defined actions].

**SCF Controls:** END-06, NET-12, TDA-18

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF contributes to file integrity monitoring through configuration drift detection, desired-state management, and configuration change auditing at the infrastructure layer, though it does not provide traditional file-level FIM for guest operating systems or endpoint file systems.

vSphere Configuration Profiles define the desired configuration state for all VMware ESX hosts in a cluster and detect when hosts drift from that baseline. vSphere Configuration Profiles must be enabled on vSphere clusters to view drift status in VCF Operations. When drift is detected, vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) can remediate the host back to the expected configuration using its desired-state API. Administrators can check the compliance of a cluster or host against the desired state and determine whether additional remediation steps are required. vSphere also supports configuring alarms that activate in response to configuration drift events and notify administrators via email or SNMP traps, enabling proactive reporting of unauthorized or unintended configuration changes.

VCF Operations extends host-level drift detection with its Configuration Drift feature, which monitors VMware vCenter instances and vSphere clusters for configuration changes and compares them against assigned desired values. The Configuration Drift dashboard provides visibility into which settings have changed across managed infrastructure. Drift detection runs on a configurable collection cycle (every 8 hours by default) and flags deviations from the established template, giving operations teams a mechanism to detect unauthorized or unintended configuration changes. Configuration drift templates can be integrated with a source control repository, enabling versioning, auditing, and management of configuration baselines outside the VCF Operations console.

VCF Operations also maintains an audit trail for its own configuration files. The Configuration Files page displays Last Modified and Modified By fields that track when each configuration file was last updated and which user modified it. These fields are available for management packs and other configuration files, and can be filtered by name, description, last modified date, and modifying user. This provides traceability for changes made to VCF Operations configuration settings.

At the activity level, VCF Operations audit logging tracks each configuration change to the authenticated user who initiated the change or scheduled the job that performed the change. Data center administrators can determine the principal identifier of the authenticated user who initiated a change to an object at a specific time, supporting forensic investigation of unauthorized modifications.

The vSphere Daily Check Dashboard provides an additional layer of configuration verification. It covers configuration changes and verifies that settings match authorized changes executed during approved change windows, giving administrators a recurring check against unauthorized modifications.

Within the storage layer, vSAN Distributed File System Snapshots provide information about files that have been changed and the changes made to each file, offering a point-in-time comparison capability at the storage level. These snapshots can be used by third-party backup vendors to copy changed files incrementally, though they are designed for data protection rather than real-time integrity monitoring.

For file-level integrity monitoring within guest operating systems or for monitoring arbitrary files on endpoints in real time, organizations need to deploy a dedicated FIM solution. ESX Agent Manager can facilitate the deployment and lifecycle management of third-party security agents, including FIM tools, within the virtualized environment by managing ESX agent virtual machine deployment across hosts in a cluster.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend checks the validity of information inputs across several of its network security subsystems, contributing to the organization's broader input validation posture.

Security Intelligence parses and validates the content format and inventory asset data in administrator-provided CSV files used during segmentation planning imports. When data mapping fails due to file errors, an error notification appears with a link to an annotated CSV file that highlights the specific errors for correction. Administrators can correct and re-upload the file, or allow analysis to continue with incorrect entries excluded from processing. Administrators are advised to validate inventory asset data for completeness before importing CSV files into the Security Services Platform.

Security Intelligence stores system-inferred infrastructure classifications in a "Not reviewed" state until an administrator explicitly accepts or modifies them. This classification review mechanism applies to compute entity classifications used in segmentation planning and policy generation, requiring human acceptance before automated classifications can influence policy decisions.

Security Intelligence Policy Recommendations includes a Monitoring capability that checks for changes in the scope of input entities every hour when enabled. When the monitoring feature detects a change in input scope, administrators are prompted to review the detected changes and rerun recommendation analysis to regenerate policy based on the updated inputs.

Security Intelligence enforces input limits on policy recommendation generation: a maximum of 100 VM entities can be selected as direct input, and the total number of effective VMs when groups are included cannot exceed 500. These constraints are enforced before analysis begins, validating that inputs fall within the range where analysis results are reliable.

The vDefend distributed firewall includes an auditing and remediation workflow that validates rule components, requiring rules to use IPv4 addresses for successful validation through the security gate before deployment.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) validates inputs across its recovery-plan, replication, and management surfaces. Configuration data exported in XML format can be validated against an XSD schema, providing structural validation of imported and exported configuration data (p.288). After PNR upgrades, administrators must re-verify that protection groups and recovery plans remain valid before they are placed back into operational use (p.108).

For recovery-plan IP customization, the DR IP Customizer tool uses a structured comma-separated value file with a header row defining column meanings and one or more rows for each placeholder virtual machine in a recovery plan (p.379, p.382). The dr-ip-customizer command supports a --verbose option that enables additional diagnostic logging for validation of customizer inputs (p.376).

PNR's Cyber Recovery workflow includes explicit validation states for VMs prior to recovery. Cyber Recovery VMs transition through a Preparing validation state and then an In validation state when an administrator selects a snapshot and initiates validation (p.575). Cyber Recovery supports on-demand vulnerability analysis through the Analysis tab to validate workload integrity before completing recovery validation (p.574).

Integrated Security and Vulnerability Analysis with CrowdStrike examines VMs in the clean room using behavioral analysis and machine learning during validation, with continuous analysis of running software and processes on the guest operating system to detect suspicious behavior (p.580). The integration performs vulnerability analysis combining Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) information with proprietary threat data and advanced modeling from Kenna Security, and identifies vulnerabilities by their CVE designation (p.582). Operators use these signals to select a clean snapshot for recovery rather than restoring a compromised one.

PNR logs certificate verification error messages in security-related log files for audit trail purposes, so that input-validation failures on certificate fields are captured (p.133). For operational health checks, PNR can adjust the number of status check attempts before declaring a check failed using the connections.hmsPingFailedDelay parameter (p.440). Trusted environment setup for the PNR Appliance requires text values for organization and organization unit during configuration (p.112). The Clean Room Orchestrator categorizes validation events using Info and Warning severities so that operators can act on input or state conditions surfaced during recovery operations (p.562).

Input validation for customer applications running on workloads protected by PNR is the responsibility of those applications and their developers. PNR provides input validation for its own recovery-plan, configuration import/export, customizer, API, and orchestration surfaces, and provides Cyber Recovery validation states and CrowdStrike-driven analysis to validate workload integrity before recovered VMs return to production.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to input data validation through mechanisms built into its configuration, policy management, and integration interfaces.

At the policy layer, DSM data service policies support allowlist creation, restricting users to selecting only from administrator-defined sets of approved parameters when provisioning or configuring databases. In 9.1, SQL Server data service policies include an "Allow with a List" option that creates a specific allowlist of approved parameter values users can select from. Policies also enforce required-field validation: administrators must supply a name, description, and target data service before a policy can be saved. When applying a policy to existing databases, DSM displays a preview of any policy violations that would result, allowing review and correction before the policy is created or modified.

DSM validates configuration inputs for backup storage at multiple points. During monitoring, DSM checks the connection to the backup storage repository, verifies the correctness of the bucket name, confirms certificate expiration dates, validates credential currency, and verifies that sufficient available space exists in the repository. Administrators can view these validation results through the database monitoring interface.

In System Settings, DSM validates SMTP configuration inputs when settings are saved and returns an error if validation fails. DSM also requires validation of the SMTP certificate as part of SMTP configuration. NTP server entries are validated and flagged for correction if invalid values are present.

For webhook integrations, DSM provides a TEST function that sends the configured payload to the endpoint URL before the webhook is created, allowing administrators to verify that the endpoint is reachable and that the payload is accepted before deployment.

DSM introduces a "latest" certificate validation mode as the default for version 9.1 and later. This mode enforces strict cryptographic and KeyUsage validations for all DSM endpoints, including the Provider VM and data service clusters. The validation mode is configurable through the advanced-system-config ConfigMap.

For PostgreSQL databases, DSM includes the amcheck extension, which provides functions for verifying relation integrity at the database engine level.

For local user accounts, DSM enforces password complexity requirements: passwords must be between 8 and 20 characters and include at least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, one number, and at least one special character. This validates credential inputs at the authentication boundary. DSM does not send a verification message to confirm the validity of local user email addresses.

DSM's input validation mechanisms cover its own administrative and configuration interfaces. They do not extend to application-layer validation for data flowing through the PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MinIO databases it manages. Input validation for workloads connected to DSM-managed databases is an organizational and application development responsibility.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Meets)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides application-layer input validation through three complementary mechanisms: WAF signatures and the Positive Security Model, DataScript programmable rules, and Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) configuration validation.

Avi WAF signatures perform input and output validation by analyzing all incoming and outgoing traffic. The WAF Positive Security Model, also called Application Specific Policy, describes expected application behavior and enforces it by setting an accepted range and length of characters for HTTP fields. Requests that do not meet those specifications are reported as policy violations before reaching backend workloads, allowing administrators to restrict inputs to known-valid character sets and field lengths on a per-application basis. The WAF also provides analytics to identify possible false positives and allows operators to add exceptions through a modal dialog that generates suggested values pre-computed from log entries and related findings, supporting ongoing policy refinement.

DataScript extends input validation with programmable, event-driven logic executed within Avi Service Engines. Using Lua-based scripting with relational, logical, and arithmetic operators, DataScript rules can inspect and validate request parameters, HTTP headers, and request body content. Specific capabilities include an external request validation API that offloads validation decisions to an external service and returns a response object containing status code, headers, and HTTP response body; XML body parsing via the Parse function, which returns a success indicator and either the parsed result or error information if parsing fails; client certificate validation via the avi.ssl.check_client_cert_validity() function, which returns distinct values when the certificate is not presented, is valid, or is invalid; conditional clearing of certificate verification errors based on error code evaluation; string pattern functions including string.contains() and string.beginswith() for field content checks; and group-based user identity validation using avi.stringgroup.equals, which checks whether a user ID is in an approved list and can redirect unauthenticated users to a login page. DataScript rules should be tested and validated after modification to confirm that the intended validation logic operates correctly without affecting other virtual services.

The Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) performs syntactical validations at the time of Custom Resource Definition (CRD) object creation to verify configuration correctness and provides effective status messages. The AKO CRD Operator condition-status field indicates whether conditions are 'True' or 'False', supporting automated validation of AKO configuration inputs in Kubernetes environments.

### Control SI-07(01)

> Perform an integrity check of [Assignment: organization-defined software, firmware, and information] [Selection (one or more): at startup; at [Assignment: organization-defined transitional states or security-relevant events]; [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]].

**SCF Controls:** END-06.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple layers of integrity checking for software, firmware, and configurations across the platform stack, from hardware boot validation through ongoing operational monitoring.

At the hardware level, VCF supports Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) to validate system firmware and boot integrity. VMware vCenter can retrieve the TPM event log for configuring firmware trust with an attestation service or validating boot-time TPM measurements, and APIs are available to list configured TPM devices and endorsement keys on each host. The hypervisor enforces code signing to restrict execution to authorized code. Each vSphere Installation Bundle (VIB) has an associated acceptance level, controlled through the Host-Image-Profile-Acceptance-Level setting, and VIB signature validation uses a cryptographic checksum to confirm that each package was produced by its authorized author.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) is the primary tool for software and firmware integrity validation. Administrators define a desired software specification for each cluster, and vLCM compares the committed specification against each host to identify drift from the expected software baseline. A compliance check can be performed against the committed draft to confirm host conformance. The Initialize-SettingsHostsSoftwareCheckSpec cmdlet in PowerCLI initializes a software check specification with a commit parameter to define the desired software version, and the Invoke-CheckHostSoftwareAsync cmdlet runs a remediation pre-check to confirm software and hardware compatibility before applying changes. When enabling a cluster to use a software specification, administrators can submit a Software.CheckSpec structure to define which preliminary checks to run. Draft specifications can be validated before committing to confirm they are complete and conflict-free, and remediation pre-checks include hardware compatibility validation to identify issues before applying updates. When vLCM downloads a software package for a host update, it verifies the SHA-256 checksum of each downloaded package to confirm integrity before applying it. The vSphere Image Library provides a complementary SHA-256 verification mechanism: administrators can compare the checksum of an imported image against the checksum generated when the image was created, supporting integrity validation when images are transferred between different vCenter instances.

vSphere Configuration Profiles extend desired-state management by enabling drift detection for vSphere clusters. When Configuration Profiles are enabled, VCF Operations monitors drift status through a collection cycle that runs every 8 hours, providing a proactive method to detect when cluster configurations have changed from their expected state.

vCenter includes File Integrity Monitoring, which maintains cryptographic checksums representing the current state of the vCenter file system and schedules periodic integrity scans. The File Integrity Monitoring Status API, accessible via standard HTTPS requests, returns a files_changed boolean field indicating whether monitored files have been altered and a check_time field containing the Unix timestamp of the last completed scan. Administrators can configure the scan frequency in seconds through the API, allowing the monitoring interval to be tuned for operational requirements.

VCF Operations extends configuration validation to the broader VCF deployment. It exposes a POST /v1/vcf-management-components/validations API endpoint that validates JSON deployment configuration files before they are applied. The VCF Installer deployment wizard similarly validates install specifications before starting the setup process, allowing administrators to review failed validations and resolve issues using the remediation information provided in error messages. An optional API operation allows administrators to verify whether an install spec is valid before beginning deployment. The configuration validation process examines configuration files for errors and conflicts, returning output that helps avoid loading an incorrect configuration.

For virtual machines, VCF supports UEFI Secure Boot to verify guest operating system integrity at boot time. VMware Tools packages are signed, with verification performed during installation and updates. VCF Operations management pack PAK files are also signed with digital signatures that indicate the original developer or publisher and provide authenticity verification; VCF Operations displays a warning if a PAK file lacks a valid signature.

Certificate validation is built into the platform at multiple levels. VCF detects when certificates have expired or are nearing their expiration dates. The Certificates tab in VCF Operations displays certificate expiration status for each workload domain. VCF Operations provides a Certificates widget to view certificates that require attention based on their expiration dates. NSX Manager certificate expiry scanning identifies appliance certificates due to expire within 31 days, and critical severity alarms are generated after certificate expiration.

At the storage layer, vSAN provides data integrity checking through end-to-end checksum verification, confirming that each copy of a file matches the source. When a checksum mismatch is detected, vSAN automatically repairs the data by overwriting the incorrect copy with the correct version. The system checks data validity during read and write operations and reports errors when repair is not possible. Object checksum can be configured through vSAN storage policies to control whether checksum calculations are performed. vSAN configuration validation checks are performed as part of the host synchronization process, confirming that vSAN settings remain consistent across cluster members. RAID-5 and RAID-6 erasure coding provide additional data protection as policy-driven attributes that can be applied to virtual machine components.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor layer extends integrity checking to containerized workloads. At the Kubernetes API layer, administrators can deploy ValidatingAdmissionPolicy resources using Common Expression Language (CEL) expressions to enforce configurable validation checks on every object submitted to the API server. The validationActions field controls whether validation failures generate audit log entries, warnings, or hard rejections, and the failurePolicy field defines how policy evaluation errors are handled, including failures from CEL parse errors, type check errors, and runtime errors. ValidatingAdmissionWebhook resources provide an additional validation layer after all mutations are complete. For container image integrity, the kubelet image verification mode is configurable per VKS cluster with four settings: NeverVerify, NeverVerifyPreloadedImages (the default), NeverVerifyAllowlistedImages, and AlwaysVerify; the AlwaysVerify setting requires cryptographic signature verification for every image before it is permitted to run. Admission controllers can automatically verify image signatures at deploy time to validate their authenticity and integrity. Harbor, available as a Supervisor service and certified by Broadcom for compatibility with Supervisor networking, storage, and authentication, provides registry-layer integrity controls including vulnerability scanning, content trust, and policy-based replication; Harbor signs images as trusted before they are available for deployment. During VKS cluster lifecycle operations, pre-checks are enforced before cluster upgrades to validate system state and confirm compatibility. The VCF CLI binary, used for Consumption layer operations, can be verified using a sha256sum.txt checksum file available alongside the CLI download from the VCF Automation Tenant Portal.

For ongoing configuration integrity monitoring, VCF Operations includes a Compliance Checks capability that supports compliance validation throughout workload transitions. Organizations can run compliance checks in parallel and review the results to identify where configurations have drifted from established security baselines. VCF supports checking system configurations against DISA STIG requirements using automated tools, providing a mechanism to validate that security configurations remain aligned with the established hardening standards. DISA STIGs are also available for Supervisor and VKS release (VKr) components, extending this hardening validation to the containerized workload layer.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides configuration integrity validation through automated pre-operation checks, on-demand consistency verification, and image integrity verification at the application delivery layer.

The Avi Controller CLI includes a `show config-consistency-check` command that reports the current consistency state of controller configuration. Operators can run this command at any time to verify that configuration state is consistent across cluster nodes and to surface any drift or discrepancies.

Before upgrade operations, the Controller runs automated pre-upgrade verification that confirms Controller Cluster readiness, detects whether an upgrade is already in progress, validates active version compatibility across the cluster, and verifies Service Engine reachability. In the Avi for VCF deployment, the Controller requires verification of image checksums or digital signatures to confirm file integrity before upgrade proceeds. A system configuration check validates the overall system state before upgrade operations begin. The upgrade pre-check system produces mandatory error messages for requirement violations that cannot be overridden and warning messages for optional requirements that can be skipped. The Avi Controller system report for upgrade operations records each pre-check result, including check code, description, state, start time, end time, and duration, providing a full audit record of the validation sequence.

The Dry Run feature performs configuration validation in an isolated container before an upgrade is committed, including partial Controller initialization, configuration migration, new schema item validation, and configuration testing. Operators can review progress using the VIEW PROGRESS option under Administration > Update > System and return to the pre-check screen to evaluate results before proceeding.

Before applying patches, the Controller performs patch compatibility checks to verify that a patch is safe to apply and that system stability will be maintained. Before restore operations, the Controller executes a pre-restore validation sequence covering Controller Cluster readiness, active upgrade operation detection, ServiceEngineGroup upgrade status, backup configuration version compatibility, FIPS mode compatibility, image object presence, configuration validity, and FIPS mode alignment between the backup file and the target environment.

The Avi Controller maintains checksums and creation timestamps for custom IPAM script files, providing integrity tracking and an audit trail for configuration scripts used in custom IPAM implementations.

For FIPS 140-3 compliance, Avi Load Balancer applies enhanced controls over software and firmware module integrity and module updates, including tighter requirements on module update procedures.

In Kubernetes deployments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) performs syntactical validation of Custom Resource Definition (CRD) objects at creation time, verifying configuration correctness and surfacing errors through effective status messages. AKO also monitors certificate files and Kubernetes secrets, with certificate and secret update events visible via AKO logs.

These mechanisms operate at the Avi application delivery layer. Software and firmware integrity for the underlying compute, storage, and networking infrastructure relies on VCF through Secure Boot, VIB acceptance levels, and TPM attestation, which are covered under VCF Coverage. Organizations should account for both layers when demonstrating full coverage of this control.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-07(02)

> VIOLATIONS ###### Employ automated tools that provide notification to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] upon discovering discrepancies during integrity verification.

**SCF Controls:** END-06.3

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple automated notification mechanisms that alert personnel when integrity verification discovers discrepancies. These capabilities span VMware vCenter alarm-based notifications, VCF Operations alert definitions, VCF Log Management alert content, configuration drift detection, and Kubernetes-native alerting for workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) clusters within the Supervisor platform, all of which can deliver outbound alerts to incident response teams through configurable channels.

vCenter includes a built-in alarm framework that can send notifications when monitored conditions are triggered. vCenter can send email notifications with configurable recipient addresses and alarm subject information when alarms fire, including escalation for critical alerts that remain unclaimed by an engineer. It can also send SNMP traps when alarms are triggered on a vCenter instance, using an embedded SNMP agent that activates on system startup or alarm events. vCenter supports SNMP trap versions v1, v2c, and v3, including v3 with all security levels, providing flexibility for organizations whose management infrastructure requires authenticated or encrypted trap delivery. VCF PowerCLI supports creating SNMP alarm actions programmatically using the New-AlarmAction -Snmp parameter, allowing administrators to automate the configuration of trap-based notifications across the environment. The vCenter events and alarms framework captures thousands of event types and allows administrators to define alarm actions that trigger when integrity-related conditions are met, such as configuration changes, state transitions, or threshold breaches.

VCF Operations extends these capabilities with alert definitions that can monitor virtual machines, hosts, and other infrastructure objects using custom symptom parameters. Symptoms can be defined based on multiple data sources: metrics and properties, message events, faults, metric events, and log entries. This flexibility allows integrity-related discrepancies, whether detected through metric threshold violations, log anomalies, or compliance drift, to be captured as symptoms and incorporated into alert definitions. Log-based alerts can be configured through the Symptom Definitions card in Infrastructure Operations, and log-based symptoms can be combined with metric-based symptoms to provide broader detection coverage.

For compliance-specific integrity monitoring, VCF Operations includes a compliance management engine that continuously monitors objects against established benchmarks and policies. When it discovers non-compliant states, it generates compliance alerts on vCenter instances, VMware ESX hosts, virtual machines, distributed port groups, and distributed switches. These compliance alerts record violation details for investigation. Administrators can also create custom compliance benchmarks that monitor selected alert definitions and policies, enabling organization-specific integrity baselines to trigger automated alerts when deviations occur.

VCF Operations supports multiple outbound notification channels to reach incident response personnel. SNMP trap alert notifications can be sent to up to four configured trap destinations, with filtering available when defining notification rules. These outbound SNMP trap notifications are a mechanism for sending alerts to external SNMP management systems, integrating detection capabilities with existing incident response workflows and security information and event management platforms. Email notifications are also available as an outbound channel. Alert definitions can include recommendations and resolution instructions, so that when notifications reach responders, they receive actionable guidance alongside the integrity violation details.

VCF Operations provides configuration drift detection through the Configuration Drift dashboard. The Detect Drift function identifies configuration changes across all configuration templates and displays results for review. A configuration drift notification alerts administrators of any deviation from assigned configuration template values, providing a proactive method to monitor configuration changes and help maintain consistency across vCenter instances and vSphere clusters. vSphere Configuration Profiles also support SNMP trap notifications when alarms are triggered by configuration changes to clusters or hosts, providing an additional path for delivering configuration integrity alerts to external network management systems.

ESX hosts generate audit logs for security-relevant events including configuration changes and authentication attempts. These logs, along with events from vCenter, NSX, and other VCF components, can be forwarded via syslog to external SIEM platforms for centralized correlation and alerting. ESX hosts support configurable syslog log levels that can be set using esxcli system syslog commands. VCF Log Management includes content packs with alert definitions for environment monitoring, enabling deployment of integrity-related alerting from log data. VCF Log Management alert definitions can be triggered on base object types including vCenter, Host System, Virtual Machine, or Container with Log Fallback Object.

For workloads running on VKS clusters within the VCF Supervisor platform, the Kubernetes consumption layer provides additional automated detection and notification mechanisms. Pod Security Admission in audit mode records security violations as an audit annotation (pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit-violations) in the Kubernetes audit log, providing a structured record of workload integrity violations without blocking admission. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy extends this at admission time through spec.auditAnnotations fields containing Common Expression Language (CEL) expressions that embed structured metadata directly into API audit events whenever a validation check fails; the annotation keys are included in audit event records and can be consumed by downstream log aggregation and alerting systems. The Kubernetes API server exposes the apiserver_audit_error_total counter metric, which tracks audit events that failed to be recorded properly, enabling monitoring of the audit pipeline itself as an integrity signal.

For metric-driven alerting on VKS clusters, the Prometheus standard package can be installed in the tanzu-system-monitoring namespace, deploying Prometheus and Alertmanager together. Prometheus instances are configured to send alerts to Alertmanager via HTTP, and Alertmanager is deployed as a StatefulSet with multiple replicas for availability. The AlertmanagerConfig custom resource defines how alerts are grouped, inhibited, and routed to external notification channels such as email endpoints or webhooks to reach incident response personnel. Kube-state-metrics provides cluster-level metrics that can be used to configure alerting rules on issues detected through metric analysis, including integrity-relevant state changes across the cluster.

Harbor, when deployed as the cluster registry in VKS environments, provides vulnerability scanning and content trust to verify container image integrity before deployment. Admission webhooks can reference Harbor's content trust status, and violations generate admission events captured in the Kubernetes audit log. For GitOps-managed workloads, the ArgoCD Supervisor Service supports an optional notification controller, configured through spec.notification.enabled and disabled by default, that alerts personnel when configuration drift is detected between desired state in a Git repository and actual cluster state.

To further streamline the response process, VCF Operations provides intelligent alert clustering that groups related alerts together based on creation time and topology distance. This helps incident response teams distinguish a single underlying integrity issue from the multiple individual alerts it may generate, reducing noise and accelerating triage.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) provides automated alerting and notification mechanisms for network security integrity violations across several product components.

vDefend IDS/IPS generates real-time alerts when network traffic matches threat signatures. The IDS/IPS engine maintains an nsx-idps-events.log file in the /var/log/nsx-idps directory on each ESX host, recording all alerts, drops, and rejects with structured metadata. Log fields include source port, destination IP address, IP protocol, and timestamp, providing incident response personnel with the network context needed to assess and prioritize detected discrepancies. The Distributed IDS/IPS engine includes signature severity values in SYSLOG output, enabling downstream systems to filter and route alerts by severity. IDS/IPS signature rules support detection filtering that triggers alerts only after a defined threshold of signature matches, reducing noise for high-volume signatures. Custom IDS/IPS profiles can be created with custom signatures assigned to specific severity levels, giving administrators control over triage priority for organization-defined threat patterns.

Security Intelligence detects and categorizes suspicious network traffic events using MITRE tactics and techniques, recording the number of occurrences during the selected time period. Detected events are categorized by impact type (Critical, High, Medium, and Low) to help prioritize response actions. Security Intelligence continuously monitors for new flows between environments and alerts administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic, supporting ongoing policy adjustment. The infrastructure classification cronjob automatically infers classifications for compute entities, which administrators can review and accept or modify through the user interface, creating a notification path when the system detects environmental changes.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) provides monitoring of current deployment status and platform-related alarms that highlight errors requiring attention. Alarms for service deployment issues are displayed on the Alarms page of the NSX Manager UI. SSP can be configured to collect and transmit log messages to a remote syslog server for identifying and resolving errors. The SSP Rule Analysis feature generates downloadable CSV reports of the last successful analysis, with separate files for each detected anomaly type, supporting integrity verification of firewall policy configuration.

The vDefend Security Overview Dashboard generates an alarm when dropped events are detected. The alarm remains active until no dropped events are found, alerting administrators through both the alarm system and a notification banner.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) integrates with SIEMs to send security-related event logs about the network infrastructure in real time. NDR generates and sends event logs to the configured SIEM when a detection event occurs, when a detection event is updated, and when a new campaign is identified. SSP SIEM integration, compatible with VCF Log Management and Splunk, forwards campaign and event logs as they occur.

These detection and alerting capabilities are scoped to the network security layer. Broader notification routing, escalation workflows, and incident response coordination depend on VCF Operations and organizational tooling outside vDefend's domain.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-07(05)

> VIOLATIONS Automatically [Selection (one or more): shut the system down; restart the system; implement [Assignment: organization-defined controls]] when integrity violations are discovered.

**SCF Controls:** END-06.4

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides automated mechanisms for detecting and remediating integrity violations through multiple complementary capabilities: VCF Operations compliance management, vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) desired-state enforcement, and VCF Diagnostics.

VCF Operations continuously assesses infrastructure objects against selected compliance policies, covering VMware vCenter instances, VMware ESX hosts, virtual machines, distributed port groups, and distributed switches. When a configuration or compliance violation is detected, VCF Operations generates alerts and notifications based on the severity of the non-compliance. Administrators can configure which alerts trigger automated remediation and define the scope of automated responses based on violation severity. VCF benchmarks can be activated to audit the compliance posture of objects across the platform, establishing the baseline against which integrity violations are measured.

The automated remediation capability in VCF Operations maps out-of-the-box workflows to compliance recommendations and associates them with specific alerts. When a recommendation is the first priority for a given alert, VCF Operations can automate the remediation action without manual intervention, bringing the non-compliant object back into a compliant state. VCF Operations also supports running workflows to remediate alerts or automate operational tasks, providing a programmable framework for responding to detected violations. Non-compliant objects and their associated compliance violations are surfaced through the compliance dashboard, where administrators can review findings and either apply automated remediation or take manual corrective action.

VCF Operations also provides unified configuration management with scheduled drift detection for vCenter and cluster objects. The Configuration Drifts feature monitors configuration settings and identifies settings that have changed from the desired configurations. Administrators can view and compare configuration drifts through the Configuration Drift dashboard, and VCF Operations audit logging tracks each configuration change to the authenticated user who initiated the change, supporting both automated and manual remediation workflows.

vSphere Configuration Profiles and image-based lifecycle management define the expected software and configuration state for ESX hosts within a cluster. Image Manager allows administrators to create a desired state that can be applied on a cluster or a standalone host, and supports validation of the desired state and detection of drifts from it. When a host deviates from the desired state, vLCM detects the deviation and can remediate the host back to compliance through the com.vmware.esx.settings.clusters.Software.apply API. Before applying a desired state, pre-checks run to evaluate how each host in the cluster is affected by the remediation and whether additional steps are needed, such as whether hosts must be rebooted or placed in maintenance mode. vSphere Host Profiles provide an additional desired-state mechanism: remediation and compliance check operations are available for hosts associated with a profile, enforcing consistent configuration across a fleet of hosts. Compliance checks and remediation pre-checks can be run at scale across groups of clusters or standalone hosts organized at the vCenter, data center, or folder level, with pre-remediation reports surfacing blocking issues at the cluster, host, and virtual machine level. This desired-state model supports both detection and correction of integrity deviations back to a known-good baseline.

VCF Diagnostics automatically detects known issues and critical vulnerabilities within the VCF software stack, providing detailed descriptions, root cause insights, and actionable remediation steps. The vSphere Automation API and ComplianceImpact data structures expose remediation information programmatically, enabling integration with external orchestration tools and custom automation workflows. PowerShell scripts such as VcfBaselineClusterTransition.ps1 support automated compliance checking through parameters like CheckTaskStatus, allowing scripted verification and response workflows.

VCF Operations supports rule-based analytics and dynamic thresholding that identify anomalous behavior and configuration drift. Dynamic thresholds use analysis of historical data to establish baselines and generate alerts when metric values deviate from expected patterns. Intelligent Alerts automate alert correlation and filtering, and outlier violation alerts fire when anomalous entities are detected. These analytics help distinguish routine configuration changes from integrity violations requiring remediation.

Integration with external SIEM and orchestration platforms is supported through syslog forwarding, REST APIs, and VCF Operations outbound notification plug-ins (email, SNMP trap, log file, and REST endpoints). The vCenter REST API provides HTTP endpoints for configuring syslog forwarding, including operations to set forwarding configuration, test remote server connections, and exchange test messages. This allows organizations to build automated response pipelines that trigger remediation actions when integrity violations are detected by external monitoring systems.

The Consumption layer of VCF, comprising VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor namespaces, extends automated integrity response into containerized workload environments through Kubernetes' declarative reconciliation model and purpose-built remediation controllers.

The Kubernetes controller-manager implements continuous reconciliation for all managed workload resources. Any unauthorized runtime modification to a Deployment, DaemonSet, StatefulSet, or ReplicaSet that causes actual state to diverge from the declared desired state is automatically corrected. When a pod is deleted, modified outside the API server's control path, or fails, the responsible controller schedules a replacement to restore the declared replica count and configuration, making unauthorized configuration changes self-correcting for managed workload resources.

At the node layer, VKS supports MachineHealthCheck for VKS service clusters. The MachineHealthCheck controller monitors control plane and worker nodes and triggers automated remediation when unhealthy nodes are detected. The remediaton.triggerIf.unhealthyLessThanOrEqualTo field specifies the threshold at which remediation fires, preventing mass node replacement during broader incidents. When remediation is active, the cluster surfaces a "Remediating" condition providing details about ongoing repair of controlled machines, giving operators visibility into automated response actions in progress.

VKS Cluster Management Policies provide an additional layer of automated enforcement. Mutation policies automate the injection of standard metadata and the enforcement of resource defaults across the VKS cluster estate, maintaining a consistent operational baseline. VKS cluster management proactively monitors the estate to verify that assigned policies are enforced correctly, generating Policy Insights when configuration conflicts or environmental errors are detected; each insight includes direct links to the affected cluster, project, and policy to streamline remediation. When an out-of-band Gatekeeper installation is detected that was not deployed via cluster management, the system generates an Incompatibility insight flagging the conflict for administrative review.

Admission controllers, including ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, block integrity violations before resources are committed to the API server. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy protects platform namespaces and sensitive resources from unauthorized modifications by rejecting non-compliant API requests at the point of admission. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy tracks observedGeneration in its status to detect out-of-date policy conditions, and its spec.auditAnnotations field supports CEL expressions that produce audit annotations for each API request, enabling review of admission decisions. Mutating admission webhooks can rewrite incoming requests to enforce conformance with required security settings before resources are persisted.

For software supply chain integrity, Harbor can be installed on VKS clusters running on Supervisor. Harbor provides vulnerability scanning, content trust enforcement, and policy-based replication. Organizations can require that container images carry valid cryptographic signatures, and admission webhooks can reject images that fail signature verification at deploy time, blocking workloads whose image integrity cannot be confirmed.

When combined with infrastructure-as-code stored in version control, the immutable infrastructure model implemented by VKS creates a durable audit record of every authorized change. Any deviation from the declared state is addressed by the reconciliation loop, providing a structural automated response to unauthorized modifications. For persistent storage within Supervisor environments, remediation can be performed by reapplying the storage policy or migrating volumes to another datastore when issues are identified.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) provides automated remediation capabilities that respond to integrity violations detected at the network and endpoint security layers through its Intelligent Assist and IDS/IPS subsystems.

The Response and Remediation feature in Security Services Platform performs automated creation of policy based on user role and permissions. When IDS/IPS or Malware Prevention detects a security event, Intelligent Assist presents two remediation strategies: a Targeted Strategy, which applies surgical and more limited remediation with lower potential impact to traffic flows, and a Comprehensive Strategy, which applies broader remediation coverage with more potential impact to traffic flows. Intelligent Assist includes a human review and approval step before remediation policies are applied, avoiding automated actions that could introduce unintended disruptions while still automating the policy creation and recommendation workflow. Applied remediation policies are accessible under Security > IDS/IPS & Malware Prevention > Emergency Threat Rules, providing a verification point for auditing active automated responses. Remediations are added as rules to the Intelligent Assist Remediation policy using the naming convention prefix 'ai-'. Objects generated during vDefend remediation that encounter errors require manual cleanup, including objects generated during Network Detection and Response (NDR) remediation workflows.

Malware Prevention Service, including Distributed Malware Prevention with Guest Introspection, provides interfaces for taking remediation actions directly on endpoints: denying access to suspicious files or deleting files identified as malicious. Security Services Platform provides a Security Overview dashboard with a breakdown of file inspections by the type of analysis performed, including local file analysis and cloud file analysis, allowing administrators to monitor Malware Prevention activity across the environment.

Security Intelligence complements these capabilities by automatically classifying infrastructure through a cronjob that infers classifications for compute entities, which administrators can review and accept or modify through the user interface. Security Intelligence also combines information from administrator-provided CSV files containing lists of infrastructure servers with its own automatic detections to generate security rules. Administrators can monitor traffic leakage between environments, establish exceptions, and implement lockdown protection rules to control inter-environment traffic. The Security Services Platform Rule Analysis feature detects anomalies in the firewall rule set and generates downloadable CSV reports with separate files for each detected anomaly type, providing automated integrity checking on the security policies governing the response environment.

vDefend's automated remediation scope is specific to network and endpoint security violations. The broader control encompasses all types of integrity violations, including configuration drift, software integrity, and compliance deviations, which are addressed by other VCF components such as vSphere Configuration Profiles, vSphere Lifecycle Manager, and VCF Operations compliance management.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-07(07)

> RESPONSE Incorporate the detection of the following unauthorized changes into the organizational incident response capability: [Assignment: organization-defined security-relevant changes to the system].

**SCF Controls:** END-06.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several capabilities that support detection of unauthorized configuration changes, though a complete endpoint detection and response program requires additional organizational processes and specialized security tools.

VCF Operations includes unified configuration management with scheduled drift detection for vCenter instances and vSphere clusters. This capability proactively identifies configuration deviations from a desired state, allowing administrators to detect when settings have been modified outside approved change processes. The Configuration Drift dashboard in VCF Operations lets administrators view and compare configuration drifts, identify which settings have changed, and quickly locate unauthorized modifications across the environment. Drift detection runs on a configurable schedule and compares actual state against the defined baseline. vSphere Configuration Profiles must be enabled on clusters for drift status to be visible in VCF Operations.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) provides a complementary layer of configuration change detection at the host level. vSphere Configuration Profiles define the expected configuration state for ESX hosts within a cluster. When a host drifts from the desired state, vLCM detects the deviation and can remediate the host back to compliance. Software specification compliance checks validate that hosts match the committed desired-state specification, reporting discrepancies for investigation. vLCM can retrieve a host's current desired state, check compliance against it, and apply remediation as needed. vSphere Configuration Profiles can also be configured to trigger alarms on cluster or host out-of-compliance events using the com.vmware.vcIntegrity.ClusterConfigurationOutOfCompliance event type; these alarms can notify administrators via email or SNMP trap.

Audit logging across VCF components records configuration changes with full attribution. VCF Operations audit logging tracks each configuration change to the authenticated user who initiated the change or scheduled the job that performed it, capturing the principal identifier, the affected object, and the time of the modification. NSX Manager generates detailed audit log messages for security policy changes, and for vDefend distributed firewall and gateway firewall configuration changes when that separate product is deployed, recording the username, module name, operation type, operation status, and both old and new values for modified policies. This level of detail supports forensic investigation when an unauthorized change is detected. vCenter also writes audit events to the VCF SSO audit log for changes to security configuration, parameters, or policies.

VCF Operations generates alerts when specific configuration changes occur. An alert is raised when any property of a Security Group is changed, and a separate alert fires when Security Group membership changes, providing near-real-time notification of modifications to network security constructs. VCF Operations also raises a Configuration Change alert when any entity property changes, identifying what changed, when, and who made the modification.

NSX Manager can trigger an unscheduled full configuration backup when the system detects any user, runtime, or configuration-related changes, preserving the state for comparison and rollback. NSX also supports triggering backups specifically for configuration changes in addition to scheduled inventory change backups, providing a recovery point tied to the moment a change was detected.

VCF Operations supports rule-based analytics and machine learning that help identify anomalous behavior patterns across the infrastructure. Dynamic thresholding is enabled by default and uses analysis of historical data to establish baselines, generating alerts when metric values deviate from expected patterns. Intelligent Alerts automate alert correlation and filtering to help administrators focus on the most critical issues. VCF Operations also generates outlier violation alerts when anomalous entities are detected. These analytics help distinguish authorized administrative changes from unauthorized modifications, supporting security teams in prioritizing investigation.

Integration with external monitoring and incident management platforms is available through VCF Operations outbound notification plug-ins, which support standard email, SNMP trap, log file, and REST endpoints. This allows configuration change alerts to feed directly into the organization's incident response workflow and SIEM platforms.

These capabilities provide the detection foundation for identifying unauthorized configuration changes. However, classifying detected changes as cybersecurity incidents and executing an incident response workflow requires organizational processes, integration with a SIEM platform, and potentially dedicated endpoint detection and response tooling that operates beyond what VCF provides natively.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer includes a Config Audit Trail that records all configuration changes made to the Avi Controller, whether initiated through the CLI, API, GUI, or by automated policies. The audit trail captures user activity events and configuration change events over a selected time period and is accessible via Operations > Events > Config Audit Trail. Each event identifies the specific configuration object modified, the tenant context, the timestamp of the change, and the user account that performed the action.

Avi Controller CONFIG_CREATE and CONFIG_DELETE events include client_IP and user_agent fields, which allow operators to identify not only which user account initiated a configuration change but also the source IP address and the client application involved. Event Logs can be filtered by event type using parameters such as event_id=CONFIG_CREATE or event_id=CONFIG_UPDATE, allowing operators to isolate and review configuration changes systematically. When a configuration event is selected, the event log displays the specific API fields that were changed, providing detailed change tracking for investigation.

Configuration change events can be forwarded to external systems via syslog. The SYSLOG_JSON alert type captures CONFIG_UPDATE events with details including the event type, affected object, tenant context, update status, and performing user, making Avi's configuration audit data suitable for ingestion into a SIEM or log aggregation platform where correlation rules can generate alerts on unexpected changes. This forwarding path is the primary mechanism by which Avi's configuration audit data integrates with an organization's broader incident detection and response workflow.

Avi also provides a Config Events overlay within Service Engine analytics and Server Pool analytics that allows operators to correlate traffic anomalies with configuration changes by time, helping identify whether a specific configuration change caused a traffic or security impact. The Controller can be configured to enable configuration warnings, which notify users when configuration changes occur.

Organizations must supplement these detection capabilities with defined authorization baselines that specify which configuration changes are expected or permitted, SIEM integration or incident management tooling that acts on CONFIG_CREATE or CONFIG_UPDATE events, and response procedures for investigating and remediating anomalies. Avi provides the detection data; the determination of what constitutes an unauthorized change and the response workflow require organizational policy and integration with external platforms.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-07(15)

> Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity | Code Authentication Implement cryptographic mechanisms to authenticate the following software or firmware components prior to installation: [Assignment: organization-defined software or firmware components].

**SCF Controls:** CHG-04.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF enforces digital signature verification for software and firmware components at multiple layers of the stack, from hypervisor boot through application deployment.

During VCF deployment, each component is assigned a certificate from a default signing certificate authority. The VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA) is an internal CA that comes integral with vSphere deployment and has been purpose-built to serve the certificate needs of the vSphere environment. From initial installation onward, all VCF components operate within a certificate-based trust framework rooted in a known signing authority. Administrators can configure VCF to use certificates from VMCA, Microsoft CA, OpenSSL CA, or external third-party certificate authorities, giving organizations flexibility to integrate their approved CA hierarchy into the VCF signing and verification workflow. All communication between the VCF Installer and ESX hosts is performed over HTTPS with certificate identity validation, and the installer validates the identity of each host by comparing the certificate common name against the provided FQDN.

At the hypervisor level, VMware ESX uses UEFI Secure Boot to verify that the boot loader and kernel modules are digitally signed before execution.
