The host acceptance level setting controls which vSphere Installation Bundles (VIBs) are permitted: it can be configured to accept only VMware-certified VIBs, restricting installation to components signed by VMware's certificate authority. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide includes recommendations for enabling Secure Boot and configuring TPM on ESX hosts. When UEFI Secure Boot is enabled on an ESX host, the host performs full signature verification of each package installed on the host at every boot, not only at initial installation. vSphere also supports UEFI Secure Boot for individual virtual machines, where the bootloader, operating system kernel, and operating system drivers must each carry a valid signature before the virtual machine is permitted to boot.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) provides desired-state image management for ESX hosts. Administrators define a target image specification for each cluster, and vLCM checks host compliance against that image. When hosts deviate from the desired image, vLCM can remediate them back to the approved configuration. This approach restricts ad-hoc installation of unsigned or unapproved software components on managed hosts.

VCF Operations enforces digital signing for its management packs and orchestrator packages. Management pack PAK files are verified against a digital signature provided by VMware. If a PAK file lacks a valid digital signature, the system displays a warning indicating the file cannot be verified as authentic, and the digital signature indicates the original developer or publisher. For orchestrator packages, all packages exported from a VCF Operations orchestrator server are digitally signed, and the orchestrator's certificate infrastructure allows signing of all elements created within the system. When a client receives an element from the orchestrator server, the client verifies the server identity and decides whether to trust the signature. Package signing certificates are a form of digital identification used to support encrypted communication and provide a signature for orchestrator packages. For production environments, VCF recommends using a certificate signed by a certificate authority rather than a self-signed certificate, allowing organizations to align package signing with their approved CA infrastructure.

For container workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) validates TLS certificates when fetching container images from registries and requires that registry certificates be signed by a trusted certificate authority. The platform requires that a certificate signing request be generated, signed by a certificate authority, and applied to the Workload Platform Management layer to secure the Kubernetes control plane. TLS certificates for the Supervisor layer are derived from the VMCA certificate, anchoring the Kubernetes control plane trust chain in the same root authority as the broader VCF infrastructure. The VCF CLI imgpkg tool supports CA certificate validation for registry connections through its --registry-ca-cert-path flag, which can be specified multiple times to add CA certificates for registry API communication. VKS clusters can also be configured to trust any number of CA certificates for private registry endpoints, directing image pulls to controlled, organization-approved sources.

Harbor is available as a standard package for VKS clusters and provides enterprise container registry capabilities including content trust and image signing. Organizations can configure Harbor to enforce signature requirements at image push or pull time, restricting deployments to images signed under an organization-approved certificate authority. Admission controllers are the primary enforcement mechanism for image signing policies within VKS clusters. The ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller allows administrators to configure an external image verification backend that is consulted before any pod is scheduled; if the backend determines that an image lacks a valid signature from an approved authority, the pod is rejected before it runs. ValidatingWebhookConfiguration objects can integrate third-party image signing solutions with the Kubernetes API server, creating an enforcement layer that intercepts pod creation requests and validates image signatures as part of the admission control chain.

The Kubernetes control plane components used by VKS are themselves distributed as signed artifacts. Kubernetes release artifacts, including standalone binaries and Software Bill of Materials files, are signed using cosign keyless signing, and control plane component images can be verified using cosign with certificate-based identity and OIDC issuer validation. Kubernetes CertificateSigningRequest signers can deny requests that do not meet approval conditions, and CertificateSigningRequests with unknown signerName values are never auto-approved, restricting unauthorized certificate issuance within the cluster. For workload certificate lifecycle management, the cert-manager standard package creates TLS certificates for workloads and automatically renews them before expiration, with VKS cluster control plane certificate rotation configured through the ClusterClass API and defaulting to renewal 90 days before expiry. The renewal window is configurable through the renewalDaysBeforeExpiry parameter in the ClusterClass specification, allowing organizations to set this period to match their certificate management policies.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-08

> a. Employ spam protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points to detect and act on unsolicited messages; and b. Update spam protection mechanisms when new releases are available in accordance with organizational configuration management policy and procedures.

**SCF Controls:** END-08

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-10

> Information Input Validation Check the validity of the following information inputs: [Assignment: organization-defined information inputs to the system].

**SCF Controls:** NET-12, TDA-18

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides built-in input data validation mechanisms across its automation, orchestration, and management components. These capabilities allow administrators and developers to enforce data quality at the point of entry, whether through interactive forms or programmatic interfaces.

The VCF Automation custom form designer supports data validation by adding constraints to form fields. Administrators can configure field-level validation using regular expressions (which must follow both Java and ECMAScript syntax), minimum and maximum value constraints, maximum character length limits, and required field checks. For example, the deploymentName field supports a maximum character length constraint of 900 characters. VCF Automation input properties also support maxLength and minLength attributes to constrain the number of characters allowed for string inputs. Validation rules can be set as constant values or as conditional values determined by expressions, allowing dynamic validation logic that adapts based on other field selections or inputs. Numeric fields support minimum value validation with configurable error messages displayed to the user when input falls outside acceptable ranges. Regular expression validation supports custom error messages following JavaScript syntax, so users receive clear feedback when their input does not match the expected pattern.

VCF Automation also supports external validation through VCF Operations orchestrator action scripts. These external validations run when a user submits a catalog request form from either the UI or the API. The validation action scripts evaluate input parameter values and return a string describing the error when invalid data is detected. When input is valid, the external validation returns nothing, allowing the request to proceed. The names of validation action input parameters must match the names of the workflow input parameters being validated, enforcing a structured approach to validation design. VCF Automation provides a Content and Policies interface to identify which custom forms contain actions that cannot be validated, giving administrators visibility into validation coverage across the environment.

The VCF Operations orchestrator Input Form Designer provides its own input validation capabilities, including regular expression-based validation, minimum and maximum value or length constraints for input parameters, and external actions to validate the inputs of custom workflows. This enables validation at the orchestration layer for automated processes and workflows that accept user or system-provided input.

At the infrastructure layer, VMware vCenter includes a configuration validation process that examines configuration files for possible errors and conflicts and returns output to help administrators avoid configuration issues or loading incorrect configuration files.

At the Consumption layer, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), the Supervisor-based Kubernetes environment embedded in VCF, brings input validation to the Kubernetes control plane. The Kubernetes API server embedded in VKS enforces schema-based field validation on all incoming API requests; when configured in strict mode via the ServerSideFieldValidation feature gate, the API server validates each request against the resource's OpenAPI schema and rejects submissions that contain unknown or incorrectly typed fields, whether submitted through kubectl, the API server, or automation tooling.

VKS supports ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, a declarative mechanism for policy-based validation of API objects before they are persisted. Policies are expressed in Common Expression Language (CEL), which supports comparisons, map key existence checks, string pattern matching (including prefix checks via methods such as startsWith()), date calculations, and list membership validation. The Kubernetes CEL format library extends this with a Format.validate(string) function that validates a string against a specified format and returns a list of error strings when invalid. The spec.validations.message field carries human-readable error text to the caller on failure, while spec.validations.reason provides a machine-readable failure code from values including Unauthorized, Forbidden, Invalid, and RequestEntityTooLarge. A spec.variables field supports defining named CEL expressions that can be composed and reused within the same policy, reducing duplication and computational expense. Policies are bound to resources through ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding objects, which scope enforcement to specific namespaces via label selectors; when multiple parameter objects match the label selector, policy rules are evaluated for each and the results are ANDed together. For validation logic that cannot be expressed declaratively in CEL, validating admission webhooks provide an extension point for integration with external validation services. The ValidatingAdmissionPolicy status.conditions array records the latest system observations of a policy's current state, and status.typeChecking records type-checking results for each CEL expression to detect missing fields and type confusion.

For user attribute validation, UserValidationRule applies a required CEL expression evaluated against UserInfo objects to enforce attribute-level constraints on who may interact with specific API resources; the message field customizes the error returned when a rule returns false. The Kubernetes declarative validation system supports tags including +k8s:required (indicating a field must be specified by clients), +k8s:minimum (a numeric minimum value constraint), and +k8s:neq (disallowing a specific value, with type inferred from the disallowed value). Custom resource definitions support FieldValueForbidden validation, which reports values that would be acceptable under some conditions but are prohibited by current security policy. Organizations are responsible for defining and maintaining the admission policies appropriate to their security requirements; VKS provides the control plane infrastructure on which those policies operate.

For integrations with external systems, the ServiceNow ITSM Plug-in for VCF Automation supports required field validation on data source choices, though regular expression and min/max value constraints are not available for all data types such as dropdown and checkbox fields.

Input validation for customer applications running on VCF is the responsibility of those applications and their developers. VCF provides infrastructure on which workloads run; the application-level input validation required by web applications, APIs, microservices, and data pipelines is implemented in the application code or in security overlays such as VMware vDefend (which provides Web Application Firewall capabilities through Avi Load Balancer) and dedicated DLP, WAF, or API security tooling deployed alongside the platform.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) provides a structured ML model validation workflow that checks the validity of model inputs before they are admitted to production use. Before a model can be uploaded to a Model Gallery for use by AI application developers, it must pass a mandatory validation sequence performed in a Deep Learning VM acting as a sandbox environment.

The validation sequence includes integrity verification by hash-code comparison, malware scanning to detect supply chain contamination, scanning for serialization attacks (a class of vulnerability specific to ML model files that can execute arbitrary code on deserialization), and inference behavior testing using Triton Inference Server. Models must be downloaded into the Deep Learning VM from trusted sources such as the NVIDIA NGC catalog, Hugging Face, or another model hub before validation. The Model Gallery preserves the integrity of stored models by assigning a unique digest to each revision when the same model is pushed multiple times, and PAIS uses a Harbor registry in the Supervisor (or an OCI-compliant registry from another vendor) as the model storage backend, requiring authentication and certificate-based trust for model retrieval.

Beyond technical file-level checks, MLOps engineers validate ML models against the security, privacy, and technical requirements of the organization before upload. This includes examining inference requests for malicious behavior and performing hands-on functional tests to confirm the model performs as expected. PAIS guidance is that models be sourced and validated internally rather than consumed directly from the Internet, where they could contain malicious code or be tuned for malicious behavior.

For agent and retrieval-augmented generation workflows, PAIS extends input validation to two additional surfaces. Knowledge Bases that supply context to model responses can be linked to a bounded number of data sources, and PAIS requires examination and approval of MCP server tools and capabilities before they are used in agents, helping prevent unsafe or untrusted external capabilities from being introduced into AI applications.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend checks the validity of information inputs across several of its network security subsystems, contributing to the organization's broader input validation posture.

Security Intelligence parses and validates the content format and inventory asset data in administrator-provided CSV files used during segmentation planning imports. When data mapping fails due to file errors, an error notification appears with a link to an annotated CSV file that highlights the specific errors for correction. Administrators can correct and re-upload the file, or allow analysis to continue with incorrect entries excluded from processing. Administrators are advised to validate inventory asset data for completeness before importing CSV files into the Security Services Platform.

Security Intelligence stores system-inferred infrastructure classifications in a "Not reviewed" state until an administrator explicitly accepts or modifies them. This classification review mechanism applies to compute entity classifications used in segmentation planning and policy generation, requiring human acceptance before automated classifications can influence policy decisions.

Security Intelligence Policy Recommendations includes a Monitoring capability that checks for changes in the scope of input entities every hour when enabled. When the monitoring feature detects a change in input scope, administrators are prompted to review the detected changes and rerun recommendation analysis to regenerate policy based on the updated inputs.

Security Intelligence enforces input limits on policy recommendation generation: a maximum of 100 VM entities can be selected as direct input, and the total number of effective VMs when groups are included cannot exceed 500. These constraints are enforced before analysis begins, validating that inputs fall within the range where analysis results are reliable.

The vDefend distributed firewall includes an auditing and remediation workflow that validates rule components, requiring rules to use IPv4 addresses for successful validation through the security gate before deployment.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) validates inputs across its recovery-plan, replication, and management surfaces. Configuration data exported in XML format can be validated against an XSD schema, providing structural validation of imported and exported configuration data (p.288). After PNR upgrades, administrators must re-verify that protection groups and recovery plans remain valid before they are placed back into operational use (p.108).

For recovery-plan IP customization, the DR IP Customizer tool uses a structured comma-separated value file with a header row defining column meanings and one or more rows for each placeholder virtual machine in a recovery plan (p.379, p.382). The dr-ip-customizer command supports a --verbose option that enables additional diagnostic logging for validation of customizer inputs (p.376).

PNR's Cyber Recovery workflow includes explicit validation states for VMs prior to recovery. Cyber Recovery VMs transition through a Preparing validation state and then an In validation state when an administrator selects a snapshot and initiates validation (p.575). Cyber Recovery supports on-demand vulnerability analysis through the Analysis tab to validate workload integrity before completing recovery validation (p.574).

Integrated Security and Vulnerability Analysis with CrowdStrike examines VMs in the clean room using behavioral analysis and machine learning during validation, with continuous analysis of running software and processes on the guest operating system to detect suspicious behavior (p.580). The integration performs vulnerability analysis combining Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) information with proprietary threat data and advanced modeling from Kenna Security, and identifies vulnerabilities by their CVE designation (p.582). Operators use these signals to select a clean snapshot for recovery rather than restoring a compromised one.

PNR logs certificate verification error messages in security-related log files for audit trail purposes, so that input-validation failures on certificate fields are captured (p.133). For operational health checks, PNR can adjust the number of status check attempts before declaring a check failed using the connections.hmsPingFailedDelay parameter (p.440). Trusted environment setup for the PNR Appliance requires text values for organization and organization unit during configuration (p.112). The Clean Room Orchestrator categorizes validation events using Info and Warning severities so that operators can act on input or state conditions surfaced during recovery operations (p.562).

Input validation for customer applications running on workloads protected by PNR is the responsibility of those applications and their developers. PNR provides input validation for its own recovery-plan, configuration import/export, customizer, API, and orchestration surfaces, and provides Cyber Recovery validation states and CrowdStrike-driven analysis to validate workload integrity before recovered VMs return to production.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to input data validation through mechanisms built into its configuration, policy management, and integration interfaces.

At the policy layer, DSM data service policies support allowlist creation, restricting users to selecting only from administrator-defined sets of approved parameters when provisioning or configuring databases. In 9.1, SQL Server data service policies include an "Allow with a List" option that creates a specific allowlist of approved parameter values users can select from. Policies also enforce required-field validation: administrators must supply a name, description, and target data service before a policy can be saved. When applying a policy to existing databases, DSM displays a preview of any policy violations that would result, allowing review and correction before the policy is created or modified.

DSM validates configuration inputs for backup storage at multiple points. During monitoring, DSM checks the connection to the backup storage repository, verifies the correctness of the bucket name, confirms certificate expiration dates, validates credential currency, and verifies that sufficient available space exists in the repository. Administrators can view these validation results through the database monitoring interface.

In System Settings, DSM validates SMTP configuration inputs when settings are saved and returns an error if validation fails. DSM also requires validation of the SMTP certificate as part of SMTP configuration. NTP server entries are validated and flagged for correction if invalid values are present.

For webhook integrations, DSM provides a TEST function that sends the configured payload to the endpoint URL before the webhook is created, allowing administrators to verify that the endpoint is reachable and that the payload is accepted before deployment.

DSM introduces a "latest" certificate validation mode as the default for version 9.1 and later. This mode enforces strict cryptographic and KeyUsage validations for all DSM endpoints, including the Provider VM and data service clusters. The validation mode is configurable through the advanced-system-config ConfigMap.

For PostgreSQL databases, DSM includes the amcheck extension, which provides functions for verifying relation integrity at the database engine level.

For local user accounts, DSM enforces password complexity requirements: passwords must be between 8 and 20 characters and include at least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, one number, and at least one special character. This validates credential inputs at the authentication boundary. DSM does not send a verification message to confirm the validity of local user email addresses.

DSM's input validation mechanisms cover its own administrative and configuration interfaces. They do not extend to application-layer validation for data flowing through the PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MinIO databases it manages. Input validation for workloads connected to DSM-managed databases is an organizational and application development responsibility.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Meets)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides application-layer input validation through three complementary mechanisms: WAF signatures and the Positive Security Model, DataScript programmable rules, and Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) configuration validation.

Avi WAF signatures perform input and output validation by analyzing all incoming and outgoing traffic. The WAF Positive Security Model, also called Application Specific Policy, describes expected application behavior and enforces it by setting an accepted range and length of characters for HTTP fields. Requests that do not meet those specifications are reported as policy violations before reaching backend workloads, allowing administrators to restrict inputs to known-valid character sets and field lengths on a per-application basis. The WAF also provides analytics to identify possible false positives and allows operators to add exceptions through a modal dialog that generates suggested values pre-computed from log entries and related findings, supporting ongoing policy refinement.

DataScript extends input validation with programmable, event-driven logic executed within Avi Service Engines. Using Lua-based scripting with relational, logical, and arithmetic operators, DataScript rules can inspect and validate request parameters, HTTP headers, and request body content. Specific capabilities include an external request validation API that offloads validation decisions to an external service and returns a response object containing status code, headers, and HTTP response body; XML body parsing via the Parse function, which returns a success indicator and either the parsed result or error information if parsing fails; client certificate validation via the avi.ssl.check_client_cert_validity() function, which returns distinct values when the certificate is not presented, is valid, or is invalid; conditional clearing of certificate verification errors based on error code evaluation; string pattern functions including string.contains() and string.beginswith() for field content checks; and group-based user identity validation using avi.stringgroup.equals, which checks whether a user ID is in an approved list and can redirect unauthenticated users to a login page. DataScript rules should be tested and validated after modification to confirm that the intended validation logic operates correctly without affecting other virtual services.

The Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) performs syntactical validations at the time of Custom Resource Definition (CRD) object creation to verify configuration correctness and provides effective status messages. The AKO CRD Operator condition-status field indicates whether conditions are 'True' or 'False', supporting automated validation of AKO configuration inputs in Kubernetes environments.

### Control SI-11

> Error Handling a. Generate error messages that provide information necessary for corrective actions without revealing information that could be exploited; and  b. Reveal error messages only to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].

**SCF Controls:** TDA-19

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF contributes to error handling by providing structured mechanisms for identifying security-relevant error conditions, centralizing error and log data, and restricting access to error details through role-based access control.

VCF Operations defines alert policies that use combinations of symptom definitions and recommendations to identify problems such as failures and high-stress conditions. Symptom definitions can evaluate message events against specific object types, using operators to compare incoming event messages against configured text strings. Event categories include System Degradation, Change, Environment, Notification, Data Availability, Collector Down, and Object Error. Administrators can create alert definitions with tighter symptom parameters to track problems and identify issues before users encounter further disruption. Risk alerts flag problems that require investigation, and alert definitions can include incremental severity levels, such as Warning for conditions approaching a threshold and Critical for conditions that have reached it. When workflows execute in VCF Operations orchestrator, failed workflows generate notifications in the alerts window with details about the resources for which the workflow failed.

VCF Operations orchestrator includes structured exception handling within workflows. Workflow elements that encounter errors send error signals to exception handlers, which react according to the specific error received. Exception bindings on individual elements define how the workflow behaves when that element encounters an error. A default error handler catches errors not addressed by custom handlers. If no handler can process a particular error, the element's exception output parameter can be bound to an Exception element, which ends the workflow run in a failed state with explicit error information. Defining exception behavior for user interaction elements is a recommended workflow development practice. Exception handling variables capture error codes and messages, providing structured diagnostic information without exposing raw system internals.

VCF Log Management is the central point for log collection, aggregation, and analysis across all VCF instances within a fleet. VCF Operations and VCF Log Management must be integrated to implement the centralized log collection architecture. This centralization gives administrators the ability to analyze error conditions in context, correlating log entries across components. When errors occur during deployments, administrators can use log files and the Supportability and Serviceability (SoS) Utility for troubleshooting. Management Pack logs record errors related to user permissions or session errors that affect data collection, helping isolate permission-related failures from application-level errors. VMware ESX system logging supports eight severity levels as defined in RFC 5424: Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notice, Informational, and Debug, providing a standardized hierarchy for categorizing and filtering error conditions. The ESX syslog message format includes an optional LINE-MARKER field to identify multiline log submissions and prevent log injection in syslog streams.

The Consumption layer of VCF, comprising VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), vSphere Supervisor, and VCF Services, extends these error handling mechanisms into the Kubernetes workload tier. The Kubernetes API server within VKS Supervisor captures security-relevant error conditions including authentication failures, authorization denials, and admission control rejections in the cluster audit log. Admission webhooks log denial events with specific error messages that identify the reason for request rejection. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy definitions are parsed for syntax errors and rejected at creation or update time if errors are found, providing early detection of misconfigurations before they reach runtime. The ValidatingAdmissionPolicy failurePolicy field controls how CEL expression parse errors, type check errors, and runtime errors are handled, with allowed values of Ignore or Fail. ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding supports multiple validationActions, including Warn and Audit, which can be applied together: in Warn mode a client-facing warning is returned without blocking the request, while in Audit mode the violation is recorded in the audit log without notifying the caller. Each audit failure object includes the validation failure message string, the resource name of the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, the resource name of the ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding, and the index of the failed validation expression. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy message expressions, defined via the spec.validations.message or messageExpression field, allow operators to provide informative, context-specific denial reasons; when a messageExpression encounters a runtime error, the failure message falls back to the static message and the runtime error is logged separately rather than being surfaced to the caller.

VKS cluster status conditions use structured machine-readable representations that include a Message field providing human-readable information about the Reason for each condition. Machine health conditions in the v1beta1 API classify events by Severity: Info for reconciliation in progress, Warning for conditions that may require attention, and Error for conditions that block operation. A CSIProvisioningFailed warning condition documents CSI addon storage provisioning failures. TLS handshake errors in Supervisor NSX networking are logged with timestamp, source IP, and port information, flagging potential routing or MTU issues in the network fabric. Supervisor Services installation distinguishes compatibility warnings, which can be acknowledged and skipped, from incompatibility errors, which must be resolved before installation continues.

Pod Security Admission in VKS Supervisor supports enforce, audit, and warn modes. In enforce mode, security violations cause the pod to be rejected. In audit mode, violations are recorded in the audit log without blocking the request. In warn mode, a user-facing warning is triggered without blocking the request. Applying the baseline Pod Security Standard in enforce mode while applying the restricted standard in warn and audit modes lets operators balance corrective feedback to users with security event capture for administrators. Contour Envoy ingress logging verbosity is configurable through the Envoy logging level setting, with supported values of trace, debug, info, warn, error, critical, and off, defaulting to info. Harbor container registry components deployed as VKS standard packages have a configurable log level that accepts values of debug, info, warning, error, or fatal, allowing operators to limit the diagnostic detail written to logs to what is operationally necessary.

Role-based access control across VCF management interfaces restricts access to logs, alerts, and error messages to authorized personnel. Users who require access to log integration must be assigned the Security Operations permission through the VCF Operations console under Administrator, Control Panel, Access Control, Roles. VCF Operations supports assigning specific roles and scope to users and groups, allowing administrators to limit who can view diagnostic information and error details. Within the Kubernetes layer, RBAC governs access to cluster and namespace logs and status conditions. Access to kubectl commands such as kubectl logs, kubectl describe, and kubectl get events requires appropriate ClusterRole or Role bindings scoped to the relevant namespace or cluster. Cluster audit logs are accessible only through cluster administrator channels and are not exposed to namespace-scoped users by default. For high-assurance environments, Kubernetes supports deploying cryptographic protections to help maintain tamper-proof and confidential log handling.

The organizational aspects of this control require processes outside VCF's scope. Defining policies for which error conditions are security-relevant, establishing standards for what information error messages may or may not reveal to different audiences, and creating procedures to review and update error handling practices over time are all activities that require organizational governance. Operators must design their ValidatingAdmissionPolicy message expressions to avoid exposing sensitive values such as secrets, token contents, or internal resource names in error output. RBAC configuration to restrict log access requires deliberate role design for each namespace and cluster, which is an organizational responsibility. VCF provides the technical mechanisms to identify, capture, and restrict access to error information, but the policies governing how those mechanisms are configured and what constitutes an appropriate level of detail in error messages must come from the organization.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) contributes to error handling requirements through several mechanisms specific to its AI workload infrastructure.

PAIS uses Kubernetes Conditions metadata for component status reporting. Component state is surfaced through typed fields including Type (for example, Ready), Status (True or False), and Reason (for example, paisAvailable). Administrators verifying a PAIS deployment inspect these structured conditions through kubectl or the VCF Automation UI, which provides diagnostic information scoped to availability status without exposing raw internal state. The PAISConfiguration custom resource must reach a Ready status with reason paisAvailable to indicate successful activation, which establishes a defined diagnostic checkpoint for identifying initialization failures as security-relevant error conditions at the component level.

For runtime visibility, PAIS supports Prometheus metrics collection with a configurable retention period and forwarding of LLM traces to an OpenTelemetry Collector. MLOps engineers and application developers monitor these signals through observability platforms such as Grafana to track model health, quality, and behavior of AI applications and identify anomalous conditions. NVIDIA vGPU license server communication errors, a failure mode specific to GPU-accelerated deployments, can be diagnosed by examining /var/log/syslog on the host for NVIDIA-related error messages.

The model validation workflow in PAIS addresses a platform-specific class of security-relevant error condition. Before a model is uploaded to a Model Gallery, the validation pipeline checks file integrity through hash codes, scans for malware, scans for serialization attacks, and runs functional inference tests through Triton Inference Server as part of a sandbox validation process on a Deep Learning VM. Models that fail these checks are contained at ingestion rather than reaching inference time. The Model Gallery further maintains revision integrity by assigning a unique digest to each pushed revision, which helps identify tampering or unexpected version drift. Harbor project access capabilities restrict who can pull models and reach the training data associated with them, which limits the audience for any diagnostic detail exposed during model retrieval failures.

The organization is responsible for implementing application-level error message generation in workloads running on PAIS, defining which application error conditions are security-relevant, and controlling access to system logs where detailed diagnostic messages may appear.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to error handling by surfacing security-relevant error conditions across its management interfaces and policy enforcement components.

Security Services Platform (SSP) surfaces policy publication failures with a red exclamation icon in the Status column; authorized administrators can click the error status to view detailed error information before republishing. SSP monitors current deployment status and surfaces platform-related alarms that highlight errors requiring attention. NSX Manager-related alarms displayed in the SSP System Dashboard indicate errors that may need attention. When CSV data mapping fails during segmentation planning, an error notification appears with a link to an annotated CSV file that highlights the specific errors for correction before re-upload, providing corrective guidance without exposing raw configuration data. Rule Analysis failures are captured in a HAR file along with NSX and SSP support bundles when filing a support ticket, providing a structured process for error diagnosis.

SSP can be configured to forward log messages to a remote syslog server, supporting centralized collection and analysis of error events by authorized operations personnel.

The IDS/IPS signature bundle management Errors Tab in vDefend Advanced Threat Protection provides a dedicated troubleshooting view that isolates failed updates and displays specific error messages and codes to help identify why a signature bundle failed to download or apply to a particular node. A Failed/Down status filter allows administrators to view only nodes that encountered errors or are currently unreachable.

Malware Prevention Service event logs record an analysisStatus field indicating completion state along with errorCode and errorMessage fields for troubleshooting analysis failures. The Network Detection and Response (NDR) Sensor provides a CLI command "get sensor details" to view registration details and display error messages if sensor registration fails. When reviewing migration alerts in the migration plan, administrators can identify specific violation messages to determine which rules require remediation.

Bare Metal Agent Health Status monitoring in SSP displays Upgrade Failed and Offboard Failed counts along with Last Operation status for each Bare Metal server, giving administrators a targeted view of operational errors.

At the host level, the Guest Introspection Platform Host Agent (MUX) supports configurable log levels and classifies messages as [ERROR], [DEBUG], or [INFO]; the EPSecLib component uses the same classification with an (EPSEC) designation for Endpoint Security-specific messages, enabling targeted review of error-severity events during troubleshooting.

vDefend does not fully address all three requirements of this control. Systematic restriction of error message visibility to authorized personnel and comprehensive sanitization across all platform components depend on the broader platform access control and logging infrastructure rather than vDefend alone.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) generates structured alerts and log records that identify error conditions relevant to database operations, authentication, and platform connectivity. The Global Alerts Reference documents categories of security-relevant errors that DSM surfaces, including authentication failures, backup and restore corruptions, and certificate errors, each mapping to conditions where administrative action may be required. DSM also tracks infrastructure policy status and surfaces policy violations before a data service policy is applied to databases, allowing review before changes take effect. A compliance report for each data service policy displays policy violations, noncompliant databases, data services, and namespaces, giving administrators a structured view of out-of-compliance objects.

DSM provides multiple mechanisms to expose error information in actionable form. The database Status indicator reflects availability, identifies in-progress operations, and flags critical operations that have failed. The Database Alerts panel surfaces configurable threshold-based alerts for CPU Health, Data Disk Health, System Disk Health, and Max Connections metrics, with separate warning and critical thresholds that administrators and users can adjust. Mitigation suggestions accompany database alerts to guide resolution. The monitoring framework supports metrics-based and status-condition-based alerts that identify critical failure conditions, including database engine degradation, non-operational status, and automated or transaction log backup failures.

DSM logs provide structured error records for administrative review. For PostgreSQL, DSM logs logical replication errors with timestamps, error codes, constraint violations, and transaction details in the log bundle; replication worker failures, including permission denied errors and replication slot operations, are recorded separately. For SQL Server, DSM supports the errorLog.numErrorLogs custom engine setting to control how many error log files are retained before recycling. When troubleshooting database provisioning failures, DSM documentation directs administrators to review VMware vCenter logs for API errors. The Consumption Operator Pod logs can be inspected directly from the running container or via command-line interface to diagnose error status conditions in VCF Automation deployments.

When deeper investigation is needed, DSM supports generation of database support bundles that capture database activity and diagnostic information, and a system-level support bundle function for broader troubleshooting of system configuration failures. Alerts can be activated or deactivated for data service operation failures, and webhook integrations forward operation failure notifications to external systems with alert level information indicating whether an alert is CRITICAL or WARNING; a TEST function is available to validate webhook payloads before deployment.

Access to error information and alert detail is governed by DSM's role-based access model. DSM distinguishes between Admin and User roles with role-based UI view restrictions that scope what each user can see and act on, limiting exposure of operational error detail to personnel with appropriate access. The broader requirement to formally identify all security-relevant error conditions, define policies for what information error messages may contain, and enforce organization-wide controls on message disclosure requires organizational policies and procedures beyond what DSM itself provides.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides mechanisms that address all three dimensions of this control at the application delivery layer.

For identifying security-relevant error conditions, the Avi Controller and Service Engines log audit record failures covering a defined set of security-significant events: initiation and termination of trusted channels, trusted channel function failures, unsuccessful certificate validation attempts, client authentication failures, and SSH session establishment failures. These records include initiator and target identification, making them useful for detecting and investigating security incidents. Audit record failures are forwarded to Events, Syslog, and Splunk. Virtual service logs record request policy rules, network security rules, request outcomes, and error conditions including abnormal request termination and HTTP response codes. The analytics profile attached to a virtual service defines what constitutes an error for the purposes of significant log classification, giving administrators control over which conditions are surfaced as noteworthy events.

For controlling what information is returned to clients in error responses, Avi supports Error Page Profiles that let operators define the content returned to clients for each HTTP status code. Administrators configure Error Page Profile templates to associate custom error page bodies with specific HTTP status codes, which are then applied per virtual service. DataScripts can return custom error messages to the client by detecting the RESP_FAILED event, which is triggered when Avi Load Balancer cannot process a transaction. Custom error responses can also be generated from policy or POOL_DOWN actions. When a DataScript encounters an execution failure, the default behavior returns a generic HTTP 500 Internal Server Error to the client, limiting exposure of internal application details in client-facing messages. For ICAP-integrated virtual services, the ICAP Profile supports configurable failure action handling, with options for Fail Closed (returns HTTP 503 on error) or Fail Open (allows traffic to pass), giving administrators explicit control over error behavior when content inspection is unavailable.

Access to detailed diagnostic logs and audit records is governed by Avi's role-based access control (RBAC) system. Administrative interfaces and detailed event and audit log views are available only to authenticated users with appropriate role assignments. The Alert feature processes system events and metrics against user-defined rules and generates alerts that initiate configured actions such as sending email, syslog messages, or SNMP traps, routing operational notifications to the authorized administrative audience.

The organizational dimension of this control requires policy decisions that Avi supports through its configuration capabilities but does not make automatically: deciding which conditions are security-relevant, specifying what information is sensitive in error output, and defining which personnel are authorized to view detailed logs.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control SI-12

> Information Management and Retention Manage and retain information within the system and information output from the system in accordance with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, guidelines and operational requirements.

**SCF Controls:** DCH-18, PRI-05

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides configurable data retention controls within VCF Operations that allow administrators to align operational data retention periods with organizational requirements. These controls do not address the full scope of media and data retention governance, but they give administrators granular tuning over how long different categories of platform-generated data are kept.

VCF Operations exposes data retention settings through Administration > Global Settings > Data Retention. Administrators can configure retention periods independently for several data categories. Metrics data supports a retention window between 1 month and 13 months. Entities and configuration data can be retained for a minimum of 1 month and a maximum of 3 months. Organization reports support a maximum data retention period of 1,440 days. At the normal retention tier, VCF Operations retains data at 5-minute granularity for 6 months, then rolls up the seventh month into 1-hour aggregations. A real-time retention tier provides 5-minute granularity, a standard tier retains data for 1 month, and a long-term tier retains data for 13 months. Different data retention policies can be configured and controlled for each category according to organizational requirements. Log data is governed separately through VCF Log Management, where administrators define log partitions that each carry their own retention period in days, with logs removed from disk automatically once a partition's period elapses and the option to archive partitions to external NFS or S3 storage for longer-term preservation.

VCF Operations for Networks provides additional granular retention controls. The default data retention period is 1 month in the advanced license edition. Flow data retention is fixed at a 1-month window within the platform. For organizations that need to retain flow data beyond one month, the Streaming Databus provides a mechanism to export flows to an external subscriber via HTTPS/HTTP for extended retention. Miscellaneous data retention can be configured with up to 100 GB of additional disk space.

VCF Operations also provides backup capabilities that support regulatory and operational retention needs. Administrators can take regular backups of both custom and out-of-the-box content. When reducing an entity metrics retention period in VCF Operations for Networks, the recommendation is to back up log files first to prevent data loss, which supports the principle of preserving data before modifying retention windows.

The Consumption layer of VCF, comprising VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), vSphere Supervisor, and vSphere namespaces, adds Kubernetes-native persistent storage lifecycle controls relevant to data retention for containerized workloads. Persistent volumes in Supervisor namespaces store application data outside the lifecycle of individual pods and workloads, so data remains available when workloads restart or scale. The central mechanism governing retention behavior is the PersistentVolume reclaim policy, which controls what happens to the underlying storage and its data when the associated PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted. The Retain policy preserves the volume and its data in a Released state for manual administrator reclamation, leaving the data intact for inspection, archival, or transfer before final disposal. The Delete policy causes the storage asset to be removed when the claim is released. Retain is the default for manually provisioned PersistentVolumes. StorageClass definitions carry a ReclaimPolicy field that sets the default behavior for all dynamically provisioned volumes within a given storage class, allowing cluster administrators to enforce organization-wide retention defaults at the storage class level rather than per-volume. For StatefulSet workloads, Kubernetes provides a persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy setting with a whenDeleted field that controls whether PVCs and their backing volumes are retained or deleted when the StatefulSet is removed; scaling a StatefulSet down does not delete the associated volumes by default. Organizations subject to retention obligations should configure StorageClasses with the Retain policy so that workload termination does not destroy data that must be kept for a defined period.

Storage Policy Based Management (SPBM) provides a unified control plane across VKS storage, enabling storage policies to be associated with persistent volumes that capture the storage characteristics required by a given workload or retention regime. VKS clusters surface a storage compliance status per volume: a Compliant status indicates that the datastore backing the volume satisfies the requirements of the associated policy; an Out of Date status indicates the policy has been updated but the new requirements have not yet been communicated to the datastore, requiring the policy to be reapplied to bring the volume back into compliance. This policy-driven model allows administrators to codify storage requirements and verify adherence continuously.

For Harbor Registry deployments on VKS clusters, administrators should note that configuring a StorageClass or PersistentVolume with a Delete reclaim policy results in all Harbor data being deleted when the Harbor package is removed. Image artifact retention requirements should drive reclaim policy selection for Harbor-backing storage. Platform teams should also manage data protection storage locations in VCF Automation to align with company-specific data residency and infrastructure standards.

These retention controls apply to the operational, monitoring, and configuration data that VCF Operations collects and manages, and to the persistent volume lifecycle for containerized workloads on VKS. They do not extend to application data stored within virtual machines outside the Kubernetes layer, user-generated content on datastores not managed by Kubernetes, or media outside the VCF platform. Organizations must establish broader data retention policies, classification schemes, and lifecycle management processes that address all media and data types subject to statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations. VCF's retention settings are one component of a larger retention program that requires organizational governance, legal review of applicable obligations, and potentially additional tools for archival and disposition of data beyond what VCF Operations and VKS storage lifecycle controls manage.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend provides configurable data retention capabilities for the security telemetry and flow data it collects, contributing to an organization's ability to retain security-relevant data in accordance with statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations.

Security Services Platform, which underpins Network Detection and Response (NDR) and related analytics services, offers control over flow data retention. Flow data is retained for 30 days by default via the flow-data-retention setting. If storage capacity is insufficient to handle flow volume from workloads, some flow metadata may be lost, flow ingestion may be paused, or data retention may be reduced dynamically to stay within available storage. Administrators can monitor retention health through the ssp_flow_storage.avg_flow_storage_ratio metric, which reports the ratio of current retention days to predicted full days for flow storage, and through the ssp_analytics_storage.avg_predicted_tabl_retention_days metric, which tracks predicted retention for the correlated flow visualization table (default 30 days). Security Services Platform persistent storage collects data even when the License Hub is offline, providing resilience against brief service interruptions that might otherwise affect data continuity.

Network Detection and Response retains detection events and campaign data for 12 months, providing a fixed retention window for threat detection artifacts.

For organizations using cloud-connected vDefend features, Security Services Platform maintains defined retention periods for data collected under active service subscriptions: IDS events and other network events, VM identifiers, rules and policies, conversation history, and security profiles are retained for one year during an active subscription, and for 90 days following subscription expiration.

Administrators should note that when the Malware Prevention Service feature is deleted, all previously gathered data analytics are permanently removed. Organizations with compliance-driven retention requirements should account for this before decommissioning that service.

These retention controls allow organizations to align vDefend's security data storage with their compliance requirements. The broader scope of media and data retention across the full infrastructure stack, including operational data, log archives, backup snapshots, and storage-level data protection, is handled by other VCF components such as VCF Operations, VCF Log Management, and vSAN Data Protection.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides configurable retention mechanisms that allow administrators to align disaster recovery and cyber recovery data with retention obligations. PNR snapshot schedules define a retention policy that determines how long each snapshot is retained, with hourly, daily, weekly, and monthly cadences and a maximum retention duration of three years. Administrators can also select predefined retention templates that combine recovery point objective (RPO) and snapshot retention parameters for quick configuration of protection groups.

For vSphere Replication, the retention policy determines the expiration of point-in-time copies, and storage consumption drops after all point-in-time copies referring to the original disk are expired. Administrators configure the number of retention slots and the spacing between recovery points, so older instances expire automatically once the configured limit is exceeded.

Cyber Recovery snapshot schedules in PNR follow the same retention model, defining a retention policy that controls how long each snapshot is retained. Administrators should account for storage capacity when configuring longer retention schedules, since extended retention periods increase storage consumption on the underlying datastores.

These retention controls allow organizations to align disaster recovery and cyber recovery data retention with applicable statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations. PNR provides the technical mechanisms; determining the specific retention durations that satisfy each obligation is an organizational policy decision.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides configurable backup retention controls that support an organization's data retention requirements for its managed databases. These controls do not define retention obligations, which come from the organization's legal and compliance functions, but they provide the technical means to implement those obligations for PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, and MinIO data.

Retention periods are configured through Data Service Policies in the DSM administration interface. Each policy supports a backup-retention-period setting that defines the duration for which backups are kept. When multiple policies apply to a namespace, DSM aggregates the retention constraints by selecting the more restrictive minimum and maximum retention day values, producing a resultant retention window that reflects all applicable policies. Data service policies can also be tailored to specific requirements and enforced across different tenants, giving administrators a consistent mechanism for applying retention standards in multi-tenant deployments.

For deleted databases, DSM retains automated backups according to the configured retention period and exposes an Archives tab in the administration interface where administrators can access and adjust the retention period of retained backups. This allows organizations to maintain access to backup data even after a database instance has been removed, supporting retention obligations that outlast the operational lifetime of a specific database. When a database is deleted, automated backups continue to be retained until the retention policy is met.

DSM also supports point-in-time restore within the backup retention period, enabling recovery to any point within the retained window. On-demand backups behave differently from automated backups: they are permanent and do not expire automatically. On-demand backups remain in the backup storage location until an administrator manually deletes them, which means they can consume storage quota indefinitely and require separate tracking for retention compliance.

Data service policies also define backup storage locations (S3-compatible targets) alongside retention duration. If a data service policy that is in use is deleted, the data services associated with that policy become non-compliant, alerting administrators to a configuration gap.

Meeting this control fully requires the organization to determine the applicable retention periods, communicate those requirements to DSM administrators, and configure policies accordingly. DSM does not automatically derive retention obligations from regulatory or contractual sources. Its retention controls also cover only the database data it manages and do not address retention of all media and data types that may fall within an organization's broader retention obligations.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control SI-13

> Predictable Failure Prevention a. Determine mean time to failure (MTTF) for the following system components in specific environments of operation: [Assignment: organization-defined system components]; and  b. Provide substitute system components and a means to exchange active and standby components in accordance with the following criteria: [Assignment: organization-defined MTTF substitution criteria].

**SCF Controls:** BCD-12.2, SEA-07

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides monitoring, health assessment, trend analysis, and failure categorization capabilities that supply the raw data and analytical foundation an organization needs to perform predictable failure analysis, including Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) determination for system components.

At the hardware layer, vSAN includes a Proactive Health Management (PHM) subsystem that checks for hardware failure events generated by the Host Storage Module (HSM) at a configurable interval, defaulting to every 10 minutes (600 seconds). This mechanism surfaces hardware degradation indicators before they result in outright component failure. vSAN also tracks the health state of storage objects across multiple categories, including reduced availability with active rebuild, inaccessible objects, and degraded components. The esxcli vsan debug object health summary get command provides a consolidated view of object health states and counts. When a failure occurs, vSAN marks affected components as absent or degraded based on the failure severity, and component failure alerts communicate the type of failure to administrators. vSAN distinguishes between temporary failures (absent state, where the component might recover) and permanent failures (degraded state, where recovery is not expected), and triggers an extra durability component during both planned failures (such as entering maintenance mode) and unplanned failures (such as disk failures, host failures, and network disconnects). The object repair timer, which defaults to 60 minutes, defines how long vSAN waits before initiating repair of non-compliant objects after a host enters a failed state, providing a measurable data point for component recovery behavior. When possible, vSAN rebuilds failed components on another host, provided the primary level of failures to tolerate is set to 1 or more. ESX also supports monitoring of critical flash device health parameters from the ESX host, including Media Wearout Indicator, Temperature, and Reallocated Sector Count, which provide leading indicators of storage device degradation that can inform component-level failure prediction.

VCF Operations adds an analytical layer on top of component-level monitoring. It correlates metrics collected over time to help administrators understand trends that may lead to potential failures. This trend correlation is directly applicable to MTTF analysis, as it allows organizations to observe degradation patterns and extrapolate expected component lifespans in their specific operating environment. VCF Operations also correlates events published by monitored systems and delivers them as faults, which signify conditions affecting the availability of objects in the environment. Fault symptoms can be defined based on these events and incorporated into alert definitions, enabling organizations to build a structured taxonomy of failure modes. The platform tracks failure-related metrics such as FailedRequestCount, ErrorRequestCount, and FaultRequestCount, providing quantitative data that feeds into reliability calculations.

VCF Operations tracks system uptime per host, measured as total time elapsed since the last system startup, and provides similar uptime metrics for virtual machines. Uptime measurement accuracy can be enhanced by creating a super metric that adds ping information or by checking processes using an agent such as application monitoring. The capacity analytics engine analyzes historical utilization and projects future workload by using real-time predictive capacity analytics based on an industry-standard statistical analysis model of demand behavior. This projected utilization data helps organizations identify components approaching capacity limits, which is a key input to failure prediction since components under sustained high utilization are more likely to experience failures.

For virtual machine workloads, the VM Operations capability in vCenter allows administrators to drill down into the distribution of failures by cause or type of initiator. This categorization of failure modes by root cause supports the kind of structured failure analysis that MTTF calculations require, enabling organizations to distinguish between different failure classes and compute component-specific reliability metrics.

Together, these capabilities provide the instrumentation, data collection, trend analysis, failure categorization, and predictive modeling that an organization needs to support MTTF determination. However, VCF does not itself compute MTTF values or maintain reliability databases. The organizational process of collecting failure data over time, performing statistical analysis, and maintaining component reliability records must be established separately.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) implements failover capabilities for its own security infrastructure to maintain continuous network security enforcement during component failures.

The vDefend gateway firewall operates in primary-passive high availability mode with non-preemptive failover. When all uplink routing links go down on the active Edge node, failover is triggered and the standby node assumes the active role. Firewall flow state is synchronized between Edge nodes, so existing sessions are preserved across failover events without requiring re-establishment. Sessions resume after a brief convergence period during Edge node failover. This flow synchronization allows security policy enforcement to continue through a Gateway Firewall failover without leaving traffic unprotected during the transition.

The Security Services Platform (SSP), which hosts vDefend's advanced threat detection functions including IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention, and Network Detection and Response, has built-in high availability by default. Enabling vSphere HA is recommended for the vSphere cluster hosting the Security Services Platform. When a host becomes unhealthy, vSphere HA migrates the affected Security Services Platform virtual machines to healthy hosts in the cluster. Admission Control should be configured with sufficient resource reservations so that recovery capacity is available. Configuring a storage policy with RAID provides datastore-level failure tolerance alongside vSphere HA. In environments using vSAN stretched clusters, the Security Services Platform benefits from storage replication across two physically distinct sites, providing site-level resilience and protection against multiple host failures within a single site. The License Hub component also supports vSphere HA for host-level failure recovery.

The Distributed Firewall operates at the hypervisor kernel level on every host, providing inherent distribution with no single point of failure for workload security policy enforcement. One operational characteristic to note: when vSphere HA is enabled and workload VMs restart on a new recovery host after a failure, all flows from those HA-impacted VMs are reinspected by the Distributed Firewall because vDefend cannot migrate Flow Table contents during host recovery. Policy enforcement applies to these VMs immediately upon restart on the recovery host.

These failover capabilities help vDefend's security enforcement layer remain operational during infrastructure failures. Network security policy enforcement, threat detection, and microsegmentation continue to function as the underlying compute and storage infrastructure undergoes failover events.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Meets)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) implements site-level failover capabilities to maintain availability of critical virtual machine workloads after a disruption.

Recovery plans automate the failover process between protected and recovery sites. During disaster recovery failover, if an error occurs on the protected site, the failover continues without interruption, prioritizing workload recovery. Recovery plans support both planned migration for orderly, scheduled failover and disaster recovery for unplanned site failures. PNR provides an API workflow through the VCF Operations Orchestrator Plug-In for PNR to initiate failover programmatically, enabling integration with automated orchestration frameworks.

PNR supports multiple replication methods that underpin the failover capability. vSphere Replication provides host-based replication with configurable recovery point objectives. A recovery option allows vSphere Replication to synchronize recent changes from the source site to the target before recovering virtual machines, reducing data loss during recovery operations. vSphere Replication supports up to 200 recovery points when using the Latest instances option, enabling selection of a specific point in time for recovery. Replication continues at the defined recovery point objective after vSphere High Availability restarts a virtual machine. Array-based replication leverages storage vendor capabilities for datastore-level failover, with a configurable option to wait for device rediscovery after failover. Automated failback is supported only for array-based replication. Virtual Volumes policy-driven replication provides a third replication method.

Failover and failback can be performed independently on sites, allowing isolated recovery operations without affecting other recovery sites. For stretched storage environments, PNR requires that the failover command isolate devices at the recovery site. IP customization is applied during the failover process, supporting DHCP, static IPv4, and IPv6 addressing so that recovered virtual machines are reachable on the recovery site network. Inventory mappings translate protected site resources to their recovery site equivalents.

PNR supports failover even when operating in degraded mode, though test failover and reprotect operations are not available in that state. Test failover allows organizations to validate their failover procedures without disrupting production replication. After a successful failover, the reprotect process reverses the direction of protection, making the recovery site the new protected site and restoring replication to prepare for failback.

PNR supports failover for Windows Server Failover Clustering (WSFC) workloads. The Cyber Recovery capability enables scheduled snapshot management through vSAN Data Protection groups for VM-level protection that integrates with the broader failover workflow.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) provides built-in high availability and failover mechanisms for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server database workloads through two HA topologies that organizations can select based on their availability requirements.

The Single vSphere Cluster HA topology co-locates all database nodes within one vSphere cluster. For PostgreSQL, DSM deploys a primary instance, one database-level synchronous replica, and a dedicated monitor node. The monitor node orchestrates automatic database-level failover: when the primary fails, the monitor detects the failure and promotes the synchronous streaming replica to the primary role without requiring manual intervention. MySQL under this topology uses a three-node configuration with a single primary and two database-level synchronous replicas sharing the same cluster. SQL Server uses a three-node configuration with Microsoft Always On availability groups, two synchronous replicas, and one configuration-only replica. After a SQL Server failover, the new primary maintains the security and management configurations from the previous primary instance. In all cases, DSM database nodes also rely on the underlying VCF platform for VM-level recovery through vSphere HA and Distributed Resource Scheduler, and on platform storage redundancy.

The Cross vSphere Cluster HA topology distributes database nodes across three separate vSphere clusters, reducing the blast radius of a single cluster failure. For PostgreSQL, this topology places a primary, a database-level replica, and a monitor on distinct clusters. For MySQL, a primary, a replica, and a monitor are distributed across three clusters. For SQL Server, Always On availability groups are configured with two synchronous replicas and one configuration-only replica, with each replica node in a separate vSphere cluster. Cross vSphere Cluster HA provides the highest availability and resilience for environments that require high availability across multiple failure domains. When configuring Cross-Cluster HA in an infrastructure policy, three clusters must be selected from the same datacenter and must share the same storage policies and distributed port group. The Enable-Cross-Cluster-HA infrastructure policy setting activates this topology. DSM raises a compliance alert if vSphere HA is not active on a cluster referenced by an infrastructure policy.

The DSM Provider Appliance management plane also relies on vSphere High Availability so the control plane can recover from a host failure without manual intervention.

For cross-site disaster recovery, DSM supports a multi-site deployment model for PostgreSQL that replicates data to a secondary site. When a failover to the secondary site is needed, DSM provides a switchover workflow to promote the secondary instance to the primary role. After the original site recovers, a failback switchover reverses the roles to restore the original topology. DSM also provides the ability to copy replication user connection strings from a primary instance so that secondary instances can be redirected to the new primary during failover operations. One documented limitation applies: PostgreSQL logical replication requires manual intervention to resume replication after the source cluster experiences a failover. When a MySQL HA cluster is used as the replication source, automatic source failover for read replicas requires the allocation of additional IP addresses from the IP pool for each HA node, and read replicas reconnect to another cluster member after several failed attempts, which can take a few minutes before the switch completes. For SQL Server, during a failover new writes are not protected with a second copy until the failed node rejoins the cluster.

DSM HA clusters require automated backups to be enabled in order to provision databases. DSM provides real-time alerts for resource and connectivity issues together with continuous performance monitoring, which supports operators in detecting conditions that may require a failover response. Webhook notifications can also be configured so that external systems are alerted on data service operation failures.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides multiple layers of real-time and near-real-time failover mechanisms for application delivery. These operate at the Service Engine data plane, at the load balancing pool level, and at the Controller management plane, working in conjunction with the availability capabilities of the VCF platform.

At the data plane, Avi's Elastic High Availability (Elastic HA) mode provides fast recovery for individual virtual services following Service Engine failure. Elastic HA supports both Active-Active and N+M configurations, giving administrators a choice of redundancy model suited to their requirements. When a virtual service is already scaled across multiple Service Engines and one fails, the virtual service switches over to the remaining available Service Engines; this recovery occurs before vSphere HA brings up a replacement Service Engine when virtual service slots are available in other Service Engines. The switchover time for Service Engines in vSphere HA failover scenarios is configurable per virtual service requirements.

For deployments requiring active/standby behavior, Avi supports Legacy Active/Standby High Availability mode, where a pair of Service Engines share a floating interface IP address for transparent failover. By default, the standby Service Engine sends health checks to pool members so that server health state is already known at the time a failover occurs, reducing recovery latency. For Layer 4 connections, enabling connection mirroring on Avi provides seamless failover and avoids disruption of existing connections during failover events.

The Autoredistribute Load option on Service Engine groups can be enabled to make fail-back automatic when load is concentrated on the failover Service Engine after a recovery event.
