At the pool level, Avi implements priority-based pool failover logic. The deactivate_primary_pool_on_down setting enables automatic deactivation of the primary pool when it becomes unavailable, triggering automatic failover to a designated secondary pool. The Avi Controller ControlScript can also execute failover operations on pool groups triggered by Pool Up and Pool Down events.

For cross-fault-domain resilience, Availability Zone support enables spreading Service Engines across transport nodes that do not share common failure points, such as shared power supplies or shared networking devices. Defining Availability Zones based on fault domains helps keep virtual services available across fault conditions that affect a single zone. At the Controller plane, Controller cluster deployments can span two availability zones with defined failure scenarios and recovery actions for each configuration.

For global application availability across data centers, Avi GSLB supports Active-Active configuration where applications are deployed in multiple data centers and instances in remaining data centers can take over user traffic if a data center becomes unavailable.

Service Engine VM-level recovery in host failure scenarios depends on vSphere HA from the underlying VCF platform. Avi documentation recommends enabling vSphere HA on the vSphere clusters where Service Engine VMs are deployed. In VCF-integrated deployments, vSphere DRS and HA must be enabled on each vSphere cluster used with Avi, with vSphere DRS configured in Fully Automate mode. VMware HA provides failure protection for a single-node Avi Controller by dynamically migrating and restarting the virtual machine on another server in the event of server failure; enabling vSphere HA is similarly recommended for the vSphere cluster where Avi License Hub is deployed. Administrators should note that if all Service Engines are placed on a single host and that host fails, there is no high availability or immediate failover, making host distribution a deployment planning consideration. Administrators must configure both Avi HA policies and the vSphere cluster settings to fully address this control.

### Control SI-16

> Memory Protection Implement the following controls to protect the system memory from unauthorized code execution: [Assignment: organization-defined controls].

**SCF Controls:** SEA-10

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides multiple layers of memory protection that safeguard system memory from unauthorized code execution and information disclosure. These protections operate at the hardware, hypervisor, virtual machine, and container levels, giving administrators a defense-in-depth approach to memory security.

At the hardware level, VMware ESX supports Secure Boot, which validates the boot chain to confirm that only signed, trusted code loads during startup. Trusted Platform Module (TPM) integration enables measured boot, recording each stage of the boot process so that the host's integrity can be attested after startup. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide includes recommendations for enabling Secure Boot and TPM on ESX hosts. VMware vCenter monitors host attestation state and can retrieve the TPM event log for configuring firmware trust with an attestation service or validating boot-time TPM measurements. Individual virtual machines can also be provisioned with a virtual Trusted Platform Module (vTPM), which provides VMs with TPM 2.0-compatible functionality for secure key storage and guest-level measured boot without requiring physical TPM hardware on the underlying ESX host.

For workloads that require hardware-enforced memory encryption, VCF supports AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization with Encrypted State (SEV-ES). When SEV-ES is enabled, the processor encrypts guest operating system memory in hardware, making the contents unreadable even to the hypervisor itself. SEV-ES also detects malicious modifications to CPU register state. Confidential vSphere Pods can run on a Supervisor with SEV-ES enabled, providing hardware memory encryption for all workloads running on the pod. This capability addresses scenarios where memory contents must be protected from physical attack vectors or from compromise at the hypervisor layer. VM classes in vSphere Supervisor can also be configured with Software Guard Extensions (vSGX) to create hardware-protected memory enclaves for sensitive computations. For Windows guest operating systems, vSphere supports Virtualization-based Security (VBS), which uses hypervisor isolation to create a protected memory region for key OS services; VBS also enables Configurable Code Integrity, which restricts code execution to signed, trusted code starting from the bootloader onward.

At the hypervisor level, ESX enforces strict memory isolation between virtual machines. Each VM operates within its own address space, and the hypervisor mediates all memory access to restrict one VM from reading or modifying another VM's memory. This isolation is a fundamental architectural property of the vSphere platform. ESX also supports eager zeroing of host memory through the Mem.MemEagerZero setting, which clears sensitive data from memory pages when they are released. This restricts residual data from being accessible to subsequently scheduled workloads, reducing the risk of information disclosure across VM boundaries.

Virtual machine encryption in vCenter extends protection to memory-related files stored on disk. When VM encryption is enabled, it protects NVRAM, VSWP (swap), and VMSN (snapshot) files, all of which may contain memory contents written to persistent storage. This means that even when memory state is paged to disk or captured in a snapshot, it remains encrypted and inaccessible without the appropriate key material. Access to encryption operations is governed by fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, which allow administrators to control who can perform encryption tasks and limit the blast radius of compromised credentials. In safe mode, ESX hosts can encrypt virtual machines without exposing key material.

vSphere Configuration Profiles and the Configuration Drifts feature in VCF Operations provide a proactive method to monitor configuration changes across vCenter instances and vSphere clusters. This drift detection capability helps confirm that host-level security settings, including those related to memory protection such as Mem.MemEagerZero and Secure Boot configuration, remain in their intended state. The drift status data is updated through a collection cycle that runs every 8 hours.

The VCF consumption layer, which encompasses VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS), vSphere Supervisor, and Kubernetes workload management, extends memory protection to containerized workloads through several Kubernetes-native controls. Pod Security Admission (PSA) enforcement is configured at the namespace level in VKS clusters; the Baseline security template restricts host namespace sharing and prohibits privileged container modes that could expose node memory to container processes, and the Restricted profile also requires containers to drop all Linux capabilities while enforcing seccomp profiles. Seccomp support in VKS clusters restricts the Linux kernel system calls available to container processes, with the RuntimeDefault profile providing a baseline syscall allowlist that reduces the attack surface for memory-manipulation exploits; Supervisor internal containers enforce RuntimeDefault seccomp profiles on their own system call access. Container memory limits bound the memory each container process can consume, and namespace-level resource quotas enforce aggregate limits across pods in a namespace, scoping the impact of any workload that attempts to exhaust node memory. The kubelet system-reserved and kube-reserved settings allow node administrators to reserve memory for system processes and the kubelet itself. Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) for Supervisor and VKr components provide hardening guidance covering seccomp enforcement and capability restrictions at the Kubernetes node level. Access to the vmware-system-privileged Pod Security Policy should be scoped to service accounts that require privileged execution; granting it broadly to all authenticated users disables these namespace-level security controls cluster-wide.

Administrators should enable eager zeroing of memory on ESX hosts where security requirements call for it, configure VM encryption for workloads handling sensitive data, and evaluate SEV-ES for environments requiring hardware-level memory confidentiality.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to memory protection through its Malware Prevention capabilities, which detect and respond to unauthorized code execution within system memory. While the broader VCF platform handles hardware-rooted memory protections (Secure Boot, TPM, SEV, TDX) and hypervisor-level workload isolation, vDefend addresses the runtime threat detection layer that identifies when attackers attempt to execute malicious code in memory.

The Malware Prevention Service includes fileless malware detection, which identifies attacks that operate within system memory using existing binaries and in-memory operations without leaving executable artifacts on disk. The service intercepts in-memory scripts through Microsoft's Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) to capture execution traces of scripts running directly within process memory, including PowerShell, VBScript, and JScript. This AMSI-based detection applies to supported versions of Microsoft Windows; whether the AMSI framework routes malicious in-memory script buffers to Malware Prevention depends on whether Windows Defender is registered as the primary scan provider on the guest. Windows virtual machines receive this protection through the Distributed Malware Prevention with Guest Introspection deployment model, which requires Guest Introspection drivers to be installed in the VM.

The Malware Prevention Service monitors process activities, parent-child relationships, and in-memory script buffer content to identify potential attacks. It tracks Indicators of Compromise (IoC) including process command lines, process hashes, process execution trees, and in-memory executions. The service collects evidence of the full process chain leading to execution of the final malicious in-memory script buffer before it executes. A single in-memory script buffer execution can be attributed to multiple malware families and classes simultaneously. When in-memory script execution is detected, the service assigns a threat verdict, providing visibility into unauthorized code execution attempts within protected workloads.

Security teams can use the Malware Prevention Dashboard's Process Inspections capability to view all in-memory script buffers executed by a process and access detailed reports submitted to the cloud for analysis. The Process Analysis Report displays verdict information for each inspected process and stores analysis data with an Analyst UUID field to track inspection events. The service also extracts artifacts and in-memory script buffers to enable security teams to define incidents of compromise and create new malware detection rules for similar future threats.

These runtime memory protection capabilities complement the hardware and hypervisor controls provided by the broader VCF platform. vDefend detects unauthorized code execution at the workload level after an attacker has bypassed lower-level protections, providing defense in depth for memory protection.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SI-17

> Fail-safe Procedures Implement the indicated fail-safe procedures when the indicated failures occur: [Assignment: organization-defined list of failure conditions and associated fail-safe procedures].

**SCF Controls:** SEA-07.3

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF implements fail-safe procedures across the compute, storage, networking, orchestration, and lifecycle management layers, providing automatic responses to failure conditions at each tier.

At the compute layer, vSphere HA defines automated restart procedures for virtual machines when host failures occur. HA detects host unavailability through network heartbeat monitoring and triggers VM restart on surviving hosts. Admission control policies can be set to Cluster Resource Percentage, Slot Policy, or Dedicated Failover Hosts, each reserving capacity so that failover procedures have the resources they need to complete. For workloads that require continuous availability, vSphere Fault Tolerance maintains a synchronized secondary VM on a separate host, providing rapid failover when the primary host experiences a failure condition. vCenter High Availability (vCenter HA) extends fail-safe protection to the VMware vCenter management layer itself, guarding against host failures, hardware failures, and vCenter application failures. Fault Tolerance failover behavior can be validated before production reliance using the Test Restart Secondary feature, which induces a secondary VM failure to confirm that the protection is functioning correctly. vMotion enables live migration of running virtual machines during planned maintenance, allowing administrators to evacuate workloads before performing host-level changes. vSphere Supervisor zones further strengthen failure isolation by treating each zone as an independent failure domain, so failures within one zone do not affect others.

At the storage layer, vSAN incorporates several fail-safe mechanisms. Fault domains distribute data replicas across physically separate groups of hosts, so that data remains available even when failures occur within a domain. The failures to tolerate (FTT) storage policy controls how many simultaneous failures vSAN can absorb while maintaining data availability. When a component failure occurs, vSAN automatically redirects writes from the failed component to a durability component, maintaining data integrity without manual intervention. On permanent failure, vSAN rebuilds affected components on other hosts in the cluster to restore the policy-defined level of protection. Administrators can configure failback behavior to suit their environment; for example, setting failback to "No" in environments using Spanning Tree Protocol avoids problems caused by automatic failback when a physical switch port recovers from failure and takes several seconds to begin forwarding traffic. Proactive Hardware Management (PHM) monitors hardware health and informs administrators of degrading devices based on predictive failure events from the OEM, enabling action before data loss occurs. VMware recommends planning and designing vSAN upgrades to be fail-safe as well.

For broader disruption scenarios, Application Disaster Recovery enables failover to a secondary site to maintain operations during major disruptions. At the networking layer, NSX provides documented recovery procedures for Global Manager failures, including scenarios where both active and standby instances fail. In failure scenarios involving risk of data loss or catastrophic failure, documented procedures direct administrators to contact Broadcom Support to review the recovery plan before taking remediation steps.

VCF Operations extends fail-safe behavior into the orchestration and automation layer. Workflows support structured exception handling, where exception handlers catch errors and react according to the specific errors received. Elements within a workflow can set exception bindings that define how the workflow behaves when it encounters an error in that element. If the built-in exception handlers cannot handle a particular error, the element's exception output parameter can be bound to an Exception element, which ends the workflow run in a controlled failed state rather than an undefined condition. For user interaction elements, defining exception behavior is a recommended practice; if a user does not provide input parameters within the timeout period, the interaction returns an exception, and administrators should define the exception behavior in a scripted function to avoid uncontrolled workflow failure.

VCF Operations also provides alerting mechanisms that detect and surface failure conditions. Policy-based alert definitions use combinations of symptoms and recommendations to identify conditions classified as problems, such as failures or high stress. Risk and warning alerts indicate conditions requiring near-term investigation. VCF Operations for Networks raises alerts when a network configuration deviates from its defined intent, marking the intent status as failed and triggering investigation workflows.

Lifecycle management operations include built-in fail-safe steps. Upgrade and patching workflows run precheck validation that must complete before changes are applied, catching potential issues before they affect production systems. The vCenter upgrade validation operation runs upgrade prechecks and allows administrators to review errors, warnings, and status data before proceeding. VCF Operations provides a separate precheck capability that validates readiness before an upgrade proceeds. After any recovery operation, VCF requires verification of the recovered system's health and remediation of any issues found.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend includes several fail-safe mechanisms within its network security platform, primarily through the Security Services Platform (SSP).

The SSP enforces healthy-state prerequisites before executing backup and restore operations, blocking recovery procedures when the platform is degraded. SSP backup or restore operations enter a failed state after 30 minutes if internal services are restarted manually or due to failure during the operation. SSP must also be in a healthy state before the Malware Prevention Service can be activated, and Network Detection and Response (NDR) activation fails if the SSP cluster is in a degraded or unstable state. These gating behaviors help prevent security service activations and recovery procedures from proceeding from a compromised baseline.

The SSP monitors current deployment status and raises platform-related alarms to highlight errors requiring attention. When a scheduled backup fails, SSP triggers a Scheduled Backup Error alarm to notify administrators. SSP generates an alarm when Bare Metal servers fail to update their certificates, and displays publish status with error indicators in the Status column to identify publication failures requiring troubleshooting. Certificate manager and security intelligence services can be verified for operational status to diagnose service availability issues. Security Intelligence suspicious traffic detectors report execution status after each run, with possible values of NOT_STARTED, SUCCESS, NOT_ENOUGH_BASELINE, or FAILURE, giving operators visibility into detection system health.

For SSP deployments, vSphere HA (a VCF platform capability) is recommended for host-level failure recovery, with Admission Control configured with sufficient resources for recovery. Storage policies with RAID configuration support datastore-level failure tolerance. For deployments on vSAN stretched configurations, resilience protects SSP data from multiple host failures within a single site.

Broader organizational fail-safe procedures covering the infrastructure as a whole, including the processes that define how and when these mechanisms are invoked, remain outside vDefend's scope.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) provides fail-safe behavior through controlled recovery operations and configurable safety thresholds. During disaster recovery, if errors occur on the protected site, PNR continues the recovery operation rather than aborting, so a failure at the protected site does not block workload recovery at the recovery site. If the protected site goes offline unexpectedly during recovery, the failure is reported but otherwise ignored, allowing recovery to proceed without both sites being available. Custom command steps within a recovery plan have type-based failure handling so an operator can specify how the plan responds when a step does not succeed.

By contrast, planned migration is treated as a controlled operation rather than an emergency. If errors occur on the protected site during a planned migration failover, the operation stops and requires manual error resolution and plan rerun before proceeding. This distinguishes routine migration from disaster recovery semantics and preserves operator control during non-emergency moves.

Site-down detection thresholds are configurable to reduce the risk of premature failover declarations from transient connectivity issues. The connections.drPingFailedDelay parameter controls how many consecutive status check failures must occur before PNR declares a disaster recovery connection failure, and connections.hmsPingFailedDelay controls the equivalent threshold for health management checks.

PNR surfaces operational health through VCF Operations dashboards and the PNR Global Console. The Configuration Summary Dashboard generates critical warnings when a recovery plan is in an error state, when a plan ran while two sites were disconnected and a split-brain condition resulted, and when reprotect fails for a plan. PNR also alerts users to minor errors including expired snapshot schedules, replication errors such as missing VMs, connectivity issues with protected or destination sites, and site misconfigurations that require investigation. The Clean Room Orchestrator raises warning-status alerts for the same conditions in cyber recovery workflows. Recovery plans should be tested before use for planned migration or disaster recovery to validate functionality before production execution.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides application-layer fail-safe mechanisms at multiple levels, enabling organizations to define predictable, controlled behavior when failure conditions occur at the load balancing and application delivery layer.

At the server pool level, Avi supports configurable fail action behavior. When all pool servers become unavailable, the Pool Fail Action setting determines how Avi responds. The HTTP Local Response option serves a custom error page to clients, presenting a controlled failure state rather than an unhandled connection timeout. DataScript and policy-driven POOL_DOWN actions provide an additional method for generating custom error responses when backend pools are unavailable.

Health monitors control failure detection sensitivity through the Failed Checks parameter, which specifies the number of consecutive failed checks required before marking a server as down. In the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO), the HealthMonitor custom resource definition exposes the same failed_checks parameter, with a valid range of 1 to 50 and a default value of 2, extending configurable fail-safe behavior to Kubernetes-native workloads. The corresponding successful_checks parameter governs recovery, requiring a configurable number of consecutive successful checks before marking a backend as UP again.

At the Service Engine level, Avi's failure detection algorithm declares a Service Engine suspect down after four consecutive echo messages fail over an eight-second window under standard settings. An aggressive failure detection mode is available for environments requiring faster detection but is recommended only for bare-metal deployments; on virtualized environments, this mode can produce false positives.

Avi's Internet Content Adaptation Protocol (ICAP) Profile supports configurable failure action handling when ICAP servers encounter errors. Administrators can select Fail Closed, which returns an HTTP 503 response, or Fail Open, which allows traffic to pass through when the ICAP service is unavailable. This gives organizations direct control over the security-versus-availability trade-off for content inspection failures.

Avi's Global Server Load Balancing (GSLB) uses both control-plane and data-plane health monitors to detect site failures. When monitors at both planes mark members at a failed site as Down, GSLB redirects application traffic to surviving sites. In partial-site failure scenarios, the leader and peer Controllers detect the failure using control-plane monitors, but data-plane health monitors at surviving sites may continue to perceive members at the failed site as UP. Administrators should be aware that automatic GSLB leader failover will not occur if both the leader and the designated backup leader sites are simultaneously unavailable. Custom scripts used in external GSLB health monitors should be thoroughly validated for long-term stability before deployment against production servers.

Avi supports Availability Zones based on fault domains associated with shared power supplies, shared networking devices, or other common infrastructure. Configuring Service Engines across these zones can keep virtual services continuously available across fault conditions. For Linux Server Cloud deployments, traffic directed on a failing link within a port channel interface is automatically redirected to other links, providing link-level fault tolerance.

Avi ControlScript alert actions execute in response to detected events such as Pool Up and Pool Down and can programmatically invoke failover operations on pool groups through the Avi Controller API. Automation workflows using Python or Ansible modules support error handling and retry logic to handle exceptions gracefully and reduce the risk of partial configurations or failed deployments.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control SR-01

> a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]: 1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System- level] supply chain risk management policy that: (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and 2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the supply chain risk management policy and the associated supply chain risk management controls; b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the supply chain risk management policy and procedures; and c. Review and update the current supply chain risk management: 1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and 2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

**SCF Controls:** GOV-02, GOV-03, TPM-01

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SR-09

> Implement a tamper protection program for the system, system component, or system service.

**SCF Controls:** AST-15

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple layers of logical integrity assessment for detecting evidence of tampering across the infrastructure stack, addressing the configuration-evaluation aspect of this control.

At the hardware and firmware level, VCF supports Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT), and confidential computing technologies such as AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV) and Intel Trust Domain Extensions (TDX). TPM event logs can be retrieved through the Trusted Infrastructure API to validate boot-time measurements. VMware vCenter monitors host attestation state to verify that hosts have booted into known-good configurations. These capabilities establish a hardware root of trust against which logical integrity can be assessed.

The VMware ESX hypervisor enforces code signing to restrict installation and execution of unauthorized code. Management pack PAK files are signed with digital signatures that indicate the original developer or publisher, and VCF Operations displays a warning if a PAK file lacks a valid signature. Workloads are isolated from each other and from the hypervisor itself.

For configuration integrity, vSphere Configuration Profiles enable desired-state configuration management with drift detection. VCF Operations extends this through its Configuration Drifts feature, which proactively monitors configuration changes on vCenter instances and vSphere clusters, compares current values against assigned desired values, and reports when settings have deviated from the intended configuration. Drift detection runs automatically every 8 hours. vSphere Configuration Profiles must be enabled on vSphere clusters for drift status to be visible in VCF Operations.

VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure by evaluating collected data against defined policies and assigns a compliance score to each object or group based on how well they adhere to those policies. Configuration drift or unauthorized changes to critical components such as vCenter instances, ESX hosts, virtual machines, distributed port groups, and distributed switches are surfaced as compliance violations and recorded for investigation. Administrators can review these violations in the compliance management interface, which highlights risk areas and provides actionable remediation recommendations. Custom compliance benchmarks can be created to monitor specific alert definitions and policies tailored to an organization's requirements, allowing integrity assessments to be scoped to the assets and configurations most critical to the environment.

VCF Operations also includes a Findings Catalog within its Diagnostics capability, which displays the complete list of signatures checked in the environment at any given time. These signatures function as integrity checks against known-good configurations and expected states, enabling operators to detect deviations that may indicate tampering or unauthorized modification. Diagnostic Findings provide a consolidated view of known product issues, security exposures, and best-practice recommendations.

VCF Operations provides compliance checking capabilities that evaluate infrastructure against the Security Configuration Guide and industry-standard benchmarks, identifying non-compliant objects and surfacing associated violations. The platform can discover objects that are not compliant with a security standard, providing a systematic method for identifying configuration deviations across the environment. The isolation.device.edit.disable property definition restricts the ability to alter device configurations on virtual machines, providing an additional hardening measure.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages host software images and checks cluster compliance against a defined image specification. Clusters can have a compliance status of COMPLIANT or NON_COMPLIANT, providing another layer of logical integrity verification by detecting when host software deviates from the intended image.

At the network layer, the NSX System Health Agent monitors component statistics, analyzes them for abnormalities, and triggers alerts and remediation actions when specific conditions are met, providing automated integrity monitoring for network infrastructure components. NSX also includes a system health dashboard and API methods to monitor the health and performance of the network environment.

For storage integrity, vSAN uses end-to-end checksums to verify data integrity during read and write operations. If a checksum mismatch is detected, vSAN automatically repairs the data by overwriting the incorrect copy with the correct one. On vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA), object checksums are always enabled and cannot be deactivated; for vSAN OSA, it is recommended to enable object checksum. Proactive Hardware Management (PHM) informs administrators of potential device failures based on disk predictive failure events generated by OEM vendors. Configurable object repair timers define how long vSAN waits before repairing non-compliant objects, with a default of 60 minutes, balancing transient versus sustained failure responses.

For virtual machine integrity, Secure Boot for virtual machines provides guest operating system integrity verification through UEFI Secure Boot support. Virtual machines can also be equipped with a Virtual Trusted Platform Module (vTPM) device, which provides VM-level cryptographic attestation for guest workloads. For Windows guest operating systems, enabling Virtualization-based Security (VBS) activates Credential Guard, which isolates key system and user secrets against compromise, and Configurable Code Integrity, which restricts code execution to trusted software from the boot loader onward. Certificate validation continuously monitors certificate validity, expiration, and trust chains across VCF components. The VCF Operations Certificates widget surfaces certificates that require attention based on their expiration date, and VCF Operations also displays certificate expiration status per workload domain. NSX Manager certificate expiry scanning identifies appliance certificates due to expire within 31 days.

For vCenter specifically, hardening guidance covers securing the vCenter installation, securing access and authentication, configuring roles and permissions, and enabling auditing and logging. These measures collectively establish a baseline configuration against which logical integrity can be assessed.

For workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor, the Consumption layer extends logical integrity assessment into the Kubernetes control plane and workload runtime. The Kubernetes API server produces a configurable audit log that records every request made to the cluster, including create, update, patch, and delete operations on configuration objects such as ConfigMaps, Secrets, Role definitions, RoleBindings, and admission policy resources. Audit records capture the requesting user, timestamp, resource type, and request and response objects at configurable verbosity levels, and logs can be forwarded to an external audit webhook backend. For high-assurance environments, cryptographic protections can be applied to audit logs to make them tamper-resistant and confidential.

Admission control provides configuration integrity enforcement at the Kubernetes API boundary. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy and ValidatingAdmissionWebhooks allow administrators to define rules that restrict which changes may be applied to Kubernetes resources, protecting critical configuration objects and platform namespaces from unauthorized modification. When a request violates a policy, the API server rejects the request and generates an audit record of the attempt.

The Pod Security Admission (PSA) controller enforces the Kubernetes Pod Security Standards across namespaces, with three operational modes: enforce mode rejects non-compliant pods, audit mode records violations in the audit log without disrupting running workloads, and warn mode generates warnings without rejection. Administrators can use audit and warn modes to continuously assess whether workload configurations deviate from the defined security baseline. The PSA controller evaluates security context fields including runAsNonRoot, allowPrivilegeEscalation, seccomp profiles, Linux capabilities, and volume types. The crictl inspect command can be used to examine the seccomp profile applied to a running container, providing a runtime verification capability for workload security configuration.

Container artifact integrity is addressed through Harbor Registry, available as a standard package on VKS clusters. Harbor provides vulnerability scanning and content trust (image signing) to assess the integrity of container artifacts before and during deployment. Kubernetes deployments can be configured to verify the cryptographic identity of container image artifacts at admission time, using digital certificates and other validation mechanisms for supply chain assurance. Harbor secures its artifact storage through policies and role-based access control, restricting who may push, pull, or modify images in the registry. VCF Services Consumption guidance directs operators to use container images that have been scanned, signed, and approved according to organizational supply chain policies. For CI/CD workflows, the Harness CI/CD supervisor service integrates with Wiz, which authenticates to the artifact registry, pulls container images, and performs vulnerability scanning as part of the pipeline.

Kubernetes supports immutable workload patterns in which containers are replaced rather than modified in place. Immutability can be enforced through automated reconciliation that overwrites unauthorized changes or through controllers configured to reject in-place modification of specific object fields. When combined with version-controlled infrastructure-as-code practices, this pattern creates a durable audit log of every authorized change to a system, making unauthorized configuration drift detectable through comparison against the declared state.

Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) are available for Supervisor and Kubernetes release (VKr) components, providing a defined set of configuration settings against which the hardening state of a Supervisor deployment can be assessed as a logical integrity baseline.

VCF's logical integrity assessment capabilities are strong for infrastructure configuration monitoring, compliance drift detection, and data integrity verification. However, this control also requires physical assessments to evaluate assets for evidence of unauthorized access or physical modifications. Physical security assessments, including inspection of hardware for tampering indicators, are organizational responsibilities that fall outside VCF's scope as a software platform. Organizations must implement separate physical security inspection programs, asset tagging, and tamper-evident controls to satisfy that aspect of the control.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) provides logical integrity mechanisms for AI model assets through a pre-upload validation workflow performed in the Deep Learning VM (DL VM) sandbox. Before a machine learning model is uploaded to the Model Gallery, the DL VM performs hash code integrity verification, malware scanning, serialization attack scanning, and inference testing using Triton Inference Server. Hash code verification detects modification to the model artifact during transit or storage, malware scanning addresses tampered executable content, and serialization attack scanning checks for malicious code embedded in the serialized data structures that represent model weights and architecture. These checks address tampering classes that are specific to AI workloads.

The validation workflow operates as an MLOps gate. MLOps engineers validate models against the organization's security, privacy, and technical requirements before publishing them to the Model Gallery, where AI application developers and other MLOps engineers can then retrieve them. Tampering-relevant checks run before models enter the trusted artifact store, which reduces the risk that a tampered model reaches production AI services.

Once stored, the Model Gallery maintains integrity of uploaded models by assigning a unique digest to each revision when the same model is pushed multiple times, which supports detection of unauthorized changes between revisions. PAIS integrates with Harbor registries as the model storage backend and requires authentication and certificate-based trust for model retrieval, so model pulls verify the registry identity and the model digest before deployment. MLOps engineers and application developers can also examine inference requests for malicious behavior and perform hands-on functional tests before deploying a model for production use.

These mechanisms address the logical integrity assessment dimension of the control for the AI model component of PAIS's technology assets. The physical tampering assessment dimension and the broader assessment of all critical technology assets beyond ML models fall outside PAIS's scope and require organizational processes and, where applicable, the underlying VCF platform.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports logical integrity assessment of its own application delivery components through several mechanisms in 9.1.

The Avi Controller maintains a checksum and creation timestamp for custom IPAM script files, providing file integrity tracking and an audit trail that can surface unauthorized modifications to scripts managing IP address management (p2551). This is a direct logical integrity mechanism within Avi's own configuration plane.

The Avi Controller provides an Application Security Report, available for download in PDF format, that captures WAF findings and security configuration status in a format accessible to both technical teams and executive stakeholders (p3479). This report gives auditors a structured view of whether the Avi deployment's WAF posture conforms to its expected security baseline and can be used as evidence during periodic integrity assessments.

Avi Load Balancer supports FIPS 140-3 compliance mode, which enforces validated cryptographic modules and stricter handling of cryptographic keys, authentication data, and random number generation (p3001). Deviations from the FIPS 140-3 configuration state can serve as indicators of unauthorized changes to the cryptographic posture.

In Kubernetes environments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) performs syntactical validations on Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) at creation time, verifying configuration correctness and providing status messages that surface configuration deviations from the expected resource state (p83).

The Avi Controller and Service Engines log audit records for certificate validation failures, authentication failures, and SSH session establishment failures, including identification of the initiator and target of failed trusted channel establishment attempts (p4157). These records provide signals that may indicate tampered configurations or certificate substitution.

Physical integrity of the underlying compute infrastructure falls under VCF, which provides hypervisor-level integrity controls including Secure Boot and TPM attestation on VMware ESX hosts. Physical assessment of the hardware on which Avi Controller and Service Engine VMs run is handled at the VCF platform layer, not within Avi itself.

Organizations are responsible for scheduling execution of integrity assessments on a recurring basis and for acting on the results to remediate detected configuration drift. Avi provides the assessment mechanisms; the operational process of scheduling, reviewing, and remediating those assessments remains an organizational responsibility.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SR-09(01)

> Tamper Resistance and Detection | Multiple Stages of System Development Life Cycle Employ anti-tamper technologies, tools, and techniques throughout the system development life cycle.

**SCF Controls:** AST-15

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

For vCenter specifically, hardening guidance covers securing the vCenter installation, securing access and authentication, configuring roles and permissions, and enabling auditing and logging. These measures collectively establish a baseline configuration against which logical integrity can be assessed.

For workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor, the Consumption layer extends logical integrity assessment into the Kubernetes control plane and workload runtime. The Kubernetes API server produces a configurable audit log that records every request made to the cluster, including create, update, patch, and delete operations on configuration objects such as ConfigMaps, Secrets, Role definitions, RoleBindings, and admission policy resources. Audit records capture the requesting user, timestamp, resource type, and request and response objects at configurable verbosity levels, and logs can be forwarded to an external audit webhook backend. For high-assurance environments, cryptographic protections can be applied to audit logs to make them tamper-resistant and confidential.

Admission control provides configuration integrity enforcement at the Kubernetes API boundary. ValidatingAdmissionPolicy and ValidatingAdmissionWebhooks allow administrators to define rules that restrict which changes may be applied to Kubernetes resources, protecting critical configuration objects and platform namespaces from unauthorized modification. When a request violates a policy, the API server rejects the request and generates an audit record of the attempt.

The Pod Security Admission (PSA) controller enforces the Kubernetes Pod Security Standards across namespaces, with three operational modes: enforce mode rejects non-compliant pods, audit mode records violations in the audit log without disrupting running workloads, and warn mode generates warnings without rejection. Administrators can use audit and warn modes to continuously assess whether workload configurations deviate from the defined security baseline. The PSA controller evaluates security context fields including runAsNonRoot, allowPrivilegeEscalation, seccomp profiles, Linux capabilities, and volume types. The crictl inspect command can be used to examine the seccomp profile applied to a running container, providing a runtime verification capability for workload security configuration.

Container artifact integrity is addressed through Harbor Registry, available as a standard package on VKS clusters. Harbor provides vulnerability scanning and content trust (image signing) to assess the integrity of container artifacts before and during deployment. Kubernetes deployments can be configured to verify the cryptographic identity of container image artifacts at admission time, using digital certificates and other validation mechanisms for supply chain assurance. Harbor secures its artifact storage through policies and role-based access control, restricting who may push, pull, or modify images in the registry. VCF Services Consumption guidance directs operators to use container images that have been scanned, signed, and approved according to organizational supply chain policies. For CI/CD workflows, the Harness CI/CD supervisor service integrates with Wiz, which authenticates to the artifact registry, pulls container images, and performs vulnerability scanning as part of the pipeline.

Kubernetes supports immutable workload patterns in which containers are replaced rather than modified in place. Immutability can be enforced through automated reconciliation that overwrites unauthorized changes or through controllers configured to reject in-place modification of specific object fields. When combined with version-controlled infrastructure-as-code practices, this pattern creates a durable audit log of every authorized change to a system, making unauthorized configuration drift detectable through comparison against the declared state.

Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) are available for Supervisor and Kubernetes release (VKr) components, providing a defined set of configuration settings against which the hardening state of a Supervisor deployment can be assessed as a logical integrity baseline.

VCF's logical integrity assessment capabilities are strong for infrastructure configuration monitoring, compliance drift detection, and data integrity verification. However, this control also requires physical assessments to evaluate assets for evidence of unauthorized access or physical modifications. Physical security assessments, including inspection of hardware for tampering indicators, are organizational responsibilities that fall outside VCF's scope as a software platform. Organizations must implement separate physical security inspection programs, asset tagging, and tamper-evident controls to satisfy that aspect of the control.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) provides logical integrity mechanisms for AI model assets through a pre-upload validation workflow performed in the Deep Learning VM (DL VM) sandbox. Before a machine learning model is uploaded to the Model Gallery, the DL VM performs hash code integrity verification, malware scanning, serialization attack scanning, and inference testing using Triton Inference Server. Hash code verification detects modification to the model artifact during transit or storage, malware scanning addresses tampered executable content, and serialization attack scanning checks for malicious code embedded in the serialized data structures that represent model weights and architecture. These checks address tampering classes that are specific to AI workloads.

The validation workflow operates as an MLOps gate. MLOps engineers validate models against the organization's security, privacy, and technical requirements before publishing them to the Model Gallery, where AI application developers and other MLOps engineers can then retrieve them. Tampering-relevant checks run before models enter the trusted artifact store, which reduces the risk that a tampered model reaches production AI services.

Once stored, the Model Gallery maintains integrity of uploaded models by assigning a unique digest to each revision when the same model is pushed multiple times, which supports detection of unauthorized changes between revisions. PAIS integrates with Harbor registries as the model storage backend and requires authentication and certificate-based trust for model retrieval, so model pulls verify the registry identity and the model digest before deployment. MLOps engineers and application developers can also examine inference requests for malicious behavior and perform hands-on functional tests before deploying a model for production use.

These mechanisms address the logical integrity assessment dimension of the control for the AI model component of PAIS's technology assets. The physical tampering assessment dimension and the broader assessment of all critical technology assets beyond ML models fall outside PAIS's scope and require organizational processes and, where applicable, the underlying VCF platform.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) supports logical integrity assessment of its own application delivery components through several mechanisms in 9.1.

The Avi Controller maintains a checksum and creation timestamp for custom IPAM script files, providing file integrity tracking and an audit trail that can surface unauthorized modifications to scripts managing IP address management (p2551). This is a direct logical integrity mechanism within Avi's own configuration plane.

The Avi Controller provides an Application Security Report, available for download in PDF format, that captures WAF findings and security configuration status in a format accessible to both technical teams and executive stakeholders (p3479). This report gives auditors a structured view of whether the Avi deployment's WAF posture conforms to its expected security baseline and can be used as evidence during periodic integrity assessments.

Avi Load Balancer supports FIPS 140-3 compliance mode, which enforces validated cryptographic modules and stricter handling of cryptographic keys, authentication data, and random number generation (p3001). Deviations from the FIPS 140-3 configuration state can serve as indicators of unauthorized changes to the cryptographic posture.

In Kubernetes environments, the Avi Kubernetes Operator (AKO) performs syntactical validations on Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) at creation time, verifying configuration correctness and providing status messages that surface configuration deviations from the expected resource state (p83).

The Avi Controller and Service Engines log audit records for certificate validation failures, authentication failures, and SSH session establishment failures, including identification of the initiator and target of failed trusted channel establishment attempts (p4157). These records provide signals that may indicate tampered configurations or certificate substitution.

Physical integrity of the underlying compute infrastructure falls under VCF, which provides hypervisor-level integrity controls including Secure Boot and TPM attestation on VMware ESX hosts. Physical assessment of the hardware on which Avi Controller and Service Engine VMs run is handled at the VCF platform layer, not within Avi itself.

Organizations are responsible for scheduling execution of integrity assessments on a recurring basis and for acting on the results to remediate detected configuration drift. Avi provides the assessment mechanisms; the operational process of scheduling, reviewing, and remediating those assessments remains an organizational responsibility.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SR-10

> Inspection of Systems or Components Inspect the following systems or system components [Selection (one or more): at random; at [Assignment: organization-defined frequency], upon [Assignment: organization-defined indications of need for inspection]   ] to detect tampering: [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].

**SCF Controls:** AST-15.1, TDA-11

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF contributes to technology asset inspection by providing logical inspection capabilities that continuously monitor infrastructure components for configuration drift, anomalous behavior, and policy violations. While VCF cannot perform physical inspections of hardware assets, its monitoring, compliance, and forensic tooling enables organizations to detect logical evidence of tampering across the managed environment.

VCF Operations compliance management continuously monitors infrastructure against industry-standard best practices and custom policies. When a managed object deviates from its expected configuration baseline, VCF Operations detects the violation, highlights the affected risk area, and provides actionable remediation recommendations. This continuous compliance monitoring functions as an automated logical inspection mechanism, alerting administrators when the state of a technology asset diverges from its approved configuration, which may indicate tampering or unauthorized modification.

VCF Operations Configuration Drifts and vSphere Configuration Profiles extend this capability by comparing the current configuration state of VMware vCenter instances and vSphere clusters against a defined desired state. Configuration Drifts provides a proactive method to monitor configuration changes and helps maintain consistent configuration across the environment. Configuration drift detection compares the current vCenter value against the configuration template value for each vCenter instance, making it possible to detect unauthorized or unintentional changes to host and cluster configurations. vSphere Configuration Profiles must be enabled on vSphere clusters to view drift status in VCF Operations.

Event auditing in VCF Operations provides visibility into platform interaction changes across the VCF infrastructure. The event audit trail captures changes to managed objects, user actions, and surfaces suspicious access events and policy violations, giving administrators a record of who changed what and when. VCF Operations auditing reports provide traceability of objects and users in the environment: the User Permissions Audit report tracks user accounts, their roles, access groups, and access privileges, while the User Activity Audit report traces user activities including logging in, actions on clusters and nodes, changes to system passwords, activating certificates, and logging out. VCF Automation extends this with its own audit logging, capturing reportable actions such as blueprint deployments, catalog item operations, IaaS resource changes, and Day 2 deployment operations. Together, these audit trails allow inspectors to review the history of changes to technology assets and identify unauthorized or unexpected modifications.

VCF Operations diagnostics provides an additional layer of logical inspection through more than 600 property-based and log-based rules. Property-based findings identify conditions that might affect the environment, while log-based findings indicate issues that have already occurred. Diagnostic Findings consolidates known product issues, VMSA-based security exposures, and best-practice recommendations, helping administrators gain visibility into potential issues and risks and prioritize resolution efforts. The Troubleshooting Workbench Potential Evidence tab displays events, property changes, and anomalous metrics relevant to a specific alert, consolidating the information an inspector needs to evaluate whether tampering has occurred.

The Forensics Widget in VCF Operations supports investigative workflows by allowing administrators to search for and select objects in the environment, filter them through progressive configuration sections, and compare metric data across two time periods. This comparison capability is useful for identifying changes in asset behavior that may indicate tampering.

At the data layer, vSAN uses end-to-end checksums to verify the integrity of stored data by confirming that each copy of a file matches the source. If a checksum mismatch is detected, vSAN automatically repairs the data by overwriting the incorrect copy with the correct data, providing a continuous data integrity verification mechanism that can surface evidence of storage-level tampering or corruption.

At the hardware level, the vCenter Trusted Infrastructure API provides endpoints to retrieve TPM event log information for configuring firmware trust with an attestation service or validating boot-time TPM measurements. Administrators can also unseal secrets bound to an endorsement key to verify reported measurements from the TPM event log, supporting hardware-level integrity validation of VMware ESX hosts.

For virtual machines running on supported AMD hardware, vSphere provides VM-level attestation through AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization-Secure Nested Paging (SEV-SNP). An SEV-SNP-enabled virtual machine can generate an attestation report using the AMD VirTEE open source tool; the report is signed by a key accessible only by the AMD hardware and contains Trusted Computing Base version information including Microcode, SNP, TEE, and Boot Loader versions, supporting verification of the guest VM's security state and integrity. On Intel hardware with Trust Domain Extensions (TDX) support, vSphere can generate TD Quotes, which are signed attestation reports that allow auditors to verify the integrity of guest VMs using Intel hardware attestation.

The VCF Audit Guide provides additional guidance on the compliance conditions required for assessing compliance of VCF environments.

Organizations deploying VCF should supplement these logical inspection capabilities with physical inspection processes for data center hardware, network equipment, and storage devices. Physical tamper-evident seals, asset tracking, scheduled hardware inspections, and the organizational processes for conducting inspections fall outside VCF's scope and require separate procedures.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to the logical inspection dimension of this control through asset monitoring and visibility capabilities in the Security Services Platform (SSP).

The Platform Inventory Monitor Inventory Assets view filters infrastructure assets by Name and Status, identifies assets that are timed out or out of sync, and tracks environment hierarchy accuracy with status indicators for success, timeout, and out-of-sync conditions. Environment rows can be expanded to view assigned tags and verify environment hierarchy accuracy. Error indicators flag regions and zones with undefined or outdated hierarchy definitions. Platform Inventory organizes monitored assets into Regions and Zones that can be viewed and filtered by status.

During segmentation analysis, SSP identifies and publishes application assets, classifying them as either critical or regular applications to support prioritized inspection of high-value assets. Segmentation Monitoring provides investigation capabilities to identify potential security risks in application traffic and enables corrective action. The application monitoring screen displays application information and potential security risks, supporting security risk assessment and remediation.

Security Intelligence, accessible through the Security Explorer within SSP, organizes workloads into asset collections including groups, infrastructure assets, environments, applications, and application tiers. Security Intelligence detects and categorizes suspicious events using MITRE tactics and techniques, recording occurrence counts over selected time periods to support detection of anomalous behavior.

VMware vDefend IDS/IPS provides logical inspection support through packet capture (PCAP) export for forensic investigation, and stores TLS/SSL certificates on disk for forensic analysis and threat investigation. Signatures and PCAP files can be exported in TAR format for detailed analysis. The Malware Prevention dashboard in SSP monitors file inspection events and provides breakdowns by analysis type (local and cloud file analysis) and by malware family, contributing to detection of indicators of compromise on monitored assets.

Physical inspection of hardware assets for evidence of tampering is outside vDefend's scope and must be addressed through physical security programs, hardware attestation, and tamper-evident controls.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SR-11

> Component Authenticity a. Develop and implement anti-counterfeit policy and procedures that include the means to detect and prevent counterfeit components from entering the system; and  b. Report counterfeit system components to [Selection (one or more): source of counterfeit component;    [Assignment: organization-defined external reporting organizations]   ;    [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]   ].

**SCF Controls:** TDA-11

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SR-11(01)

> Train [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] to detect counterfeit system components (including hardware, software, and firmware).

**SCF Controls:** TDA-11.1

**Aggregate Coverage:** Not Applicable

Not applicable for this control: VCF, VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SR-11(02)

> Maintain configuration control over the following system components awaiting service or repair and serviced or repaired components awaiting return to service: [Assignment: organization-defined system components].

**SCF Controls:** MNT-07

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several capabilities that help organizations maintain configuration control over infrastructure components during maintenance activities, though the physical security aspect of this control falls outside the platform's scope and requires separate organizational procedures.

VCF enforces a structured maintenance mode workflow for ESX hosts and other managed objects. ESX hosts can be placed into maintenance mode through multiple interfaces: the vCenter UI, the Set-VMHost PowerCLI cmdlet, or programmatically through the EnterMaintenanceMode_Task and ExitMaintenanceMode_Task API methods on the HostSystem managed object. Access to maintenance mode operations is governed by the Host.Config.Maintenance privilege in vCenter, which must be explicitly assigned before a user can place hosts into or out of maintenance mode or perform associated shutdown and restart operations. When a host enters maintenance mode, VCF coordinates the migration of running workloads to other hosts in the cluster and restricts certain operations on the host, helping to preserve a known-good configuration state while service or repair activities are underway. vSphere Lifecycle Manager integrates with maintenance mode during remediation. If a host fails to enter maintenance mode during remediation, administrators can configure the failureAction policy to specify retry attempts and delay intervals before the remediation fails. By default, vSphere Lifecycle Manager tries to put a host into maintenance mode three times with a five-minute delay between each try. Update Manager also integrates with maintenance mode, allowing administrators to configure maintenance mode settings when remediating hosts with patches or updates. VCF policy requires that configuration updates be performed during a designated maintenance window.

VCF Operations extends configuration control through scheduled and manual maintenance mode capabilities. Administrators can place objects into maintenance mode from a specified start time until a specified end time on recurring days, or manually for an indefinite or fixed period. During maintenance windows, VCF Operations suppresses metric collection and alert generation for the affected objects, which prevents false-positive alerts from interfering with the maintenance workflow while still maintaining the underlying configuration baseline. Policies can be assigned to groups with maintenance schedule settings, enabling consistent maintenance practices across related infrastructure objects. Administrators can also schedule automation jobs to run during maintenance windows for actions that do not require manual supervision, such as reclaim actions for idle VMs or old snapshots. VCF Operations maps out-of-the-box workflows to recommendations and associates them with specific alerts for automated remediation.

For ongoing configuration assurance, VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure against industry-standard policies and custom configuration baselines. Configuration drift detection compares the current state of infrastructure objects against known-good baselines and flags deviations. Configuration templates can be managed through a supported source control solution, providing version-controlled configuration state that can be synchronized to and from a repository. This means that before and after any maintenance activity, the configuration state of an asset can be compared against the known-good baseline to detect unauthorized or unintended changes. After maintenance is complete, compliance monitoring resumes and flags configuration drift that may have been introduced during the service window.

Maintenance releases for VCF itself are scoped to include bug and security fixes, hardware enablement changes, driver updates, and backward-compatible features with limited scope to minimize operational impact.

Hardware integrity verification is available through Trusted Platform Module (TPM) support. ESX host TPM module status can be verified, and hosts with TPM modules must run the latest 2.0 firmware. The TPM event log can be retrieved for configuring firmware trust with an attestation service or validating boot-time TPM measurements. TPM, combined with Secure Boot, provides a hardware root of trust that helps detect tampering with the host boot process, which is relevant when assets return from maintenance or repair. The TPM uses Platform Configuration Register (PCR) measurements to seal the ESX host configuration to the hardware; if the host is modified during maintenance, the PCR state will no longer match the sealed configuration and the host will fail attestation, providing a mechanism to detect unauthorized changes after service. For this sealing to function correctly, the TPM must be configured in BIOS to use the SHA-256 hashing algorithm and the TIS/FIFO (First-In, First-Out) interface rather than the CRB (Command Response Buffer) interface. ESX host hardening guidance covers configuring authentication and authorization settings, disabling unnecessary services, and enabling security features like Secure Boot and TPM.

Physical security measures for assets awaiting service or repair, such as restricted storage areas, chain of custody documentation, and physical tamper seals, are organizational responsibilities outside VCF's scope.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend supports configuration control during maintenance through centralized policy management, configuration backup and restore capabilities in Security Services Platform, and draft-based policy change management in the Distributed Firewall.

Security Services Platform provides backup and restore of configuration data, protecting against data corruption or accidental deletion during maintenance activities. The backup scope includes tokens, Bare Metal servers, and Bare Metal server interfaces as part of the entire inventory backup. Backup and restore operations are restricted to the Enterprise Admin role and require the platform to be in a healthy state.

The publication control capability in Security Services Platform allows administrators to stop publishing of pending inventory assets and policies while leaving already-published assets unaffected. Environment rules, inventory assets, and policies can be published individually or collectively as distinct operations. This gives administrators granular control over what configuration reaches the live enforcement plane during maintenance windows.

vDefend (DFW) includes a draft-based policy management system. Policy configurations can be saved as named manual drafts, cloned, and locked with a required comment. Auto drafts are also maintained automatically. Saved drafts can be loaded and published to become the active configuration, providing a structured path to stage, review, and commit firewall policy changes before and after maintenance activities.

In federated deployments, a Global Manager can centrally manage IDS/IPS signatures, profiles, DFW and Gateway Firewall (GFW) policies, oversubscription settings, and syslog settings across all Local Manager sites. Security Services Platform role-based access control assigns the Security Engineer role responsibility for building and maintaining security policies, while the Enterprise Admin role governs backup and restore operations.

Physical security of technology assets awaiting service or repair is outside vDefend's scope and must be addressed through physical security controls and asset management processes.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control SR-12

> Component Disposal Dispose of [Assignment: organization-defined data, documentation, tools, or system components] using the following techniques and methods: [Assignment: organization-defined techniques and methods].

**SCF Controls:** AST-09

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides several capabilities that support secure logical disposal and sanitization of virtual resources, though the physical destruction or secure disposal of hardware remains an organizational responsibility outside VCF's scope.

vSAN data-at-rest encryption uses a two-tier key architecture with Key Encryption Keys (KEK) and Data Encryption Keys (DEK), managed through an external Key Management Server (KMS) or vSphere Native Key Provider. When decommissioning encrypted storage, destroying the encryption keys renders the data unrecoverable without requiring a full disk wipe, a technique known as cryptographic erasure. vSAN also provides an "Erase disks before use" option during encryption configuration that wipes existing data from storage devices. For devices being added to or removed from vSAN disk groups, residual data and partition information can be cleaned before the device is reclaimed or released. Administrators can wipe ineligible disks through the vSAN Management API after verifying the partition table and stored data. vSphere provides API methods for cryptographic key lifecycle management, including removeKey and removeKeys operations that delete encryption keys from host caches. These methods support a force parameter for definitive key removal.

VCF Operations includes a Reclamation dashboard and associated settings that help administrators identify virtual resources eligible for cleanup and disposal. The Reclamation Settings interface surfaces powered off VMs, idle VMs, snapshots, and orphaned disks, quantifying the resources that can be reclaimed. The Datastore Capacity Dashboard and ESX Capacity Dashboard both identify three specific reclamation opportunities: powered off VMs, snapshots, and orphaned VMDKs. These dashboards give operations and capacity teams visibility into resources that should be reviewed for secure removal before equipment is repurposed or decommissioned. The Reclamation Report tracks CPUs reclaimed, memory reclaimed, and storage reclaimed across different time periods, providing evidence of resource cleanup activity.

For the actual deletion of virtual resources, VCF Operations provides a DELETE VM(s) capability that removes selected powered off VMs, and a RECLAIM RESOURCES function that allows immediate execution of resource reclamation processes. VCF Automation extends this with a Delete action on Deployments that destroys an entire deployment and deletes all associated resources for reclamation. VCF Automation also supports the creation and deletion of secret reusable properties through its secrets management interface under Administration Secrets, which is relevant when sanitizing credential material before equipment reuse.

VCF Operations can disable virtual disk wiper and disk shrink operations on virtual machines through the isolation.tools.diskWiper.disable setting, which blocks data recovery attempts from within guest operating systems.

Core dump handling is another area where VCF provides disposal-relevant guidance. VMware recommends considering disabling core dumps on VMware vCenter systems to protect sensitive information. Unlike virtual machine core dumps, which are encrypted when VM encryption is active, vCenter core dumps are not encrypted by default; vCenter systems support core dump encryption through the core-dump-encryption setting, and encrypted core dumps can be decrypted or re-encrypted as needed. Core dumps may contain sensitive information such as host keys, and organizations should follow their security and privacy policies to protect this data. When decommissioning or repurposing hosts, addressing core dumps is an important step for reducing the likelihood of information recovery.

VCF also recommends securely deleting decrypted backup files after restore operations for VCF Operations, vCenter, and NSX components.

Auditors should verify that the organization has defined procedures for securely disposing of or repurposing VCF-managed infrastructure. This includes confirming that virtual resources (VMs, snapshots, orphaned disks, secrets) are deleted through VCF's reclamation and deletion tools before physical equipment is removed from service, and that encryption keys are destroyed via cryptographic erasure for storage that held sensitive data. Physical chain-of-custody procedures, media sanitization verification, and equipment tracking through the disposal lifecycle are organizational processes outside the scope of VCF.

Not applicable for this control: VMware vDefend, VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager, VMware Avi Load Balancer. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.
